topics.title: Analysis of Steam Cycle, Steam Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Steam Cycle, Steam Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Steam Power Cycle — Foundational Definition

In thermal power plant engineering, the **Rankine Cycle** is the fundamental thermodynamic model used to analyze the energy conversion process in a steam-based power generation system. It is an *idealized closed loop* that describes how thermal energy from a fuel (coal, biomass, or nuclear) is converted into useful mechanical work — and ultimately, electricity — using water as the working fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The Rankine Cycle is the *theoretical standard* against which the performance of all practical steam power plants is benchmarked. It is a *vapor-compression cycle* that mimics the operation of a steam engine, but in a continuous, closed-loop fashion.

### 1.2 The Four Cornerstones of the Rankine Cycle

| Process | Component | Thermodynamic Nature |
|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | **Feedwater Pump** | Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Compression |
| 2 → 3 | **Boiler (Steam Generator)** | Isobaric Heat Addition |
| 3 → 4 | **Steam Turbine** | Isentropic Expansion |
| 4 → 1 | **Condenser** | Isobaric Heat Rejection |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Recycled River" Engine

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy (The Mountain Stream):**
> Imagine a closed pipeline loop running between a mountain top and a valley:
> 1. At the valley (Condenser), water vapor cools back into liquid (condensation).
> 2. A small electric pump (Feedwater Pump) pushes the liquid up the mountain — this requires a *small* input work.
> 3. At the mountain top (Boiler), a fire heats the water into high-pressure steam — *large* amount of heat is added.
> 4. The high-pressure steam rushes back down the mountain through a turbine blade (Turbine), spinning a generator and producing electricity.
> 5. The used steam returns to the valley to repeat the cycle.
> 
> The Rankine cycle is essentially this exact closed loop, but with superheated steam instead of water. The *net useful work* is the difference between the turbine work output and the pump work input.

### 1.4 Why Not the Carnot Cycle?

A natural question that arises is — why don't we just use the Carnot cycle for steam plants? The answer lies in the **wetness problem**:

- In a Carnot cycle, isothermal heat addition requires the working fluid to be a **two-phase mixture (wet steam)**.
- Expanding wet steam in a turbine causes severe **blade erosion** due to water droplets.
- The Rankine cycle elegantly solves this by adding a **superheater** that ensures the steam entering the turbine is dry and superheated.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** The Rankine cycle is a *modified* version of the Carnot cycle, designed to overcome the practical limitations of using wet steam in turbines while maintaining a reasonable thermal efficiency.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics in Steam Cycle Analysis

The following metrics are standard in KTU examinations:

- **Net Work Output (W_net):** $W_{net} = W_{turbine} - W_{pump}$
- **Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$):** $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$
- **Specific Steam Consumption (SSC):** Amount of steam required per unit work output.
- **Heat Rate (HR):** Energy input per kWh of electrical output — typically **860 kcal/kWh** for an ideal cycle.
- **Back Work Ratio (BWR):** Ratio of pump work to turbine work — typically **less than 5%** for modern plants.
- **Specific Enthalpy Drop (SED):** $\Delta h = h_3 - h_4$ across the turbine.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Processes — Deep Operational Breakdown

#### **Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression in the Feedwater Pump**

- The saturated liquid leaving the condenser (State 1) is at a *low pressure* and *low temperature*.
- The pump compresses it to the *high boiler pressure* while keeping entropy constant.
- The work input to the pump is small because liquid water is nearly *incompressible*.
- The fluid remains in the **sub-cooled (compressed liquid)** region.

**Operational Logic:**
> Why isentropic? An *ideal* pump has no friction losses and is perfectly insulated, so the process follows constant entropy lines. In reality, actual pumps have isentropic efficiencies below 100%.

#### **Process 2 → 3: Isobaric Heat Addition in the Boiler**

- Heat from fuel combustion is transferred to the working fluid at *constant pressure*.
- Three sub-stages occur: Sensible heating → Evaporation → Superheating.
- The fluid transitions from compressed liquid → saturated liquid → saturated vapor → superheated vapor.
- State 3 is typically at the **turbine inlet** with high pressure and high temperature.

**Operational Logic:**
> Constant pressure is maintained because the boiler is designed to deliver steam at a steady pressure to the turbine. Any pressure drop would reduce the Rankine efficiency.

#### **Process 3 → 4: Isentropic Expansion in the Turbine**

- High-pressure, high-temperature steam expands through the turbine blades, producing rotational work.
- The process is *adiabatic and reversible* in the ideal case.
- State 4 typically lies in the **wet steam region** (mixture of saturated liquid and vapor).
- The steam is *wetter* at the exit than the inlet — this is acceptable for an ideal Rankine cycle.

**Operational Logic:**
> Turbine blades are designed to handle a small degree of moisture, but excessive wetness causes erosion. This is why superheating is critical before the turbine.

#### **Process 4 → 1: Isobaric Heat Rejection in the Condenser**

- Exhaust steam from the turbine is condensed back to saturated liquid at *constant pressure*.
- Heat is rejected to a cooling medium (river water, cooling tower, or air-cooled condensers).
- State 1 is the **saturated liquid** at the condenser pressure.

**Operational Logic:**
> Condenser pressure is kept as low as possible to maximize the pressure ratio across the turbine, but lower condenser pressure leads to higher moisture content at the turbine exit.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Rankine Cycle)

| Quantity | Ideal Formula | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pump Work | $W_P = v_1 (P_2 - P_1) = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Work input to pump (small) |
| Turbine Work | $W_T = h_3 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Work output from turbine (large) |
| Net Work | $W_{net} = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)$ | kJ/kg | Useful work output |
| Heat Added | $Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ | kJ/kg | Heat input in boiler |
| Heat Rejected | $Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Heat rejected in condenser |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_3-h_4)-(h_2-h_1)}{h_3-h_2}$ | Dimensionless | Cycle efficiency |
| Carnot Efficiency | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cond}}{T_{boiler}}$ | Dimensionless | Theoretical maximum |
| Back Work Ratio | $BWR = \dfrac{W_P}{W_T}$ | Dimensionless | Pump work as fraction of turbine work |
| Specific Steam Consumption | $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}}$ | kg/kWh | Steam per kWh |
| Heat Rate | $HR = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th}}$ | kJ/kWh | Energy per kWh |
| Specific Volume of Liquid | $v_1 \approx v_f$ at $T_1$ | m³/kg | Used in pump work |
| Quality at Turbine Exit | $x_4 = \dfrac{s_3 - s_f}{s_{fg}}$ at $P_4$ | Dimensionless | Moisture check |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Notice that the pump work term is often written as $v_1 (P_2 - P_1)$ rather than $(h_2 - h_1)$ when steam tables are not provided for the compressed liquid state. The two expressions are mathematically equivalent for an incompressible liquid approximation.

### 2.3 The Idealized Rankine Cycle T-s and h-s Diagrams

The cycle is represented on a **Temperature-Entropy (T-s)** diagram and an **Enthalpy-Entropy (h-s or Mollier)** diagram.

- **1 → 2:** Nearly vertical line (isentropic compression) — slight T increase.
- **2 → 3:** Curved line moving right (heat addition at constant P) — large T increase.
- **3 → 4:** Vertical line downward (isentropic expansion) — T drops significantly.
- **4 → 1:** Horizontal line leftward (heat rejection at constant P and T).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On the h-s diagram (Mollier chart):** The Rankine cycle is a quadrilateral with sides that are vertical (isentropic processes) and nearly horizontal (isobaric processes). The **area enclosed** represents the *net work output* of the cycle.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Rankine cycle is the *backbone* of the global power generation industry:

- **Coal-fired power plants** — typically operate at 150–250 bar and 540–600°C.
- **Nuclear power plants** — operate at lower pressures (60–70 bar) and saturated steam conditions.
- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** — uses solar collectors to provide heat input to the boiler.
- **Geothermal plants** — use low-temperature heat sources with organic Rankine cycles.
- **Combined Cycle Plants** — combine a gas turbine (Brayton) and steam turbine (Rankine) for higher overall efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Rankine Cycle

We begin from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to each component as a *steady-flow, control volume* system.

#### **Step 1: Energy Balance Across the Pump (1 → 2)**

For an adiabatic pump (no heat transfer, negligible kinetic and potential energy changes):

$$
h_1 + W_P = h_2
$$

Rearranging for pump work input:

$$
W_P = h_2 - h_1
$$

For an *incompressible liquid* with specific volume $v_1$:

$$
W_P = v_1 \, (P_2 - P_1)
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This step uses the thermodynamic identity for an incompressible fluid where $dh = v \, dP$ at constant temperature. Integrating from state 1 to state 2 yields the pump work per unit mass.

#### **Step 2: Energy Balance Across the Boiler (2 → 3)**

For a boiler (no work done, heat input $Q_{in}$):

$$
h_2 + Q_{in} = h_3
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The boiler is a constant-pressure heat exchanger. The first law reduces to $Q = \Delta h$ when no work is done and kinetic/potential energies are negligible.

#### **Step 3: Energy Balance Across the Turbine (3 → 4)**

For an adiabatic turbine (no heat transfer, negligible KE and PE):

$$
h_3 = h_4 + W_T
$$

Therefore:

$$
W_T = h_3 - h_4
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The turbine is treated as a *control volume* that delivers shaft work. The first law gives the work output as the enthalpy drop across the turbine.

#### **Step 4: Energy Balance Across the Condenser (4 → 1)**

For a condenser (no work, heat rejected $Q_{out}$):

$$
h_4 = h_1 + Q_{out}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The condenser is also a constant-pressure heat exchanger. Heat is rejected at constant temperature because the steam is in the two-phase region.

#### **Step 5: Net Work Output of the Cycle**

The net work output is the *difference* between turbine work and pump work:

$$
W_{net} = W_T - W_P
$$

$$
W_{net} = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the *only useful work* available to drive the electrical generator. The pump work is an *internal* work that the cycle must "give back" to itself.

#### **Step 6: Thermal Efficiency Definition**

By definition:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Net Work Output}}{\text{Heat Input}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **fundamental Rankine cycle efficiency equation** that students will use in 80% of all KTU numerical problems on steam cycles.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Simplified Efficiency (Neglecting Pump Work)

In many introductory problems, the pump work is **neglected** because it is typically **less than 1%** of the turbine work.

If $W_P \approx 0$, then $h_2 \approx h_1$, and the efficiency simplifies to:

$$
\eta_{th} \approx \frac{h_3 - h_4}{h_3 - h_1}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This approximation is valid when the *Back Work Ratio* is very small — typically the case for high-pressure modern plants. KTU problems often specify whether to include or neglect pump work.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Work Done Ratio (WDR) and Its Significance

The Work Done Ratio is defined as:

$$
WDR = \frac{W_{net}}{W_T} = 1 - \frac{W_P}{W_T} = 1 - BWR
$$

For an ideal Rankine cycle, WDR is close to **1.0** (typically 0.95–0.99), indicating that the pump work is a tiny fraction of the turbine work.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Quality at Turbine Exit

At the turbine exit (State 4), the steam is in the wet region. Using the entropy relationship:

$$
s_3 = s_4 = s_f + x_4 \, s_{fg} \quad \text{at } P_4
$$

Solving for quality $x_4$:

$$
x_4 = \frac{s_3 - s_f}{s_{fg}}
$$

The **dryness fraction** is $x_4$ and the **moisture content** is $(1 - x_4)$.

> **Conversion Logic:** This is critical for checking whether the turbine exit moisture is within acceptable limits. KTU boards typically expect $x_4 \geq 0.85$ for safe turbine operation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Operating Parameters on Rankine Cycle Performance

| Parameter Change | Effect on Thermal Efficiency | Effect on Turbine Exit Moisture | Practical Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Boiler Pressure | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases (wetter exit) | Higher plant cost, better steel required |
| ↑ Superheat Temperature | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases (drier exit) | Limited by metallurgical constraints |
| ↓ Condenser Pressure | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases (wetter exit) | Limited by cooling water temperature |
| Reheating | ↑ Increases significantly | ↓ Decreases dramatically | Adds complexity and capital cost |
| Regeneration | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | Standard in modern sub-critical plants |

### 3.6 Comprehensive Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
In an ideal Rankine cycle, steam enters the turbine at 3 MPa and 350°C. The condenser pressure is 10 kPa. Determine:
1. Pump work
2. Turbine work
3. Heat added
4. Thermal efficiency
5. Quality at turbine exit

**Given:**
- State 3: $P_3 = 3 \text{ MPa}$, $T_3 = 350°C$
- State 1: $P_1 = 10 \text{ kPa}$ (saturated liquid)
- Process 1→2: Isentropic
- Process 3→4: Isentropic

**Solution:**

**State 1: Saturated liquid at 10 kPa**
From steam tables:
- $h_1 = h_f \text{ at } 10 \text{ kPa} = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $v_1 = v_f \text{ at } 10 \text{ kPa} = 0.001010 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $s_1 = s_f \text{ at } 10 \text{ kPa} = 0.6492 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**State 3: Superheated steam at 3 MPa and 350°C**
From superheated steam tables:
- $h_3 = 3115.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $s_3 = 6.7428 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**State 2: After Isentropic Pump Compression**
Since pump work is for an incompressible liquid:

$$
W_P = v_1 \, (P_2 - P_1)
$$

$$
W_P = 0.001010 \times (3000 - 10) = 0.001010 \times 2990
$$

$$
W_P = 3.0199 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Therefore:

$$
h_2 = h_1 + W_P = 191.81 + 3.02 = 194.83 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**State 4: After Isentropic Turbine Expansion**
Since process 3→4 is isentropic:

$$
s_4 = s_3 = 6.7428 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

At $P_4 = 10 \text{ kPa}$, from saturated steam tables:
- $s_f = 0.6492 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $s_{fg} = 7.4996 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $h_f = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $h_{fg} = 2392.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$

Since $s_f < s_4 < s_g$, the steam is wet. Quality:

$$
x_4 = \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7428 - 0.6492}{7.4996}
$$

$$
x_4 = \frac{6.0936}{7.4996} = 0.8125
$$

Turbine exit enthalpy:

$$
h_4 = h_f + x_4 \, h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.8125 \times 2392.1
$$

$$
h_4 = 191.81 + 1943.58 = 2135.39 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Turbine Work:**

$$
W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 3115.3 - 2135.39 = 979.91 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Net Work Output:**

$$
W_{net} = W_T - W_P = 979.91 - 3.02 = 976.89 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Heat Added in Boiler:**

$$
Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3115.3 - 194.83 = 2920.47 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{976.89}{2920.47} = 0.3345
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = 33.45\%
$$

**Back Work Ratio:**

$$
BWR = \frac{W_P}{W_T} = \frac{3.02}{979.91} = 0.00308 = 0.308\%
$$

**Summary Table of Results:**

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Pump Work, $W_P$ | 3.02 | kJ/kg |
| Turbine Work, $W_T$ | 979.91 | kJ/kg |
| Net Work, $W_{net}$ | 976.89 | kJ/kg |
| Heat Added, $Q_{in}$ | 2920.47 | kJ/kg |
| Thermal Efficiency, $\eta_{th}$ | 33.45 | % |
| Quality at Exit, $x_4$ | 0.8125 | — |
| Back Work Ratio, BWR | 0.308 | % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic Block Diagram of the Ideal Rankine Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[STATE 1\nSaturated Liquid\nP1 Low, T1 Low] --> P1
    P1[FEEDWATER PUMP\nIsentropic Compression\nW_P = v1 P2 minus P1] --> A2[STATE 2\nCompressed Liquid\nP2 High, T2 Slightly Higher]
    A2 --> B1
    B1[BOILER and SUPERHEATER\nIsobaric Heat Addition\nQ_in = h3 minus h2] --> A3[STATE 3\nSuperheated Steam\nP3 High, T3 Very High]
    A3 --> T1
    T1[STEAM TURBINE\nIsentropic Expansion\nW_T = h3 minus h4] --> A4[STATE 4\nWet Steam Mixture\nP4 Low, x4 less than 1]
    A4 --> C1
    C1[CONDENSER\nIsobaric Heat Rejection\nQ_out = h4 minus h1] --> A1
```

### 4.2 T-s Diagram Representation of the Rankine Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TS_PLOT["T-s DIAGRAM - RANKINE CYCLE"]
        direction TB
        N1["STATE 1\nSaturated Liquid\nLow T, Low s"] -->|Process 1 to 2\nIsentropic Pump| N2["STATE 2\nCompressed Liquid\nSlightly Higher T"]
        N2 -->|Process 2 to 3\nIsobaric Heat Addition| N3["STATE 3\nSuperheated Steam\nHighest T"]
        N3 -->|Process 3 to 4\nIsentropic Expansion| N4["STATE 4\nWet Steam\nLower T than 3"]
        N4 -->|Process 4 to 1\nIsobaric Heat Rejection| N1
    end
```

### 4.3 h-s (Mollier) Diagram Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HS_PLOT["h-s DIAGRAM - RANKINE CYCLE"]
        direction LR
        M1["STATE 1\nLow h, Low s\nSaturated Liquid Line"] -->|Vertical Up\nPump Process| M2["STATE 2\nSlightly Higher h\nConstant s"]
        M2 -->|Move Right Upward\nBoiler Process| M3["STATE 3\nHighest h\nSuperheated Region"]
        M3 -->|Vertical Down\nTurbine Process| M4["STATE 4\nLower h than 3\nWet Steam Region"]
        M4 -->|Horizontal Left\nCondenser Process| M1
    end
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Complete Steam Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FUEL_HANDLING["FUEL HANDLING SYSTEM"]
        FH1[Coal Storage Yard] --> FH2[Crushers and Pulverizers]
    end
    
    subgraph BOILER_SYSTEM["BOILER AND STEAM GENERATION"]
        B1[Combustion Chamber] --> B2[Water Walls and Tubes]
        B2 --> B3[Superheater]
        B3 --> B4[Reheater Optional]
    end
    
    subgraph TURBINE_SYSTEM["TURBINE AND POWER GENERATION"]
        T1[High Pressure Turbine] --> T2[Intermediate Pressure Turbine]
        T2 --> T3[Low Pressure Turbine]
        T3 --> G1[Electrical Generator]
    end
    
    subgraph CONDENSER_SYSTEM["CONDENSER AND FEEDWATER"]
        C1[Condenser] --> C2[Cooling Tower or Water Source]
        C2 --> C1
        C1 --> P1[Feedwater Pump]
        P1 --> ECO[Economizer]
        ECO --> B2
    end
    
    FH2 --> B1
    B4 --> T1
    T3 --> C1
    G1 --> GRID[Electrical Grid Output]
```

### 4.5 Energy Flow Sankey Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["FUEL CHEMICAL ENERGY\n100 percent Q_fuel"] --> BOILER_EFF["BOILER LOSSES\n5 to 10 percent\nFlue Gas and Radiation"]
    BOILER_EFF --> TURBINE_EFF["TURBINE LOSSES\n5 to 15 percent\nIsentropic Efficiency"]
    TURBINE_EFF --> GENERATOR_EFF["GENERATOR LOSSES\n1 to 2 percent"]
    GENERATOR_EFF --> USEFUL["USEFUL ELECTRICAL OUTPUT\n30 to 40 percent Net"]
    TURBINE_EFF --> CONDENSER_LOSS["CONDENSER HEAT REJECTION\n45 to 55 percent"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Rankine cycle. List its four ideal processes with the corresponding state points.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Rankine cycle is the ideal thermodynamic cycle that forms the theoretical basis for analyzing steam power plants. It describes the continuous conversion of heat into work using a working fluid that undergoes phase changes.
>
> The four ideal processes are:
>
> 1. **Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression** in the feedwater pump — saturated liquid is compressed to boiler pressure.
> 2. **Process 2 → 3: Isobaric Heat Addition** in the boiler — water is converted to superheated steam at constant pressure.
> 3. **Process 3 → 4: Isentropic Expansion** in the turbine — steam expands to produce shaft work.
> 4. **Process 4 → 1: Isobaric Heat Rejection** in the condenser — exhaust steam is condensed to saturated liquid at constant pressure.

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the significance of superheating in the Rankine cycle. What happens if superheating is not done?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Superheating is the process of heating saturated steam to a temperature **above its saturation temperature** at a given pressure, before it enters the turbine.
>
> **Significance:**
> 1. It **increases the thermal efficiency** of the cycle by raising the average temperature of heat addition.
> 2. It **reduces the moisture content** of steam at the turbine exit, thereby preventing blade erosion.
> 3. It **increases the specific work output** of the turbine (greater enthalpy drop).
>
> **Without superheating:** The steam entering the turbine would be saturated, leading to excessive moisture formation during expansion. This causes **erosion of turbine blades**, reduced efficiency, and potential mechanical failure.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern

---

**Question B1 (A).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Derive an expression for the thermal efficiency of an ideal Rankine cycle. A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at 4 MPa and 400°C, and the condenser pressure is 20 kPa. Determine:**

**(a)** The pump work, turbine work, and net work output per kg of steam.

**(b)** The thermal efficiency of the cycle and the quality of steam at the turbine exit.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- State 3: $P_3 = 4 \text{ MPa}$, $T_3 = 400°C$ (Superheated steam)
- State 1: $P_1 = 20 \text{ kPa}$ (Saturated liquid)
- All processes are ideal (isentropic pump and turbine)

**Step 1: Properties at State 1 — Saturated Liquid at 20 kPa**

From steam tables at $P_1 = 20 \text{ kPa}$:
- $h_1 = h_f = 251.42 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $v_1 = v_f = 0.001017 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $s_1 = s_f = 0.8320 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**Step 2: Properties at State 3 — Superheated Steam at 4 MPa, 400°C**

From superheated steam tables:
- $h_3 = 3213.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $s_3 = 6.7690 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**Step 3: Calculate Pump Work and State 2 Enthalpy**

For an isentropic pump compressing an incompressible liquid:

$$
W_P = v_1 \, (P_2 - P_1) = 0.001017 \times (4000 - 20)
$$

$$
W_P = 0.001017 \times 3980 = 4.048 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

The enthalpy at state 2:

$$
h_2 = h_1 + W_P = 251.42 + 4.05 = 255.47 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Part (a) — Pump Work, Turbine Work, and Net Work:**

**Step 4: State 4 — After Isentropic Turbine Expansion**

Since process 3 → 4 is isentropic:

$$
s_4 = s_3 = 6.7690 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

At condenser pressure $P_4 = 20 \text{ kPa}$:
- $s_f = 0.8320 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $s_{fg} = 7.0752 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $h_f = 251.42 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $h_{fg} = 2357.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$

Since $s_4 = 6.7690 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ is between $s_f$ and $s_g$, the steam at exit is wet.

Quality of steam at turbine exit:

$$
x_4 = \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7690 - 0.8320}{7.0752} = \frac{5.9370}{7.0752}
$$

$$
x_4 = 0.8391
$$

Enthalpy at state 4:

$$
h_4 = h_f + x_4 \, h_{fg} = 251.42 + 0.8391 \times 2357.5
$$

$$
h_4 = 251.42 + 1978.16 = 2229.58 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Work Values**

Turbine work:

$$
W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 3213.6 - 2229.58 = 984.02 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Pump work (already computed):

$$
W_P = 4.05 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Net work output:

$$
W_{net} = W_T - W_P = 984.02 - 4.05 = 979.97 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Part (b) — Thermal Efficiency and Quality:**

Heat added in boiler:

$$
Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3213.6 - 255.47 = 2958.13 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{979.97}{2958.13} = 0.3313
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = 33.13\%
$$

Quality at turbine exit (already computed):

$$
x_4 = 0.8391
$$

**Final Results Table:**

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Pump Work, $W_P$ | 4.05 | kJ/kg |
| Turbine Work, $W_T$ | 984.02 | kJ/kg |
| Net Work, $W_{net}$ | 979.97 | kJ/kg |
| Heat Input, $Q_{in}$ | 2958.13 | kJ/kg |
| Thermal Efficiency, $\eta_{th}$ | 33.13 | % |
| Turbine Exit Quality, $x_4$ | 0.8391 | — |

---

**Question B1 (B) — Alternative Choice.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**With the help of a T-s diagram, explain the working of an ideal Rankine cycle. Compare it with the Carnot cycle operating between the same temperature limits. State any two reasons why the Carnot cycle is not preferred for steam power plants.**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Working of Ideal Rankine Cycle (T-s Diagram Explanation):**

The T-s diagram of the Rankine cycle consists of four processes forming a closed loop:

- **State 1:** Saturated liquid at the condenser pressure (low T, low s). Located on the saturated liquid line.
- **State 2:** Compressed liquid at boiler pressure. Slightly higher temperature than State 1, same entropy.
- **State 3:** Superheated steam at boiler pressure and high temperature. Located in the superheated region, far to the right of State 2.
- **State 4:** Wet steam at condenser pressure. Lower temperature than State 3, same entropy.

**Processes:**
- **1 → 2:** Near-vertical line upward (isentropic pump work). Small temperature rise.
- **2 → 3:** Curved line moving rightward and upward (isobaric heat addition in boiler).
- **3 → 4:** Vertical line downward (isentropic turbine work). Large temperature drop.
- **4 → 1:** Horizontal line leftward (isobaric heat rejection in condenser).

The area enclosed by the cycle on the T-s diagram represents the **net work output** of the cycle.

**Comparison with Carnot Cycle (Same Temperature Limits):**

| Feature | Carnot Cycle | Rankine Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| Heat Addition | Isothermal (requires wet steam) | Isobaric (with superheating) |
| Working Fluid State in Boiler | Two-phase mixture | Liquid → Vapor → Superheated Vapor |
| Turbine Inlet State | Wet steam (two-phase) | Superheated dry steam |
| Practical Realization | Difficult (pump must handle two-phase flow) | Easy (pump handles only liquid) |
| Cycle Shape on T-s | Two isothermal + two isentropic | Two isobaric + two isentropic |
| Thermal Efficiency (same T limits) | Higher | Slightly lower |
| Practical Use in Power Plants | Not used | Widely used |

**Two Reasons Why Carnot Cycle is Not Used for Steam Power Plants:**

1. **Wet Steam in Turbine:** In the Carnot cycle, heat addition is isothermal, which requires the working fluid to be a two-phase mixture. Expanding this wet steam in a turbine causes **severe blade erosion** due to the high-velocity water droplets, drastically reducing the turbine's operational life.

2. **Compressor/Pump Limitations:** Isothermal heat addition implies that the working fluid is in the two-phase region. A pump handling two-phase flow suffers from **cavitation and flow instabilities**, making it practically impossible to compress a wet mixture efficiently.

> Additional reason (often accepted in KTU): The Rankine cycle uses an **isobaric** heat addition process which is easily realized in a boiler operating at constant pressure, whereas the Carnot cycle's isothermal heat addition requires a heat source that can maintain constant temperature — practically impossible to achieve with typical combustion processes.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the pump work term** in the net work calculation. Many students compute $W_{net} = h_3 - h_4$ directly, which is *wrong*. The correct expression is $W_{net} = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)$.
>
> 2. **Using wrong steam table property** at the condenser. State 1 is **saturated liquid** ($h_1 = h_f$, not $h_g$). Using $h_g$ gives a wildly incorrect answer.
>
> 3. **Confusing $s_3$ and $s_4$**. They are **equal** for an *isentropic* turbine — $s_4 = s_3$. Students sometimes write $s_4 = s_3 + W_T/T$ which is dimensionally and conceptually wrong.
>
> 4. **Not checking if State 4 is in the wet region.** Always verify whether $s_4 < s_g$ at $P_4$. If yes, the steam is wet and you must calculate $x_4$ before finding $h_4$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units** — kJ/kg for enthalpy, kJ/kg·K for entropy, m³/kg for specific volume.
>
> 6. **Neglecting the sign convention** in the work terms: pump work is **input** (positive when computed as $h_2 - h_1$), turbine work is **output** (positive when computed as $h_3 - h_4$).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Steam Cycle Analysis:**

- The **Rankine cycle** is the ideal cycle for steam power plants, consisting of **two isentropic** and **two isobaric** processes.
- The four components are: **Feedwater Pump → Boiler → Turbine → Condenser**, returning to the pump in a closed loop.
- The **fundamental efficiency equation** is:
  $$\eta_{th} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$$
- The **pump work** is computed as $W_P = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ using the *incompressible liquid approximation*.
- The **turbine work** is $W_T = h_3 - h_4$ (isentropic).
- The **heat added** in the boiler is $Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ (isobaric).
- The **heat rejected** in the condenser is $Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ (isobaric).
- The **quality at turbine exit** is found using $x_4 = (s_3 - s_f)/s_{fg}$ at condenser pressure.
- The **Back Work Ratio (BWR)** is typically **less than 1%** for high-pressure plants, justifying the simplified efficiency formula $\eta_{th} \approx (h_3 - h_4)/(h_3 - h_1)$.
- The **Carnot cycle is impractical** for steam plants because it requires wet steam in the turbine, causing blade erosion.
- **Superheating** increases both efficiency and dryness of turbine exhaust — always check that $x_4 \geq 0.85$ for safe operation.
- On the **T-s diagram**: isentropic processes are vertical lines; isobaric processes are curved lines moving rightward in the superheat region.
- On the **h-s (Mollier) diagram**: the area enclosed by the cycle represents **net work output**.
- For a typical ideal Rankine cycle, thermal efficiency lies in the range **30–40%** for the given example parameters.
- **Specific Steam Consumption (SSC)** = $3600/W_{net}$ in kg/kWh — lower SSC means a more efficient plant.
- **Heat Rate (HR)** = $3600/\eta_{th}$ in kJ/kWh — lower HR is better.
- The **simplified efficiency** (neglecting pump work) is acceptable when the problem statement says *"neglect pump work"* or when BWR < 0.5%.
- Always **begin numerical problems** by identifying all four state points and listing known properties from steam tables before any calculation.
- The **direction of state numbering** follows the working fluid: 1 (pump inlet) → 2 (pump exit) → 3 (turbine inlet) → 4 (turbine exit).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Entropy Diagram
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature Entropy Diagram

# 📘 Module 1: Temperature–Entropy (T–s) Diagram

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Temperature–Entropy (T–s) Diagram** is a thermodynamic coordinate system in which the **absolute temperature (T)** of a working fluid is plotted on the **vertical (Y) axis** against the **specific entropy (s)** on the **horizontal (X) axis**. It is a rectangular plot whose primary purpose in power plant engineering is the **graphical analysis of energy transfers and cycle efficiencies** of thermodynamic cycles such as the **Rankine cycle (steam power plants)**, **Brayton cycle (gas turbine plants)**, and **Otto/Diesel cycles (IC engine plants)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The T–s diagram is the *single most important graphical tool* in PEMET631. KTU examiners specifically test the student's ability to **(a) identify the four states of a Rankine cycle on a T–s diagram**, **(b) compute the area enclosed by the cycle as net work output**, and **(c) interpret the saturation dome and critical point**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hilly landscape where the **height** of a hill represents the **temperature (T)** and the **horizontal distance** you walk represents the **entropy (s)**.

- A **flat horizontal walk** (constant T) means heat is being added or removed at a fixed temperature — just like walking along a level plateau.
- A **vertical climb** (constant s, no horizontal movement) means the process is **isentropic** — a perfectly insulated (reversible adiabatic) process, like climbing straight up a cliff with no sideways drift.
- The **shaded area under your walking path** is the **heat energy** that flowed into or out of the system.

In a **power plant cycle**, the fluid takes a closed "loop walk" on this landscape. The **net area enclosed by the loop** is the **net work output** of the cycle — this single insight is what makes the T–s diagram the engineer's favourite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric (KTU Board Standard):** The area under any process curve on a T–s diagram, $\int T \, ds$, gives the **heat transfer per unit mass (kJ/kg)** for that process.

### 1.3 Standard Reference States

- **Critical Point of Water:** $T_{cr} = 647.1 \text{ K} \; (374.15^\circ\text{C})$, $P_{cr} = 22.06 \text{ MPa}$
- **Triple Point of Water:** $T_{tp} = 273.16 \text{ K}$, $P_{tp} = 0.6113 \text{ kPa}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the T–s Diagram (Logical Breakdown)

A T–s diagram for a pure working substance (water for Rankine, air-fuel mixture for Otto/Diesel) has the following features:

1. **Saturated Liquid Line (Left Curve of the Dome):** Connects the saturated liquid states — at any point on this line, quality $x = 0$.
2. **Saturated Vapor Line (Right Curve of the Dome):** Connects the saturated vapor states — quality $x = 1$.
3. **Critical Point (C):** The apex of the dome where saturated liquid and vapor lines meet. Above this point, no distinction exists between liquid and vapor (supercritical region).
4. **Sub-cooled (Compressed Liquid) Region:** Lies to the **left** of the saturated liquid line.
5. **Superheated Vapor Region:** Lies to the **right** of the saturated vapor line.
6. **Wet Vapor / Two-Phase Region:** Lies **inside the dome**, where liquid and vapor co-exist.
7. **Constant Pressure (Isobaric) Lines:** In the wet region, these coincide with **constant-temperature lines** (horizontal). In the superheated region, they **diverge upward** with a positive slope.
8. **Constant Volume (Isochoric) Lines:** Steeper than isobars in the superheated region; both originate from a common tangent at the critical point.
9. **Constant Entropy (Isentropic) Lines:** Vertical straight lines.
10. **Constant Temperature (Isothermal) Lines:** Horizontal in wet region; upward curving in superheated region.

### 2.2 Why Each Process Looks the Way It Does

- **Boiling at Constant Pressure:** In the wet region, an isobar and an isotherm coincide. So water evaporating in a boiler travels **horizontally to the right** at constant T.
- **Turbine Expansion (Isentropic):** A turbine is assumed reversible adiabatic → $s = $ constant → **vertical line downward**.
- **Pump Compression (Isentropic):** Compressed liquid → vertical line down (very small horizontal change because liquid is nearly incompressible).
- **Condensation (Isobaric + Isothermal):** Horizontal line at the saturation temperature, moving from right to left.
- **Throttling:** A constant enthalpy process — moves down and to the right.

### 2.3 The "Area" Theorem (Central to KTU Problems)

$$\text{Area under process curve} = \oint T\,ds = \text{Heat Transfer } (Q)$$

- For a **closed cycle (loop)**, the **net enclosed area = Net Work Output** of the cycle.
- For a **counter-clockwise** loop → Refrigeration/Heat Pump cycle.
- For a **clockwise** loop → Power/Heat Engine cycle.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — KTU 2024 BOARD EXAM ESSENTIALS**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Heat transfer from T–s area | $Q = \int_{s_1}^{s_2} T\,ds$ | kJ/kg |
| 2 | Isothermal heat transfer | $Q = T\,(s_2 - s_1)$ | kJ/kg |
| 3 | Isentropic process heat | $Q = 0$ | (since $ds=0$) |
| 4 | Rankine net work (per kg) | $W_{net} = (h_1 - h_4) + (h_2 - h_3)$ | kJ/kg |
| 5 | Rankine thermal efficiency | $\eta_{Rankine} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_4) - (h_3 - h_2)}{h_1 - h_4}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Brayton net work | $W_{net} = c_p\,[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)]$ | kJ/kg |
| 7 | Brayton efficiency | $\eta_B = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | $r_p$ = pressure ratio |
| 8 | Otto cycle efficiency | $\eta_O = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{(\gamma-1)}}$ | $r$ = compression ratio |
| 9 | Diesel cycle efficiency | $\eta_D = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | $\rho$ = cutoff ratio |
| 10 | Mean Effective Pressure (Otto) | $P_{m,Otto} = \dfrac{P_1\, r\,[r^{\gamma-1} - 1]\,(\gamma - 1)}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)}$ | kPa |
| 11 | Mean Effective Pressure (Diesel) | $P_{m,Diesel} = \dfrac{P_1\,\gamma\,[r^{\gamma} - \rho^{\gamma} - r(\rho - 1)]}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)}$ | kPa |
| 12 | Carnot efficiency | $\eta_C = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world power plant design, the T–s diagram is used to **(i) visualize irreversibilities** (entropy increase shows up as rightward drift), **(ii) compute isentropic efficiencies of turbines and compressors**, and **(iii) compare ideal vs. actual cycle performance** without solving complex differential equations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Area Under T–s Curve = Heat Transfer (First Law Bridge)

**Starting Point:** The First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system (reversible):

$$dQ = dU + dW$$

For a **reversible process**, the Second Law gives:

$$dQ_{rev} = T\,ds$$

Now, integrate both sides between state 1 and state 2:

$$Q_{1\to 2} = \int_1^2 T\,ds$$

**Geometric Interpretation:** On a T–s diagram, the element $T\,ds$ is the area of an infinitesimal rectangle of height $T$ and width $ds$. Summing (integrating) these rectangles from $s_1$ to $s_2$ gives the **area under the process curve**. Therefore:

$$\boxed{Q_{1\to 2} = \text{Area under the process curve on T–s diagram}}$$

For a **cyclic process**, the integral over a complete closed loop gives the **net work**:

$$W_{net} = \oint T\,ds = \text{Net enclosed area of the cycle}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This result is the **theoretical foundation of every KTU problem** that asks for cycle efficiency from a T–s diagram.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Effective Pressure for Otto Cycle

The **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP or $P_m$)** is a fictitious constant pressure which, if acted on the piston during the full power stroke, would produce the same work as the actual variable-pressure cycle.

**Step 1 — Net work per cycle (Otto):** For the ideal Otto cycle with processes 1→2 (isentropic compression), 2→3 (constant volume heat addition), 3→4 (isentropic expansion), 4→1 (constant volume heat rejection):

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = m\,c_v\,[(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$$

**Step 2 — Express temperatures using compression ratio $r = V_1/V_2$ and $\gamma = c_p/c_v$:**

$$T_2 = T_1\,r^{\gamma - 1}, \quad T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{T_3}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $W_{net}$:**

$$W_{net} = m\,c_v \left[ T_3 - T_1 r^{\gamma-1} - \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} + T_1 \right]$$

Group the terms:

$$W_{net} = m\,c_v \left[ T_1 (1 - r^{\gamma-1}) + T_3 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \right) \right]$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $r^{\gamma-1}$:

$$W_{net} = m\,c_v \left[ \frac{T_1(r^{\gamma-1} - 1)(r^{\gamma-1} - 1)}{r^{\gamma-1}} + \frac{T_3(r^{\gamma-1} - 1)}{r^{\gamma-1}} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Use ideal gas law $P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$ and $V_1 - V_2 = V_1(1 - 1/r)$:**

$$P_m = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{P_1 \cdot r \cdot (r^{\gamma - 1} - 1) (\gamma - 1)}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)}$$

The $(\gamma-1)$ terms cancel to give the **final compact KTU-board expression**:

$$\boxed{P_{m,Otto} = \frac{P_1\, r\, (r^{\gamma - 1} - 1)}{r - 1}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mean Effective Pressure for Diesel Cycle

For Diesel: processes 1→2 (isentropic compression), 2→3 (constant pressure heat addition with cutoff ratio $\rho = V_3/V_2$), 3→4 (isentropic expansion), 4→1 (constant volume heat rejection).

**Step 1 — Net work:**

$$W_{net} = m \left[ c_p(T_3 - T_2) - c_v(T_4 - T_1) \right]$$

**Step 2 — State relations:**

$$T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}, \quad T_3 = T_2 \rho = T_1 r^{\gamma-1} \rho$$

$$T_4 = T_3 \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma-1} = T_3 \left(\frac{\rho V_2}{V_1}\right)^{\gamma-1} = T_1 r^{\gamma-1} \rho \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$

Simplify $T_4$:

$$T_4 = T_1 \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma}}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify the work expression using $c_p = \gamma R/(\gamma-1)$ and $c_v = R/(\gamma-1)$:**

$$\boxed{P_{m,Diesel} = \frac{P_1\, \gamma \left[ r^{\gamma} - \rho^{\gamma} - r(\rho - 1) \right]}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Both MEP derivations are **favourite 14-mark questions** in KTU ESE. Students are expected to derive the formula starting from the first law.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Process Slopes on T–s Diagram

| Process | Equation | Slope on T–s | Direction of Travel |
|---------|----------|--------------|---------------------|
| Isothermal | $T$ = constant | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial s}\right) = 0$ | Horizontal |
| Isentropic | $s$ = constant | Vertical | Vertical |
| Isobaric (ideal gas) | $ds = c_p\,dT/T$ | $dT/ds = T/c_p$ | Upward curve |
| Isochoric (ideal gas) | $ds = c_v\,dT/T$ | $dT/ds = T/c_v$ | Steeper than isobar |
| Polytropic | $PV^n$ = constant | $dT/ds = T/c_n$ | Depends on $n$ |
| Throttling | $h$ = constant | — | Downward + rightward |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — T–s Diagram Architecture with Saturation Dome

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["T-s Diagram Coordinate System"]:::root
    B["Critical Point C 647.1 K 22.06 MPa"]:::crit
    C["Subcooled Liquid Region Left of Dome"]:::sub
    D["Wet Vapor Two-Phase Inside Dome"]:::wet
    E["Superheated Vapor Region Right of Dome"]:::sup
    F["Saturated Liquid Line x=0"]:::line
    G["Saturated Vapor Line x=1"]:::line
    H["Constant Pressure Isobar Lines"]:::proc
    I["Constant Volume Isochore Lines"]:::proc
    J["Isentropic Lines Vertical"]:::proc
    K["Rankine Cycle 1-2-3-4-1 Clockwise Loop"]:::rank
    L["Brayton Cycle a-b-c-d-a Clockwise Loop"]:::bray
    M["Otto Cycle 1-2-3-4-1 Clockwise Loop"]:::otto

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    D --> F
    D --> G
    E --> H
    E --> I
    D --> J
    E --> K
    E --> L
    E --> M

    classDef root fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#1a252f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef crit fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#c0392b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sub fill:#3498db,stroke:#2874a6,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef wet fill:#1abc9c,stroke:#16a085,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sup fill:#e67e22,stroke:#d35400,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef line fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#6c3483,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#f39c12,stroke:#d68910,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef rank fill:#16a085,stroke:#0e6655,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bray fill:#2874a6,stroke:#1a5276,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef otto fill:#a93226,stroke:#922b21,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of T–s Diagram in Power Plant Cycle Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["State 1 Pump Inlet Saturated Liquid"]:::state1
    P2["State 2 Pump Outlet Compressed Liquid"]:::state2
    P3["State 3 Boiler Outlet Superheated Steam"]:::state3
    P4["State 4 Turbine Outlet Wet Vapor"]:::state4

    T1["Turbine Isentropic Expansion s3 s4"]:::turb
    B1["Boiler Isobaric Heat Addition Q_in"]:::boil
    C1["Condenser Isobaric Heat Rejection Q_out"]:::cond
    PP1["Pump Isentropic Compression s1 s2"]:::pump

    P1 --> PP1 --> P2
    P2 --> B1 --> P3
    P3 --> T1 --> P4
    P4 --> C1 --> P1

    AREA["Net Enclosed Area W_net Output"]:::area
    EFF["Thermal Efficiency W_net divided by Q_in"]:::eff

    T1 --> AREA
    B1 --> AREA
    C1 --> AREA
    PP1 --> AREA
    AREA --> EFF

    classDef state1 fill:#3498db,stroke:#1f618d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef state2 fill:#5dade2,stroke:#2874a6,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef state3 fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#922b21,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef state4 fill:#e67e22,stroke:#ba4a00,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef turb fill:#16a085,stroke:#0e6655,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef boil fill:#d35400,stroke:#a04000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cond fill:#2874a6,stroke:#1a5276,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pump fill:#7d3c98,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef area fill:#f39c12,stroke:#b9770e,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef eff fill:#27ae60,stroke:#196f3d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Process Line Identification Key

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["Boiler Process 3 to 4 Horizontal Right Isobar plus Isothermal in wet region"]:::boil
    A2["Turbine Process 1 to 2 Vertical Down Isentropic"]:::turb
    A3["Condenser Process 2 to 3 Horizontal Left Isobar plus Isothermal"]:::cond
    A4["Pump Process 4 to 1 Vertical Up Isentropic Liquid"]:::pump

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A1

    classDef boil fill:#d35400,stroke:#a04000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef turb fill:#16a085,stroke:#0e6655,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cond fill:#2874a6,stroke:#1a5276,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pump fill:#7d3c98,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note:** State numbering convention used here — `1` is pump inlet, `2` is pump outlet, `3` is turbine inlet, `4` is turbine outlet. This is the **standard KTU convention** (also matches Cengel & Boles textbook convention).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

*State the significance of the area under a process curve on a Temperature–Entropy (T–s) diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The area under a process curve on a T–s diagram, mathematically represented as $\int_{s_1}^{s_2} T\,ds$, gives the **heat transferred during that reversible process** (in kJ/kg). For a closed thermodynamic cycle, the **net area enclosed by the loop** represents the **net work output** of the cycle. This graphical integration technique is fundamental in power plant cycle analysis because it allows quick estimation of heat and work transfers without solving differential equations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**

*List the various processes of an ideal Rankine cycle on a steam power plant and represent them on a T–s diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four processes are:
1. **Process 1–2: Compression in pump** — isentropic (vertical line) in compressed liquid region.
2. **Process 2–3: Heat addition in boiler** — isobaric + isothermal (horizontal line) crossing the wet region, then curving upward in the superheated region.
3. **Process 3–4: Expansion in turbine** — isentropic (vertical line) ending in the wet region.
4. **Process 4–1: Heat rejection in condenser** — isobaric + isothermal (horizontal line) in the wet region returning to saturated liquid.

The cycle is **clockwise** on the T–s diagram, indicating net work output.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) of an Otto cycle**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A spark-ignition engine works on the ideal Otto cycle with a compression ratio of **8:1**. The initial pressure and temperature are **100 kPa** and **27 °C** respectively. The maximum temperature in the cycle is **2000 K**. Determine:
  (i) Pressure and temperature at all four states of the cycle,
  (ii) Thermal efficiency of the cycle,
  (iii) Mean effective pressure.

Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of MEP for Otto cycle (7 marks):**

The Otto cycle consists of:
- **1–2:** Isentropic compression
- **2–3:** Constant volume heat addition
- **3–4:** Isentropic expansion
- **4–1:** Constant volume heat rejection

**Step 1:** Net work per cycle is:

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = m\,c_v\,[(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$$

**Step 2:** For isentropic processes, $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$:

$$T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}, \quad T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting into $W_{net}$:

$$W_{net} = m\,c_v \left[ T_3 - T_1 r^{\gamma-1} - \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} + T_1 \right]$$

**Step 4:** Mean effective pressure:

$$P_m = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1(1 - 1/r)}$$

Using $P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$ and $c_v = R/(\gamma-1)$:

$$\boxed{P_{m,Otto} = \frac{P_1\, r\,(r^{\gamma-1} - 1)}{r - 1}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $r = 8$, $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $T_3 = 2000 \text{ K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.

**(i) State 2 (end of compression, isentropic):**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 300 \times 8^{0.4}$$

Compute $8^{0.4}$: $\ln(8) = 2.0794$, $0.4 \times 2.0794 = 0.8318$, $e^{0.8318} = 2.297$.

$$T_2 = 300 \times 2.297 = 689.1 \text{ K}$$

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 100 \times 8^{1.4}$$

Compute $8^{1.4}$: $1.4 \times 2.0794 = 2.9112$, $e^{2.9112} = 18.38$.

$$P_2 = 100 \times 18.38 = 1838 \text{ kPa}$$

**State 3 (end of heat addition, constant volume):**

$$T_3 = 2000 \text{ K}, \quad P_3 = P_2 \cdot \frac{T_3}{T_2} = 1838 \times \frac{2000}{689.1} = 5334 \text{ kPa}$$

**State 4 (end of expansion, isentropic):**

$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{2000}{2.297} = 870.7 \text{ K}$$

$$P_4 = \frac{P_3}{r^{\gamma}} = \frac{5334}{18.38} = 290.2 \text{ kPa}$$

**(ii) Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.297} = 1 - 0.4354 = 0.5646 = 56.46\%$$

**(iii) Mean effective pressure:**

$$P_m = \frac{P_1 \cdot r \cdot (r^{\gamma-1} - 1)}{r - 1} = \frac{100 \times 8 \times (2.297 - 1)}{8 - 1}$$

$$P_m = \frac{100 \times 8 \times 1.297}{7} = \frac{1037.6}{7} = 148.2 \text{ kPa}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]** *(Alternative choice)*

**(a)** Draw the **T–s diagram for an ideal Brayton cycle** and explain all four processes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A gas turbine operates on an ideal Brayton cycle with **air as the working fluid**. The compressor inlet conditions are **100 kPa, 300 K**, the pressure ratio is **10**, and the maximum cycle temperature is **1300 K**. Determine:
  (i) Temperatures at all four states,
  (ii) Net work output per kg of air,
  (iii) Thermal efficiency.

Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Brayton Cycle on T–s Diagram (7 marks):**

The Brayton cycle consists of:
1. **Process 1–2: Isentropic compression** in the compressor — **vertical downward line** (s = constant).
2. **Process 2–3: Constant pressure heat addition** in the combustion chamber — **line curving upward to the right** in the superheated region.
3. **Process 3–4: Isentropic expansion** in the turbine — **vertical downward line**.
4. **Process 4–1: Constant pressure heat rejection** — **line curving downward to the left**, returning to state 1.

**The cycle forms a closed clockwise loop** on the T–s diagram in the **superheated region** (since air remains a gas throughout — no phase change, no saturation dome relevant).

**Characteristics:**
- All four processes lie **above the critical point** of air (no phase change).
- The two isobaric lines (2–3 and 4–1) **diverge** from each other as entropy increases.
- The two isentropic lines (1–2 and 3–4) are **parallel vertical lines**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $r_p = 10$, $T_3 = 1300 \text{ K}$.

**(i) Temperatures at all four states:**

**State 2 (after isentropic compression):**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 10^{0.4/1.4} = 300 \times 10^{0.2857}$$

Compute $10^{0.2857}$: $0.2857 \times \ln(10) = 0.2857 \times 2.3026 = 0.6577$, $e^{0.6577} = 1.930$.

$$T_2 = 300 \times 1.930 = 579.0 \text{ K}$$

**State 3:** $T_3 = 1300 \text{ K}$ (given, end of heat addition).

**State 4 (after isentropic expansion):**

$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = \frac{1300}{1.930} = 673.6 \text{ K}$$

**(ii) Net work output per kg:**

$$W_{turbine} = c_p(T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 673.6) = 1.005 \times 626.4 = 629.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$W_{compressor} = c_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (579.0 - 300) = 1.005 \times 279.0 = 280.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$W_{net} = 629.5 - 280.4 = 349.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(iii) Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{Brayton} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.930} = 1 - 0.518 = 0.482 = 48.2\%$$

**Verification using $W_{net}/Q_{in}$:**

$$Q_{in} = c_p(T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1300 - 579.0) = 1.005 \times 721.0 = 724.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta = \frac{349.1}{724.6} = 0.482 = 48.2\% \checkmark$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS (READ BEFORE EXAM!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Saturation Dome:** Students often draw the Rankine cycle processes in the wrong region. The condenser (4→1) MUST end on the **saturated liquid line**, not deep in the subcooled region.
> 2. **Confusing Compression Ratios:** $r$ (volume ratio, $V_1/V_2$) is **NOT the same** as $r_p$ (pressure ratio, $P_2/P_1$). For the Otto cycle use $r$; for Brayton use $r_p$ with exponent $(\gamma-1)/\gamma$.
> 3. **Sign of $T_2$ in Otto derivation:** Many students drop the negative sign during algebraic manipulation of $T_4 = T_3/r^{\gamma-1}$ and end up with $W_{net}$ that is **less than zero**. Always verify the final answer is positive.
> 4. **Units mismatch:** $c_p$ in **kJ/kg·K** vs. $R$ in **kJ/kg·K** — never use $R = 8.314$ for specific gas constant. For air, $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.
> 5. **Brayton cycle is superheated-only:** Do NOT attempt to draw a saturation dome on the Brayton T–s diagram — air does not condense in the cycle.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The T–s diagram has **T on Y-axis**, **s on X-axis**, and the area under a curve = **heat transfer (Q)**.
- ✅ The **saturation dome** is the boundary between liquid, two-phase, and vapor regions — it is a **KTU favourite** to draw and label.
- ✅ **Critical point** of water: $T_c = 647.1 \text{ K}$, $P_c = 22.06 \text{ MPa}$ — the apex of the dome.
- ✅ **Rankine cycle** on T–s: 1→2 vertical (pump), 2→3 horizontal then upward (boiler), 3→4 vertical (turbine), 4→1 horizontal (condenser). **Clockwise loop** = power cycle.
- ✅ **Brayton cycle** on T–s: Two vertical isentropics + two diverging isobars, all in the **superheated region** (no dome).
- ✅ **Otto cycle** on T–s: Two vertical isentropics + two isochors, drawn in the superheated gas region.
- ✅ **Diesel cycle** on T–s: Two isentropics + one isobar (heat addition) + one isochor (heat rejection).
- ✅ **Net work of any cycle = net enclosed area** on the T–s diagram.
- ✅ **Otto efficiency** depends only on $r$ and $\gamma$: $\eta_O = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$.
- ✅ **Brayton efficiency** depends only on $r_p$ and $\gamma$: $\eta_B = 1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$.
- ✅ For pure substance: **isobar = isotherm** in the two-phase region (horizontal line on T–s).
- ✅ **Isentropic efficiency of turbine** $\eta_T = (h_3 - h_{4,actual})/(h_3 - h_{4,ideal})$ — the actual state deviates to the right (higher entropy) on T–s.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rankine Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rankine Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Rankine Cycle** is a thermodynamic cycle that describes the operating principle of steam power plants (also called Rankine engines). It is the fundamental idealized model used to analyze the conversion of heat from a fuel source into mechanical work via a working fluid (water/steam) that undergoes a phase change.

The cycle operates on a closed loop where the working fluid alternates between liquid and vapor phases, consisting of four reversible processes in the ideal case:
1. **Isentropic compression** in a pump
2. **Isobaric heat addition** in a boiler
3. **Isentropic expansion** in a turbine
4. **Isobaric heat rejection** in a condenser

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Rankine Cycle forms the foundational basis of all vapor power cycles studied in Module 1. KTU examiners frequently test the T-s and P-v diagrams, the identification of state points, and the calculation of cycle efficiency. Mastery of the ideal Rankine cycle is mandatory before studying Rankine cycle improvements (reheat, regeneration).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of the Rankine Cycle as a **"water elevator"** in a high-rise building:

- Imagine a building where water needs to be lifted to the top to create potential energy, then released to do useful work (like turning a wheel) as it falls back down.
- The **pump** acts like an electric pump at the ground floor that pushes water (cold liquid) to the top floor using a small amount of electrical work.
- The **boiler** is the "energy station" at the top where heat is added to convert the pressurized water into high-energy steam.
- The **turbine** is like a water wheel at the top — as the high-pressure steam rushes out, it spins the wheel to generate electricity (large work output).
- The **condenser** is the cooling tower at the bottom that condenses the spent steam back into liquid water so the cycle can start over.

Just as a water elevator needs to be refilled and re-energized to keep running, the Rankine cycle continuously recirculates the working fluid.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard state variables are essential:

- **Atmospheric Pressure ($P_0$):** **101.325 kPa** (used as condenser pressure in real plants)
- **Saturated Liquid Reference Temperature ($T_{ref}$):** **0.01 °C** (273.16 K) at triple point
- **Universal Gas Constant ($R$):** **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**
- **Specific Heat of Water ($c_{p,liquid}$):** **4.18 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Specific Volume of Water at Room Temp:** **≈ 0.001 m³/kg**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE):** For a control volume operating at steady state with negligible kinetic and potential energy changes, the energy transfer per unit mass is $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \Delta h$, where $h$ is the specific enthalpy. This is the governing equation for all Rankine cycle component analyses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Processes of the Ideal Rankine Cycle

The cycle is composed of four internal reversible processes connecting four state points (1, 2, 3, 4):

**Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression (Pump)**
- **What:** Saturated liquid at low pressure (condenser pressure) is compressed to high pressure (boiler pressure).
- **How:** The pump consumes a small amount of work. The liquid is nearly incompressible, so temperature rise is small.
- **State 1:** Saturated liquid exiting condenser ($x_1 = 0$).
- **State 2:** Compressed liquid entering boiler.

**Process 2 → 3: Isobaric Heat Addition (Boiler)**
- **What:** Compressed liquid is heated at constant pressure until it becomes superheated or saturated vapor.
- **How:** External heat source (coal, gas, nuclear) transfers energy to the fluid. This is the primary energy input to the cycle.
- **State 3:** Superheated steam (or saturated vapor) leaving boiler, entering turbine.

**Process 3 → 4: Isentropic Expansion (Turbine)**
- **What:** High-pressure steam expands to low pressure, producing shaft work.
- **How:** Steam pushes turbine blades, generating mechanical work (then converted to electricity).
- **State 4:** Low-pressure wet steam (typically $x_4 \approx 0.85$ to $0.90$ in design).

**Process 4 → 1: Isobaric Heat Rejection (Condenser)**
- **What:** Wet steam is condensed to saturated liquid at constant pressure.
- **How:** Cooling water in a heat exchanger absorbs heat from steam.
- **State 1:** Saturated liquid leaving condenser, entering pump.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Applicable Process** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pump Work | $w_P$ | $w_P = h_2 - h_1 = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ | kJ/kg | 1 → 2 |
| Turbine Work | $w_T$ | $w_T = h_3 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | 3 → 4 |
| Net Work Output | $w_{net}$ | $w_{net} = w_T - w_P$ | kJ/kg | Entire cycle |
| Heat Added | $q_{in}$ | $q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ | kJ/kg | 2 → 3 |
| Heat Rejected | $q_{out}$ | $q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | 4 → 1 |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | $\eta_{th} = w_{net} / q_{in}$ | dimensionless | Entire cycle |
| Thermal Efficiency (Alt.) | $\eta_{th}$ | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{q_{out}}{q_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Entire cycle |
| Work Ratio (Back Work Ratio) | $r_{bw}$ | $r_{bw} = w_P / w_T$ | dimensionless | Entire cycle |
| Specific Steam Consumption | $SSC$ | $SSC = 3600 / w_{net}$ | kg/kWh | Performance |
| Work of Compression (Liquid) | $w_P$ | $w_P \approx v_f (P_2 - P_1)$ | kJ/kg | 1 → 2 (approx.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pump Work Approximation (KTU-Favorite Trick):** Since the working fluid in the pump is essentially incompressible liquid, the specific volume is taken as $v_1 = v_f$ at the condenser temperature. This gives $w_P = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ rather than the exact $h_2 - h_1$. This approximation is valid for typical boiler pressures (≤ 100 bar) and is the standard KTU board exam assumption.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Rankine Cycle is the **backbone of global electricity generation**. Approximately **75-80%** of the world's electricity is generated using steam cycles based on the Rankine principle. Real-world applications include:

- **Coal-fired thermal power plants** (dominant in India and China)
- **Nuclear power plants** (pressurized water reactors use Rankine cycle on the secondary side)
- **Solar thermal power plants** (parabolic trough, solar tower)
- **Geothermal power plants**
- **Waste-to-energy plants**
- **Combined Cycle Power Plants (CCPP)** — the bottoming Rankine cycle recovers exhaust heat from a Brayton (gas) cycle.

The cycle's popularity stems from water being **non-toxic, non-flammable, inexpensive, and having a high latent heat of vaporization** ($h_{fg} \approx 2257$ kJ/kg at 100 °C).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Rankine Cycle

The thermal efficiency is defined as the ratio of net work output to the total heat input.

**Step 1:** Apply the Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to each component, neglecting kinetic energy ($\Delta KE \approx 0$) and potential energy ($\Delta PE \approx 0$) changes.

**Step 2:** Apply SFEE to the **Pump (Process 1 → 2)**:
The pump consumes work and increases the enthalpy of the fluid. Heat transfer is negligible (assumed adiabatic reversible).

$$w_P = h_2 - h_1$$

For an incompressible liquid, enthalpy change is approximated as:

$$h_2 - h_1 \approx v_1 (P_2 - P_1)$$

**Step 3:** Apply SFEE to the **Boiler (Process 2 → 3)**:
The boiler adds heat at constant pressure with no work transfer.

$$q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$$

**Step 4:** Apply SFEE to the **Turbine (Process 3 → 4)**:
The turbine produces work as steam expands isentropically.

$$w_T = h_3 - h_4$$

**Step 5:** Apply SFEE to the **Condenser (Process 4 → 1)**:
The condenser rejects heat at constant pressure.

$$q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$$

**Step 6:** Compute the net work output:

$$\begin{aligned}
w_{net} &= w_T - w_P \\
&= (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1) \\
&= (h_3 - h_2) + (h_1 - h_4) \\
&= q_{in} - q_{out}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7:** Apply the First Law to the entire cycle. Since the working fluid returns to its initial state, the cyclic integral of $dh$ is zero, so:

$$w_{net} = q_{in} - q_{out}$$

**Step 8:** Define the thermal efficiency:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th} &= \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} \\
&= \frac{q_{in} - q_{out}}{q_{in}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{q_{out}}{q_{in}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{h_4 - h_1}{h_3 - h_2}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem Statement:** A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at **3 MPa** and **350 °C** and is condensed in the condenser at a pressure of **10 kPa**. Determine:
(a) The pump work, turbine work, heat added, and heat rejected (per kg of steam).
(b) The thermal efficiency of the cycle.
(c) The work ratio (back work ratio).

**Given Data:**
- $P_2 = P_3 = 3$ MPa (boiler pressure)
- $T_3 = 350$ °C (turbine inlet)
- $P_4 = P_1 = 10$ kPa (condenser pressure)

**Step 1: Determine State 1 (Saturated liquid at 10 kPa)**

From steam tables at 10 kPa:
- $T_{sat} = 45.81$ °C
- $h_f = h_1 = 191.81$ kJ/kg
- $v_f = v_1 = 0.001010$ m³/kg
- $h_{fg} = 2392.1$ kJ/kg
- $s_f = 0.6492$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $s_{fg} = 7.5010$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 2: Determine State 2 (Pump exit)**

Pump work using the incompressible liquid approximation:

$$\begin{aligned}
w_P &= v_1 (P_2 - P_1) \\
&= 0.001010 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} \times (3000 - 10) \text{ kPa} \\
&= 0.001010 \times 2990 \text{ kPa} \cdot \text{m}^3/\text{kg} \\
&= 3.020 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

(Note: 1 kPa·m³ = 1 kJ)

$$h_2 = h_1 + w_P = 191.81 + 3.020 = 194.83 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3: Determine State 3 (Turbine inlet)**

At 3 MPa and 350 °C (superheated steam tables):
- $h_3 = 2988.1$ kJ/kg
- $s_3 = 6.7450$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 4: Determine State 4 (Turbine exit, isentropic expansion)**

For isentropic process: $s_4 = s_3 = 6.7450$ kJ/(kg·K)

At condenser pressure 10 kPa, check phase:

$$s_f = 0.6492 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}, \quad s_g = 8.1502 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

Since $s_f < s_4 < s_g$, the steam is wet at state 4. Calculate quality $x_4$:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_4 &= \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} \\
&= \frac{6.7450 - 0.6492}{7.5010} \\
&= \frac{6.0958}{7.5010} \\
&= 0.8126
\end{aligned}$$

Enthalpy at state 4:

$$\begin{aligned}
h_4 &= h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} \\
&= 191.81 + 0.8126 \times 2392.1 \\
&= 191.81 + 1944.13 \\
&= 2135.94 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute all work and heat quantities**

Turbine work:

$$w_T = h_3 - h_4 = 2988.1 - 2135.94 = 852.16 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Net work:

$$w_{net} = w_T - w_P = 852.16 - 3.020 = 849.14 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Heat added in boiler:

$$q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 2988.1 - 194.83 = 2793.27 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Heat rejected in condenser:

$$q_{out} = h_4 - h_1 = 2135.94 - 191.81 = 1944.13 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6: Compute thermal efficiency and work ratio**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th} &= \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} \\
&= \frac{849.14}{2793.27} \\
&= 0.3040 \\
&= 30.40\%
\end{aligned}$$

Back work ratio:

$$r_{bw} = \frac{w_P}{w_T} = \frac{3.020}{852.16} = 0.00354 = 0.354\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Notice the back work ratio is extremely small (≈ 0.35%). This is the major advantage of vapor cycles over gas cycles — the pump work is negligible because liquid is nearly incompressible. This is why Rankine cycles are preferred for large-scale power generation. Always compute the back work ratio in exam problems to demonstrate this understanding.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Operating Parameters on Efficiency

| **Parameter Change** | **Effect on $\eta_{th}$** | **Physical Reason** | **KTU Examiner Tip** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase Boiler Pressure ($P_2$) | ↑ Increases | Higher mean temperature of heat addition | Higher $T_{avg}$ → higher Carnot equivalent |
| Increase Turbine Inlet Temp ($T_3$) | ↑ Increases | Higher mean temperature of heat addition | Superheating moves state 3 to higher $T$ |
| Decrease Condenser Pressure ($P_1$) | ↑ Increases | Lower $T_{rejection}$, more work from turbine | But $x_4$ decreases → blade erosion risk |
| Use Reheat | ↑ Increases | Higher average $T$ in turbine expansion | Additional reheat stages required |
| Use Regeneration (Feedwater Heating) | ↑ Increases | Heat is added at higher $T$ in boiler | Uses open or closed feedwater heaters |
| Use Cogeneration | ↑ Increases (overall) | Utilizes rejected heat for process steam | Total energy utilization improves |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Rankine Cycle Component Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    condA["CONDENSER 4 to 1"] --> pumpB["PUMP 1 to 2"]
    pumpB --> boilC["BOILER 2 to 3"]
    boilC --> turbD["TURBINE 3 to 4"]
    turbD --> condA
    heat1["Q in from fuel"] -.-> boilC
    boilD["Q out to cooling water"] -.-> condA
    workOut["W net shaft work"] -.-> turbD
    workIn["W pump small work"] -.-> pumpB
```

### 4.2 Rankine Cycle State-Point Identification (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["STATE 1: Saturated Liquid P1 low h1"]
    s2["STATE 2: Compressed Liquid P2 high h2"]
    s3["STATE 3: Superheated Steam P3 high h3"]
    s4["STATE 4: Wet Steam P4 low h4 x4 0.8 to 0.9"]
    s1 -->|"Pump wP"| s2
    s2 -->|"Boiler qin"| s3
    s3 -->|"Turbine wT"| s4
    s4 -->|"Condenser qout"| s1
```

### 4.3 T-s Diagram Description (Block Representation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagram:** A Mermaid representation of the T-s diagram is provided as a descriptive block. The T-s diagram is the most important graphical tool for KTU board exams and must be drawn freehand by students.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dome["Saturation Dome Liquid Vapor"]
    p1["P1 isobar condenser pressure low T"]
    p2["P2 isobar boiler pressure high T"]
    pt3["T3 turbine inlet temperature 350 C"]
    pt4["T4 turbine exit temperature wet region"]
    s3["S3 s3 constant"]
    s4["S4 s4 s3 isentropic"]
    pt3 --> s3
    pt4 --> s4
    p1 --- dome
    p2 --- dome
```

**Description of T-s Diagram for Students:**

The T-s diagram of the ideal Rankine cycle is a **rectangle on a T-s plot** that intersects the saturation dome:
- **State 1** lies on the saturated liquid line (left boundary of the dome).
- **Process 1→2** is a nearly vertical line (isentropic pump — entropy unchanged).
- **State 2** lies slightly to the right of state 1, just outside the dome in the compressed liquid region.
- **Process 2→3** is a curved line going right and up (heat addition in boiler — entropy increases).
- **State 3** lies high above the dome, deep in the superheated region.
- **Process 3→4** is a vertical line (isentropic expansion — entropy constant).
- **State 4** lies inside the dome in the wet region (typically $x_4 \approx 0.80$ to $0.90$).
- **Process 4→1** is a horizontal line at constant T (heat rejection in condenser, isothermal phase change).

The area enclosed by 1-2-3-4 represents the **net work output** of the cycle.

### 4.4 P-v Diagram (Block Description)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pvdom["Saturation Curve on P v diagram"]
    s1p["State 1 saturated liquid"]
    s2p["State 2 compressed liquid higher P"]
    s3p["State 3 superheated vapor high v"]
    s4p["State 4 wet vapor"]
    s1p --> s2p
    s2p --> s3p
    s3p --> s4p
    s4p --> s1p
    pvdom --- s1p
    pvdom --- s2p
    pvdom --- s3p
    pvdom --- s4p
```

**Description of P-v Diagram:**
- The dome separates the compressed liquid, wet vapor, and superheated regions.
- The cycle traces a path: 1 (saturated liquid, very low $v$) → 2 (compressed liquid, slightly higher $v$ and much higher $P$) → 3 (superheated vapor, very high $v$ and high $P$) → 4 (wet vapor, large $v$ and low $P$).
- The area enclosed by the cycle in P-v is also equal to net work (thermodynamic identity: $\oint v \, dP = w_{net}$).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

*Define the Rankine cycle. Sketch the cycle on a T-s diagram and label the four state points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Rankine cycle is an idealized thermodynamic cycle that describes the operation of steam power plants, where a working fluid (water) undergoes phase change between liquid and vapor to convert heat into work.

**Four state points:**
- **State 1:** Saturated liquid leaving the condenser
- **State 2:** Compressed liquid leaving the pump
- **State 3:** Superheated steam (or saturated vapor) leaving the boiler
- **State 4:** Wet steam leaving the turbine

**T-s diagram:** The cycle appears as a closed loop, with the heat addition and rejection processes being isobaric (horizontal at constant pressure for phase change) and the pump and turbine processes being isentropic (vertical at constant entropy). The cycle encloses an area proportional to net work output. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for state points, 1 mark for T-s sketch description)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*State any three advantages of the Rankine cycle over the Carnot cycle for steam power plants.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Practical wet steam handling at turbine exit:** In the Carnot cycle, isentropic compression of wet steam is impractical because droplets damage the compressor. The Rankine cycle uses a pump (for liquid) and a turbine (for vapor) — a more realistic arrangement. **(1 mark)**

2. **Saturated liquid feed to pump:** The Rankine cycle pumps saturated liquid, which requires very small work due to low specific volume. The Carnot cycle would require isentropic compression of wet vapor — far more work-intensive. **(1 mark)**

3. **Easier temperature control:** The Rankine cycle allows superheating of steam (state 3 above saturation), which increases efficiency and prevents excessive moisture in the turbine. The Carnot cycle restricts heat addition to two isotherms only. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the thermal efficiency of the ideal Rankine cycle in terms of enthalpies. Define each term clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at 4 MPa and 400 °C, and the condenser pressure is 20 kPa. Determine:
- (i) The pump work per kg
- (ii) The turbine work per kg
- (iii) The heat supplied per kg
- (iv) The thermal efficiency
- (v) The work ratio **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1:** Apply the SFEE to each component.

For an ideal cycle, applying the First Law (with no kinetic/potential energy changes):

**Pump (1→2):** Work input, no heat transfer
$$w_P = h_2 - h_1$$

**Boiler (2→3):** Heat addition at constant pressure
$$q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$$

**Turbine (3→4):** Work output, isentropic expansion
$$w_T = h_3 - h_4$$

**Condenser (4→1):** Heat rejection at constant pressure
$$q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$$

**Step 2:** Net work output:
$$w_{net} = w_T - w_P = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)$$

**Step 3:** Thermal efficiency:
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2} = 1 - \frac{h_4 - h_1}{h_3 - h_2}$$

Where:
- $h_1$ = enthalpy at condenser exit (saturated liquid)
- $h_2$ = enthalpy at pump exit (compressed liquid)
- $h_3$ = enthalpy at turbine inlet (superheated steam)
- $h_4$ = enthalpy at turbine exit (wet steam)

**Step 4:** Approximation for pump work using incompressible liquid:
$$w_P = h_2 - h_1 \approx v_1(P_2 - P_1)$$

This is the final expression. **(Full 7 marks for complete derivation with term definitions)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $P_3 = P_2 = 4$ MPa
- $T_3 = 400$ °C
- $P_4 = P_1 = 20$ kPa

**Step 1: State 1 — Saturated liquid at 20 kPa**

From steam tables:
- $h_1 = h_f = 251.42$ kJ/kg
- $v_1 = v_f = 0.001017$ m³/kg
- $s_1 = s_f = 0.8320$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $s_{fg} = 7.0752$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $h_{fg} = 2357.5$ kJ/kg

**Step 2: State 2 — Pump exit**

$$w_P = v_1(P_2 - P_1) = 0.001017 \times (4000 - 20) = 4.047 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$h_2 = h_1 + w_P = 251.42 + 4.05 = 255.47 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3: State 3 — Turbine inlet**

At 4 MPa and 400 °C (superheated steam):
- $h_3 = 3213.6$ kJ/kg
- $s_3 = 6.7690$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 4: State 4 — Turbine exit (isentropic)**

Since $s_4 = s_3 = 6.7690$ kJ/(kg·K), and at 20 kPa: $s_f = 0.8320 < s_4 < s_g = 7.9072$, the steam is wet.

$$x_4 = \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7690 - 0.8320}{7.0752} = \frac{5.9370}{7.0752} = 0.8392$$

$$h_4 = h_f + x_4 h_{fg} = 251.42 + 0.8392 \times 2357.5 = 251.42 + 1978.41 = 2229.83 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5: Compute quantities**

(i) Pump work:
$$w_P = 4.05 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

(ii) Turbine work:
$$w_T = h_3 - h_4 = 3213.6 - 2229.83 = 983.77 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

(iii) Heat supplied:
$$q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3213.6 - 255.47 = 2958.13 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

(iv) Net work:
$$w_{net} = w_T - w_P = 983.77 - 4.05 = 979.72 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Thermal efficiency:
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{979.72}{2958.13} = 0.3312 = 33.12\% \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

(v) Work ratio:
$$r_{bw} = \frac{w_P}{w_T} = \frac{4.05}{983.77} = 0.00412 = 0.412\% \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Total: 7 marks for part (b)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain with a neat T-s diagram why the Rankine cycle is preferred over the Carnot cycle for steam power plants. **(7 marks)**
