**(b)** In an ideal Rankine cycle, the enthalpy at turbine inlet is 3200 kJ/kg, enthalpy at turbine exit is 2200 kJ/kg, enthalpy at pump inlet is 200 kJ/kg, and enthalpy at pump exit is 205 kJ/kg. Determine:
- (i) The turbine work
- (ii) The pump work
- (iii) The heat input
- (iv) The thermal efficiency
- (v) The work of heat rejection **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Carnot cycle limitations on T-s diagram (2 marks)**

The Carnot cycle on a T-s diagram consists of two isotherms and two isentropes. When applied to a steam cycle, the isentropic compression process (1→2) would have to compress **wet vapor**, which is impractical because:
- Liquid droplets in the wet mixture cause erosion of compressor blades.
- Compression of vapor requires substantially more work than compression of liquid.

**Step 2: Rankine cycle advantage (2 marks)**

The Rankine cycle resolves this by separating the working fluid handling:
- **Pump (1→2):** Handles saturated **liquid** only — minimal work due to low specific volume.
- **Turbine (3→4):** Handles **superheated or saturated vapor** only — practical expansion.

**Step 3: T-s diagram description (2 marks)**

On the T-s diagram:
- The Rankine cycle appears as a closed loop intersecting the saturation dome.
- 1→2: vertical isentropic pump line (very short, low entropy change).
- 2→3: curved heat addition line moving rightward and upward, passing through the dome into the superheated region.
- 3→4: vertical isentropic turbine line, dropping into the wet region.
- 4→1: horizontal condensation line at the saturation temperature of the condenser.

**Step 4: Efficiency and practicality (1 mark)**

The Rankine cycle allows **superheating** (state 3 above the dome), which:
- Increases the mean temperature of heat addition.
- Improves thermal efficiency.
- Reduces moisture content at turbine exit, protecting blades.

Thus, the Rankine cycle is the practical choice for steam power plants, sacrificing only marginal theoretical efficiency for substantial practical benefits.

**Total: 7 marks**

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $h_3 = 3200$ kJ/kg (turbine inlet)
- $h_4 = 2200$ kJ/kg (turbine exit)
- $h_1 = 200$ kJ/kg (pump inlet)
- $h_2 = 205$ kJ/kg (pump exit)

**Step 1: Compute each quantity**

(i) Turbine work:
$$w_T = h_3 - h_4 = 3200 - 2200 = 1000 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

(ii) Pump work:
$$w_P = h_2 - h_1 = 205 - 200 = 5 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

(iii) Heat input:
$$q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3200 - 205 = 2995 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

(iv) Net work:
$$w_{net} = w_T - w_P = 1000 - 5 = 995 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Thermal efficiency:
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{995}{2995} = 0.3322 = 33.22\% \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

(v) Heat rejected:
$$q_{out} = h_4 - h_1 = 2200 - 200 = 2000 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Verification (optional):**
$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{q_{out}}{q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{2000}{2995} = 0.3322 \checkmark$$

**Total: 7 marks for part (b)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to find $h_2$ from pump work:** Many students use $h_3 - h_1$ instead of $h_3 - h_2$ for heat input. This loses 2-3 marks. **Always compute $h_2$ first using $w_P = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$.**
> 2. **Not labeling the saturation dome on the T-s diagram:** A T-s sketch without the saturation dome is incomplete. Always draw the dome and identify which state points lie inside, on, or outside.
> 3. **Using $h_f$ instead of $h_g$ for $h_4$ at turbine exit:** Students forget to compute quality $x_4$ and just use $h_f$. This gives wrong turbine work. **Always compute $x_4$ from entropy balance when $s_4$ lies between $s_f$ and $s_g$.**
> 4. **Confusing Pump and Turbine work signs:** Pump work is consumed (positive input), turbine work is produced (positive output). The sign convention matters in SFEE.
> 5. **Ignoring the $v_1(P_2-P_1)$ approximation in theoretical questions:** When asked to "derive," use the exact expression $h_2 - h_1$; when asked to "compute" with limited data, use the approximation.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **The Rankine cycle consists of 4 processes:** isentropic pump compression (1→2), isobaric boiler heat addition (2→3), isentropic turbine expansion (3→4), and isobaric condenser heat rejection (4→1).

✅ **The cycle is named after William Rankine** (1859), a Scottish engineer and physicist.

✅ **Thermal efficiency formula:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{h_4 - h_1}{h_3 - h_2}$

✅ **Pump work is approximated as** $w_P = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ for incompressible liquid.

✅ **Back work ratio is very small** (typically 0.3% to 0.5%) — a major advantage over gas cycles.

✅ **T-s diagram** is rectangular on T-s plot intersecting the saturation dome. The enclosed area = net work.

✅ **State 1 is saturated liquid**, **State 3 is superheated steam** (or saturated vapor), **State 4 is wet steam** with $x_4 \approx 0.80$ to $0.90$ in design.

✅ **Higher boiler pressure and higher turbine inlet temperature** increase efficiency.

✅ **Lower condenser pressure** increases efficiency, but excessive lowering reduces $x_4$ and risks turbine blade erosion.

✅ **The Rankine cycle is preferred over the Carnot cycle** because it avoids impractical compression of wet vapor.

✅ **Steam table values needed:** $h_f, h_{fg}, h_g, s_f, s_{fg}, s_g, v_f$ at the respective pressures.

✅ **Steam tables at 10 kPa:** $h_f = 191.81$, $h_{fg} = 2392.1$, $s_f = 0.6492$, $s_{fg} = 7.5010$, $v_f = 0.001010$ (memorize these for rapid calculation).

✅ **First Law for entire cycle:** $w_{net} = q_{in} - q_{out}$ (cyclic integral of $dh = 0$).

✅ **Always state the four assumptions of the ideal Rankine cycle:** (1) Steady-state operation, (2) No pressure drops in boiler/condenser piping, (3) Isentropic pump and turbine processes, (4) Saturated liquid at pump inlet.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Power Plant, Internally Irreversible and Externally Irreversible Rankine Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Steam Power Plant, Internally Irreversible and Externally Irreversible Rankine Cycle # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Steam Power Plant — The Academic Definition A **Steam Power Plant** (also called a **Steam Electric Station** or **Thermal Power Station**) is an installation that converts the chemical energy stored in a fossil fuel (coal, oil, or natural gas) or the nuclear energy from a fission reaction into **electrical energy** by using steam as the working fluid. The conversion chain follows the path: > **Fuel Energy → Heat → Mechanical Work (via Turbine) → Electrical Energy (via Generator)** The thermodynamic backbone of every steam power plant is the **Rankine Cycle**, which describes the closed loop of phase changes and energy transfers the working fluid undergoes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET631 — Module 1):** > The student must be able to (i) draw and label the layout of a steam power plant, (ii) identify all four essential components (Boiler, Turbine, Condenser, Pump), (iii) analyze the ideal Rankine cycle, and (iv) extend the analysis to **internally irreversible** and **externally irreversible** cycles by introducing realistic deviations from the ideal model. ## 1.2 The Ideal Rankine Cycle — Formal Definition The **Rankine Cycle** is an idealised, fully reversible thermodynamic cycle that acts as the benchmark model for steam power plants. It consists of four internally reversible processes: | Process No. | Component | Thermodynamic Process | |:-----------:|:---------:|:---------------------:| | 1 → 2 | Pump | Isentropic compression of saturated liquid | | 2 → 3 | Boiler | Isobaric heat addition (in boiler / evaporator / superheater) | | 3 → 4 | Turbine | Isentropic expansion of superheated steam | | 4 → 1 | Condenser | Isobaric heat rejection (condensation) | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Elevator" Think of the steam power plant as a **water elevator in a tall building**: - The **boiler** is the *rooftop tank* where water is heated and lifted (in energy form) to a high elevation. - The **turbine** is the *elevator going down* — as the steam "falls" from high pressure to low pressure, it spins the shaft and generates useful work (like a passenger reaching the ground floor with kinetic energy). - The **condenser** is the *ground-floor cooling pool* that resets the working fluid back to a low-energy liquid state. - The **pump** is the *return elevator* that pushes the condensed water back up to the rooftop tank, but with very little energy cost. In an **ideal** cycle, every "ride" in the elevator is smooth and frictionless. In a **real** cycle, friction (irreversibility) wastes part of the energy as heat — and this is where internal and external irreversibilities enter the picture. > [!NOTE] > The term **irreversibility** denotes any real process that violates the second law of thermodynamics. In the Rankine cycle, these losses show up as **entropy generation** ($s_{gen} > 0$) within the system boundary (internal) or across the boundary (external). ## 1.4 Defining Internal vs External Irreversibility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Internal Irreversibility** — losses that occur **inside** the system boundary, e.g., friction in the turbine blades, pump inefficiencies, pressure drops in pipes. The system exchanges heat with external reservoirs reversibly, but entropy is generated internally. > > **External Irreversibility** — losses that occur **across** the system boundary due to finite temperature difference during heat transfer (e.g., hot gases in the furnace are at a much higher temperature than the steam being boiled). No irreversibility exists inside the system itself. A cycle may be: 1. **Internally reversible + Externally reversible** → Ideal Rankine Cycle 2. **Internally irreversible + Externally reversible** → Turbine/pump losses only 3. **Internally reversible + Externally irreversible** → Finite ΔT in heat exchangers only 4. **Internally irreversible + Externally irreversible** → The actual plant (most realistic) # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Ideal Rankine Cycle on T-s and P-v Diagrams The cycle consists of four steady-flow processes. Each state point is conventionally labelled: - **State 1:** Saturated liquid leaving the condenser - **State 2:** Compressed liquid leaving the pump - **State 3:** Superheated steam leaving the boiler - **State 4:** Wet steam leaving the turbine For each state we refer to **steam tables** to obtain enthalpy ($h$), entropy ($s$), specific volume ($v$), and quality ($x$). ## 2.2 Heat and Work Interactions (Per kg of working fluid) Applying the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to each component (neglecting KE and PE changes): ### Boiler (Heat Input, $q_{in}$) $$ q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 $$ ### Turbine (Work Output, $w_T$) $$ w_T = h_3 - h_4 $$ ### Condenser (Heat Rejected, $q_{out}$) $$ q_{out} = h_4 - h_1 $$ ### Pump (Work Input, $w_P$) $$ w_P = h_2 - h_1 \approx v_1(P_2 - P_1) $$ The approximation $h_2 - h_1 \approx v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ comes from the incompressible liquid relation. ### Net Work Output $$ w_{net} = w_T - w_P = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1) $$ ### Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Rankine Cycle $$ \eta_{th,\,ideal} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2} $$ ## 2.3 Internally Irreversible Rankine Cycle When the **turbine** and **pump** operate with finite efficiencies (η_T < 100%, η_P < 100%), the expansions/compressions are no longer isentropic. The actual processes become: - **Actual turbine (3 → 4a):** $s_{4a} > s_3$ (entropy rises) - **Actual pump (1 → 2a):** $s_{2a} > s_1$ (entropy rises) The actual enthalpies are calculated using **isentropic efficiencies**: ### Turbine Isentropic Efficiency $$ \eta_T = \frac{h_3 - h_{4a}}{h_3 - h_{4s}} $$ ### Pump Isentropic Efficiency $$ \eta_P = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1} $$ ### Work in Actual Turbine $$ w_{T,\,actual} = h_3 - h_{4a} $$ ### Work in Actual Pump $$ w_{P,\,actual} = h_{2a} - h_1 = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{\eta_P} = \frac{v_1(P_2 - P_1)}{\eta_P} $$ ### Thermal Efficiency (Internally Irreversible) $$ \eta_{th,\,int\,irr} = \frac{(h_3 - h_{4a}) - (h_{2a} - h_1)}{h_3 - h_{2a}} $$ ## 2.4 Externally Irreversible Rankine Cycle In an **externally irreversible** cycle, the internal processes are reversible (turbine and pump are isentropic), but heat is transferred across a **finite temperature difference** between the heat source/sink and the working fluid. The external irreversibility is characterised by: $$ s_{gen,\,external} = \frac{q_{out}}{T_{sink}} - \frac{q_{in}}{T_{source}} $$ For heat addition from a combustion gas at variable temperature $T_g$: $$ q_{in} = \int_{T_2}^{T_3} c_p \, dT \quad \text{(working fluid side)} $$ $$ q_{in} = \int_{T_g,\,in}^{T_g,\,out} c_{p,g} \, dT \quad \text{(gas side)} $$ A finite ΔT (e.g., $T_g - T_s$ at every point) creates **lost work**: $$ w_{lost} = T_0 \cdot s_{gen} = T_0 \left[ \frac{q_{in}}{T_{source,\,avg}} - \frac{q_{in}}{T_{steam,\,avg}} \right] $$ The thermal efficiency becomes: $$ \eta_{th,\,ext\,irr} = 1 - \frac{T_{sink,\,avg}}{T_{source,\,avg}} < \eta_{Carnot} $$ ## 2.5 Combined (Fully Irreversible) Rankine Cycle For a real plant, both effects exist together: $$ \eta_{th,\,actual} = \eta_{th,\,ideal} - \Delta\eta_{int} - \Delta\eta_{ext} $$ The dominant external loss in a steam plant is the **stack-gas loss** (combustion gases leaving the chimney at 150–200 °C carrying away enthalpy). ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are examinable. Memorise the symbols, units, and limits of validity.** | Quantity | Ideal Cycle | Internally Irreversible | Externally Irreversible | |:---------|:-----------:|:-----------------------:|:-----------------------:| | Turbine work | $w_T = h_3 - h_4$ | $w_{T,a} = \eta_T(h_3 - h_{4s})$ | $w_T = h_3 - h_4$ | | Pump work | $w_P = h_2 - h_1$ | $w_{P,a} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{\eta_P}$ | $w_P = h_2 - h_1$ | | Net work | $w_T - w_P$ | $w_{T,a} - w_{P,a}$ | $w_T - w_P$ | | Heat input | $q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ | $q_{in} = h_3 - h_{2a}$ | $q_{in} = \int c_p \, dT$ | | Efficiency | $\dfrac{w_{net}}{q_{in}}$ | $\dfrac{w_{net,\,a}}{q_{in,\,a}}$ | $1 - \dfrac{T_{sink}}{T_{source}}$ (avg) | | Entropy gen. | $0$ | $s_{gen} > 0$ (inside) | $s_{gen} > 0$ (boundary) | **Universal auxiliary relations:** - $h_{2s} - h_1 = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ — pump work approximation - $s_{4s} = s_3$ — isentropic expansion exit - $x_4 = \dfrac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}}$ at $P_4$ — quality after expansion - $\text{SFC} = \dfrac{3600}{w_{net} \cdot \eta_{mech}}$ — Specific Steam Consumption (kg/kWh) - $\text{HR} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th}}$ — Heat Rate (kJ/kWh) ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Significance - Modern **supercritical** plants operate above 22 MPa and 600 °C to reduce external irreversibility (smaller ΔT between flame and steam). - **Reheat** and **regeneration** are engineering strategies specifically designed to minimise external and internal irreversibilities, respectively. - A well-designed 800 MW subcritical plant achieves ~38% efficiency, while a modern ultra-supercritical plant reaches 45% — every percentage point gain reflects reduced irreversibility. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Rankine Cycle ### Starting Point: Energy Balance on Each Component For a **steady-flow** system with one inlet and one outlet, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes: $$ q - w = h_{out} - h_{in} $$ We apply this to the four components in series. ### Step 1 — Boiler (Process 2 → 3) Heat is added isobarically, no work is done. $$ q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 \tag{3.1} $$ ### Step 2 — Turbine (Process 3 → 4) Adiabatic, isentropic expansion produces work. $$ w_T = h_3 - h_4 \tag{3.2} $$ ### Step 3 — Condenser (Process 4 → 1) Heat is rejected isobarically, no work is done. $$ q_{out} = h_4 - h_1 \tag{3.3} $$ ### Step 4 — Pump (Process 1 → 2) Adiabatic, isentropic compression consumes work. $$ w_P = h_2 - h_1 \tag{3.4} $$ ### Step 5 — Net Work Subtracting the pump work from the turbine work: $$ \begin{aligned} w_{net} &= w_T - w_P \\ &= (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1) \tag{3.5} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Thermal Efficiency By definition: $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{th,\,ideal} &= \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} \\ &= \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2} \tag{3.6} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Approximation of Pump Work For an incompressible liquid, $dh = v\,dP$ at constant entropy (approximately): $$ h_2 - h_1 \approx v_1(P_2 - P_1) \tag{3.7} $$ This is much smaller than $w_T$ (typically $< 1\%$ of $w_T$), which is why in many introductory problems it is **neglected** to obtain: $$ \eta_{th,\,ideal} \approx \frac{h_3 - h_4}{h_3 - h_2} \tag{3.8} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Work Lost Due to Internal Irreversibility Consider an actual turbine with isentropic efficiency $\eta_T$. The actual exit enthalpy is: $$ h_{4a} = h_3 - \eta_T (h_3 - h_{4s}) \tag{3.9} $$ The lost work in the turbine: $$ \begin{aligned} w_{lost,\,T} &= w_{T,\,isentropic} - w_{T,\,actual} \\ &= (h_3 - h_{4s}) - (h_3 - h_{4a}) \\ &= h_{4a} - h_{4s} \tag{3.10} \end{aligned} $$ Similarly, lost work in the pump: $$ \begin{aligned} w_{lost,\,P} &= w_{P,\,actual} - w_{P,\,isentropic} \\ &= (h_{2a} - h_1) - (h_{2s} - h_1) \\ &= h_{2a} - h_{2s} \tag{3.11} \end{aligned} $$ Total irreversibility loss rate (per unit mass flow): $$ i_{internal} = w_{lost,\,T} + w_{lost,\,P} \tag{3.12} $$ The **Second-Law Efficiency** of the internally irreversible cycle: $$ \eta_{II} = \frac{w_{net,\,actual}}{w_{net,\,reversible}} \tag{3.13} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation of External Irreversibility (Lost Work in Heat Addition) The lost work during heat transfer across a finite ΔT is governed by **Gouy-Stodola theorem**: $$ w_{lost} = T_0 \cdot s_{gen} \tag{3.14} $$ For heat addition from a furnace at average gas temperature $T_g$ to steam at average temperature $T_s$: $$ \begin{aligned} s_{gen} &= \frac{q_{in}}{T_s} - \frac{q_{in}}{T_g} \\ &= q_{in} \left( \frac{1}{T_s} - \frac{1}{T_g} \right) \tag{3.15} \end{aligned} $$ Hence: $$ \begin{aligned} w_{lost,\,ext} &= T_0 \cdot q_{in} \left( \frac{1}{T_s} - \frac{1}{T_g} \right) \\ &= T_0 \cdot q_{in} \cdot \frac{T_g - T_s}{T_g T_s} \tag{3.16} \end{aligned} $$ This loss is **inversely proportional to the closeness** of $T_g$ and $T_s$ — driving the modern trend toward **supercritical and ultra-supercritical** steam conditions. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Cycle Variants | Parameter | Ideal Rankine | Internal Irr. Only | External Irr. Only | Fully Irreversible (Real) | |:----------|:-------------:|:------------------:|:------------------:|:-------------------------:| | $\eta_T$ | 100 % | 80–90 % | 100 % | 80–90 % | | $\eta_P$ | 100 % | 70–85 % | 100 % | 70–85 % | | $s_{gen,\,inside}$ | 0 | $> 0$ | 0 | $> 0$ | | $s_{gen,\,outside}$ | 0 | 0 | $> 0$ | $> 0$ | | $w_{net}$ | Highest | Lower | Same as ideal | Lowest | | $\eta_{th}$ | Highest | Lower | Lower than ideal | Lowest (real) | | Practical example | Textbook | Real pump/turbine | Ideal comp. with finite ΔT | Actual power plant | ## 3.5 Numerical Demonstration — A Typical KTU Problem **Given:** - $P_1 = 10$ kPa, saturated liquid at condenser exit - $P_2 = P_3 = 3$ MPa - $T_3 = 400$ °C - $\eta_T = 85\%$, $\eta_P = 80\%$ **Steam Table Reads:** | State | $h$ (kJ/kg) | $s$ (kJ/kg·K) | Notes | |:-----:|:-----------:|:-------------:|:------| | 1 | 191.81 | 0.6492 | $s_f$ at 10 kPa | | 2s | 192.30 | 0.6492 | pump work isentropic | | 3 | 3230.7 | 6.9211 | superheated at 3 MPa, 400 °C | | 4s | 2244.4 | 6.9211 | wet steam at 10 kPa, $s = s_3$ | **Pump Work (Actual):** $$ \begin{aligned} w_{P,\,actual} &= \frac{v_1 (P_2 - P_1)}{\eta_P} \\ &= \frac{0.00101 \times (3000 - 10)}{0.80} \\ &= 3.74 \, \text{kJ/kg} \tag{3.17} \end{aligned} $$ **Turbine Work (Actual):** $$ \begin{aligned} w_{T,\,actual} &= \eta_T (h_3 - h_{4s}) \\ &= 0.85 \times (3230.7 - 2244.4) \\ &= 838.4 \, \text{kJ/kg} \tag{3.18} \end{aligned} $$ **Net Work:** $$ \begin{aligned} w_{net,\,actual} &= w_{T,\,actual} - w_{P,\,actual} \\ &= 838.4 - 3.74 \\ &= 834.7 \, \text{kJ/kg} \tag{3.19} \end{aligned} $$ **Heat Input:** $$ \begin{aligned} q_{in} &= h_3 - h_{2a} = h_3 - (h_1 + w_{P,\,actual}) \\ &= 3230.7 - (191.81 + 3.74) \\ &= 3035.2 \, \text{kJ/kg} \tag{3.20} \end{aligned} $$ **Thermal Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{th,\,actual} &= \frac{834.7}{3035.2} \\ &= 0.275 \\ &= 27.5\% \tag{3.21} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > For comparison, the **ideal** efficiency for the same state points is: > $\eta_{th,\,ideal} = \dfrac{(3230.7 - 2244.4) - 0.49}{3230.7 - 192.3} \approx 31.0\%$ > The drop from 31 % → 27.5 % represents the **internal irreversibility penalty**. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Steam Power Plant Layout ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Coal Yard / Fuel Storage] --> B[Coal Handling Plant] B --> C[Pulveriser] C --> D[Boiler / Steam Generator] D --> E[Superheater] E --> F[Turbine] F --> G[Condenser] G --> H[Cooling Tower] H --> I[Cooling Water Pump] I --> G G --> J[Feedwater Pump] J --> K[Economiser] K --> D F --> L[Generator] L --> M[Electrical Switchyard] M --> N[Transmission Lines] D --> O[Air Preheater] O --> D P[Forced Draft Fan] --> O Q[Induced Draft Fan] --> R[Electrostatic Precipitator] R --> S[Chimney / Stack] O --> R ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow — Rankine Cycle States on T-s Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Cycle_Rankine S1["State 1: Saturated Liquid at Condenser Pressure"] S2["State 2: Compressed Liquid at Boiler Pressure"] S3["State 3: Superheated Steam at Boiler Exit"] S4["State 4: Wet Steam at Turbine Exit"] end S1 -- Pump: 1 to 2
Isentropic Compression --> S2 S2 -- Boiler: 2 to 3
Isobaric Heat Addition --> S3 S3 -- Turbine: 3 to 4
Isentropic Expansion --> S4 S4 -- Condenser: 4 to 1
Isobaric Heat Rejection --> S1 ``` ## 4.3 Classification Tree — Types of Irreversibility ```mermaid flowchart TB R[Real Rankine Cycle] --> IR[Internally Irreversible] R --> ER[Externally Irreversible] IR --> T1["Turbine: blade friction, leakage"] IR --> P1["Pump: mechanical & hydraulic loss"] IR --> B1["Pipe friction in boiler tubes"] ER --> HA1["Finite ΔT in boiler / economiser"] ER --> HR1["Finite ΔT in condenser"] ER --> CH1["Stack gas heat loss"] R --> FULL["Fully Irreversible = IR + ER combined"] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Energy Flow with Losses ```mermaid flowchart LR FUEL["Fuel Chemical Energy Q_fuel"] --> COMB[Combustion Chamber] COMB --> HGAS["Hot Flue Gas Enthalpy"] HGAS -- Q_in_actual --> STEAM["Steam Cycle"] HGAS -- Loss A: Stack gas --> STACK["Chimney"] HGAS -- Loss B: Radiation --> RAD["Radiation to Surroundings"] STEAM -- Loss C: Turbine irreversibility --> WT[Turbine Work] STEAM -- Loss D: Pump irreversibility --> WP[Pump Work] STEAM -- Loss E: Condenser rejection --> QOUT[Condenser Heat Rejection] WT --> GEN[Generator] GEN --> ELEL["Electrical Output"] WP --> STEAM ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Part A consists of short-answer questions. Answers must be crisp, 4–6 sentences, with a labelled diagram where applicable. Marks are awarded for **definition + example + key formula**. ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **Distinguish between internal and external irreversibility in the Rankine cycle. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** *Internal irreversibility* refers to entropy generation **inside the system boundary** caused by dissipative effects such as fluid friction, turbulence, and unrestrained expansion. Example: friction in the turbine blades causes the actual expansion (3→4a) to deviate from the ideal isentropic line, raising $s_4$ above $s_3$. *External irreversibility* refers to entropy generation **across the system boundary** due to heat transfer across a finite temperature difference. Example: heat addition in the boiler occurs between hot flue gas at ~1500 K and steam at ~600 K — the finite ΔT is a classic external irreversibility. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] **Why is the pump work in a Rankine cycle often neglected in preliminary calculations? Justify with an order-of-magnitude estimate.** **Model Answer:** The pump handles liquid water with very low specific volume ($v \approx 0.001$ m³/kg). For a pressure ratio of 100:1, $w_P \approx v\,\Delta P \approx 0.001 \times 9\times10^6 \approx 9$ kJ/kg. The turbine work for the same plant is typically 800–1000 kJ/kg. Hence $w_P/w_T < 1\%$, justifying its neglect in preliminary analyses. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each. The student answers EITHER Question A OR Question B in full. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]** A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at 3 MPa and 350 °C and is condensed in the condenser at 10 kPa. Determine: **(a)** The thermal efficiency of the cycle. **(b)** The mass flow rate of steam required for a net power output of 50 MW, neglecting pump work. #### (a) Model Solution — Thermal Efficiency **Given:** - $P_3 = 3$ MPa, $T_3 = 350$ °C - $P_1 = P_4 = 10$ kPa **State 3 — Superheated Steam Tables (3 MPa, 350 °C):** $$ h_3 = 3115.3 \, \text{kJ/kg}, \quad s_3 = 6.7428 \, \text{kJ/kg·K} $$ **State 4 — Saturated Mixture at 10 kPa:** At 10 kPa: $s_f = 0.6492$, $s_{fg} = 7.4996$, $h_f = 191.81$, $h_{fg} = 2392.1$ kJ/kg. Isentropic condition: $s_4 = s_3 = 6.7428$. $$ \begin{aligned} x_4 &= \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} \\ &= \frac{6.7428 - 0.6492}{7.4996} \\ &= 0.8128 \tag{5.1} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} h_4 &= h_f + x_4 \, h_{fg} \\ &= 191.81 + 0.8128 \times 2392.1 \\ &= 2136.2 \, \text{kJ/kg} \tag{5.2} \end{aligned} $$ **Heat Input (neglecting pump work):** $$ \begin{aligned} q_{in} &\approx h_3 - h_1 \quad (\text{since } h_1 \approx h_f \text{ at 10 kPa}) \\ &= 3115.3 - 191.81 \\ &= 2923.5 \, \text{kJ/kg} \tag{5.3} \end{aligned} $$ **Turbine Work:** $$ \begin{aligned} w_T &= h_3 - h_4 \\ &= 3115.3 - 2136.2 \\ &= 979.1 \, \text{kJ/kg} \tag{5.4} \end{aligned} $$ **Thermal Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{th,\,ideal} &= \frac{w_T}{q_{in}} \\ &= \frac{979.1}{2923.5} \\ &= 0.3349 \\ &= 33.49\% \tag{5.5} \end{aligned} $$ #### (b) Model Solution — Mass Flow Rate $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{W}_{net} &= \dot{m} \cdot w_{net} \\ 50 \times 10^3 \, \text{kW} &= \dot{m} \times 979.1 \, \text{kJ/kg} \tag{5.6} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m} &= \frac{50{,}000}{979.1} \\ &= 51.07 \, \text{kg/s} \tag{5.7} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]** A Rankine cycle with internal irreversibilities has turbine and pump isentropic efficiencies of 85 % and 80 % respectively. Boiler pressure is 8 MPa with steam superheated to 500 °C, condenser pressure is 20 kPa. **(a)** Sketch the cycle on a T-s diagram and mark all state points. **(b)** Calculate the actual thermal efficiency and compare with the ideal cycle. #### (a) Model Solution — T-s Diagram Sketch (Description) The T-s diagram contains: - **Saturated liquid line** (left), **saturated vapour line** (right), with the **wet dome** between. - **State 1:** Saturated liquid at 20 kPa — bottom-left of the dome. - **State 2:** Compressed liquid just to the right of state 1 (very small horizontal distance). - **State 3:** Deep inside the superheat region, far above and right of the dome. - **State 4s:** On the isentrope $s = s_3$, inside the dome — ideal turbine exit. - **State 4a:** Slightly right of 4s — actual turbine exit (entropy rose due to internal irreversibility). The processes: 1→2 (pump, near-vertical up), 2→3 (heat addition at constant $P$, curving up-right), 3→4a (turbine, down with $s$ increasing), 4a→1 (condenser, horizontal left at $P_4$). #### (b) Model Solution — Efficiency Comparison **Steam Table Reads (8 MPa, 500 °C):** $$ h_3 = 3398.3 \, \text{kJ/kg}, \quad s_3 = 6.7240 \, \text{kJ/kg·K} $$ **At 20 kPa:** $h_f = 251.42$, $h_{fg} = 2357.5$, $s_f = 0.8320$, $s_{fg} = 7.0752$ kJ/kg·K. **State 4s (Isentropic):** $$ x_{4s} = \frac{6.7240 - 0.8320}{7.0752} = 0.8328 $$ $$ h_{4s} = 251.42 + 0.8328 \times 2357.5 = 2214.8 \, \text{kJ/kg} $$ **Actual Turbine Exit (η_T = 0.85):** $$ \begin{aligned} h_{4a} &= h_3 - \eta_T (h_3 - h_{4s}) \\ &= 3398.3 - 0.85 \times (3398.3 - 2214.8) \\ &= 3398.3 - 0.85 \times 1183.5 \\ &= 3398.3 - 1005.97 \\ &= 2392.3 \, \text{kJ/kg} \tag{5.8} \end{aligned} $$ **Actual Turbine Work:** $$ w_{T,\,a} = h_3 - h_{4a} = 3398.3 - 2392.3 = 1006.0 \, \text{kJ/kg} $$ **Actual Pump Work (η_P = 0.80, $v_1 = 0.001017$ m³/kg):** $$ \begin{aligned} w_{P,\,a} &= \frac{v_1 (P_2 - P_1)}{\eta_P} \\ &= \frac{0.001017 \times (8000 - 20)}{0.80} \\ &= 10.14 \, \text{kJ/kg} \tag{5.9} \end{aligned} $$ $$ h_{2a} = h_1 + w_{P,a} = 251.42 + 10.14 = 261.56 \, \text{kJ/kg} $$ **Actual Net Work and Heat Input:** $$ \begin{aligned} w_{net,\,a} &= 1006.0 - 10.14 = 995.86 \, \text{kJ/kg} \\ q_{in,\,a} &= h_3 - h_{2a} = 3398.3 - 261.56 = 3136.7 \, \text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Actual Thermal Efficiency:** $$ \eta_{th,\,actual} = \frac{995.86}{3136.7} = 0.3175 = 31.75\% $$ **Ideal Thermal Efficiency (for comparison):** $$ \eta_{th,\,ideal} = \frac{1183.5 - 4.97}{3398.3 - 256.4} = \frac{1178.5}{3141.9} = 0.3751 = 37.51\% $$ **Efficiency Penalty due to Internal Irreversibility:** $$ \Delta\eta = 37.51\% - 31.75\% = 5.76\% \text{ (absolute)} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks in KTU examinations:** > 1. **Forgetting to include pump work** in the net work expression. KTU examiners explicitly test the difference between $w_T$ and $w_{net} = w_T - w_P$. > 2. **Mixing up isentropic efficiencies**: $\eta_T$ uses *actual/ideal* work; $\eta_P$ uses *ideal/actual* work. Reversing this causes sign errors. > 3. **Neglecting $h_2 \neq h_1$** — when pump work is included, $h_2 = h_1 + v_1(P_2 - P_1)/\eta_P$. Using $h_2 = h_1$ loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Steam table interpolation** — at non-tabulated pressures, linear interpolation between two adjacent rows is mandatory. Showing the interpolation step earns full credit. > 5. **Not drawing the T-s or P-v diagram** when asked — KTU mandates a labelled sketch for any 7-mark sub-part. > 6. **Quality $x$ must lie in [0,1]** — if a student calculates $x_4 > 1$, the steam is superheated at the turbine exit, requiring $h_4$ from the superheat table, not the wet-mixture formula. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Steam Power Plant & Rankine Cycle** - **Plant components (4 essential):** Boiler, Turbine, Condenser, Pump. Plus auxiliaries: Economiser, Superheater, Reheater, Air Preheater, Cooling Tower, Condensate Extraction Pump, Feedwater Pump. - **Ideal Rankine cycle:** 4 reversible processes — isentropic pump, isobaric heat addition, isentropic turbine, isobaric heat rejection. - **State points:** 1 (sat. liquid) → 2 (comp. liquid) → 3 (superheated) → 4 (wet steam). - **Thermal efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{w_T - w_P}{q_{in}}$ - **Internal irreversibility sources:** Turbine friction, pump inefficiency, pipe friction — raises $s$ inside the system. - **External irreversibility sources:** Finite ΔT in boiler, condenser; stack gas loss — entropy generated across the boundary. - **Isentropic efficiencies:** $\eta_T = \dfrac{h_3 - h_{4a}}{h_3 - h_{4s}}$, $\eta_P = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$. - **Pump work approximation:** $w_P \approx v_1(P_2 - P_1)$, valid for liquids. - **Quality after expansion:** $x_4 = \dfrac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}}$ at $P_4$. - **Second-Law Efficiency:** $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{w_{net,\,actual}}{w_{net,\,reversible}} \leq 1$. - **Gouy-Stodola Theorem:** $w_{lost} = T_0 \cdot s_{gen}$ — quantifies lost work due to irreversibility. - **Specific Steam Consumption (SSC):** $\dfrac{3600}{w_{net}}$ kg/kWh. - **Heat Rate (HR):** $\dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th}}$ kJ/kWh. - **Trick to remember irreversibility direction:** Internal = *inside* the loop on T-s diagram (entropy rises within the curve); External = *outside* the loop (entropy rises because of ΔT with surroundings). - **Engineering strategies to reduce irreversibility:** - **Supercritical / ultrasupercritical steam** → reduces external irreversibility (smaller ΔT). - **Reheating** → reduces moisture in low-pressure stages, improves internal efficiency. - **Regenerative feedwater heating** → reduces external irreversibility by bleeding steam. - **Typical exam scenarios:** (i) Given all four state enthalpies, find $\eta_{th}$. (ii) Given pressures and one temperature, find all states from steam tables and compute $\eta_{th}$. (iii) Given isentropic efficiencies, find the *actual* cycle efficiency. - **Always state assumptions:** Steady state, negligible KE & PE changes, no heat loss in pipes. - **Always include units** in the final numerical answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

topics.title: Mean Temperature of Heat Addition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mean Temperature of Heat Addition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mean Temperature of Heat Addition (T₁ or Tₘₕₐ):** It is the **equivalent constant temperature** at which heat would have to be supplied to the working fluid so as to yield the **same change of entropy** as the actual **variable-temperature** heat addition process in a vapor power cycle (e.g., the Rankine cycle).

Mathematically, it is the ratio of the **total heat added** during the process to the **net change of entropy** of the working fluid during heat addition.

$$\boxed{\;T_{MHA} \;=\; \dfrac{q_{in}}{\Delta s} \;=\; \dfrac{h_{1} - h_{4}}{s_{1} - s_{4}}\;}$$

where state **1** corresponds to the **turbine inlet** (boiler exit) and state **4** corresponds to the **pump exit** (compressed liquid entering the boiler).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Averaging" of a Variable Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you are pouring hot water into a bath tub for 10 minutes. The water temperature coming out of your geyser **fluctuates** — sometimes too hot, sometimes lukewarm. But what if you wanted to describe the *overall warming effect* of that 10-minute bath using a **single number**? That single number is exactly the **mean temperature of heat addition** — an *effective constant temperature* that delivers the same total energy and produces the same entropy change as the real fluctuating one.

In the Rankine cycle, the working fluid (water/steam) is heated in the boiler from a **compressed liquid** to a **superheated vapor**. This heating happens over a **range of temperatures** (from saturation to superheat). Since a Carnot cycle is the *most efficient* reversible cycle operating between two reservoirs, we cannot directly apply Carnot efficiency to a non-isothermal heat addition. The concept of **Tₘₕₐ** allows us to *replace* the variable-temperature heat addition by an *equivalent isothermal* one and then compare the Rankine cycle with a Carnot cycle operating between **Tₘₕₐ** and **Tₘᵢₙ** (the condenser saturation temperature).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Universal Gas Constant, R** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (used only for ideal-gas approximations).
> - **Specific enthalpy (h)** and **specific entropy (s)** of water/steam are read from **Steam Tables** (saturation and superheat tables).
> - **Tₘₕₐ** is expressed in **Kelvin (K)** when used inside Carnot-type efficiency expressions.
> - Typical **Tₘₕₐ** for modern Rankine cycles lies in the range **600 K – 900 K** (≈ 327 °C – 627 °C).
> - Typical **Tₘᵢₙ** (condenser) ≈ **300 K – 320 K** (≈ 27 °C – 47 °C).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module-1, the concept of Tₘₕₐ is the **bridge** between the *real Rankine cycle* and the *ideal Carnot cycle*. You will be examined on both its **derivation** and its **application** in computing the *Carnot equivalent efficiency* of a Rankine cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Underlying Thermodynamic Logic

The heat addition in a Rankine cycle is **NOT isothermal** — it occurs across a range of temperatures. To compare it with a Carnot cycle, we conceptually replace the curve (4 → 1) on the **T-s diagram** with a *straight horizontal line* of equivalent area under it.

**Step-by-step logic of the concept:**

- The **Second Law** of Thermodynamics, for a quasi-equilibrium (reversible) process, gives:
$$dq_{rev} = T\,ds$$
- For heat addition from state 4 to state 1, integrate along the process path:
$$q_{in} = \int_{4}^{1} T\,ds$$
- The total heat added is therefore represented by the **area under the curve** on the T-s diagram from state 4 to state 1.
- The *horizontal width* of this area on the T-s axis is:
$$\Delta s = s_{1} - s_{4}$$
- Define an **equivalent constant temperature** Tₘₕₐ such that:
$$T_{MHA}\,(s_{1} - s_{4}) = \int_{4}^{1} T\,ds = q_{in}$$
- Solving for Tₘₕₐ:
$$T_{MHA} = \dfrac{q_{in}}{s_{1} - s_{4}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric interpretation:** On the T-s diagram, Tₘₕₐ is the height of a *rectangle* whose **area** equals the area under the **actual heat-addition curve** (4 → 1) and whose **width** is the entropy change (s₁ − s₄). It is essentially a *weighted-average temperature* of the heat-addition process, weighted by the local **dT/ds** slope.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Concept — Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Tₘₕₐ important?**
> 1. It allows direct comparison of the **actual Rankine cycle** with the **ideal Carnot cycle**.
> 2. It pinpoints the *irreversibility* in heat addition — the gap between Tₘₕₐ and Tₘₐₓ.
> 3. It guides the design of cycle modifications: **superheating**, **reheating**, and **regenerative feedwater heating** all aim to **raise Tₘₕₐ**, thereby improving thermal efficiency.
> 4. It is a *figure of merit* used in thermodynamics textbooks (P.K. Nag, R.K. Rajput) to assess the **relative performance** of vapor cycles.

The **Carnot-equivalent efficiency** of a Rankine cycle, using Tₘₕₐ and Tₘᵢₙ, is:

$$\eta_{Rankine,\,equiv} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{MHA}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{3}}{T_{MHA}}$$

This efficiency is **always less than** the Carnot efficiency between Tₘₐₓ and Tₘᵢₙ, because Tₘₕₐ < Tₘₐₓ.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition | Typical Units |
|---|---------|----------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $T_{MHA} = \dfrac{q_{in}}{s_{1} - s_{4}}$ | Core definition of mean temperature of heat addition | K |
| 2 | $T_{MHA} = \dfrac{h_{1} - h_{4}}{s_{1} - s_{4}}$ | Expressed using steam-table enthalpies & entropies | K |
| 3 | $q_{in} = h_{1} - h_{4}$ | Heat added per kg of working fluid in the boiler | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $\Delta s = s_{1} - s_{4} = s_{1} - s_{3}$ | Isentropic pump ⇒ $s_{4} = s_{3}$ | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 5 | $\eta_{Rankine,\,equiv} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{MHA}}$ | Carnot-equivalent efficiency using Tₘₕₐ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{max}}$ | Reference Carnot efficiency between Tₘₐₓ and Tₘᵢₙ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $h_{4} = h_{3} + v_{f}\,(P_{4} - P_{3})$ | Pump exit enthalpy (compressed liquid) | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $s_{4} \approx s_{3}$ | Isentropic pump (reversible adiabatic) | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 9 | $T_{3} = T_{sat}\,@\,P_{cond}$ | Condenser temperature (minimum temperature of cycle) | K |
| 10 | $T_{max} = T_{1}$ | Maximum temperature (turbine inlet / superheat) | K |
| 11 | $\eta_{Rankine} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_{1}-h_{2}) - (h_{4}-h_{3})}{h_{1}-h_{4}}$ | Actual Rankine efficiency (cross-check) | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Useful relationships to remember:**
> - Since $s_{4} = s_{3}$, you can always read $s_{4}$ directly from the **saturated-liquid entropy** at the *condenser* pressure.
> - $h_{3} = h_{f}\,@\,P_{cond}$ and $h_{1}$ is read from the **superheated steam table** at boiler pressure and turbine-inlet temperature.
> - $v_{f}$ is the **specific volume of saturated liquid** at the condenser pressure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Tₘₕₐ

### Step 1 — Start with the First Law for the boiler (open / steady-flow system)

For a steady-flow boiler, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes, the heat added per unit mass is:

$$q_{in} = h_{1} - h_{4}$$

where:
- $h_{1}$ = enthalpy of steam leaving the boiler (turbine inlet)
- $h_{4}$ = enthalpy of compressed liquid entering the boiler (pump exit)

---

### Step 2 — Apply the Second Law for a reversible process

For a **reversible (quasi-equilibrium)** heat transfer, the differential heat transfer is related to the differential entropy change by:

$$dq_{rev} = T\,ds$$

> The boiler heat-addition process in the **ideal Rankine cycle** is treated as internally reversible.

---

### Step 3 — Integrate along the heat-addition path (state 4 → state 1)

$$\int_{4}^{1} dq_{rev} = \int_{4}^{1} T\,ds$$

$$q_{in} = \int_{4}^{1} T\,ds$$

This integral represents the **area under the curve** 4 → 1 on the T–s diagram.

---

### Step 4 — Geometrically replace the area with an equivalent rectangle

Define a **constant equivalent temperature** $T_{MHA}$ such that the rectangular area equals the actual area:

$$T_{MHA} \cdot (s_{1} - s_{4}) = \int_{4}^{1} T\,ds = q_{in}$$

---

### Step 5 — Solve for Tₘₕₐ

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;T_{MHA} = \dfrac{q_{in}}{s_{1} - s_{4}} = \dfrac{h_{1} - h_{4}}{s_{1} - s_{4}}\;}$$

This is the **final, board-exam ready expression** for the mean temperature of heat addition.

---

### Step 6 — Isentropic pump assumption

Since the pump is modelled as **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic):

$$s_{4} = s_{3}$$

Therefore the entropy change during heat addition is:

$$\Delta s = s_{1} - s_{4} = s_{1} - s_{3}$$

This is often easier to evaluate because $s_{3}$ is simply the **saturated-liquid entropy** at the condenser pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Rankine-Cycle Efficiency from Tₘₕₐ and Tₘᵢₙ

For the *equivalent* Carnot cycle operating between $T_{MHA}$ and $T_{min}$:

$$\eta_{equiv} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{MHA}}$$

For a **pure Carnot cycle** between $T_{max}$ and $T_{min}$:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{max}}$$

Since $T_{MHA} < T_{max}$ (because the average temperature during heat addition is always less than the peak temperature reached), it follows that:

$$\dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{MHA}} > \dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{max}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \eta_{Rankine,\,equiv} < \eta_{Carnot}$$

This proves rigorously that **the Rankine cycle efficiency is always less than the Carnot efficiency** between the same $T_{max}$ and $T_{min}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem:** An ideal Rankine cycle operates between a boiler pressure of **10 MPa** with turbine-inlet temperature **500 °C**, and a condenser pressure of **10 kPa**. Compute the **mean temperature of heat addition** and the **Carnot-equivalent thermal efficiency** of the cycle. Compare with the Carnot efficiency between 500 °C and 45.81 °C.

### Step 1 — Read steam-table properties

**State 1:** Superheated steam at 10 MPa, 500 °C

$$h_{1} = 3373.7 \;\text{kJ/kg}, \qquad s_{1} = 6.5966 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**State 3:** Saturated liquid at condenser pressure = 10 kPa

$$h_{3} = h_{f}\,@\,10\,\text{kPa} = 191.81 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

$$s_{3} = s_{f}\,@\,10\,\text{kPa} = 0.6492 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

$$v_{3} = v_{f}\,@\,10\,\text{kPa} = 0.001010 \;\text{m}^{3}\text{/kg}$$

**State 4:** Pump exit (isentropic compression to 10 MPa)

$$h_{4} = h_{3} + v_{3}\,(P_{4} - P_{3}) = 191.81 + 0.001010\,(10000 - 10) \approx 201.91 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

$$s_{4} = s_{3} = 0.6492 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute heat added

$$q_{in} = h_{1} - h_{4} = 3373.7 - 201.91 = 3171.79 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute entropy change

$$\Delta s = s_{1} - s_{4} = 6.5966 - 0.6492 = 5.9474 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

---

### Step 4 — Compute Tₘₕₐ

$$T_{MHA} = \dfrac{q_{in}}{\Delta s} = \dfrac{3171.79}{5.9474} = 533.31 \;\text{K} \;\approx\; 260.16\;°\text{C}$$

---

### Step 5 — Compute the Carnot-equivalent efficiency

$$T_{min} = T_{sat}\,@\,10\,\text{kPa} = 45.81\;°\text{C} = 318.96 \;\text{K}$$

$$\eta_{Rankine,\,equiv} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{MHA}} = 1 - \dfrac{318.96}{533.31} = 1 - 0.5981 = 0.4019$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{Rankine,\,equiv} = 40.19\,\%\;}$$

---

### Step 6 — Compare with pure Carnot efficiency (500 °C ↔ 45.81 °C)

$$T_{max} = 500\;°\text{C} = 773.15 \;\text{K}$$

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{max}} = 1 - \dfrac{318.96}{773.15} = 1 - 0.4125 = 0.5875$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{Carnot} = 58.75\,\%\;}$$

---

### Step 7 — Interpretation

$$\eta_{Carnot} - \eta_{Rankine,\,equiv} = 58.75 - 40.19 = 18.56\,\%$$

The Rankine cycle loses ~**18.6 percentage points** of efficiency because of **variable-temperature heat addition** (the fluid absorbs heat at temperatures well below the peak of 500 °C). Methods like **superheating**, **reheating**, and **regeneration** are used to push $T_{MHA}$ closer to $T_{max}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Rankine Cycle with Tₘₕₐ Concept

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **cycle states**, the **components**, and the **relationship between actual heat addition and the equivalent mean temperature**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS3["STATE 3\nSATURATED LIQUID\nP CONDENSER"] --> nodePUMP["PUMP\nISENTROPIC COMPRESSION"]
    nodePUMP --> nodeS4["STATE 4\nCOMPRESSED LIQUID\nP BOILER"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeBOILER["BOILER\nVARIABLE T HEAT ADDITION\nQIN H1 MINUS H4"]
    nodeBOILER --> nodeS1["STATE 1\nSUPERHEATED STEAM\nT MAX"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeTURB["TURBINE\nISENTROPIC EXPANSION"]
    nodeTURB --> nodeS2["STATE 2\nLOW QUALITY MIXTURE\nP CONDENSER"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeCOND["CONDENSER\nHEAT REJECTION\nQOUT H2 MINUS H3"]
    nodeCOND --> nodeS3

    nodeBOILER -.->|"AREA UNDER CURVE EQUAL"| nodeMHA["MEAN TEMPERATURE\nOF HEAT ADDITION\nTMHA QIN OVER DELTA S"]
    nodeCOND -.->|"T MIN RESERVOIR"| nodeMHA

    nodeMHA -.->|"CARNOT EQUIVALENT\n1 MINUS TMIN OVER TMHA"| nodeEFF["RANKINE EQUIVALENT\nTHERMAL EFFICIENCY"]

    style nodeBOILER fill:#ffd1a4
    style nodeCOND fill:#b3d9ff
    style nodeMHA fill:#c2f0c2
    style nodeEFF fill:#f0c2f0
```

---

## 4.2 T–s Diagram — Geometric Interpretation of Tₘₕₐ (Schematic Block Map)

The diagram below illustrates *geometrically* how the area under the **actual heat-addition curve (4 → 1)** equals the area of a **rectangle of height Tₘₕₐ and width (s₁ − s₄)**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["ORIGIN\nLOW T LOW S"] --> nodeCURVE["CURVE FOUR TO ONE\nVARIABLE TEMPERATURE\nACTUAL HEAT ADDITION"]
    nodeCURVE --> nodeS1["S1\nSUPERHEATED VAPOR"]

    nodeA --> nodeH["HORIZONTAL LINE\nAT HEIGHT TMHA\nCONSTANT T"]
    nodeH --> nodeS1

    nodeCURVE -.->|"AREA = QIN"| nodeEQ["EQUIVALENT AREA\nRECTANGLE HEIGHT TMHA\nWIDTH S1 MINUS S4"]
    nodeH -.->|"AREA = TMHA TIMES DELTA S"| nodeEQ

    style nodeCURVE fill:#ffd1a4
    style nodeH fill:#c2f0c2
    style nodeEQ fill:#f0e68c
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **orange curve (4 → 1)** is the actual heat-addition path on the T-s plane.
> - The **green horizontal line at height Tₘₕₐ** is the *equivalent* isothermal heat-addition path.
> - Both enclose the **same area** (= qᵢₙ). This is the **definition** of Tₘₕₐ.
> - The yellow block represents the **rectangle of equivalent area** that establishes the value of Tₘₕₐ.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

### Q1. Define *mean temperature of heat addition*. State its formula and physical significance. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> The mean temperature of heat addition, $T_{MHA}$, is the *equivalent constant temperature* at which heat would have to be transferred to the working fluid during the boiler process to produce the same entropy change as the actual *variable-temperature* heat addition.
>
> $$T_{MHA} = \dfrac{q_{in}}{s_{1} - s_{4}} = \dfrac{h_{1} - h_{4}}{s_{1} - s_{4}}$$
>
> **Physical significance:** It is used to compute the *Carnot-equivalent efficiency* of the Rankine cycle, $\eta = 1 - T_{min}/T_{MHA}$, and to quantify the gap between the real cycle and an ideal Carnot cycle.

---

### Q2. Why is the Rankine cycle efficiency always less than the Carnot efficiency between the same temperature limits? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> In the Rankine cycle, heat is added over a *range of temperatures* (from compressed-liquid to superheated-vapor), not at a single constant temperature. Consequently, the **mean temperature of heat addition $T_{MHA}$ is always less than the maximum temperature $T_{max}$** of the cycle.
>
> Since Carnot efficiency is $1 - T_{min}/T_{max}$ and Rankine-equivalent efficiency is $1 - T_{min}/T_{MHA}$, and since $T_{MHA} < T_{max}$, it follows that
> $\eta_{Rankine} < \eta_{Carnot}$. The difference $(T_{max} - T_{MHA})$ represents the *irreversibility* due to variable-temperature heat addition.

---

## 5.2 Part-B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, B.Tech PEMET631]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** An ideal Rankine cycle uses water as the working fluid. The boiler pressure is **8 MPa** and the turbine-inlet temperature is **480 °C**. The condenser operates at **10 kPa**. Determine:
1. The enthalpy and entropy at every state. [3 Marks]
2. The mean temperature of heat addition, $T_{MHA}$. [4 Marks]

**(b)** Using the value of $T_{MHA}$ obtained in (a), compute:
1. The Carnot-equivalent thermal efficiency of the Rankine cycle. [3 Marks]
2. The pure Carnot efficiency between 480 °C and the condenser temperature, and the percentage efficiency gap. [4 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — State-point properties**

**State 1:** Superheated steam at 8 MPa, 480 °C (interpolating between 8 MPa/450 °C and 8 MPa/500 °C in steam tables):
$$h_{1} = 3348.4 \;\text{kJ/kg}, \qquad s_{1} = 6.6586 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**State 3:** Saturated liquid at 10 kPa:
$$h_{3} = 191.81 \;\text{kJ/kg}, \quad s_{3} = 0.6492 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}, \quad v_{3} = 0.001010 \;\text{m}^{3}\text{/kg}$$

**State 4:** Pump exit (isentropic):
$$h_{4} = 191.81 + 0.001010 \times (8000 - 10) = 199.89 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$
$$s_{4} = s_{3} = 0.6492 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Part (a) — Mean Temperature of Heat Addition**

$$q_{in} = h_{1} - h_{4} = 3348.4 - 199.89 = 3148.51 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

$$\Delta s = s_{1} - s_{4} = 6.6586 - 0.6492 = 6.0094 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

$$T_{MHA} = \dfrac{3148.51}{6.0094} = 523.93 \;\text{K} \;\approx\; 250.78\;°\text{C}$$

$$\boxed{\;T_{MHA} = 523.93 \;\text{K}\;}$$

**Part (b) — Carnot-equivalent efficiency**

$T_{min} = T_{sat}\,@\,10\,\text{kPa} = 45.81\;°\text{C} = 318.96 \;\text{K}$

$$\eta_{Rankine,\,equiv} = 1 - \dfrac{318.96}{523.93} = 1 - 0.6089 = 0.3911$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{Rankine,\,equiv} = 39.11\,\%\;}$$

**Part (b) — Pure Carnot and gap**

$T_{max} = 480\;°\text{C} = 753.15 \;\text{K}$

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{318.96}{753.15} = 1 - 0.4235 = 0.5765$$

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 57.65\,\%$$

$$\text{Efficiency gap} = 57.65 - 39.11 = 18.54 \;\text{percentage points}$$

$$\text{Relative gap} = \dfrac{18.54}{57.65} \times 100 = 32.16\,\%$$

> The Rankine cycle operates at only **67.84 %** of the ideal Carnot efficiency under identical limits.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a)** Derive the expression for the *mean temperature of heat addition* $T_{MHA}$ for an ideal Rankine cycle, starting from the first and second laws of thermodynamics. State clearly the assumptions made. [7 Marks]

**(b)** With the help of a *T-s diagram*, explain why $T_{MHA} < T_{max}$ in any practical Rankine cycle, and discuss **two cycle modifications** that can raise $T_{MHA}$ and thereby improve the thermal efficiency. [7 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation**

*Assumptions:*
1. The Rankine cycle is *ideal*: all processes are internally reversible.
2. The pump and turbine are isentropic, so $s_{3} = s_{4}$ and $s_{1} = s_{2}$.
3. Steady-state, steady-flow operation; kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible.
4. The working fluid is pure substance (water/steam) with properties read from steam tables.

*Derivation:*

For the boiler, applying the **First Law** to an open system:
$$q_{in} = h_{1} - h_{4}$$

Applying the **Second Law** for a reversible process:
$$dq_{rev} = T\,ds$$

Integrating along the heat-addition path from state 4 to state 1:
$$q_{in} = \int_{4}^{1} T\,ds$$

Define an equivalent constant temperature $T_{MHA}$ such that the *rectangular area* on the T-s diagram equals the *actual* area under the curve:
$$T_{MHA} \cdot (s_{1} - s_{4}) = \int_{4}^{1} T\,ds = q_{in}$$

Solving:
$$\boxed{\;T_{MHA} = \dfrac{q_{in}}{s_{1} - s_{4}} = \dfrac{h_{1} - h_{4}}{s_{1} - s_{4}}\;}$$

Since the pump is isentropic, $s_{4} = s_{3}$, so the denominator can also be written as $s_{1} - s_{3}$.

**Part (b) — Geometric explanation and cycle modifications**

*Why $T_{MHA} < T_{max}$:*
On the T-s diagram, the heat-addition curve from state 4 (compressed liquid) to state 1 (superheated vapor) starts at a *low temperature* (close to the saturation temperature at boiler pressure) and rises continuously up to $T_{max}$ at state 1. The *average* (i.e., Tₘₕₐ) of this monotonically rising curve is **necessarily less** than its peak value $T_{max}$. Geometrically, Tₘₕₐ is the *height of the equivalent rectangle* inscribed under the curve, which is always shorter than the curve's highest point.

*Two cycle modifications to raise $T_{MHA}$:*

1. **Superheating the steam:** Raising the turbine-inlet temperature shifts state 1 to a *higher-entropy, higher-temperature* point. This shifts the entire heat-addition curve upward on the T-s diagram, increasing both Tₘₐₓ and the *average* Tₘₕₐ. Modern supercritical and ultra-supercritical plants operate at 600 °C – 700 °C to push Tₘₕₐ above 800 K.

2. **Reheating the steam:** After partial expansion in a high-pressure turbine, the steam is sent back to the boiler and reheated (typically to the original turbine-inlet temperature) before entering a low-pressure turbine. This *adds another high-temperature heat-addition leg* on the T-s diagram, raising the *weighted average* temperature at which heat is supplied, and hence raising Tₘₕₐ.

*(A third option for completeness: regenerative feedwater heating using open or closed feedwater heaters, which extracts a fraction of steam to preheat the condensate, effectively *shifts* the heat-addition curve to higher temperatures.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the isentropic-pump assumption.** Many students incorrectly compute $s_{4}$ from compressed-liquid tables. The shortcut $s_{4} = s_{3}$ (saturated-liquid entropy at condenser pressure) is the KTU-board-preferred simplification.
> 2. **Using $T_{max}$ instead of $T_{MHA}$ in the denominator of $\eta$.** This is the most common error — it *overstates* the Rankine efficiency and inflates the answer by ~15–20 percentage points. Always verify which temperature is being asked.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency.** $T_{MHA}$ must be in **Kelvin** when plugged into the Carnot expression. Celsius values will yield a *negative* efficiency.
> 4. **Skipping the pump-work correction.** For a 7-mark problem, omitting $h_{4} = h_{3} + v_{f}\Delta P$ is treated as a *minor error*; KTU examiners usually deduct **1 mark** for this.
> 5. **Sign convention mistakes.** $q_{in} = h_{1} - h_{4}$ is *positive* (heat *added*). Reversing the sign will propagate errors throughout.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Mean Temperature of Heat Addition**
>
> - **Definition:** Equivalent constant temperature at which heat is *conceptually* added to yield the same $\Delta s$ as the actual variable-T heat addition. [Definition type question, 2 marks]
> - **Master formula:** $T_{MHA} = \dfrac{h_{1} - h_{4}}{s_{1} - s_{4}}$ (with $s_{4} = s_{3}$ for an ideal cycle).
> - **Carnot-equivalent efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{MHA}}$.
> - **Always in Kelvin** when used in efficiency expressions.
> - **$T_{MHA} < T_{max}$** — this is the *fundamental* reason the Rankine cycle is less efficient than Carnot.
> - **Geometric meaning on T-s diagram:** Height of the rectangle whose area = area under the heat-addition curve.
> - **Methods to raise $T_{MHA}$:** Superheating, reheating, regeneration — all are favourite 7- to 14-mark topics in KTU ESE.
> - **Numerical setup sequence:** Read state 1 from superheat tables → read state 3 from saturation tables → compute state 4 using pump work → compute $T_{MHA}$ → compute efficiency.
> - **Common errors:** wrong sign of $q_{in}$, Celsius instead of Kelvin, omitting pump work, using $T_{max}$ instead of $T_{MHA}$.
> - **Cross-check:** The Rankine efficiency $\eta = W_{net}/q_{in}$ computed from turbine and pump work should match $1 - T_{min}/T_{MHA}$ to within rounding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Superheat and Inlet Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Superheat and Inlet Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the analysis of the **Ideal Rankine Cycle**, two of the most important parameters governing the thermal performance of a steam power plant are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Superheat (ΔT_sup):** The quantity of heat added to *saturated (dry) steam* in the superheater section of the boiler, raising its temperature **above the saturation temperature** corresponding to the boiler pressure. Mathematically,
> $$\Delta T_{sup} = T_3 - T_{sat@P_{boiler}}$$
> where $T_3$ is the actual turbine inlet temperature and $T_{sat}$ is the saturation temperature at the boiler pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inlet Pressure (P₁ or P_boiler):** The pressure at which steam is generated/superheated in the boiler and admitted to the turbine. In Rankine cycle notation, this is the pressure at state point **1** (after pump) / **2** (after boiler exit / superheater exit) entering the turbine.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker vs Tava" Intuition

Imagine you are boiling water in two different setups to make tea:

- **Setup A (Low Pressure + Wet Steam):** You boil water in an open pan (at 1 atm, 100 °C). The steam produced is "wet", full of water droplets, and cannot push a piston with much force. This is analogous to a Rankine cycle operating at **low boiler pressure with saturated (or slightly wet) steam**.

- **Setup B (High Pressure + Superheated Steam):** You use a pressure cooker (2–3 atm, ~120 °C) AND additionally let the steam pass over a hot coil (superheater) so that the steam is "bone dry" and much hotter (say 350–600 °C). This dry, high-energy steam expands powerfully in the turbine, doing far more work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Connection:** Modern 600 MW / 800 MW supercritical units operate with steam at **~250 bar and 600 °C** precisely because superheat + high inlet pressure together extract maximum useful work per kg of steam, while keeping the turbine exhaust dry enough to prevent blade erosion.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Parameters?

| Parameter | Primary Effect | Secondary Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Superheat** ↑ | ↑ Turbine work output (W_T) | ↑ Thermal efficiency (η_th); ↓ exhaust moisture |
| **Inlet Pressure** ↑ | ↑ Mean temperature of heat addition | ↑ η_th; ↑ W_T; ↓ exhaust moisture; ↑ W_P (penalty) |

> [!NOTE]
> Both parameters are **design choices** made by the plant engineer before the plant is built. Understanding their individual and combined effects is therefore a **Module-1 core learning outcome** under KTU 2024 Scheme (CO1: *Understand the thermodynamic principles of steam power cycles*).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Effect of Superheating — Mechanism Step-by-Step

Consider the simple Rankine cycle **1-2-3-4-1** on a T-s diagram. State points:
- **1** → Saturated liquid at condenser pressure (P₄)
- **2** → Compressed liquid (after pump) at boiler pressure (P₁)
- **3** → Steam leaving superheater (state at turbine inlet)
- **4** → Steam leaving turbine at condenser pressure

### Step 1 — Location on T-s Diagram
Superheating moves state point **3** vertically **upward** along a constant-pressure line (since boiler pressure is fixed during this comparison).

### Step 2 — Heat Added Increases
$$q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$$
Because $h_3$ rises with superheat while $h_2$ (pump exit) is unchanged, $q_{in}$ **increases**.

### Step 3 — Turbine Work Increases
$$w_T = h_3 - h_4$$
Since $h_3$ rises and $h_4$ (at fixed P₄, fixed s₄=s₃) also rises but **by a smaller amount**, the net turbine work $w_T$ **increases substantially**.

### Step 4 — Thermal Efficiency
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_T - w_P}{q_{in}}$$
Both numerator and denominator grow, but **the numerator grows faster in relative terms**, giving a **higher thermal efficiency**.

### Step 5 — Exhaust Moisture
During expansion, the isentropic line from a higher $T_3$ ends at a state point **4** with **higher dryness fraction** ($x_4 \uparrow$). This is critical because:
> [!WARNING]
> **Blade Erosion Limit:** Steam moisture exceeding **~12–15%** causes rapid erosion of low-pressure turbine blades. Superheating protects against this.

### Step 6 — Practical Limit
There exists an **economic and metallurgical limit** to superheating. Modern ferritic steels permit ~**620 °C**; advanced austenitic steels and Ni-based superalloys go up to ~**700 °C**. Beyond this, the cost of exotic materials outweighs efficiency gains.

---

## 2.2 Effect of Inlet (Boiler) Pressure — Mechanism Step-by-Step

Now hold superheat temperature $T_3$ **fixed** and vary boiler pressure $P_1$.

### Step 1 — Location on T-s Diagram
Increasing $P_1$ shifts the **constant-pressure line** at state 3 to the **right** on the T-s chart (saturation temperature rises, latent heat drops).

### Step 2 — Average Temperature of Heat Addition
$$\bar{T}_{add} = \frac{q_{in}}{\Delta s_{12}} = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{s_3 - s_2}$$
The mean temperature $\bar{T}_{add}$ **rises** with $P_1$ because heat is now being added over a higher temperature range.

### Step 3 — Efficiency Rises (Carnot Logic)
By analogy with the Carnot principle, raising $\bar{T}_{add}$ (while keeping $\bar{T}_{reject}$ fixed at condenser) **increases thermal efficiency**:
$$\eta_{th} \approx 1 - \frac{T_{cond}}{\bar{T}_{add}}$$

### Step 4 — Turbine Work Increases
$$w_T = h_3 - h_4$$
The enthalpy drop across the turbine increases because of the larger pressure ratio.

### Step 5 — Exhaust Moisture Decreases (Bonus Benefit)
Higher boiler pressure means the expansion line ends at a **higher quality** state 4 (less moisture) → less blade erosion.

### Step 6 — The Pumping Penalty
$$w_P = v_1 (P_1 - P_4)$$
Pump work grows **linearly** with $P_1$ because liquid specific volume $v_1$ is small but finite. However, since $v_1 \ll$ specific volume of steam, the **net work** still grows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Trade-off:** Increasing pressure beyond ~**220–250 bar** (supercritical region) gives *diminishing* returns and requires very thick, expensive boiler drums / once-through designs.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w_P = h_2 - h_1 = v_1(P_1 - P_4)$ | Pump work per kg | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $w_T = h_3 - h_4$ | Turbine work per kg | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ | Heat added in boiler + superheater | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ | Heat rejected in condenser | kJ/kg |
| 5 | $w_{net} = w_T - w_P$ | Net cycle work per kg | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{q_{out}}{q_{in}}$ | Thermal efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| 7 | $s_3 = s_4 = s_{f@P_4} + x_4 \cdot s_{fg@P_4}$ | Isentropic expansion | kJ/kg·K |
| 8 | $x_4 = \dfrac{s_3 - s_{f4}}{s_{fg4}}$ | Exhaust dryness fraction | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{SSC} = \dfrac{3600}{w_{net}}$ | Specific Steam Consumption | kg/kWh |
| 10 | $\text{Heat Rate} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th}}$ | Heat rate | kJ/kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions for all KTU problems on this topic:**
> - Condenser pressure $P_4$ is **fixed** (typically 0.1 bar or 0.07 bar).
> - Turbine is **isentropic** (ideal cycle unless stated otherwise).
> - Pump work is **often neglected** unless the question specifically asks for it (in which case always include $v_1(P_1-P_4)$).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Relevance |
|---|---|
| **Coal-fired thermal power stations** | Higher superheat and pressure directly improve plant efficiency → less coal per kWh |
| **Nuclear power plants (PWR/BWR)** | Lower pressures but use **moisture separator + reheater** because exhaust would otherwise be too wet |
| **Supercritical & Ultra-Supercritical plants** | Operate at 250–300 bar and 600–700 °C to push η_th beyond 45% |
| **Combined Cycle (Rankine + Brayton)** | Gas turbine exhaust reheats steam → effectively using "free" superheat |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Net Work and Thermal Efficiency

Starting from First-Law steady-flow energy equations for each component, with **negligible KE and PE changes**:

### Step 1 — Pump (1 → 2)
$$w_{pump,in} = h_2 - h_1$$
For an incompressible liquid approximation:
$$h_2 - h_1 \approx v_1(P_1 - P_4)$$
This is the **work input to the cycle**.

### Step 2 — Boiler + Superheater (2 → 3)
$$q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$$
This is the **heat absorbed** from combustion of fuel.

### Step 3 — Turbine (3 → 4) — Isentropic
$$w_{turb,out} = h_3 - h_4$$
where $h_4$ is determined from $s_4 = s_3$ at $P_4$.

### Step 4 — Condenser (4 → 1)
$$q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$$
This is the **heat rejected** to cooling water.

### Step 5 — Net Work per kg of Steam
$$w_{net} = w_T - w_P = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)$$

### Step 6 — Thermal Efficiency
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style)

> **Problem Statement:** A steam power plant operates on an ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at **3 MPa and 350 °C**, and the condenser pressure is **10 kPa**. Determine:
> (a) Turbine work, pump work, heat added, and thermal efficiency.
> (b) Dryness fraction at turbine exhaust.
> *(Use steam tables — values given below.)*

### Given Properties (read from superheated steam tables and saturated tables):

**State 1 — Saturated liquid at P₄ = 10 kPa:**
$$h_1 = h_{f@10\,kPa} = 191.83\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$v_1 = v_{f@10\,kPa} = 0.00101\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$
$$s_1 = s_{f@10\,kPa} = 0.6493\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

**State 2 — Pump Exit at P₁ = 3 MPa:**
$$h_2 = h_1 + v_1(P_1 - P_4)$$
Substituting numerically:

$$h_2 = 191.83 + (0.00101)(3000 - 10)$$
$$h_2 = 191.83 + (0.00101)(2990)$$
$$h_2 = 191.83 + 3.0199$$
$$h_2 \approx 194.85\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**State 3 — Superheated Steam at 3 MPa, 350 °C:**
$$h_3 = 2988.1\ \text{kJ/kg}, \quad s_3 = 6.7424\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$$

**State 4 — Isentropic Expansion to 10 kPa, with s₄ = s₃ = 6.7424 kJ/kg·K:**

At 10 kPa:
$$s_f = 0.6493,\quad s_g = 8.1502$$
$$s_{fg} = 7.5009$$

Quality at state 4:
$$x_4 = \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7424 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = \frac{6.0931}{7.5009}$$
$$x_4 \approx 0.8124$$
$$h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.83 + (0.8124)(2392.8)$$
$$h_4 = 191.83 + 1943.8 \approx 2135.6\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

### Final Answers (a):
$$w_P = h_2 - h_1 = 194.85 - 191.83 = 3.02\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$w_T = h_3 - h_4 = 2988.1 - 2135.6 = 852.5\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$w_{net} = w_T - w_P = 852.5 - 3.02 = 849.48\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 2988.1 - 194.85 = 2793.25\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{849.48}{2793.25} = 0.3041 = 30.41\%$$

### Final Answers (b):
$$x_4 \approx 0.8124 \approx 81.24\%$$
Moisture content at exhaust ≈ **18.76%** — this is high; in practice a **reheater** would be needed.

---

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — Effect of Each Variable

The table below summarizes the trend when **one variable is changed** while others are held constant:

| Parameter changed | Direction | w_T | w_P | w_net | q_in | η_th | x₄ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Superheat ↑** (T₃ ↑, P₁ fixed) | ↑ | ↑↑ | ≈ | ↑↑ | ↑ | **↑** | ↑ (less moisture) |
| **Inlet Pressure ↑** (P₁ ↑, T₃ fixed) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (small) | ↑ | ↑ (less than w_net) | **↑** | ↑ (less moisture) |
| **Condenser Pressure ↓** (P₄ ↓) | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ (negligible) | ↑ | ≈ constant | **↑** | ↓ (more moisture) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU students must remember:** Among the three parameters (superheat, inlet pressure, condenser pressure), the **last one (lower condenser pressure)** has the *largest* numerical effect on efficiency — but it is bounded by the **cooling water temperature** (~30–35 °C) available at the plant site.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 T-s Diagram — Effect of Superheat and Inlet Pressure

The Mermaid diagram below renders the comparative T-s chart logic (since Mermaid cannot plot thermodynamic curves directly, we represent the state-point shifts conceptually).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Saturated Cycle 1-2-3s-4s-1: Reference"] --> B["Increase Superheat: 3 to 3a, 4 to 4a"]
    A --> C["Increase Boiler Pressure: 3 to 3b, 4 to 4b"]
    B --> D["Higher T at turbine inlet"]
    B --> E["More turbine work Wt"]
    B --> F["Higher thermal efficiency"]
    B --> G["Dryness fraction x4 improves"]
    C --> H["Higher mean T of heat addition"]
    C --> I["More turbine work Wt"]
    C --> J["Higher thermal efficiency"]
    C --> K["Dryness fraction x4 improves"]
    C --> L["Pump work Wp slightly rises"]
```

## 4.2 h-s (Mollier) Diagram Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Boiler["Boiler + Superheater Zone"]
        B1["State 2: Compressed Liquid"]
        B2["State 2a: Saturation Begins"]
        B3["State 2b: Saturated Vapor"]
        B4["State 3: Superheated Steam T3 > Tsat"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4
    end
    subgraph Turbine["Turbine Isentropic Expansion"]
        T1["State 3: High h, high s"]
        T2["State 4: Lower h, same s = s3"]
        T1 --> T2
    end
    B4 --> T1
    subgraph Condenser["Condenser Zone"]
        C1["State 4: Wet Steam"]
        C2["State 1: Saturated Liquid"]
        C1 --> C2
    end
    T2 --> C1
    subgraph Pump["Pump Compression"]
        P1["State 1: Low P"]
        P2["State 2: High P"]
        P1 --> P2
    end
    C2 --> P1
    P2 --> B1
```

## 4.3 Component-Level Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feed["Feedwater Pump"] -->|"W_P = v1(P1-P4)"| econ["Economizer"]
    econ --> evap["Evaporator Drum"]
    evap --> sup["Superheater Coils"]
    sup -->|"h3 - h4 = W_T"| turbine["Steam Turbine"]
    turbine -->|"h4 - h1 = q_out"| cond["Condenser"]
    cond -->|"Condensate"| feed
    furnace["Coal-fired Furnace: q_in source"] -.supplies heat.-> evap
    furnace -.supplies heat.-> sup
    gen["Electrical Generator"] --- turbine
```

## 4.4 Heat Addition Temperature Profile (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["T1 (Pump exit) ~ 45 C"] -->|"Economizer"| B["T_sat @ P1 ~ 235 C"]
    B -->|"Evaporator"| C["T_sat constant ~ 235 C"]
    C -->|"Superheater"| D["T3 up to 600 C modern plant"]
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style D fill:#ffcdd2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight from Diagrams:** Notice how the **latent-heat region** (where temperature stays constant at $T_{sat}$) shrinks as boiler pressure rises. This is the **physical reason** why higher-pressure boilers are physically smaller and more compact per MW of capacity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
*How does superheating of steam affect the (i) thermal efficiency, and (ii) dryness fraction of steam at the turbine exhaust, of an ideal Rankine cycle?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) **Thermal Efficiency:** Superheating increases the **mean temperature of heat addition** $\bar{T}_{add}$ in the cycle. Since
$$\eta_{th} \approx 1 - \frac{T_{cond}}{\bar{T}_{add}}$$
a higher $\bar{T}_{add}$ increases $\eta_{th}$. Turbine work $w_T = h_3 - h_4$ also increases because $h_3$ rises substantially while $h_4$ rises only slightly (since $s_4 = s_3$ and $h_4$ is fixed at condenser P₄).

(ii) **Dryness Fraction:** During isentropic expansion from state 3, the constant-entropy line starting from a higher $T_3$ lands at state 4 with a **higher $x_4$** (i.e., less moisture). This **reduces blade erosion risk** in the low-pressure stages.

**[Marks distribution: 1.5 + 1.5]**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*With the help of a T-s diagram, explain the effect of increasing the boiler pressure on the Rankine cycle performance, assuming superheat temperature is held constant.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

On the T-s diagram, increasing boiler pressure $P_1$ shifts the constant-pressure line at state 3 to the **right** (higher $T_{sat}$, lower $s$ for the same T₃).

Effects (with superheat T₃ fixed):
1. **Thermal efficiency increases** because the mean temperature of heat addition rises.
2. **Net work output increases** because turbine enthalpy drop grows with pressure ratio.
3. **Pump work increases** as $w_P = v_1(P_1 - P_4)$ — but this increase is small relative to turbine gain.
4. **Dryness fraction $x_4$ improves** because expansion ends at a state farther from the saturated-vapor line.
5. **Heat input decreases** slightly because the latent-heat portion shrinks at higher pressures — this further boosts efficiency.

**[Marks distribution: 1 for diagram + 2 for effects]**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at **40 bar and 400 °C** and the condenser operates at **0.1 bar**.
(a) Determine the thermal efficiency, net work per kg of steam, and the dryness fraction at turbine exhaust. **[7 marks]**
(b) If the steam is superheated to **500 °C** keeping boiler pressure at 40 bar, find the new thermal efficiency and compare it with part (a). Comment on the effect of superheat. **[7 marks]**

#### Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Properties at each state (from steam tables):**

State 1 — Saturated liquid at 0.1 bar:
$h_1 = 191.83\ \text{kJ/kg}$, $v_1 = 0.00101\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, $s_1 = 0.6493\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$

State 2 — Pump exit:
$$h_2 = 191.83 + (0.00101)(4000 - 10) = 191.83 + 4.0299 \approx 195.86\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

State 3 — Superheated at 40 bar, 400 °C:
$h_3 = 3213.6\ \text{kJ/kg}$, $s_3 = 6.7690\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$

State 4 — Isentropic expansion to 0.1 bar, $s_4 = 6.7690$:
At 0.1 bar: $s_f = 0.6493$, $s_g = 8.1502$, $s_{fg} = 7.5009$
$$x_4 = \frac{6.7690 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = \frac{6.1197}{7.5009} = 0.8158$$
$$h_4 = 191.83 + (0.8158)(2392.8) = 191.83 + 1951.95 = 2143.78\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

Calculations:
$$w_T = 3213.6 - 2143.78 = 1069.82\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$w_P = 195.86 - 191.83 = 4.03\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$w_{net} = 1069.82 - 4.03 = 1065.79\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$q_{in} = 3213.6 - 195.86 = 3017.74\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{1065.79}{3017.74} = 0.3532 = 35.32\%$$
$$x_4 = 0.8158\ (\text{moisture} = 18.42\%)$$

#### Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

State 3' — Superheated at 40 bar, **500 °C**:
$h_{3'} = 3445.3\ \text{kJ/kg}$, $s_{3'} = 7.0901\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$

State 4' — Isentropic to 0.1 bar:
$$x_{4'} = \frac{7.0901 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = \frac{6.4408}{7.5009} = 0.8587$$
$$h_{4'} = 191.83 + (0.8587)(2392.8) = 191.83 + 2054.71 = 2246.54\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$w_T' = 3445.3 - 2246.54 = 1198.76\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$q_{in}' = 3445.3 - 195.86 = 3249.44\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$\eta_{th}' = \frac{1198.76 - 4.03}{3249.44} = \frac{1194.73}{3249.44} = 0.3676 = 36.76\%$$

**Comment:** Increasing superheat from 400 °C → 500 °C raises $\eta_{th}$ from 35.32% → 36.76% (a gain of **~1.44 percentage points**). It also raises $x_4$ from 0.816 → 0.859, reducing exhaust moisture from 18.4% → 14.1%. Hence superheating improves both **efficiency and blade safety**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
A steam power plant operates on an ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at **3 MPa and 350 °C** and the condenser pressure is **10 kPa**.
(a) Calculate the thermal efficiency and specific steam consumption. **[7 marks]**
(b) If the boiler pressure is raised to **10 MPa** keeping turbine inlet temperature at 350 °C, determine the new thermal efficiency. Comment on the effect of boiler pressure. **[7 marks]**

#### Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

(This is the same numerical already computed in Section 3.2 of these notes.)

State 1: $h_1 = 191.83\ \text{kJ/kg}$, $v_1 = 0.00101\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$
State 2: $h_2 = 194.85\ \text{kJ/kg}$
State 3: $h_3 = 2988.1\ \text{kJ/kg}$, $s_3 = 6.7424\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$
State 4: $x_4 = 0.8124$, $h_4 = 2135.6\ \text{kJ/kg}$

$$w_T = 852.5\ \text{kJ/kg},\ w_P = 3.02\ \text{kJ/kg},\ w_{net} = 849.48\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$\eta_{th} = 30.41\%$$
$$\text{SSC} = \frac{3600}{w_{net}} = \frac{3600}{849.48} = 4.238\ \text{kg/kWh}$$

#### Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

State 1 — same: $h_1 = 191.83$, $v_1 = 0.00101$
State 2' — Pump exit at 10 MPa:
$$h_{2'} = 191.83 + (0.00101)(10000 - 10) = 191.83 + 10.09 = 201.92\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
State 3' — Superheated at 10 MPa, 350 °C:
$h_{3'} = 2923.3\ \text{kJ/kg}$, $s_{3'} = 6.1402\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$

State 4' — Isentropic expansion to 10 kPa:
At 10 kPa: $s_f = 0.6493$, $s_{fg} = 7.5009$
$$x_{4'} = \frac{6.1402 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = \frac{5.4909}{7.5009} = 0.7320$$
$$h_{4'} = 191.83 + (0.7320)(2392.8) = 191.83 + 1751.53 = 1943.36\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$w_T' = 2923.3 - 1943.36 = 979.94\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$w_P' = 201.92 - 191.83 = 10.09\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$w_{net}' = 979.94 - 10.09 = 969.85\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$q_{in}' = 2923.3 - 201.92 = 2721.38\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$\eta_{th}' = \frac{969.85}{2721.38} = 0.3564 = 35.64\%$$

**Comment:** Raising boiler pressure from 3 MPa → 10 MPa (with T₃ fixed) increases $\eta_{th}$ from **30.41% → 35.64%** (gain of ~5.2 percentage points — a *much* larger gain than superheat alone).
