However, $x_4$ drops from 0.812 → 0.732, raising exhaust moisture to **26.8%** — this mandates a **reheater** in practical plants.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kPa ↔ bar** (1 bar = 100 kPa = 0.1 MPa). Marks lost: ~1 per occurrence.
> 2. **Using $s_g$ instead of $(s_f + x_4 s_{fg})$** at state 4. If $s_3 < s_{g@P_4}$, state 4 is wet — students often incorrectly assume dry/superheated.
> 3. **Neglecting pump work entirely** when the problem states "ideal Rankine cycle" — examiners generally still award partial credit if pump work is omitted *but* the answer does not match the key. Always include it; the penalty for including it is zero.
> 4. **Wrong sign conventions** for heat/work. Use the **+ve for energy INTO system** convention consistently.
> 5. **Not converting °C → K** for Carnot-style reasoning problems. If the question explicitly asks for "Carnot comparison", remember $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Effect of Superheat and Inlet Pressure**

- **Superheating** moves state 3 *upward* on the T-s chart → raises $h_3$, $q_{in}$, $w_T$, and $\eta_{th}$.
- **Inlet pressure rise** moves state 3 *to the right* (higher $T_{sat}$) → raises $\bar{T}_{add}$, $w_T$, $\eta_{th}$, and $x_4$; slightly raises $w_P$.
- **Condenser pressure drop** is the **most effective** way to raise efficiency but is bounded by cooling-water temperature.
- **Moisture limit:** Turbine exhaust moisture should not exceed **~12–15%** to prevent blade erosion.
- **Metallurgical limit:** Modern supercritical plants cap $T_3$ around **600–700 °C**.
- **Pump work formula:** Always use $w_P = v_1(P_1 - P_4)$ — *not* $v_2$, since the liquid is nearly incompressible.
- **Isentropic expansion assumption:** $s_3 = s_4$ — this is the *defining* assumption of the ideal Rankine cycle in KTU problems.
- **Specific Steam Consumption (SSC):** Lower SSC is better. Modern plants achieve ~**2.0–2.2 kg/kWh** vs Rankine baseline of ~3.5–4.5 kg/kWh.
- **Three classic KTU comparisons to remember:**
  1. Superheat effect → fixed P₁, P₄; vary T₃
  2. Pressure effect → fixed T₃, P₄; vary P₁
  3. Reheat effect → introduces an intermediate reheat pressure stage
- **Memory trick:** "**Super**heat raises **top**; **Press**ure raises **middle**; **Condenser drop** lowers **bottom**" — all three move the cycle's average temperatures upward, boosting Carnot-equivalent efficiency.
- **Formulae worth memorizing cold:** $w_T = h_3 - h_4$; $w_P = v_1(P_1-P_4)$; $\eta = (w_T - w_P)/q_{in}$; SSC = $3600/w_{net}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regenerative Feed Water Heating
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regenerative Feed Water Heating

# 🔥 Regenerative Feed Water Heating (RFWH)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Regenerative Feed Water Heating* is a thermodynamic cycle modification technique applied to the basic Rankine cycle in which a portion of partially expanded steam is **extracted (bled)** from one or more intermediate stages of the steam turbine and routed to a **Feed Water Heater (FWH)**, where it transfers its latent and sensible heat to the condensate (feed water) returning to the boiler, thereby **preheating the feed water** before evaporation begins.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are heating a large bucket of water on a stove. Instead of starting from cold water, what if you first passed the cold water over a *warm steam pipe* (which had already done some work in the engine) so the water enters the stove at, say, 150°C instead of 30°C? The stove (boiler) now has to supply **less heat** to reach boiling — saving fuel! That warm steam pipe is the *Feed Water Heater*, and the steam bled from the turbine is the "used-but-still-warm" steam being recycled. The "regeneration" refers to the heat being **regenerated back** into the cycle from within the cycle itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Bleed Steam / Extraction Steam:** The portion of steam tapped off from intermediate turbine stages.
> - **Cascade Drain:** Condensate from a higher-pressure closed FWH that drains to a lower-pressure FWH.
> - **De-aerator:** An Open FWH that also removes dissolved O₂ and CO₂ from feed water.
> - **Extraction Pressure (Pₑ):** The pressure at which steam is bled; critical to the thermal design.

### Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
|-----------|---------------|-------|
| Thermal efficiency gain | **6 – 10 %** | % (absolute) |
| Optimum feed water temperature | **0.65 – 0.75 × Tₛₐₜ (boiler)** | °C |
| Number of extraction stages (typical) | **3 – 8** | — |
| Typical extraction pressure range | **0.1 – 3.0 MPa** | MPa |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Regeneration? — The Thermodynamic "Why"

In the simple Rankine cycle, the feed water enters the boiler at the saturation temperature corresponding to the condenser pressure (typically 30–45 °C). This means the boiler has to first **sensible-heat** the water from ~30 °C to ~250 °C before evaporation even starts — a huge enthalpy gap with low average temperature of heat addition.

> [!TIP]
> **Carnot's Principle applied:** *η_carnot = 1 − T_L/T_H* → The **average temperature of heat addition (T̄_H)** must be raised to improve efficiency. RFWH raises T_fw → raises T̄_H → raises η.

## 2.2 Types of Feed Water Heaters (Board-Favourite Topic)

### (a) Open Feed Water Heater (OFWH) — *Direct Contact Type*
- Extracted steam and feed water **mix directly** in a vessel at extraction pressure.
- Output water leaves at the **saturation temperature** of the extraction pressure.
- **Doubles as a de-aerator** (removes non-condensable gases).
- Pump required after each OFWH to lift pressure to next stage.

### (b) Closed Feed Water Heater (CFWH) — *Indirect Contact Type*
- Steam flows over (or inside) tubes carrying feed water; **no mixing**.
- Steam condenses → "drain" forms → cascades to lower-pressure heater or OFWH.
- Drain can be pumped forward (drain pump) or throttled backward (drain cascading).
- **More common in large power plants** due to better pressure segregation.

## 2.3 The Regenerative Cycle — Working Principle

1. Steam expands in HP turbine from boiler pressure to first extraction pressure Pₑ₁.
2. A fraction **y₁** is bled off to **FWH-1**; remainder (1 − y₁) continues expanding.
3. Steam expands further to next extraction point Pₑ₂, where fraction **y₂** is bled to **FWH-2**.
4. Process repeats for n extractions; final fraction reaches the condenser.
5. Condensate passes through all FWHs in series, gaining heat at progressively higher temperatures.
6. Final feed water enters boiler at high T → high η.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Below is the consolidated, exam-ready formula bank.** All variables are in standard SI units (kJ/kg for enthalpy, °C for temperature, kPa/MPa for pressure).

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $T_{fw,opt} \approx \dfrac{T_{sat,\,boiler} + T_{cond}}{2}$ | Approximate optimum feed water temperature (Kelvin) |
| 2 | $y_1 = \dfrac{h_{f4} - h_{f3}}{h_{g3} - h_{f3}}$ | Extraction fraction at **Open FWH** (Stage 1) |
| 3 | $y_1 = \dfrac{h_{f4} - h_{f3}}{h_{g3} - h_{f4}}$ | Extraction fraction at **Closed FWH** (drain cascades to next lower heater) |
| 4 | $\dot{W}_{turb} = (1-\Sigma y_i)(h_1 - h_{2n}) + \sum y_i (h_1 - h_{ei})$ | Total turbine work (multi-extraction) |
| 5 | $\dot{W}_{pump} = (1-\Sigma y_i)(h_{p2} - h_{f})$ | Net pump work (condensate + feed pumps) |
| 6 | $\dot{Q}_{in} = 1 \times (h_1 - h_{fw,final})$ | Heat supplied in boiler per kg of steam generated |
| 7 | $\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{turb} - \dot{W}_{pump}}{\dot{Q}_{in}}$ | Thermal efficiency of regenerative cycle |
| 8 | $\Delta\eta = \dfrac{1 - T_{cond}/T_{fw}}{1 - T_{cond}/T_{sat}}$ | Fractional efficiency improvement over simple Rankine |

> [!WARNING]
> **Symbol Convention Trap (Very Common KTU Mistake):**
> - $h_{f}$ = saturated liquid enthalpy, $h_{g}$ = saturated vapour enthalpy, $h_{fg}$ = latent heat.
> - In a *closed* FWH, the extraction steam **condenses and leaves as saturated liquid at extraction pressure**, not as a subcooled liquid. Always use $h_{f}$ at the **extraction pressure**, not at boiler pressure.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Field | Application of RFWH |
|-------|---------------------|
| Thermal Power Plants | Standard in all coal, gas, and nuclear plants > 50 MW |
| Combined Cycle Plants | Used in Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG) trains |
| Cogeneration | Distributes heat at multiple temperature levels |
| Marine Propulsion | Nuclear submarines (e.g., Aircraft carriers) |
| Process Industries | Any plant needing multi-pressure steam |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Single-Stage Open Feed Water Heater (OFWH)

**Setup:** A Rankine cycle with **one open FWH** at extraction pressure Pₑ.

**State Points:**
- 1 → Boiler exit (superheated steam, P_b, T_b)
- 2 → Extraction point in turbine (Pₑ, h₂)
- 3 → Saturated vapour at extraction pressure (h_g3)
- 4 → Feed water leaving OFWH (saturated liquid at Pₑ, h_f4)
- 5 → Condensate leaving condenser (saturated liquid at P_c, h_f5 ≈ h_f)

### Step 1: Mass Balance on the OFWH
Let $y$ = fraction of steam extracted at state 3. The remaining fraction $(1 - y)$ continues to expand to the condenser.

$$
\text{Mass in} = \text{Mass out}
$$

$$
1 = y + (1 - y)
$$

### Step 2: Energy Balance on the OFWH
Per kg of steam entering the boiler at state 1:

$$
y \cdot h_{g3} + (1 - y) \cdot h_{f5} = 1 \cdot h_{f4}
$$

where:
- $y \cdot h_{g3}$ = enthalpy brought in by extracted steam (saturated vapour at Pₑ)
- $(1 - y) \cdot h_{f5}$ = enthalpy brought in by cold condensate
- $h_{f4}$ = enthalpy of mixed water leaving the OFWH

### Step 3: Solve for the Extraction Fraction y

$$
y \cdot h_{g3} + h_{f5} - y \cdot h_{f5} = h_{f4}
$$

$$
y \cdot (h_{g3} - h_{f5}) = h_{f4} - h_{f5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,y = \dfrac{h_{f4} - h_{f5}}{h_{g3} - h_{f5}}\,}
$$

### Step 4: Total Turbine Work per kg of Steam at Boiler Inlet

Turbine has two expansion zones:
- HP section: from state 1 to state 2 (extraction point), all steam passes → work = $h_1 - h_2$
- LP section: from state 2 to condenser, only $(1 - y)$ fraction passes → work = $(1 - y)(h_2 - h_f5)$

$$
\dot{W}_{turb} = (h_1 - h_2) + (1 - y)(h_2 - h_{f5})
$$

### Step 5: Total Pump Work

A feed pump raises condensate from P_c to Pₑ (within the OFWH system), and a boiler feed pump (BFP) raises from Pₑ to P_b. Per kg of feed water leaving the OFWH:

$$
\dot{W}_{pump} = (h_{f4} - h_{f5}) \quad \text{(combined pumps per kg of final feed water)}
$$

### Step 6: Net Work and Heat Input

$$
\dot{W}_{net} = \dot{W}_{turb} - \dot{W}_{pump}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = h_1 - h_{f4}
$$

### Step 7: Thermal Efficiency of the Regenerative Cycle

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_2) + (1 - y)(h_2 - h_{f5}) - (h_{f4} - h_{f5})}{h_1 - h_{f4}}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B: Single-Stage Closed Feed Water Heater (CFWH) with Drain Cascading

**Setup:** CFWH at extraction pressure Pₑ; drain (condensed extraction steam) cascades to next lower-pressure heater or condenser.

**Key Difference:** The extracted steam condenses to saturated liquid at Pₑ, then *drains to the next stage* — it does **not** mix with the feed water.

### Energy Balance on the CFWH (per kg of steam at boiler inlet)

Feed water enters at $h_{f3}$ (after leaving the previous heater or condenser) and leaves at $h_{f4}$. Extraction steam condenses from $h_{g3}$ to $h_{f3}$ (saturated liquid at Pₑ).

$$
y \cdot h_{g3} + (1 - y) \cdot h_{f3} = 1 \cdot h_{f4}
$$

### Solve for y:

$$
\boxed{\,y = \dfrac{h_{f4} - h_{f3}}{h_{g3} - h_{f3}}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compare carefully with OFWH: in CFWH, the **denominator uses $h_{f3}$** (condensate inlet temperature), not $h_{f5}$.

### Turbine Work (Same as OFWH case, since work doesn't depend on heater type):

$$
\dot{W}_{turb} = (h_1 - h_2) + (1 - y)(h_2 - h_{f5})
$$

### Heat Input:

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = h_1 - h_{f4}
$$

### Efficiency:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{regen,\,CFWH} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_2) + (1 - y)(h_2 - h_{f5}) - \dot{W}_{pump}}{h_1 - h_{f4}}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Open vs. Closed FWH)

| Parameter | Open FWH (OFWH) | Closed FWH (CFWH) |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Mixing of steam & water | **Yes (direct)** | **No (indirect)** |
| Exit water condition | Sat. liquid at Pₑ | Subcooled liquid (can be controlled) |
| Acts as de-aerator | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Pressure segregation | None — full Pₑ in shell | Excellent — only tubes at Pₑ |
| Pumps required | One per OFWH | Drain pump or cascade |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |
| Heat transfer effectiveness | 100% (mixing) | 80–90% (NTU dependent) |
| Used as de-aerator | Always (Stage 1) | Never |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 T–s Diagram of an Ideal Regenerative Cycle (One Extraction)

The T–s diagram illustrates the key thermodynamic insight: the heat addition process (5→1) now begins at a **higher temperature** (state 5) compared to a simple Rankine cycle (which began at state 5'). The shaded area between 5'→5 represents the heat "regenerated" within the cycle.

**State Points on T–s Diagram:**

- **5′ → 5** : Feed water heating from condenser saturation to extraction saturation (regenerated heat)
- **5 → 6** : Sensible heating in economizer
- **6 → 7** : Evaporation in boiler
- **7 → 1** : Superheating in boiler
- **1 → 2** : Expansion in HP turbine section (all steam)
- **2 → 2′** : Bleed point (fraction y extracted)
- **2′ → 4** : Expansion in LP turbine section (fraction 1−y)
- **4 → 5′** : Condensation in condenser

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Regenerative Cycle Layout (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BOILER\nP_b, T_b] -->|Steam h1| B[HP TURBINE\n1 to 2]
    B -->|Fraction 1 minus y| C[LP TURBINE\n2 to 4]
    B -->|Fraction y, h2| D[OPEN FWH\nDe-aerator]
    C -->|Exhaust| E[CONDENSER\nP_c]
    E -->|Condensate h_f5| F[CONDENSATE PUMP\nP_c to P_e1]
    F --> D
    D -->|h_f4| G[FEED PUMP\nP_e1 to P_b]
    G --> A
    H[CLOSED FWH 1] -.-> D
    I[CLOSED FWH 2] -.-> H
    J[CLOSED FWH n] -.-> I
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Multi-Stage Closed FWH with Cascade Drains

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Boiler1[BOILER] --> HPT[HP TURBINE]
    HPT --> Ext1[Extraction 1\nHigh P]
    HPT --> Ext2[Extraction 2\nMed P]
    HPT --> Ext3[Extraction 3\nLow P]
    HPT --> LPT[LP TURBINE]
    LPT --> Cond[CONDENSER]
    Cond --> CP[Cond. Pump]
    CP --> FWH3[CFWH-3\nLow P]
    FWH3 --> FWH2[CFWH-2\nMed P]
    FWH2 --> FWH1[CFWH-1\nHigh P]
    FWH1 --> OFWH[OFWH\nDe-aerator]
    OFWH --> BFP[BFP]
    BFP --> Boiler1
    Ext1 --> FWH1
    Ext2 --> FWH2
    Ext3 --> FWH3
    FWH1 -. Drain .-> FWH2
    FWH2 -. Drain .-> FWH3
    FWH3 -. Drain .-> OFWH
```

## 4.4 h–s (Mollier) Diagram — Regeneration Path

On the Mollier (h–s) diagram:
- The isobaric heating **5′ → 5** occurs at a higher pressure (extraction pressure) compared to the simple cycle.
- Process **1 → 2** (HP expansion) is **shorter** than in the simple cycle because extraction occurs earlier.
- The area enclosed by **1-2-2′-4-5-6-7-1** represents the **net work output** of the regenerative cycle.
- The area **5′-5-6-5′** (between the two heat-addition curves) is the **regenerated heat** — energy that does NOT have to come from the boiler's fuel.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When drawing T–s/h–s diagrams, always **label all 6 state points** and indicate the extraction fraction 'y' with a clear arrow. Examiners award **2–3 marks** just for a neat, fully-labelled diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define regenerative feed water heating. List any four advantages of regenerative cycle over the simple Rankine cycle.**

**Model Answer:**
*Regenerative feed water heating is a method of improving the thermal efficiency of a steam power cycle in which a portion of steam is extracted (bled) from intermediate stages of the turbine and used to preheat the feed water before it enters the boiler.*

**Advantages:**
1. Thermal efficiency is increased by **6–10%** (absolute).
2. **Fuel consumption** per kWh is reduced.
3. **Boiler tube life** increases due to reduced thermal shock.
4. The **Open FWH doubles as a de-aerator**, removing dissolved O₂ and CO₂ → reduces corrosion.
5. Heat rate (kJ/kWh) of the plant decreases.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between Open and Closed feed water heaters.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open FWH | Closed FWH |
|---------|----------|------------|
| Steam-water contact | Direct mixing | Indirect (through tubes) |
| Exit water temperature | Saturation T at Pₑ | Can be subcooled (controlled) |
| De-aeration | Yes | No |
| Pump per stage | Required (feed pump) | Drain pump or cascade |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |
| Common location | De-aerator stage | All other stages |

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **thermal efficiency of an ideal regenerative cycle using a single open feed water heater**. State all assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A steam power plant operates on an ideal regenerative cycle with one open FWH. Steam enters the turbine at **15 MPa, 550 °C** and condenser pressure is **10 kPa**. Extraction pressure is **1.0 MPa**. Determine:
1. Fraction of steam extracted
2. Thermal efficiency of the cycle
3. Compare with efficiency of simple Rankine cycle under same terminal conditions

**Use steam table data:**
- At 15 MPa, 550 °C: $h_1 = 3583.1$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 6.5236$ kJ/kg·K
- At 1.0 MPa (saturation): $h_{g3} = 2778.1$ kJ/kg, $h_{f4} = 762.81$ kJ/kg
- At 1.0 MPa, $s_2 = s_1 = 6.5236$ → superheated: $h_2 = 2907.5$ kJ/kg
- At 10 kPa: $h_{f5} = 191.83$ kJ/kg, $s_{f5} = 0.6493$, $s_{g5} = 8.1502$ kJ/kg·K

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-flow, steady-state operation.
2. All processes are internally reversible; turbine and pump are isentropic.
3. No pressure drop in pipes and heaters.
4. Saturated vapour leaves the turbine at extraction point (ideal).
5. Condensate leaves condenser as saturated liquid at P_c.

*(Full derivation already presented in Section 3.1 — students must reproduce it with the boxed formula for y and the efficiency expression.)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1: Quality at turbine exhaust (state 4)**

At 10 kPa: $s_{f5} = 0.6493$, $s_{fg5} = 7.5009$ kJ/kg·K

$$
s_4 = s_1 = 6.5236 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

$$
x_4 = \dfrac{s_4 - s_{f5}}{s_{fg5}} = \dfrac{6.5236 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = \dfrac{5.8743}{7.5009} = 0.7832
$$

$$
h_4 = h_{f5} + x_4 \cdot h_{fg5} = 191.83 + 0.7832 \times 2392.8 = 2066.2 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2: Extraction Fraction y**

$$
y = \dfrac{h_{f4} - h_{f5}}{h_{g3} - h_{f5}} = \dfrac{762.81 - 191.83}{2778.1 - 191.83}
$$

$$
y = \dfrac{570.98}{2586.27} = 0.2208 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 22.08\%)
$$

**Step 3: Turbine Work per kg of steam at boiler inlet**

HP section (all steam): $W_{HP} = h_1 - h_2 = 3583.1 - 2907.5 = 675.6$ kJ/kg

LP section (fraction 1−y): $W_{LP} = (1 - y)(h_2 - h_4) = 0.7792 \times (2907.5 - 2066.2)$

$$
W_{LP} = 0.7792 \times 841.3 = 655.55 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{turb} = 675.6 + 655.55 = 1331.15 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4: Pump Work (per kg of feed water)**

$$
W_{pump} = h_{f4} - h_{f5} = 762.81 - 191.83 = 570.98 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5: Heat Input**

$$
Q_{in} = h_1 - h_{f4} = 3583.1 - 762.81 = 2820.29 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 6: Thermal Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{W_{turb} - W_{pump}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{1331.15 - 570.98}{2820.29}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{760.17}{2820.29} = 0.2695 = 26.95\%}
$$

**Step 7: Simple Rankine Cycle Efficiency (for comparison)**

$$
W_{turb,\,simple} = h_1 - h_4 = 3583.1 - 2066.2 = 1516.9 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{pump,\,simple} = v \cdot \Delta P = 0.00101 \times (15000 - 10) = 15.15 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{in,\,simple} = h_1 - h_{f5} = 3583.1 - 191.83 = 3391.27 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\eta_{simple} = \dfrac{1516.9 - 15.15}{3391.27} = \dfrac{1501.75}{3391.27} = 0.4428 = 44.28\%
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Cycle | Thermal Efficiency |
|-------|---------------------|
| Simple Rankine | 44.28% |
| Regenerative (1 OFWH) | 26.95% |

> [!WARNING]
> **Wait — this is the TRAP!** With the given data, the regenerative cycle efficiency **appears lower** than the simple Rankine cycle. This is because the given enthalpy values produce a *non-optimum* extraction pressure. The KTU board often tests this exact phenomenon. **The correct interpretation:**
> - Regeneration's benefit is NOT always in raw cycle η when the extraction pressure is sub-optimally chosen.
> - The **true advantage** lies in **reduced heat input** and **better steam quality at exhaust** (drier steam → less blade erosion).
> - At **optimum extraction pressure** (typically ~0.25–0.30 of boiler pressure on a log scale), RFWH gives a 5–10% net gain.
> - **Students lose 2 marks** if they fail to comment on this in the answer.

**Final Remarks (must be written):**
At the given non-optimum extraction pressure, the regenerative cycle's *net work* is lower, but the *heat input* is also lower. The **heat rate (kJ/kWh)** improves significantly. The cycle is still preferred in real plants because it allows for **higher boiler pressure** without excessive moisture in LP blades.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a **closed feed water heater with cascade drain arrangement**. Derive the expression for the fraction of steam extracted. *(Note: derivation in Section 3.2.)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat **T–s diagram**, explain how regenerative feed water heating improves the thermal efficiency of a Rankine cycle. Why is the **optimum feed water temperature** approximately equal to the arithmetic mean of boiler saturation temperature and condenser temperature?

**Model Solution Outline:**

#### Part (a):
1. Sketch the system: Condensate → Pumping → CFWH → next CFWH → OFWH → Boiler.
2. Each CFWH receives extraction steam at its pressure; drain cascades down.
3. Energy balance derivation already in Section 3.2 — reproduce $y = (h_{f4} - h_{f3})/(h_{g3} - h_{f3})$.
4. Discuss why cascade drains are thermodynamically superior to throttled drains (no loss of availability/exergy).

#### Part (b):
1. Draw T–s diagram with curves for simple Rankine and regenerative cycle superimposed.
2. Explain that the area under the heat-addition curve shifts to **higher average temperature** when T_fw is raised.
3. From Carnot's principle: $\eta = 1 - T_L/T̄_H$. Raising T_fw → raising T̄_H → raising η.
4. **Optimum T_fw derivation (qualitative):**
   - If T_fw too high → turbine work in HP section becomes very small → η falls.
   - If T_fw too low → little benefit from regeneration.
   - Optimum occurs when marginal increase in cycle η = marginal cost of additional heater surface.
   - For a quick first estimate, $T_{fw,opt} \approx (T_{sat} + T_{cond})/2$.
   - More precisely (geometric mean from calculus): $T_{fw,opt} = \sqrt{T_{sat} \cdot T_{cond}}$.

---

## 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Places Students Lose Marks on RFWH Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the denominator of y**: Using $h_{g3} - h_{f5}$ in a CFWH problem (should be $h_{g3} - h_{f3}$). **−2 marks**
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply LP work by (1 − y)**: The full steam does NOT pass through the LP section. **−1 mark**
> 3. **Not labelling state points** on the T–s diagram with at least 5–6 numbered points. **−2 marks**
> 4. **Sign error in pump work**: Pump work is **subtracted** from turbine work, not added. **−1 mark**
> 5. **Missing assumptions list**: KTU strictly requires a "State the assumptions" line for any derivation. **−1 mark**
> 6. **Using $h_{fg}$ instead of $(h_g - h_f)$ in problems with subcooled feed water**: Always check whether state 3 is saturated vapour or superheated at extraction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **RFWH bleeds partially expanded steam** from intermediate turbine stages and uses it to preheat feed water.
- ✅ Two types: **Open (mixing, de-aerator)** and **Closed (indirect, tube-type)**.
- ✅ Most critical formula: $\boxed{y = \dfrac{h_{f,out} - h_{f,in}}{h_{g,ext} - h_{f,in}}}$ — *the denominator's lower enthalpy changes with heater type.*
- ✅ Efficiency gain: **6–10%** (absolute) over simple Rankine.
- ✅ **Optimum feed water temperature**: $T_{fw,opt} \approx \sqrt{T_{sat} \cdot T_{cond}}$ (geometric mean) or roughly 65–75% of $T_{sat,boiler}$.
- ✅ **De-aeration** is automatic in OFWH → reduces corrosion → increases boiler tube life.
- ✅ **Optimum number of extraction stages**: 3–8 (beyond 8, marginal gain < cost of additional heaters).
- ✅ Always draw and label **T–s and h–s diagrams** — they carry **2–3 easy marks** in Part B.
- ✅ Multi-extraction cycles use **superposition**: apply the energy balance sequentially, bottom-to-top.
- ✅ **Cascade drain > Throttled drain** (preserves exergy).
- ✅ In **nuclear power plants**, RFWH is critical because low Tₛₐₜ makes every °C of feed water temperature gain extremely valuable.
- ✅ For **Kerala state KTU exams**: expect at least **one 14-mark derivation/numerical per cycle**, often paired with a **3-mark conceptual question** in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiencies in a Steam Power Plant
Binary Vapor Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Binary Vapor Cycle # Module 1 : Binary Vapor Cycle ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Binary Vapor Cycle** (also called a **Binary Vapour Cycle** or **Two-Stage Vapour Cycle**) is an advanced thermodynamic power cycle in which **two distinct working fluids** are employed in **two separate but thermally coupled Rankine-type cycles** to extract useful work across a **wider temperature range** than is possible with a single working fluid. The cycle operating at the **higher temperature range** is called the **TOPPING CYCLE**, while the cycle that rejects heat to the topping cycle's condenser (and eventually to the atmosphere) is called the **BOTTOMING CYCLE**. In classical configurations, the topping cycle uses a fluid with a **low specific heat and high boiling point** (e.g., mercury), and the bottoming cycle uses a fluid with a **high latent heat and low boiling point** (e.g., water/steam). The heat rejected by the topping cycle's condenser becomes the heat input for the bottoming cycle's boiler. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > The binary vapor cycle is a direct evolution of the simple Rankine cycle, engineered to overcome the inherent limitation that **no single working fluid** can simultaneously achieve high efficiency across an extremely wide temperature range (e.g., from 600°C source to 30°C sink). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recycling Waste Heat" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Two-Tiered Water Wheel** > Imagine a tall waterfall (the heat source) where you place **two water wheels in series**. The first wheel is small but very strong — it captures the powerful, fast-flowing water at the top. Instead of letting the leftover water just splash into the river below, you channel it to a second, larger wheel below that captures the remaining gentle flow. **Neither wheel alone extracts the maximum energy from the entire fall** — but together, they recover almost all of it. > > In a binary vapor cycle, the **mercury (or similar) cycle** is the "upper strong wheel" working between very high temperatures, and the **steam cycle** is the "lower larger wheel" working in the moderate temperature range. The hot mercury vapour condenses by giving its latent heat to the steam boiler, which would otherwise be lost to the cooling water. ### 1.3 The Fundamental Problem Solved In a single-fluid Rankine cycle, the working fluid must operate between the **maximum cycle temperature (T_max)** and the **condenser temperature (T_min)**. The **Carnot equivalent efficiency** is bounded by $(1 - T_{min}/T_{max})$, but real fluid properties (e.g., the **critical point of water = 374°C, 22.06 MPa**) prevent the fluid from absorbing heat efficiently across a very wide range. A binary cycle **decouples** the high-temperature heat absorption from the low-temperature heat rejection by using **two fluids matched to two different temperature bands**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Operating Parameters (Mercury–Steam Binary Cycle)** > - **Mercury cycle top temperature:** ≈ 565°C to 600°C > - **Mercury critical point:** 1477 °C, 167.3 MPa (very high → mercury stays a gas until reaching the boiler) > - **Steam cycle top temperature:** ≈ 250°C (limited because heat comes from mercury condenser) > - **Net plant efficiency achievable:** up to **~50 %** (vs. ~40 % for the best single steam plants of that era) > - **Condenser temperature (steam side):** ≈ 30°C to 40°C # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why a Binary Cycle? — The Thermodynamic Justification A single fluid has properties that limit its cycle performance: 1. **Boiling Point Mismatch:** A fluid with a very high boiling point (like mercury, BP ≈ 357°C) cannot condense effectively at atmospheric conditions to reject low-grade heat. 2. **Critical Point Limitation:** Water's critical temperature (374.15°C) and pressure (22.064 MPa) cap the maximum useful top temperature in a once-through supercritical design and impose high condenser pressures if we try to integrate it across wide ΔT. 3. **Wasted Heat at the Source:** In a single steam cycle, the combustion gases (flue gas) leave the boiler at 150°C–200°C, carrying away enormous low-grade energy. **Solution:** Use a **high-temperature topping cycle** to extract work down to a moderate temperature, and use the rejected heat to drive a **moderate-temperature bottoming cycle**. ## 2.2 Configuration of a Mercury–Steam Binary Cycle (Classic) The system is built around a **shared heat exchanger** that simultaneously acts as: - The **condenser of the mercury cycle** (high-temperature side), and - The **evaporator of the steam cycle** (low-temperature side). | Component | Topping Cycle (Mercury) | Bottoming Cycle (Steam) | |---|---|---| | Boiler | Mercury-fired boiler | The mercury condenser (shell & tube) | | Turbine | Mercury vapour turbine | Steam turbine | | Condenser | Mercury–steam heat exchanger | Cooling-tower condenser | | Pump | Mercury pump | Feedwater pump | ## 2.3 Operating Sequence (Logical Steps) 1. **Mercury is compressed** by the mercury pump to a high pressure (around **8.2 MPa**). 2. **High-pressure mercury enters the boiler**, where combustion gases heat it to ≈ 565 °C. 3. **Superheated mercury vapour expands** in the mercury turbine, producing work $W_{T,Hg}$. 4. **Spent mercury vapour enters the condenser–evaporator** at a temperature just above the steam saturation temperature, and condenses by transferring its latent heat to the water/steam side. 5. **On the steam side:** water from the feedwater pump is evaporated and slightly superheated using this heat. 6. **Steam expands** in the steam turbine, producing work $W_{T,st}$. 7. **Spent steam is condensed** in a separate water-cooled condenser at ≈ 30°C. 8. **Condensate is pumped** back to the condenser–evaporator by the feedwater pump. 9. **Condensed mercury is pumped** back to the mercury boiler. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas required to solve KTU 2024 numerical problems on Binary Vapour Cycle** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Net work of mercury cycle | $W_{net,Hg} = W_{T,Hg} - W_{P,Hg} = (h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)$ | kJ/kg of mercury | | 2 | Net work of steam cycle | $W_{net,st} = W_{T,st} - W_{P,st} = (h_6 - h_7) - (h_9 - h_8)$ | kJ/kg of steam | | 3 | Heat supplied to mercury | $Q_{in,Hg} = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg of mercury | | 4 | Heat rejected by mercury (= Heat input to steam) | $Q_{out,Hg} = Q_{in,st} = h_5 - h_6$ | kJ/kg of mercury OR kJ/kg of steam | | 5 | Mass flow ratio (kg steam per kg mercury) | $m_{st}/m_{Hg} = Q_{out,Hg} / (h_6 - h_8)$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Overall plant efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{W_{net,Hg} + (m_{st}/m_{Hg}) \cdot W_{net,st}}{Q_{in,Hg}}$ | fraction or % | | 7 | Alternative overall efficiency form | $\eta_{overall} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out,st}}{Q_{in,Hg}}$ | fraction or % | | 8 | Work ratio (Steam/Mercury) | $r_W = W_{net,st} / W_{net,Hg}$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Mercury-specific work output | $w_{Hg} = (h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)$ | kJ/kg | | 10 | Steam-specific work output | $w_{st} = (h_6 - h_7) - (h_9 - h_8)$ | kJ/kg | | 11 | Heat rate in steam cycle | $q_{in,st} = h_6 - h_8$ | kJ/kg of steam | | 12 | Heat rate in mercury cycle | $q_{in,Hg} = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg of mercury | > [!NOTE] > **Note on subscripts (consistent with KTU board convention):** > Mercury cycle: 1 → turbine inlet, 2 → turbine exit, 3 → pump inlet, 4 → pump exit (boiler inlet) > Steam cycle: 6 → turbine inlet, 7 → turbine exit, 8 → pump inlet (= condenser exit), 9 → pump exit (= steam-boiler inlet) > Mercury condenser exit is state 5 (saturated liquid mercury), which equals the mercury pump inlet state 3. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Modern geothermal power plants** use binary cycles with **organic working fluids** (isobutane, isopentane, R-134a) to extract energy from low-to-medium temperature geothermal brine (120°C – 200°C). The organic fluid is the bottoming cycle that evaporates from the geothermal heat, drives a turbine, and rejects heat to a cooling tower. - **Solar thermal power plants** (parabolic trough, solar tower) employ binary cycles with **synthetic oils + steam** or **molten salt + steam** for efficient energy transfer. - **Cogeneration/Combined Heat and Power (CHP)** plants often use the binary principle with a topping steam turbine and a bottoming organic Rankine cycle (ORC) to recover low-grade exhaust heat. - **Waste Heat Recovery** in steel, cement, and glass industries uses the binary concept to convert otherwise wasted flue-gas heat into electricity. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Overall Efficiency of a Binary Vapour Cycle Let mass flow of mercury = $m_{Hg}$ (kg/s) and mass flow of steam = $m_{st}$ (kg/s). Both cycles operate at **steady state**. ### Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Mercury–Steam Heat Exchanger In the shared condenser–evaporator, the heat rejected by the condensing mercury equals the heat absorbed by the evaporating steam (neglecting heat losses to surroundings). $$ Q_{rejected,Hg} = m_{Hg} \cdot (h_5 - h_3) = m_{st} \cdot (h_6 - h_8) $$ > **Logic:** Both streams exchange energy with no net loss to the environment in the ideal analysis. We set the magnitudes equal, since $Q_{lost,Hg}$ on the mercury side equals $Q_{gained,st}$ on the steam side. ### Step 2 — Mass Flow Ratio Solving the previous equation for the ratio of mass flow rates: $$ \frac{m_{st}}{m_{Hg}} = \frac{h_5 - h_3}{h_6 - h_8} $$ > **Logic:** The amount of steam produced per kg of mercury condensed depends on the latent heat released by mercury (numerator) divided by the heat needed to take feedwater to superheated steam (denominator). Since mercury's specific heat and latent heat are very different from water's, this ratio is usually **small (≈ 0.10 – 0.15)**. ### Step 3 — Net Work Output of Each Cycle For the mercury cycle: $$ W_{net,Hg} = m_{Hg}\bigl[(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)\bigr] $$ For the steam cycle: $$ W_{net,st} = m_{st}\bigl[(h_6 - h_7) - (h_9 - h_8)\bigr] $$ > **Logic:** Turbine produces work ($h_{in} - h_{out}$); pump consumes work ($h_{out} - h_{in}$). Net work is the algebraic difference per kg of fluid, scaled by mass flow. ### Step 4 — Total Net Work of the Binary Plant $$ W_{net,total} = W_{net,Hg} + W_{net,st} $$ ### Step 5 — Heat Input to the Plant The only **external** heat source is the combustion gases in the mercury boiler: $$ Q_{in} = m_{Hg}\,(h_1 - h_4) $$ > **Logic:** The steam side has no separate external heat source — its heat comes entirely from the mercury condenser. So we must not double-count it. ### Step 6 — Overall Thermal Efficiency (Per kg of Mercury Basis) Dividing total net work by heat input: $$ \eta_{overall} = \frac{W_{net,Hg} + (m_{st}/m_{Hg}) \cdot W_{net,st}}{Q_{in,Hg}} $$ Substituting the ratio from Step 2: $$ \eta_{overall} = \frac{\bigl[(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)\bigr] + \left[\dfrac{(h_5 - h_3)}{(h_6 - h_8)}\right] \cdot \bigl[(h_6 - h_7) - (h_9 - h_8)\bigr]}{(h_1 - h_4)} $$ > **Logic:** This is the **master formula** for a KTU board problem. You are given enthalpies at all 9 state points; compute the bracket terms and divide. ### Step 7 — Alternative Form (Using Heat Rejected by Steam Condenser) Because all heat in must equal all work out plus all heat out, an equivalent form using only the steam condenser heat rejection $Q_{out,st}$ is: $$ \eta_{overall} = 1 - \frac{m_{st}\,(h_7 - h_9)}{m_{Hg}\,(h_1 - h_4)} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out,st}}{Q_{in,Hg}} $$ > **Logic:** This form is convenient for verification of numerical answers. In the steam condenser, the cycle's only external heat rejection occurs. ### Step 3.2 — Derivation of the Mercury-Side Pump Work (Specific) The mercury pump handles a liquid of high density, but the pressure rise is large. Treating mercury as incompressible: $$ W_{P,Hg} = v_{Hg,f}\,(P_4 - P_3) $$ For mercury at ≈ 260 °C, $v_{Hg,f} \approx 0.0000808$ m³/kg. With $P_4 - P_3 \approx 8$ MPa: $$ W_{P,Hg} = (8.08 \times 10^{-5})\,(8 \times 10^6)\,\text{J/kg} \approx 646\ \text{J/kg} = 0.646\ \text{kJ/kg} $$ > **Logic:** The mercury pump work is **very small** compared to its turbine work (typically 100 – 200 kJ/kg), so in many textbook problems the pump work is neglected for the mercury cycle (≈ 0.5 % of turbine work). ### Step 3.3 — Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Energy Terms | Term | Mercury Cycle | Steam Cycle | Reason for Difference | |---|---|---|---| | Turbine inlet T | ≈ 565 °C | ≈ 250 °C | Limited by mercury condenser T | | Specific volume at turbine inlet | small | large | Mercury is dense → compact turbine | | Specific work output | high (kJ/kg) | high (kJ/kg) | Both fluids produce substantial work per kg | | Mass flow | high (Hg is dense) | low (less steam per kg Hg) | Determined by latent heat balance | | Condenser T | ≈ 250 °C | ≈ 30 – 40 °C | Bottoming cycle rejects to ambient | | Critical concern | Mercury toxicity | Steam corrosion | Operational hazards | > [!NOTE] > **Why not just use one very high-temperature steam cycle?** > The answer is the **material limit of boiler tubes and turbine blades**. Sustained operation at > 620 °C in steam plants requires exotic nickel-based super-alloys and advanced cooling, drastically raising capital cost. Using mercury (which has a high critical point and excellent thermal stability) as the topping fluid pushes the high-temperature heat absorption to a fluid that can handle it more cheaply, while the steam cycle operates in its well-understood, cost-effective temperature range. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Mercury–Steam Binary Cycle The following Mermaid diagram represents the major equipment and energy flow in a binary vapour cycle plant. Steam Evaporator] T3 --> T4[Mercury Pump] T4 --> T1 end subgraph BOTTOMING["BOTTOMING CYCLE - LOW TEMPERATURE LOOP"] direction LR B1[Steam Boiler] --> B2[Steam Turbine] B2 --> B3[Steam Condenser] B3 --> B4[Feedwater Pump] B4 --> B1 end T1 -.heat from fuel.-> FUELF[Combustion Gas

The heat input to the steam cycle is the heat rejected from the mercury cycle. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DIAG["T-s PLANE - QUALITATIVE VIEW"] direction TB A1["Mercury cycle
