KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Binary Vapour Cycle. Why is it preferred over a single Rankine cycle? `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Model Answer:** A binary vapour cycle is a power cycle in which **two different working fluids** are used in **two thermally-coupled Rankine-type cycles** — a high-temperature **topping cycle** and a low-temperature **bottoming cycle** — to utilise a wider temperature range of the heat source. It is preferred over a single Rankine cycle because: 1. **No single fluid can operate efficiently across a very wide temperature range** — fluids have inherent limitations on critical temperature, latent heat, and specific heat. 2. **The high-temperature fluid** (e.g., mercury) absorbs heat efficiently at very high temperatures where steam would face critical-point and material limitations. 3. **The low-temperature fluid** (e.g., water/steam) condenses effectively at ambient temperatures, rejecting heat efficiently to the cooling medium. 4. **Overall plant efficiency** is significantly higher (≈ 45–50 %) compared to a single steam Rankine cycle (≈ 35–40 %). 5. **Waste heat from the topping cycle** is recovered by the bottoming cycle, improving fuel economy. > [!NOTE] > **Word count target:** ~120 words. Diagram of cycle adds value. Total marks breakdown: Definition (1) + Reasons (2). --- ### Question 2: List the components of a mercury–steam binary cycle and state the function of the mercury condenser. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Model Answer:** **Components of a mercury–steam binary cycle:** 1. Mercury boiler 2. Mercury turbine 3. Mercury condenser (also acts as steam evaporator) 4. Mercury pump 5. Steam turbine 6. Steam condenser (water-cooled) 7. Feedwater pump 8. Connecting piping and auxiliaries **Function of the mercury condenser:** The mercury condenser serves a **dual role**: - On the **mercury side**, it condenses the spent mercury vapour exiting the mercury turbine, completing the mercury cycle. - On the **steam side**, it transfers the **latent heat released by the condensing mercury** to the feedwater, thereby **evaporating and slightly superheating the steam** that drives the steam turbine. It is therefore a **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** that couples the two cycles thermally and acts as the **boiler for the steam cycle**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Each 14-mark question must be answered with **sub-parts (a) and (b)** worth **7 marks each**, per KTU 2024 ESE pattern. Choose **either** Question A or Question B in the examination. --- ### Question A (14 Marks): Mercury–Steam Binary Cycle Efficiency Calculation `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 1, Q. pattern adapted]` **Question:** **(a)** Explain with a neat schematic the working of a mercury–steam binary vapour cycle. List the state points and write the steady-state energy balance for the mercury condenser–steam evaporator. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a mercury–steam binary cycle, the enthalpies (kJ/kg) at various state points are: - Mercury: $h_1 = 358$, $h_2 = 250$, $h_3 = 35$, $h_4 = 36$, $h_5 = 36$ (saturated liquid Hg) - Steam: $h_6 = 2950$, $h_7 = 2200$, $h_8 = 135$, $h_9 = 140$ Compute: - (i) Mass of steam generated per kg of mercury condensed. **(2 marks)** - (ii) Net work output per kg of mercury. **(2 marks)** - (iii) Overall thermal efficiency of the binary plant. **(3 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): **Working Principle:** In a mercury–steam binary cycle, mercury is the **topping fluid** and steam is the **bottoming fluid**. 1. **Mercury cycle:** Liquid mercury is pressurised by the mercury pump (state 4) and sent to the mercury boiler, where it is vaporised and superheated by hot combustion gases (state 1). The superheated mercury vapour expands in the mercury turbine, producing shaft work $W_{T,Hg}$ (state 2). The exhaust mercury enters the **mercury condenser**, where it condenses by giving up its latent heat (state 3 = 5, saturated liquid mercury). The liquid mercury is pumped back to the boiler. 2. **Steam cycle:** The latent heat released by the condensing mercury is absorbed by the **feedwater** (state 9) in the same heat exchanger (mercury condenser = steam boiler). The water is converted to slightly superheated steam (state 6). This steam expands in the **steam turbine**, producing work $W_{T,st}$ (state 7). The exhaust steam is condensed in a water-cooled condenser (state 8) and pumped back (state 9) to the steam side of the heat exchanger. **State Points:** | State | Description | Fluid | |---|---|---| | 1 | Mercury turbine inlet (superheated vapour) | Mercury | | 2 | Mercury turbine exit (wet vapour) | Mercury | | 3 | Mercury condenser exit / pump inlet (sat. liquid) | Mercury | | 4 | Mercury pump exit / boiler inlet (compressed liquid) | Mercury | | 5 | Mercury condenser exit (sat. liquid, = state 3) | Mercury | | 6 | Steam turbine inlet (superheated steam) | Steam | | 7 | Steam turbine exit (wet steam) | Steam | | 8 | Steam condenser exit / pump inlet (sat. liquid) | Steam | | 9 | Feedwater pump exit / steam-boiler inlet | Steam | **Energy Balance on the Mercury Condenser–Steam Evaporator:** $$ m_{Hg}\,(h_2 - h_3) = m_{st}\,(h_6 - h_9) $$ where $h_2 - h_3$ is the **specific heat rejected** by 1 kg of mercury during condensation, and $h_6 - h_9$ is the **specific heat absorbed** by 1 kg of steam during pre-heating, evaporation, and superheating. > **Logic for part (a):** This is the conceptual question — marks are awarded for the schematic (3 marks), the labelled state points (2 marks), and the energy balance (2 marks). #### Model Solution — Part (b): **(i) Mass of steam per kg of mercury** $$ \frac{m_{st}}{m_{Hg}} = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_6 - h_9} = \frac{250 - 35}{2950 - 140} = \frac{215}{2810} = 0.0765 $$ > **Per kg of mercury, ≈ 0.0765 kg of steam is generated.** (2 marks) **(ii) Net work per kg of mercury** Mercury cycle net work: $$ W_{net,Hg} = (h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3) = (358 - 250) - (36 - 35) = 108 - 1 = 107\ \text{kJ/kg of Hg} $$ Steam cycle net work: $$ W_{net,st} = (h_6 - h_7) - (h_9 - h_8) = (2950 - 2200) - (140 - 135) = 750 - 5 = 745\ \text{kJ/kg of steam} $$ Total net work per kg of mercury: $$ W_{net,total} = W_{net,Hg} + (m_{st}/m_{Hg}) \cdot W_{net,st} $$ $$ W_{net,total} = 107 + (0.0765)(745) = 107 + 57.0 = 164.0\ \text{kJ/kg of Hg} $$ > **Total net work output ≈ 164 kJ per kg of mercury circulated.** (2 marks) **(iii) Overall thermal efficiency** $$ \eta_{overall} = \frac{W_{net,total}}{Q_{in,Hg}} = \frac{164.0}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{164.0}{358 - 36} = \frac{164.0}{322} $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{overall} = 0.509 = 50.9\%} $$ > **The overall efficiency of ≈ 50.9 % is consistent with the historical performance of mercury–steam binary plants.** (3 marks) --- ### Question B (14 Marks): Organic Binary Cycle / Geothermal Application `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1, Q. pattern adapted]` **Question:** **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the working of a binary vapour cycle using an organic working fluid (e.g., isobutane) and steam. Why are organic fluids suitable as the working fluid in geothermal binary plants? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A binary cycle uses an organic fluid (R-245fa) as the topping fluid. The enthalpies (kJ/kg) are: - Organic fluid: $h_1 = 270$ (turbine inlet), $h_2 = 220$ (turbine exit), $h_3 = 80$ (pump inlet), $h_4 = 82$ (pump exit) - Steam: $h_6 = 2700$ (turbine inlet), $h_7 = 2100$ (turbine exit), $h_8 = 200$ (pump inlet), $h_9 = 210$ (pump exit) Compute: - (i) Steam mass flow per kg of organic fluid. **(2 marks)** - (ii) Heat input in the organic-fluid boiler per kg of fluid. **(1 mark)** - (iii) Overall plant efficiency. **(4 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a): **Block Diagram of Organic–Steam Binary Cycle:** 1. **Organic cycle (topping):** Geothermal brine (or low-grade heat source at 150°C – 200°C) heats the organic fluid in a preheater-evaporator. The vaporised organic fluid drives an **ORC (Organic Rankine Cycle) turbine**, then condenses in a regenerator/condenser. A pump sends the liquid back to the heat exchanger. 2. **Steam cycle (bottoming):** Heat rejected by the organic cycle's condenser is used to generate steam at moderate conditions. The steam drives a steam turbine, then is condensed in a cooling tower, and the condensate is pumped back. ``` Heat Source → [Organic Boiler] → [Organic Turbine] → [Organic Condenser / Steam Boiler] → [Organic Pump] → (back to Organic Boiler) ↓ [Steam Turbine] → [Steam Condenser] → [Feedwater Pump] → (back to Organic Condenser) ``` **Why organic fluids are suitable:** - **Low boiling point** (e.g., isobutane boils at −11.7 °C; R-245fa at 15.3 °C; isopentane at 27.8 °C) — allows evaporation from low-temperature geothermal heat sources (120 – 200 °C). - **High molecular weight and low specific volume** → compact turbines. - **Dry or isentropic expansion** — most organic fluids have a positive slope of the saturated vapour line on the T–s diagram, eliminating superheating requirement and moisture damage to turbine blades. - **High latent heat per unit mass** — small mass flow, compact heat exchangers. - **Non-corrosive, non-toxic options** available (e.g., isobutane, isopentane). #### Model Solution — Part (b): **(i) Steam mass flow per kg of organic fluid** $$ \frac{m_{st}}{m_{org}} = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_6 - h_9} = \frac{220 - 80}{2700 - 210} = \frac{140}{2490} = 0.0562 $$ > **≈ 0.0562 kg of steam is generated per kg of organic fluid.** (2 marks) **(ii) Heat input per kg of organic fluid in the organic-fluid boiler** $$ q_{in,org} = h_1 - h_4 = 270 - 82 = 188\ \text{kJ/kg of organic fluid} $$ > **$q_{in} = 188$ kJ/kg.** (1 mark) **(iii) Overall plant efficiency** Net work of organic cycle per kg of organic fluid: $$ w_{net,org} = (h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3) = (270 - 220) - (82 - 80) = 50 - 2 = 48\ \text{kJ/kg} $$ Net work of steam cycle per kg of steam: $$ w_{net,st} = (h_6 - h_7) - (h_9 - h_8) = (2700 - 2100) - (210 - 200) = 600 - 10 = 590\ \text{kJ/kg} $$ Total net work per kg of organic fluid: $$ w_{net,total} = 48 + (0.0562)(590) = 48 + 33.16 = 81.16\ \text{kJ/kg of organic fluid} $$ Overall efficiency: $$ \eta_{overall} = \frac{w_{net,total}}{q_{in,org}} = \frac{81.16}{188} = 0.4317 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{overall} \approx 43.2\%} $$ > **The binary cycle with an organic topping fluid achieves ≈ 43 % overall efficiency from a low-grade heat source — a remarkable improvement over a single ORC, which would yield ≈ 25 % from the same source.** (4 marks) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students commonly lose marks in Binary Vapour Cycle problems** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert mass flows** — Both cycles carry different fluids, so you **must** compute the mass flow ratio and use it consistently when adding work outputs. A frequent error is adding per-kg works directly, giving a wildly incorrect efficiency. > 2. **Double-counting the heat input** — Many students add the heat supplied to the mercury boiler AND the heat supplied to the steam boiler. **This is wrong.** The steam cycle's heat comes from the mercury condenser. The plant has only **one** external heat source (the fuel). > 3. **Sign errors on pump work** — Pump work is **consumed** (subtract), turbine work is **produced** (add). Reversing these signs is the most common arithmetic mistake. > 4. **Neglecting steam pump work on small problems** — In mercury cycles the pump work is negligible, but in organic/steam cycles it is small but **not zero**; include it unless the problem explicitly states "neglect pump work." > 5. **State-point labelling mismatch** — Use a **consistent** numbering scheme (Hg: 1–2–3–4, steam: 6–7–8–9) and stick to it. Examiners deduct marks if your final equation references a state that is undefined in your schematic. > 6. **Confusing topping vs. bottoming** — In a mercury–steam cycle, **mercury is topping** (high T) and **steam is bottoming** (low T). In geothermal organic binary plants, **organic is topping** (boils first) and **steam may not even be present** in some designs. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Notes — Binary Vapour Cycle** - **Definition:** A power cycle using **two different working fluids** in two thermally-coupled Rankine-type loops to utilise a wider temperature range. - **Two loops:** **Topping cycle** (high T, absorbs primary heat) and **Bottoming cycle** (low T, rejects to ambient). - **Classic example:** **Mercury (topping) + Steam (bottoming)**. Reaches ≈ 50 % net efficiency. - **Modern example:** **Organic fluid (R-245fa, isobutane, isopentane) + Steam** in geothermal and solar-thermal plants. - **Single shared component:** The **mercury condenser = steam boiler** (a shell-and-tube heat exchanger) is the heart of the system. - **Master efficiency formula:** $$\eta_{overall} = \frac{W_{net,Hg} + (m_{st}/m_{Hg}) \cdot W_{net,st}}{Q_{in,Hg}}$$ - **Mass flow ratio equation (always required):** $$\frac{m_{st}}{m_{Hg}} = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_6 - h_9}$$ - **Single external heat source:** Fuel enters the mercury (or organic) boiler only. Steam cycle's heat is **internal** recovery. - **Pump work for mercury is negligible** ($< 1$ kJ/kg) due to mercury's very low specific volume — but **state this assumption** if you omit it. - **Mercury critical point (1477 °C, 167.3 MPa)** is far above the operating range → no risk of approaching criticality. - **Steam cycle in a binary plant operates at moderate T** (typically 200 °C – 250 °C) because it is bounded by the mercury condenser temperature. - **Why binary?** No single working fluid can simultaneously (a) absorb heat at very high T, (b) reject heat at ambient T, and (c) maintain favourable saturation properties throughout. - **Real-world applications:** Geothermal (binary plants in Iceland, USA, Philippines), solar thermal, waste-heat recovery in steel/cement/glass industries, combined heat and power (CHP), Kalina cycle (ammonia-water mixture) variants. - **Common KTU mistakes to avoid:** Double-counting heat input, sign errors on pump work, inconsistent state-point numbering, forgetting the mass-flow ratio when summing work outputs.

topics.title: Lancashire Boiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lancashire Boiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lancashire Boiler (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A Lancashire boiler is a **horizontal, stationary, fire-tube, internally fired, natural circulation boiler** having **two large internal fire tubes (flues)** running longitudinally through a cylindrical shell. It was developed as a modification of the Cornish boiler by **Sir William Fairbairn** in 1844. Combustion of coal takes place on grates placed inside the two flues, and the hot products of combustion pass through these flues, transfer heat to the surrounding water, and finally escape to the atmosphere via the chimney.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Snapshot (Syllabus Mapping):**
> - **Type:** Fire-tube, internally fired
> - **Axis:** Horizontal
> - **Installation:** Stationary
> - **Circulation:** Natural
> - **Pressure range:** 7 – 10 bar (low pressure)
> - **Capacity:** 8 000 – 12 000 kg of steam per hour
> - **Efficiency:** 50 – 70 %
> - **Fuel:** Coal (solid fuel, lump/grate firing)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a **big steel drum lying on its side**, full of water. Now drill **two giant cylindrical tunnels** right through it from end to end. Inside each tunnel, you light a coal fire. The fire heats the tunnel walls, the walls heat the water around them, and the water boils into steam which collects at the top of the drum.

That is essentially a Lancashire boiler — but with a clever twist. After the fire's smoke travels through the two tunnels (flues), it does **not** go straight out. It is forced to **double back underneath the drum** through a brick-lined passage before reaching the chimney, squeezing every last bit of heat into the water. Think of it as **two frying pans heating a large pot of water, with the steam from the pan also being routed back under the pot** for a second round of cooking.

### 1.3 Why "Lancashire" and Not "Cornish"?

| Boiler | Number of Flues | Capacity | Modern Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cornish** | 1 | Smaller | Obsolete |
| **Lancashire** | **2** | **~40 % higher** | Still taught; low-pressure plants |

The **two-flue design** doubles the heat transfer area exposed to fire, hence the higher steam output. This is the single most important comparative point in the KTU 2024 exam.

---

## 1.4 Standard Salient Specifications (KTU Board Data)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Shell diameter | **2.0 – 2.75 m** |
| Shell length | **7.0 – 9.0 m** |
| Number of flues | **2** |
| Flue diameter | **0.4 – 0.6 × shell diameter** |
| Working pressure | **7 – 10 bar** (gauge) |
| Steam output | **8 000 – 12 000 kg/h** |
| Grate area | **4 – 10 m²** |
| Chimney diameter | **0.6 – 0.9 m** |
| Chimney height | **15 – 25 m** |
| Thermal efficiency | **50 – 70 %** |

> [!TIP]
> Memorise the **pressure range (7–10 bar)** and **steam output (8 000–12 000 kg/h)** — these two values appear verbatim in nearly every KTU 2-mark question on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principal Parts of a Lancashire Boiler

The boiler has **two distinct halves** in terms of function:

1. **The "wet" side** — the cylindrical shell filled with water up to the working level.
2. **The "hot" side** — flues + combustion chamber + bottom flue passage carrying the hot gases.

### 2.1.1 Cylindrical Shell

- Made of **mild steel plates** riveted (in older designs) or **welded** (in modern designs).
- Hemispherical or **cambered end-plates** are used to resist internal pressure.
- Design pressure is taken as **1.5 × working pressure** (with a minimum of 3.5 bar above atmospheric as per Indian Boiler Regulations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a sphere resists pressure best:**
> A spherical pressure vessel needs only half the plate thickness of a cylindrical one for the same diameter and pressure — a fact derived from the **thin-cylinder hoop-stress equation** which KTU asks frequently in linked problems.

### 2.1.2 Two Internal Flues (Fire Tubes)

- Diameter ≈ **40–60 %** of the shell diameter.
- Made of **thicker plates** than the shell because they are in direct contact with fire on one side and water on the other — a **thermal-stress** hot-spot.
- Flues are stayed to the shell by **longitudinal stays (stay tubes)** to prevent collapse under external pressure of steam + collapse under fire-side buckling.

### 2.1.3 Grates and Fire Bridge

- **Grates** sit at the bottom of each flue, made of cast-iron bars.
- A **fire-bridge (fire wall)** of refractory bricks is built at the rear, separating the flues from the combustion chamber. It forces the hot gases to rise and loop back.

### 2.1.4 Combustion Chamber

- Built at the **rear** of the boiler between the two flues.
- Lined with **fire-bricks** (refractory lining) to withstand ~ 1 500 °C.
- Provides the **residence time** for complete combustion of volatile gases before they reverse direction.

### 2.1.5 Bottom Flue Passage

- The hot gases, after combustion, are routed **below the shell** (under the water) all the way to the front, entering a **smoke box**.
- This is the **"second pass"** of the heat-exchanger geometry, raising efficiency to 50–70 %.

### 2.1.6 Smoke Box and Chimney

- Located at the **front** below the shell.
- A **damper (regulator)** in the smoke box controls the draught by restricting the chimney outlet area.
- A **blower (fan-assisted draught)** may be added for forced draught operation.

### 2.1.7 Superheater (Optional but common in KTU diagram)

- A set of **U-tubes** placed in the path of hot gases, just before they enter the chimney.
- Raises steam temperature **above saturation** (typically by 50–100 °C) to **improve Rankine-cycle efficiency** and reduce cylinder condensation losses in the downstream engine/turbine.

---

## 2.2 Mountings and Accessories (MUST-KNOW for 3-Mark Question)

### Mountings (fitted on the boiler for safe operation)

| # | Mounting | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pressure gauge** | Indicates steam pressure in the shell |
| 2 | **Water level indicator (gauge glass)** | Shows water level inside the shell |
| 3 | **Safety valve** (Lever or Spring loaded) | Releases steam automatically if pressure > set value |
| 4 | **Stop valve** | Isolates the boiler from the steam main |
| 5 | **Feed check valve** | One-way valve; admits feed water, prevents back-flow |
| 6 | **Blow-off cock** | Removes sludge and scales from the bottom |
| 7 | **Fusible plug** | Melts at ~ 200 °C if water level falls dangerously low |
| 8 | **Manhole & Mud-hole** | Access for inspection and cleaning |

### Accessories (improve efficiency or treat the steam)

- **Superheater** — raises steam temperature.
- **Economiser** — pre-heats feed water using flue gas heat.
- **Air pre-heater** — pre-heats combustion air using flue gas heat.
- **Feed pump** / **Injector** — supplies feed water to the boiler.
- **Steam separator / steam trap** — removes moisture from steam.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> A common error is to call a feed pump a "mounting". It is an **accessory** — it is *not* mounted *on* the boiler for safety, but rather assists the process. Marks are deducted if you list accessories under mountings.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Working (with Thermodynamic Mapping)

1. Coal is fed through the fire-hole on the front, onto the grates inside the two flues.
2. Combustion air enters through the **air damper** under the grate, heated by the surrounding fire.
3. **Hot gases (≈ 1 500 °C)** travel through the **first pass** (the two flues) — heat is transferred to water through the flue walls by **conduction + convection**.
4. Gases strike the **fire-bridge**, rise into the **combustion chamber**, where unburnt particles complete combustion.
5. Gases are forced to **reverse direction** and pass through the **bottom flue passage** — the **second pass** — transferring more heat to water below the shell.
6. Gases enter the **smoke box** at the front, pass through the **superheater** (if fitted), and exit via the **chimney** at ≈ 250–350 °C (exhaust temperature).
7. **Water (≈ 90 °C)** in the shell absorbs the heat, boils at the local saturation pressure (≈ 170 °C for 7 bar), and **steam rises** to the steam space at the top.
8. Steam passes through the **stop valve** to the prime-mover.

> [!TIP]
> The principle of the **second pass** (gas reversal) is what differentiates a Lancashire boiler from a simple **Cochran** (single-pass vertical) boiler. Always mention the **second pass / gas reversal** when explaining the higher efficiency.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are tested in **Part B 14-mark** numerical sub-parts almost every semester. The unit conversions are part of the marks.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Factor of Evaporation (F.E.)** | $F.E. = \dfrac{h_g - h_{fw}}{2257}$ | dimensionless | $h_g$ = enthalpy of steam leaving boiler (kJ/kg); $h_{fw}$ = enthalpy of feed water (kJ/kg); 2257 = latent heat at 100 °C in kJ/kg |
| 2 | **Equivalent Evaporation (E.E.)** | $E.E. = m_s \times F.E.$ | kg/h or kg/s | $m_s$ = actual steam generation |
| 3 | **Coal Consumption (B)** | $B = \dfrac{m_s}{E_c}$ | kg/h | $E_c$ = evaporative capacity of coal (kg steam / kg coal) |
| 4 | **Heat supplied by coal** | $Q_{in} = B \times GCV$ | kJ/h | GCV in kJ/kg |
| 5 | **Heat utilised by water** | $Q_{out} = m_s \times (h_g - h_{fw})$ | kJ/h |  |
| 6 | **Boiler Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$)** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{m_s (h_g - h_{fw})}{B \times GCV}$ | fraction or % | Compare with 50–70 % range for Lancashire |
| 7 | **Equivalent Evaporation per kg coal** | $E.E./B = \dfrac{Q_{out}/B}{2257}$ | kg/kg | Dimensionally equal to F.E. × $(m_s/B)$ |
| 8 | **Hoop stress in shell** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ | N/mm² | $p$ = pressure (N/mm²), $D$ = shell diameter (mm), $t$ = plate thickness (mm) |
| 9 | **Longitudinal stress** | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | N/mm² | Half of hoop stress |
| 10 | **Required plate thickness** | $t = \dfrac{pD}{2\eta \sigma_t} + c$ | mm | $\eta$ = joint efficiency, $\sigma_t$ = allowable tensile stress, $c$ = corrosion allowance |
| 11 | **Grate area for given coal rate** | $A_g = \dfrac{B}{\rho_{fuel} \times r_{burn}}$ | m² | $r_{burn}$ = rate of burning (kg/m²·h), typically 100–150 for Lancashire |

> [!NOTE]
> In every KTU 2024 problem, **report the efficiency in %** and the **evaporation in kg of equivalent evaporation "from and at 100 °C"** — the textbook phrase examiners love to write in the answer key.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoop Stress in the Cylindrical Shell of a Lancashire Boiler

### Context
A Lancashire boiler is essentially a **thin cylindrical pressure vessel**. Understanding the **circumferential (hoop) stress** is essential for plate-thickness design — a topic frequently linked to this boiler in KTU 14-mark questions.

### Assumptions
- Shell is **thin-walled**: $t \le D/20$.
- Material is **homogeneous, isotropic**, and obeys Hooke's law.
- Pressure is **uniformly distributed** on the inner wall.
- End-caps are properly supported so that the cylinder acts as a long shell.

### Derivation

Consider a **half-cylinder** of length $L$, diameter $D$, and internal gauge pressure $p$. Cut the cylinder along a diametrical plane. The fluid pushes the curved surface outward.

**Step 1 — Force balance along the longitudinal axis of the cut:**

The pressure force trying to split the cylinder is

$$
F_p \;=\; p \times D \times L
$$

The resisting tensile force in the **two wall sections** (one on each side of the cut) is

$$
F_t \;=\; 2 \times \sigma_h \times t \times L
$$

**Step 2 — Equate them (static equilibrium):**

$$
2 \sigma_h t L \;=\; p D L
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify by cancelling L:**

$$
\sigma_h \;=\; \dfrac{p D}{2 t}
$$

**Step 4 — For an *internal* pressure vessel, the longitudinal stress is half the hoop stress (from a similar half-cut along a transverse plane):**

$$
\sigma_L \;=\; \dfrac{p D}{4 t}
$$

**Step 5 — Plate thickness required (rearranging hoop-stress equation and adding joint efficiency + corrosion allowance):**

$$
t \;=\; \dfrac{p D}{2 \, \eta \, \sigma_t} \;+\; c
$$

where:
- $\eta$ = joint (riveted/welded) efficiency, dimensionless ($\eta = 1$ for seamless)
- $\sigma_t$ = permissible tensile stress of the plate material (N/mm²)
- $c$ = corrosion allowance (typically 1–3 mm)

### Numerical Illustration (Past KTU Pattern)

A Lancashire boiler has shell diameter $D = 2.5$ m and is to operate at $p = 1.0$ MPa gauge. The joint efficiency is $\eta = 0.85$, permissible tensile stress $\sigma_t = 120$ N/mm², corrosion allowance $c = 2$ mm. Find the **required shell plate thickness**.

**Step 1 — Convert units to N and mm:**

$$
p = 1.0 \text{ MPa} = 1.0 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
D = 2.5 \text{ m} = 2\,500 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute in the thickness equation:**

$$
t \;=\; \dfrac{1.0 \times 2\,500}{2 \times 0.85 \times 120} \;+\; 2
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1.0 \times 2\,500 = 2\,500
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 2 \times 0.85 \times 120 = 204
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the quotient and add the allowance:**

$$
t = \dfrac{2\,500}{204} + 2
$$

$$
t = 12.255 + 2
$$

$$
t = 14.255 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Round up to a commercial plate size and verify thin-wall assumption:**

$$
\boxed{t \;\approx\; 15 \text{ mm (next standard plate size)}}
$$

Check: $D/t = 2\,500/15 = 166.7 \;\gg\; 20$ → thin-wall assumption **valid** ✓

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often specify the **permissible stress for mild steel as 120–130 N/mm²** and **joint efficiency for riveted seams as 0.7–0.85**. Memorise these two.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Boiler Efficiency from Fuel and Steam Data

A Lancashire boiler **evaporates 6 000 kg/h** of steam at **1.0 MPa** (absolute) from feed water at **40 °C**. Coal is fired at the rate of **800 kg/h** with GCV of **25 000 kJ/kg**. Find (i) the factor of evaporation, (ii) the equivalent evaporation from and at 100 °C, and (iii) the thermal efficiency.

**Step 1 — Steam enthalpy at 1.0 MPa (from steam tables):**

$$
h_g = 2\,778.1 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Feed water enthalpy at 40 °C (sensible heat only):**

$$
h_{fw} = 4.186 \times 40 = 167.44 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor of Evaporation:**

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{h_g - h_{fw}}{2\,257} = \dfrac{2\,778.1 - 167.44}{2\,257}
$$

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{2\,610.66}{2\,257} = 1.157
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Evaporation:**

$$
E.E. = m_s \times F.E. = 6\,000 \times 1.157
$$

$$
\boxed{E.E. = 6\,940.6 \text{ kg/h from and at 100 °C}}
$$

**Step 5 — Heat supplied per hour by coal:**

$$
Q_{in} = B \times GCV = 800 \times 25\,000
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 2.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ kJ/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Heat utilised by water per hour:**

$$
Q_{out} = m_s \times (h_g - h_{fw}) = 6\,000 \times 2\,610.66
$$

$$
Q_{out} = 1.5664 \times 10^{7} \text{ kJ/h}
$$

**Step 7 — Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{1.5664 \times 10^7}{2.0 \times 10^7}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th} = 0.7832 = 78.32 \,\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result 78 % is **above the typical 50–70 %** range because the example uses a high GCV coal. In real KTU exam problems, the efficiency will lie in **60–80 %** for good operating conditions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Grate Area Sizing

The rate of coal burning on the grates of a Lancashire boiler is typically **120 kg/m²·h**. Find the **grate area** required for the previous example (B = 800 kg/h).

$$
A_g = \dfrac{B}{r_{burn}} = \dfrac{800}{120} = 6.67 \text{ m}^2
$$

Choose a **practical split** between the two flues:

$$
A_{per\ flue} = \dfrac{6.67}{2} = 3.33 \text{ m}^2
$$

If the length of the grate in each flue is the boiler length (≈ 8 m), then the **width per flue** is:

$$
W = \dfrac{3.33}{8} = 0.416 \text{ m}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This value (~ 0.4 m) matches the empirical rule of thumb that the **flue diameter ≈ 40–60 % of shell diameter**. For $D = 2.5$ m, flue diameter $\approx 1.0$ m, and grate width is slightly less than that. **Consistent geometry**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Lancashire vs. Other Fire-Tube Boilers)

| Feature | **Lancashire** | **Cornish** | **Cochran** | **Locomotive** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Axis | Horizontal | Horizontal | Vertical | Horizontal |
| Flues | 2 internal | 1 internal | 1 internal shell + many smoke tubes | 1 firebox + many smoke tubes |
| Pressure (bar) | 7–10 | 7–10 | 6–10 | 10–15 |
| Capacity (kg/h) | 8 000–12 000 | 4 000–6 000 | 500–4 000 | 5 000–10 000 |
| Efficiency (%) | 50–70 | 50–60 | 65–75 | 70–85 |
| Mobility | Stationary | Stationary | Stationary | Mobile |
| Application | Industrial steam plants | Obsolete | Small industry, hospitals | Locomotive propulsion |
| Construction complexity | Moderate (2 flues + combustion chamber) | Lowest | Moderate | High (many small tubes) |

> [!WARNING]
> A frequent KTU question is: *"Why does the Lancashire boiler have higher capacity than the Cornish?"* — Answer in **two crisp points**: (1) **double the heating surface** because of two flues; (2) **double the grate area** allows more coal to be burnt per hour.

---

## 3.5 Energy-Flow Sankey-Style Reasoning (Textual Diagram)

```
   Coal (800 kg/h, GCV 25 000 kJ/kg)  →  Q_in  =  2.00 × 10^7 kJ/h
                                                       │
                                                       ▼
                              ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
                              │        Lancashire Boiler            │
                              │  (Flues + Combustion Chamber +     │
                              │   Bottom Flue Passage)             │
                              └────────────────────────────────────┘
                                  │              │             │
                                  ▼              ▼             ▼
                         Steam 1.566×10^7   Flue gas   Radiation +
                         (78.3 %)            ~ 3.5×10^6  Unaccounted
                                             (~17.5 %)   ~ 8.4×10^5
                                                          (~4.2 %)
```

This **78 %** utilisation figure is a reasonable upper-bound for a Lancashire boiler; real installations show 50–70 %.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Hot-Gas Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coal on Grates] --> B[Two Internal Flues - First Pass]
    B --> C[Fire Bridge]
    C --> D[Combustion Chamber]
    D --> E[Bottom Flue Passage - Second Pass]
    E --> F[Smoke Box with Damper]
    F --> G[Optional Superheater]
    G --> H[Chimney to Atmosphere]
    
    I[Cold Feed Water 40 C] --> J[Feed Check Valve]
    J --> K[Water inside Shell]
    K --> L[Steam Space at Top]
    L --> M[Stop Valve to Steam Main]
    
    K -. Heat transfer via flue walls .-> B
    E -. Heat transfer via bottom plate .-> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> Read the diagram in the **direction of arrows**. The hot gases (top row) flow **left-to-right**, then **reverse** at the combustion chamber (hence the U-shape), passing under the shell. The water/steam path (bottom row) is a one-way loop. Dotted arrows represent **heat transfer**, not mass flow.

---

## 4.2 Detailed Component-Level Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHELL[Boiler Shell - filled with water]
        W[Water - up to working level]
        S[Steam space at top]
        FL1[Flue 1 - left]
        FL2[Flue 2 - right]
        ST1[Stay tubes - longitudinal]
        SPH[Superheater U-tubes]
    end
    
    subgraph FRONT[Front of Boiler]
        FH[Fire hole and door]
        DAM[Air damper]
        SMK[Smoke box]
        DMP[Damper regulator]
        STV[Stop valve]
    end
    
    subgraph REAR[Rear of Boiler]
        FB[Fire bridge - refractory]
        CC[Combustion chamber]
    end
    
    subgraph MOUNT[Mountings on shell]
        PG[Pressure gauge]
        WGL[Water level gauge]
        SVL[Safety valve]
        FPL[Fusible plug]
        FCH[Feed check valve]
        BOC[Blow off cock]
        MAN[Manhole]
    end
    
    FH --> FL1
    FH --> FL2
    FL1 --> FB
    FL2 --> FB
    FB --> CC
    CC --> SMK
    SMK --> SPH
    SPH --> DMP
    DMP --> CH[Chimney]
    DAM --> FH
    
    WGL -. monitors .-> W
    PG -. monitors .-> S
    SVL -. protects .-> S
    FPL -. protects .-> FL1
    FPL -. protects .-> FL2
    FCH --> W
    BOC -. drains .-> W
    ST1 -. stays .-> FL1
    ST1 -. stays .-> FL2
    S --> STV
```

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners look for the **eight mountings** in the diagram. If a student draws the shell but **forgets the safety valve or fusible plug**, marks are reserved for the missed component.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Cornish vs. Lancashire

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CORNISH[Cornish Boiler - 1 Flue]
        C1[Grate] --> C2[Single internal flue]
        C2 --> C3[Combustion chamber]
        C3 --> C4[Bottom passage]
        C4 --> C5[Chimney]
    end
    
    subgraph LANC[Lancashire Boiler - 2 Flues]
        L1[Grate 1] --> L2[Flue 1]
        L3[Grate 2] --> L4[Flue 2]
        L2 --> L5[Joint combustion chamber]
        L4 --> L5
        L5 --> L6[Bottom passage]
        L6 --> L7[Chimney]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **doubled grate + doubled flue** path in the Lancashire version is what makes it **roughly twice the capacity** of the Cornish boiler.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. State any **three** differences between a Lancashire and a Cornish boiler. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Number of internal flues:** Lancashire boiler has **two** flues, whereas the Cornish boiler has only **one** flue.
2. **Steam-generating capacity:** Lancashire boiler has a higher capacity of **8 000 – 12 000 kg/h** compared to **4 000 – 6 000 kg/h** of the Cornish boiler.
3. **Grate area:** The Lancashire boiler has a **larger grate area** (two grates, one per flue) allowing more coal to be burnt per hour, while the Cornish boiler has a single grate.

> [!NOTE]
> Any 3 of the following accepted: number of flues, capacity, grate area, efficiency, length, heating surface.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q2. List the **mountings** fitted on a Lancashire boiler. Explain the function of a **fusible plug**. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mountings fitted on a Lancashire boiler:**
- Pressure gauge
- Water level indicator (gauge glass)
- Safety valve
- Stop valve
- Feed check valve
- Blow-off cock
- Fusible plug
- Manhole / Mud-hole

**Function of the fusible plug:**
A fusible plug is fitted at the **lowest water level** of the boiler shell, typically on the **crown of the furnace flues**. It contains a **low-melting-point alloy (e.g., tin or lead) that melts at ~ 200 °C**. If the water level falls dangerously low, exposing the plug to hot gases, the alloy melts, allowing steam and water to rush into the furnace, **extinguishing the fire** and preventing the shell from burning through. It is a **last-resort safety device**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q1 Choice A]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Lancashire Boiler Construction, Working & Comparison

**(a)** With the help of a **neat line sketch**, describe the **construction and working** of a Lancashire boiler. List its **mountings and accessories**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare a Lancashire boiler with a **Cornish boiler** and a **Cochran boiler** under the heads: **type, capacity, working pressure, efficiency, application**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction:**

The Lancashire boiler consists of a **horizontal cylindrical shell** made of mild-steel plates, closed at both ends by hemispherical or dished end-plates. The shell is mounted on a **brick setting** and is filled with water up to a working level (about **two-thirds** of the shell diameter). Two large **internal flues** of diameter equal to **40–60 % of the shell diameter** run longitudinally through the shell. At the bottom of each flue is a **cast-iron grate** on which coal is fired. A **fire-bridge** of refractory bricks is built at the rear end, dividing the flues from the **combustion chamber**. Below the shell is the **bottom flue passage** leading to a **smoke box** at the front, fitted with a **damper**. From the smoke box, a chimney leads to the atmosphere. An optional **superheater** is placed in the smoke box.

**Working:**

Coal is fed through the **fire hole** onto the grates. Combustion air enters through the **air damper**. The hot gases produced travel through the two flues (**first pass**), transferring heat to the surrounding water. On reaching the **fire-bridge**, the gases are diverted upward into the **combustion chamber**, where unburnt particles complete combustion. The gases are then forced to **reverse direction** and pass through the **bottom flue passage** beneath the shell (**second pass**), transferring additional heat to the water. The gases then enter the **smoke box**, pass through the **superheater** (if fitted), and escape via the **chimney**. Steam formed at the top of the shell is taken out through the **stop valve** to the prime mover.

**Mountings:** Pressure gauge, water level indicator, safety valve, stop valve, feed check valve, blow-off cock, fusible plug, manhole. **(4 × 0.5 = 2 marks)**

**Accessories:** Superheater, economiser, air pre-heater, feed pump, injector. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table** (5 × 1.4 = 7 marks; allocate proportionally):

| Feature | **Lancashire** | **Cornish** | **Cochran** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Horizontal, fire-tube, internally fired, 2 flues | Horizontal, fire-tube, internally fired, 1 flue | Vertical, fire-tube, internally fired, 1 furnace + many smoke tubes |
| Capacity | 8 000 – 12 000 kg/h | 4 000 – 6 000 kg/h | 500 – 4 000 kg/h |
| Working pressure | 7 – 10 bar | 7 – 10 bar | 6 – 10 bar |
| Efficiency | 50 – 70 % | 50 – 60 % | 65 – 75 % |
| Application | Industrial steam plants, textile mills, paper mills | Largely obsolete; older small industries | Small industries, hospitals, processing plants |

> [!TIP]
> Always **underline the headers** in the comparison table and present it in a **box** for visual clarity — KTU examiners reward neatness.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q1 Choice B]**

### Question B (14 Marks) — Numerical on Efficiency and Evaporation

**(a)** A Lancashire boiler generates **5 500 kg/h** of dry saturated steam at **1.2 MPa (abs)** from feed water at **45 °C**. Coal is burnt at the rate of **720 kg/h** with a GCV of **26 000 kJ/kg**. Calculate the **(i) Factor of Evaporation**, **(ii) Equivalent Evaporation from and at 100 °C**, and **(iii) Boiler thermal efficiency**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the **grate area** of the same boiler is **6 m²**, determine the **rate of burning of coal per m² of grate per hour**, and comment on whether this value lies in the **typical range for a Lancashire boiler (100–150 kg/m²·h)**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Required enthalpies (from steam tables):**

At 1.2 MPa (abs), $h_g = 2\,784.8$ kJ/kg (dry saturated steam).
Feed water at 45 °C: $h_{fw} = 4.186 \times 45 = 188.37$ kJ/kg.
Latent heat at 100 °C = 2 257 kJ/kg.

**Step 2 — Factor of Evaporation:**

$$
F.E. = \dfrac{h_g - h_{fw}}{2\,257} = \dfrac{2\,784.8 - 188.37}{2\,257} = \dfrac{2\,596.43}{2\,257} = 1.150
$$

**(i) Factor of Evaporation = 1.150 (dimensionless)** ✓

**Step 3 — Equivalent Evaporation:**

$$
E.E. = m_s \times F.E. = 5\,500 \times 1.150
$$

**(ii) Equivalent Evaporation = 6 325 kg/h from and at 100 °C** ✓

**Step 4 — Heat supplied per hour:**

$$
Q_{in} = B \times GCV = 720 \times 26\,000 = 1.872 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Heat utilised per hour:**

$$
Q_{out} = m_s \times (h_g - h_{fw}) = 5\,500 \times 2\,596.43 = 1.428 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \dfrac{1.428 \times 10^7}{1.872 \times 10^7} = 0.7628
$$

**(iii) Boiler thermal efficiency = 76.28 %** ✓

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Rate of burning of coal per m² per hour:**

$$
r_{burn} = \dfrac{B}{A_g} = \dfrac{720}{6} = 120 \text{ kg/m}^2 \cdot \text{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Comment:**

The value **120 kg/m²·h** lies **within the typical range of 100–150 kg/m²·h** for a Lancashire boiler operating under natural-draught conditions. This indicates that the boiler is being fired at a **moderate, sustainable rate**. If $r_{burn}$ were below 100, it would suggest under-utilisation; if above 150, it would indicate forced-draught or over-firing, possibly leading to clinker formation and reduced life of the grates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do not omit units** ($kg/m^2 \cdot h$ is mandatory; writing only "120" loses 0.5 marks).
> - **Use the exact 2 257 kJ/kg** for latent heat at 100 °C (don't approximate).
> - **Show the steam-table reference** (e.g., "From steam tables, at 1.2 MPa…"). Examiners want to see **the lookup step**, not just the value.
> - **Round off only at the final answer**, not at intermediate steps — 4-decimal accuracy is the norm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Punch-List for Lancashire Boiler**

- ✅ **Inventor:** Sir William Fairbairn (1844); modification of the Cornish boiler.
- ✅ **Type:** Horizontal, stationary, fire-tube, internally fired, **two flues**.
- ✅ **Pressure:** **7 – 10 bar** (low pressure).
- ✅ **Capacity:** **8 000 – 12 000 kg of steam per hour**.
- ✅ **Efficiency:** **50 – 70 %**.
- ✅ **Salient dimensions:** Shell Ø 2.0–2.75 m, length 7–9 m; flue Ø = 40–60 % of shell Ø.
- ✅ **Working principle:** **Two-pass** hot-gas flow — through the two flues, then **reversed** through the bottom passage for second-pass heating.
- ✅ **Fire-bridge** is at the rear; **smoke box + damper** is at the front.
- ✅ **Mountings (8):** Pressure gauge, water level indicator, safety valve, stop valve, feed check valve, blow-off cock, fusible plug, manhole.
- ✅ **Fusible plug** melts at ~ **200 °C**; protects the crown of the furnace if water level drops.
- ✅ **Superheater** improves Rankine cycle efficiency by raising steam temperature above saturation.
- ✅ **Lancashire vs. Cornish:** Two flues vs. one flue ⇒ **higher capacity, ~40 % more steam**.
- ✅ **Thin-cylinder formula:** $\sigma_h = pD/(2t)$; plate thickness $t = pD/(2\eta\sigma_t) + c$.
- ✅ **Factor of Evaporation:** $F.E. = (h_g - h_{fw})/2\,257$ — denominator is always **2 257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C.
- ✅ **Thermal efficiency range:** Real installations 50–70 %; well-maintained with economiser can reach 75–80 %.
- ✅ **Draw the two-pass gas path** in every 7-mark descriptive answer — examiners award **at least 1 mark** for the sketch alone.
- ✅ **Distinguish mountings vs. accessories** in any list-type question.

> **End of Notes — Lancashire Boiler (Module 2, PEMET631)**
