topics.title: Cornish Boiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cornish Boiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cornish Boiler** is a **horizontal, stationary, internally fired, fire-tube boiler** that employs a **single, large-diameter furnace/flue tube** which is enlarged at one end to accommodate a combustion chamber. The hot combustion gases traverse the length of this internal flue, transfer heat to the surrounding water, and then return through the bottom of the shell to the front before escaping up the chimney.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Origin:** Named after Cornwall (England), where it was popularized in the early 19th century for mine pumping. It is sometimes attributed to engineer **Richard Trevithick's** design lineage, though it evolved from the earlier "haystack" boiler concept.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a giant **horizontal steel water tank** lying on its side. Inside this tank, you push a **large pipe** (open at both ends) all the way through. You light a fire inside this pipe at one end. The water surrounds the pipe and gets heated from the inside. As the fire burns, hot smoke travels down the pipe to the far end, then sneaks back along the bottom of the tank (under the pipe), and finally exits through a chimney at the front. This is essentially a Cornish boiler — a "fire-on-the-inside" boiler where the heat source is **immersed in** the water it is heating.

## 1.3 Key Design & Operating Specifications

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Orientation | **Horizontal, Stationary** |
| Shell Diameter | **1.5 m to 2.5 m** |
| Shell Length | **4.0 m to 7.0 m** |
| Working Pressure | **10 – 15 bar** |
| Steam Output Capacity | **Low (up to ~6500 kg/hr)** |
| Thermal Efficiency | **60 % – 70 %** |
| Fuel | Solid fuels (coal, wood) historically; biomass in modern retrofits |
| Heat Transfer Surface | Single large internal fire tube |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus Point:** A Cornish boiler is distinguished from a Lancashire boiler (which has **two** internal fire tubes) by having a **single, internally enlarged flue/furnace tube**. Examiners often test this one-tubed vs. two-tubed distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy (Component-wise Breakdown)

The Cornish boiler is built up from a handful of well-defined sub-assemblies. Each part is engineered for a specific role in transferring heat safely from the flame to the water.

### 2.1.1 The Cylindrical Shell
- Constructed from **riveted (older units) or welded (modern units) steel plates**.
- The ends are closed by **flat end plates** (called *end plates* or *tube plates*).
- The flat ends resist pressure-induced bulging by using **stay bolts** that tie the flat surfaces together.

### 2.1.2 The Internal Fire Tube / Furnace
- A **single large-diameter steel tube** runs through the centre of the shell.
- At the front, the tube is **enlarged into a combustion chamber** (the furnace) lined with **fire-bricks** to withstand direct flame impingement.
- The rear of the tube opens into a **smokebox** at the back end of the boiler.

### 2.1.3 The Grate Assembly
- A **fire-grate** is mounted at the furnace end of the fire tube.
- Solid fuel is fed manually (or via a stoker in improved designs) onto the grate.
- Primary air enters through the ash-pit doors below the grate; secondary air admits through the fire-door above.

### 2.1.4 Combustion Gas Path
The hot gases follow a specific two-pass route:

1. **Upward pass:** Flame rises from the grate into the firebrick-lined combustion chamber (the enlarged part of the flue).
2. **Forward pass:** Hot gases travel down the length of the main fire tube toward the back of the boiler.
3. **Return pass:** Gases exit into the smokebox, then reverse direction and travel back along the **bottom of the shell** (under the fire tube) toward the front.
4. **Exhaust:** Gases ascend through a **brick-lined chimney** at the front of the boiler, escaping to the atmosphere.

### 2.1.5 Mountings and Fittings (Mandatory Per Indian Boiler Regulations)
- **Safety valve** — relieves over-pressure conditions.
- **Pressure gauge** — indicates internal steam pressure.
- **Water level indicator (gauge glass)** — shows the water level inside the shell.
- **Steam stop valve** — controls outgoing steam.
- **Feed check valve** — admits fresh feedwater only inward.
- **Blow-off cock** — empties the boiler for maintenance.
- **Manhole / Mud hole** — for internal inspection and cleaning.
- **Fusible plug** — melts if water level falls dangerously, alerting operators.

## 2.2 Why a Two-Pass Flue Design?

The single large fire tube provides a **long, slow path** for the hot gases, giving them ample time to release heat into the surrounding water. The bottom return pass recovers residual heat from gases that would otherwise exit hot. This **counter-flow heat exchange** principle (water flows generally opposite to gas flow) is the "why" behind the improved efficiency over the older haystack boiler.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Power Plants

Although largely obsolete in modern utility-scale power generation (replaced by water-tube boilers like Babcock-Wilcox), Cornish boilers **still find niche use** in:
- Small industrial process steam plants.
- Sugar mills and paper mills in developing economies.
- Rural biomass-fired cogeneration units.
- Heritage steam engines and locomotive training simulators.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Q = m \cdot C_{p} \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat absorbed by feedwater | kW |
| $Q = m_{f} \cdot \text{GCV}$ | Total heat released by fuel combustion | kW |
| $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{supplied}}} \times 100$ | Boiler efficiency (by direct method) | % |
| $S = \pi \cdot D \cdot L$ | Approximate heat-transfer surface (shell) | $\text{m}^{2}$ |
| $\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{h_{g} - h_{f}}$ | Steam generation rate from heat duty | kg/s |
| $\dot{V}_{\text{chimney}} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{H_{c} \cdot (\rho_{a} - \rho_{g})}{\rho_{g}}}$ | Natural-draft chimney velocity (qualitative) | m/s |
| $P \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$ | Ideal-gas relation for flue gas density | Pa, m³, kg, J/kg·K |
| $t_{\text{evap}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{water}} \cdot (h_{g}-h_{f})}{Q_{\text{rate}}}$ | Time to raise water to saturation (transient) | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **diameter $D$ and length $L$** of a Cornish boiler are **not** arbitrary — they are constrained by the need to provide at least $\mathbf{1 \, m^{2}}$ of heating surface per $\mathbf{30 \, kg/hr}$ of steam (a classical thumb rule). This ratio is sometimes tested as a **"Compare and Justify" question in KTU Part A**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through every quantitative relationship a KTU student is expected to master on the Cornish boiler. Each step is fully expanded, with a one-line justification for every algebraic move.

## 3.1 Derivation: Evaporative Capacity From Heating Surface

**Statement:** *The evaporative capacity of a boiler is directly proportional to its rate of heat absorption, and inversely proportional to the latent heat of vaporisation.*

### Given Data (typical KTU problem values)
- Heating surface area $A = 60 \, \text{m}^{2}$
- Rate of heat transfer per unit area $\dfrac{q}{A} = 25 \, \text{kW/m}^{2}$
- Feedwater inlet temperature $T_{fw} = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Working pressure $P = 10 \, \text{bar (saturated)}$

### Step 1 — Total Rate of Heat Absorption

The total heat absorbed by the water per unit time is the heating surface area multiplied by the heat flux.

$$
Q_{\text{abs}} = A \times \frac{q}{A}
$$

Substitute the numerical values.

$$
Q_{\text{abs}} = 60 \, \text{m}^{2} \times 25 \, \text{kW/m}^{2}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{abs}} = 1500 \, \text{kW}
$$

This is the **total rate of heat absorbed** by the water from the fire-side.

### Step 2 — Enthalpy Rise of Feedwater

From steam tables at $P = 10 \, \text{bar}$:

- $h_{f} \text{ (saturated liquid at 10 bar)} \approx 762.81 \, \text{kJ/kg}$
- $h_{g} \text{ (saturated vapour at 10 bar)} \approx 2778.10 \, \text{kJ/kg}$
- $h_{fw} \text{ at } 30^{\circ}\text{C} \approx 125.79 \, \text{kJ/kg}$

The total enthalpy rise per kilogram of feedwater is the difference between the saturated-vapour enthalpy and the inlet feedwater enthalpy.

$$
\Delta h = h_{g} - h_{fw}
$$

$$
\Delta h = 2778.10 - 125.79
$$

$$
\Delta h = 2652.31 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert Total Heat Power to kJ/s

The heat rate $Q_{\text{abs}} = 1500 \, \text{kW}$ equals $1500 \, \text{kJ/s}$.

$$
\dot{Q} = 1500 \, \text{kJ/s}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Evaporative Capacity

Evaporative capacity is the ratio of total heat absorbed per second to the enthalpy required per kilogram of steam.

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\Delta h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} = \frac{1500 \, \text{kJ/s}}{2652.31 \, \text{kJ/kg}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} = 0.5655 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to kg/hr (Industrial Convention)

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} = 0.5655 \times 3600
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \approx 2035.8 \, \text{kg/hr}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \approx 2036 \, \text{kg/hr}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Cornish vs. Lancashire Boiler

This is the **most frequently asked comparison** in KTU 2024 Part A. A side-by-side matrix trains the student to articulate differences in board exams.

| Feature | Cornish Boiler | Lancashire Boiler |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of internal fire tubes** | One (single, enlarged) | Two (symmetric) |
| **Furnace location** | Inside the internal fire tube | Inside the internal fire tubes |
| **Combustion gas pass** | Two-pass (forward + bottom return) | Three-pass (forward + two side returns) |
| **Thermal efficiency** | 60 – 70 % | 70 – 80 % |
| **Steam capacity** | Lower (up to ~6500 kg/hr) | Higher (up to ~30000 kg/hr) |
| **Floor area required** | Comparatively less | More (larger diameter) |
| **Initial cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Maintenance** | Easier | Slightly more complex |
| **Heating surface per unit volume** | Lower | Higher |
| **Best suited for** | Small scale, low pressure, batch operations | Medium scale, continuous process steam |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common valuation pitfall: students write "Cornish has **one** furnace" but forget to mention that this single furnace is **enlarged into a combustion chamber** lined with fire-bricks. The combustion-chamber detail is what differentiates it from a simple single-flue "smoke-tube" boiler.

## 3.3 Heat Balance Equation (Direct Method)

For a steady-state boiler, the energy entering equals the energy leaving plus losses.

$$
Q_{\text{fuel}} = Q_{\text{steam}} + Q_{\text{flue gas loss}} + Q_{\text{radiation}} + Q_{\text{moisture}}
$$

Where:

- $Q_{\text{fuel}} = m_{f} \times \text{GCV}$
- $Q_{\text{steam}} = m_{s} \times (h_{g} - h_{fw})$
- $Q_{\text{flue gas loss}} = m_{g} \times C_{pg} \times (T_{g,\text{out}} - T_{\text{ref}})$
- $Q_{\text{radiation}} \approx 1 \text{ to } 2 \% \text{ of } Q_{\text{fuel}}$ (typical empirical value)

By the **direct method**, efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \frac{m_{s} (h_{g} - h_{fw})}{m_{f} \times \text{GCV}} \times 100 \, \%
$$

This compact form is what students must memorise for the 14-mark Part B derivations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

Below is a Mermaid block-rendered **functional architecture** of a Cornish boiler, mapping the physical layout of all key components and the direction of flow of the working fluids (water/steam) and combustion gases.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Coal on Grate] -->|Combustion| B[Firebrick Lined Furnace Chamber]
    B -->|Hot Flue Gas Forward| C[Main Fire Tube of Length L]
    C -->|Gas Reversal at Rear| D[Smoke Box at Back End Plate]
    D -->|Bottom Return Pass Under Tubes| E[Bottom Passage of Shell]
    E -->|Gas Upward| F[Brick Lined Chimney at Front]
    F -->|Exhaust| G[Atmosphere]
    W1[Feed Water Inlet] -->|Feed Check Valve| H[Water Surrounding Fire Tube]
    H -->|Heat Absorbed| I[Saturated Steam Space Top of Shell]
    I -->|Steam Stop Valve| J[Steam to Process]
    M1[Safety Valve] -.->|Pressure Relief| I
    M2[Pressure Gauge] -.->|Monitors| I
    M3[Water Level Gauge Glass] -.->|Reads| H
    M4[Blow Off Cock] -.->|Drains| H
    M5[Fusible Plug] -.->|Melts on Low Water| B
```

## 4.1 Reading the Diagram

- The **red-line trajectory (A → B → C → D → E → F → G)** represents the **flue gas path**. Observe the U-turn at the smokebox and the return pass along the bottom of the shell.
- The **blue-line trajectory (W1 → H → I → J)** represents the **feedwater-to-steam path**. The water is heated, partially evaporates, and the steam is collected in the upper steam space.
- The **dashed lines (M1…M5)** represent **safety mountings and instrumentation** that are not part of the main flow path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram emphasises the **counter-flow principle**: feedwater enters cold near the chimney side, while the hottest gases are in the furnace. The gases cool as they travel, and the water heats as it approaches saturation — maximising the log-mean temperature difference (LMTD) for heat transfer.

## 4.2 Component-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph PressureVesselAssembly
        SP[Cylindrical Shell Steel Plate]
        EP1[Front End Plate Flat]
        EP2[Rear End Plate Flat]
        SB1[Stay Bolts Connecting EP1 to EP2]
        SP --- EP1
        SP --- EP2
        EP1 -.- SB1
        EP2 -.- SB1
    end
    subgraph InternalFlueAssembly
        FT[Single Fire Tube of Diameter D]
        FF[Enlarged Furnace Chamber Front End]
        FB[Firebrick Lining of Furnace]
        GR[Cast Iron Grate]
        FT --- FF
        FF --- GR
        FF --- FB
    end
    subgraph MountingsFittings
        SV[Safety Valve]
        PG[Pressure Gauge]
        WG[Water Gauge Glass]
        SSV[Steam Stop Valve]
        FCV[Feed Check Valve]
        BOC[Blow Off Cock]
        FP[Fusible Plug]
        MH[Manhole Mud Hole]
    end
    SP --- InternalFlueAssembly
    SP --- MountingsFittings
```

This second diagram separates the boiler into **three logical sub-assemblies** for clarity:
1. **Pressure Vessel Assembly** — holds the water and steam under pressure.
2. **Internal Flue Assembly** — houses the combustion process.
3. **Mountings & Fittings** — ensure safe, controllable, and observable operation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*State the constructional difference between a Cornish boiler and a Lancashire boiler.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **Cornish boiler** has a **single, large-diameter internal fire tube** with an enlarged furnace at one end (1 mark).
- The **Lancashire boiler** has **two parallel internal fire tubes**, each with its own enlarged furnace (1 mark).
- Consequently, the Lancashire boiler provides a **larger heating surface** and higher steam capacity, while the Cornish boiler is **simpler in construction and cheaper** (1 mark).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any six essential mountings fitted on a Cornish boiler.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. Safety valve
2. Pressure gauge
3. Water level indicator (gauge glass)
4. Steam stop valve
5. Feed check valve
6. Blow-off cock

*(Acceptable additions: Manhole, Mud hole, Fusible plug, Dead-weight safety valve.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, students answer **one full 14-mark question** from a choice of two. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 (Module 2 Comprehensive)]**
*(a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Cornish boiler. (7 marks)*
*(b) A Cornish boiler generates 2000 kg/hr of dry saturated steam at 10 bar from feedwater at 40 °C. Coal of GCV 30000 kJ/kg is fired at 280 kg/hr. Calculate (i) the thermal efficiency of the boiler, and (ii) the equivalent evaporation per kg of coal. (7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks)

**Construction (4 marks):**
- **Cylindrical shell** made of steel plates, with a length-to-diameter ratio typically 3:1 (1 mark).
- **Single internal fire tube** of large diameter runs centrally through the shell (1 mark).
- The front portion of this tube is **enlarged into a furnace** lined with **firebricks** to withstand direct flame impingement (1 mark).
- A **grate** holds the solid fuel; **flat end plates** are reinforced with **stay bolts** to resist bulging (1 mark).

**Working (3 marks):**
- Coal is fed onto the grate and ignited; combustion produces hot gases (1 mark).
- Hot gases rise in the furnace, then pass through the fire tube toward the rear **smokebox** (1 mark).
- Gases then **return along the bottom** of the shell (counter-flow to water) and escape up the **chimney** at the front, while water absorbs heat and generates steam in the upper shell region (1 mark).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Steam output $\dot{m}_{s} = 2000 \, \text{kg/hr} = 0.5556 \, \text{kg/s}$
- Working pressure $P = 10 \, \text{bar (saturated)}$
- Feedwater temperature $T_{fw} = 40^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Coal GCV = 30000 kJ/kg
- Coal consumption $\dot{m}_{f} = 280 \, \text{kg/hr}$

**From Steam Tables (at 10 bar):**
- $h_{g} = 2778.10 \, \text{kJ/kg}$
- $h_{fw} \text{ at } 40^{\circ}\text{C} \approx 167.57 \, \text{kJ/kg}$

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Heat absorbed by water per kg of coal (useful heat)**

First, compute the heat absorbed per kg of steam generated.

$$
\Delta h = h_{g} - h_{fw}
$$

$$
\Delta h = 2778.10 - 167.57 = 2610.53 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Steam generated per kg of coal.

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_{s}}{\dot{m}_{f}} = \frac{2000}{280} = 7.143 \, \text{kg steam/kg coal}
$$

Heat absorbed per kg of coal.

$$
q_{\text{useful}} = 7.143 \times 2610.53
$$

$$
q_{\text{useful}} = 18645.0 \, \text{kJ/kg coal}
$$

**Step 2: Boiler thermal efficiency**

$$
\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \frac{q_{\text{useful}}}{\text{GCV}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \frac{18645.0}{30000} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{boiler}} \approx 62.15 \, \%}
$$

**Step 3: Equivalent evaporation per kg of coal**

"Equivalent evaporation" is the mass of water that would be evaporated **from 100 °C to dry saturated steam at 100 °C** by the same heat input. From steam tables, latent heat at 100 °C is $h_{fg} = 2257 \, \text{kJ/kg}$.

$$
\text{Equivalent evaporation} = \frac{q_{\text{useful}}}{h_{fg,100^{\circ}\text{C}}}
$$

$$
\text{Equivalent evaporation} = \frac{18645.0}{2257}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Equivalent evaporation} \approx 8.26 \, \text{kg of water per kg of coal}}
$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*(a) Explain with a sketch the path of hot gases and water in a Cornish boiler. List any four advantages and any three disadvantages. (7 marks)*
*(b) Compare the performance of a Cornish boiler and a Lancashire boiler under the heads: (i) Heating surface, (ii) Steam capacity, (iii) Efficiency, (iv) Initial cost, (v) Maintenance, (vi) Floor area, (vii) Suitability of fuel. (7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) — Gas/Water Path + Pros & Cons (7 Marks)

**Gas path (2 marks):**
Grate → Furnace (enlarged tube) → Forward through main fire tube → Smokebox at rear → Bottom return pass → Up the chimney.

**Water/steam path (1 mark):**
Feedwater enters the shell → surrounds the fire tube → absorbs heat → steam collects in the upper steam space → exits via the steam stop valve.

**Four advantages (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. Simple, rugged construction; easy to fabricate.
2. Low initial capital cost.
3. Easy access for maintenance and cleaning.
4. Can burn low-grade coal and biomass.

**Three disadvantages (2 marks): 1 mark for first, 1 mark each for the next two**
1. **Low steam-generating capacity** compared to water-tube boilers.
2. **Low thermal efficiency** (60 – 70 %) due to limited heating surface.
3. **Cannot handle fluctuating loads** — large thermal inertia.
4. Large floor area required; cannot be used for high-pressure applications.

---

#### Part (b) — Tabular Comparison (7 Marks)

| S.No | Head | Cornish Boiler | Lancashire Boiler | Marks |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Heating surface | Lower (single fire tube) | Higher (two fire tubes) | 1 |
| 2 | Steam capacity | Lower | Higher | 1 |
| 3 | Thermal efficiency | 60 – 70 % | 70 – 80 % | 1 |
| 4 | Initial cost | Lower | Higher | 1 |
| 5 | Maintenance | Easier (single tube) | Slightly complex (two tubes) | 1 |
| 6 | Floor area | Smaller | Larger | 1 |
| 7 | Suitable fuel | Low-grade coal/biomass | Better-grade coal; cleaner fuels | 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When asked for a comparison, students often write a paragraph of mixed prose, mixing up which boiler has what feature. Examiners expect a **clean side-by-side tabular format** with **one feature per row**. Failure to use a table often results in **1–2 marks deducted** for "presentation". Also, **do not exceed the 4-advantage / 3-disadvantage limit** specified in the question — extraneous points waste time but earn no extra credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cornish Boiler**

- [x] **Type:** Horizontal, stationary, internally fired, **fire-tube** boiler.
- [x] **Defining feature:** A **single, large-diameter internal fire tube** whose front end is **enlarged into a furnace chamber** lined with firebricks.
- [x] **Flue gas path:** Furnace → forward through fire tube → smokebox at rear → bottom return pass → up the front chimney. **Two-pass** design.
- [x] **Water/steam path:** Feedwater → surrounds fire tube → steam collects in upper shell space → out via steam stop valve.
- [x] **Heating surface:** $\sim 60$ – $80 \, \text{m}^{2}$ for typical units; **much less** than a Lancashire of the same size.
- [x] **Working pressure:** **10 – 15 bar** (low to medium pressure).
- [x] **Efficiency:** **60 – 70 %** (lower than Lancashire).
- [x] **Steam capacity:** Low to moderate, typically $\leq 6500 \, \text{kg/hr}$.
- [x] **Combustion gas travel length:** = shell length + bottom return length = approx. **2L**.
- [x] **Mandatory mountings:** Safety valve, pressure gauge, water gauge glass, steam stop valve, feed check valve, blow-off cock, manhole, fusible plug.
- [x] **Advantages:** Simple, cheap, easy maintenance, fuel-flexible.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Low capacity, low efficiency, slow steam rise, poor load-following, occupies large floor area.
- [x] **Key distinguishing number:** **One fire tube** (Lancashire has two). This is the **#1 board-exam trap**.
- [x] **Lining material inside the furnace:** **Firebricks** (refractory) — important for protecting the tube from direct flame impingement.
- [x] **Stay bolts:** Connect the two flat end plates to resist pressure-induced bulging.
- [x] **Comparison pair to memorise:** Cornish (1 tube) vs. Lancashire (2 tubes); same family — both are **fire-tube, horizontal, internally fired** boilers.
- [x] **Modern relevance:** Largely superseded by water-tube boilers; still used in small industries and rural biomass plants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Locomotive Boiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Locomotive Boiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Locomotive Boiler** is a **horizontal**, **multitubular**, **internally fired**, **forced circulation**, **mobile** (portable) **fire-tube boiler**. It is a **self-contained** steam generator specifically engineered to be mounted on a movable chassis (locomotive frame) and to supply **high-pressure saturated or superheated steam** to drive the reciprocating steam engine of railway locomotives.

The boiler operates on the principle of **natural circulation** (thermo-siphon effect) where the difference in density between the hot water/steam mixture in the fire-tubes and the cooler water in the shell generates a continuous convective flow. The standard working pressure is **10–20 bar (≈ 1–2 MPa)**, and the steaming capacity typically ranges from **2000 to 6000 kg/hr**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Taxonomy Markers (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Shell Type:** Horizontal cylindrical
> - **Tube Type:** Fire-tube (hot gases pass *inside* the tubes, water surrounds them outside)
> - **Firing Mode:** Internally fired (furnace is built *inside* the shell)
> - **Mobility:** Mobile / portable
> - **Circulation:** Natural / Thermo-siphon
> - **Application:** Railway traction (locomotive propulsion)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine a giant horizontal **pressure cooker mounted on wheels** that has a small fire burning *inside* a brick-lined box at one end. The fire's hot smoke is forced to travel back and forth hundreds of times through narrow metal pipes surrounded by water before escaping out of a tall chimney at the front. As the smoke passes through these pipes, it boils the surrounding water into steam. This steam collects at the top, and is piped to the locomotive's pistons, which turn the wheels of the train.

> The "pipes" are the **fire tubes**, the "brick-lined box" is the **firebox**, the "giant cylinder" is the **shell**, and the "chest collecting steam" is the **steam dome**. The longer the hot smoke stays in contact with the water, the more steam is produced — which is why locomotive boilers have **hundreds of fire tubes** packed tightly inside.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Working Pressure | **10 – 20 bar** |
| Steam Generation | **2000 – 6000 kg/hr** |
| Efficiency (η) | **60 – 70 %** |
| Number of Fire Tubes | **100 – 250 tubes** |
| Tube Diameter | **40 – 75 mm** |
| Chimney Draft | **25 – 35 mm of water column** |
| Firebox Temperature | **≈ 1500 °C – 1700 °C** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Philosophy (Why and How)

The locomotive boiler is governed by **three simultaneous engineering objectives**:

1. **Maximum heat transfer** in a minimum envelope (because the boiler must fit on a moving vehicle).
2. **Maximum mobility** (compact, self-contained, with forced draft from a blast pipe).
3. **Rapid steam generation** to support high transient locomotive loads (e.g., starting from rest on a gradient).

#### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Working Logic

- **Step 1 — Combustion Initiation:** Coal is fed onto the **grate** in the firebox. The refractory lining (firebrick) of the firebox walls reflects heat back into the fuel bed, sustaining combustion temperatures of approximately **1500 °C**.
- **Step 2 — Draft Generation (Blast Pipe Principle):** A fraction of the exhaust steam from the locomotive cylinders is diverted through the **blast pipe** and injected up the chimney. The high-velocity steam jet creates a **partial vacuum** (induced draft) at the base of the chimney, pulling fresh air through the firebox grate and **forcing the hot gases** through the fire-tubes.
- **Step 3 — Heat Transfer via Fire Tubes:** Hot flue gases travel from the firebox → combustion chamber → through the **fire tubes** (≈ 100–250 in number) → into the **smoke box** at the front → up the **chimney**. Heat transfers from the gas to the **water-surrounded tubes** primarily by **conduction and convection**.
- **Step 4 — Steam Accumulation:** Saturated steam, being lighter, rises and accumulates in the **steam dome** at the top of the shell. The **steam dome** is intentionally raised to keep entrained water droplets out of the steam line — only dry steam leaves the boiler.
- **Step 5 — Superheating (if equipped):** In modern locomotives, a **superheater** (a U-tube bank in the smoke box) re-heats the saturated steam to ≈ 300–350 °C, raising thermal efficiency by **≈ 15–20 %** and reducing cylinder condensation losses.
- **Step 6 — Feedwater Return:** Exhaust steam condenses in the condenser (or hot well), is treated, and is re-introduced via the **feed check valve** into the water space to maintain the water level (visible on the **water gauge glass**).

### 2.2 Constructional Anatomy — The 16 Critical Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick (Mnemonic): "S-C-F-G-A-S-W-M-S-D-B-P-R-S-F"**
> *Shell, Course, Foundation, Grate, Ash-pan, Smokebox, Water-gauge, Mud-hole, Steam-dome, Dry-pipe, Blow-off, Pressure-gauge, Regulator, Safety-valve, Fusible-plug, Superheater.*

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shell** | Outer cylindrical pressure vessel; holds water and steam. |
| 2 | **Firebox (Combustion Chamber)** | Inner refractory-lined chamber where coal is burnt. |
| 3 | **Grate** | Perforated cast-iron bars supporting the fuel bed; admits primary air. |
| 4 | **Ash Pan** | Collects ash and unburnt residue below the grate. |
| 5 | **Fire Tubes (Smoke Tubes)** | 100–250 tubes carrying hot gases from firebox to smoke box. |
| 6 | **Smoke Box** | Front cylindrical chamber collecting flue gases before they enter the chimney. |
| 7 | **Chimney** | Vertical stack exhausting flue gases; integrates the **blast pipe**. |
| 8 | **Steam Dome** | Raised hemispherical chamber where dry steam collects. |
| 9 | **Dry Pipe / Superheater Header** | Collects dry steam and routes it to the superheater (if fitted). |
| 10 | **Safety Valve** | Spring / dead-weight valve set at ≈ 1.25 × working pressure; prevents explosion. |
| 11 | **Water Gauge Glass** | Transparent column indicating the water level inside the shell. |
| 12 | **Pressure Gauge** | Bourdon-tube dial indicating internal steam pressure. |
| 13 | **Regulator Valve** | Throttle valve controlling steam flow to the engine cylinders. |
| 14 | **Feed Check Valve** | One-way valve admitting treated feedwater from the injector / pump. |
| 15 | **Blow-off Cock** | Drain valve for removing sludge and sediment during maintenance. |
| 16 | **Fusible Plug** | Lead-bismuth plug (melts at ≈ 250 °C) — last-resort safety against crown-sheet burnout. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-favourite and directly derivable in ESE (End Semester Examination) problems.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{Q_{abs}}{h_{fg}}$ | Steam generation rate from heat absorbed | kg/s |
| 2 | $Q_{abs} = \dot{m}_{f} \times GCV \times \eta_{comb}$ | Heat absorbed by water per second | kW |
| 3 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{s}(h_{g}-h_{f})}{\dot{m}_{f}\times GCV}$ | Thermal efficiency of the boiler | dimensionless (%) |
| 4 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.042 \cdot E \cdot t^{2}}{L \cdot D} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{\nu^{2}}{1+\nu^{2}}\right)$ | **Thin cylindrical shell hoop stress** (Birnie's equation) | N/mm² |
| 5 | $P_{safe} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{FOS}$ | Safe working pressure (FOS = 4–6 for boilers) | N/mm² |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2\eta_{j} \cdot E} + c$ | Shell plate thickness (Indian Boiler Reg. formula) | mm |
| 7 | $A_{grate} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}}{R_{comb}}$ | Grate area required for coal consumption | m² |
| 8 | $D_{chimney} = 0.06 \sqrt{\dot{m}_{s}}$ | Empirical chimney diameter (rule of thumb) | m |
| 9 | $\dot{m}_{air} = 14 \cdot \dot{m}_{f}$ | Stoichiometric air for coal (theoretical) | kg/s |
| 10 | $\Delta h = h_{g,sh} - h_{g,sat}$ | Superheat enthalpy gain (efficiency boost) | kJ/kg |

**Where the variables mean:**
- $E$ = efficiency of long joint (riveted) or welded efficiency
- $t$ = shell thickness
- $L$ = length between stiffener rings
- $D$ = internal shell diameter
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (≈ 0.3 for steel)
- $c$ = corrosion allowance (≈ 1–3 mm)
- $R_{comb}$ = rate of combustion (kg/m²·hr)
- $FOS$ = Factor of Safety

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Historical significance:** Powered the entire **Industrial Revolution's railway expansion** (Stephenson, 1829 — *Rocket*).
- **Modern revival:** In hybrid bio-mass locomotives, the principle of internally-fired multitubular boilers is being re-deployed for **bio-energy traction** in heritage and industrial railways.
- **Trainee design exercise:** Locomotive boiler design is the *canonical first design problem* in undergraduate thermal engineering because it integrates **fluid mechanics, heat transfer, strength of materials, and combustion** into one compact package.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Shell Plate Thickness (Indian Boiler Regulation Formula)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in any KTU 2024 ESE on Power Plant Engineering. Memorize the **starting assumption** and **each algebraic transition**.

#### Statement
*"Derive an expression for the thickness of the cylindrical shell of a locomotive boiler, accounting for the efficiency of the longitudinal (or circumferential) riveted joint."*

#### Given / Assumptions
- Internal steam pressure = $P$ (N/mm²)
- Internal shell diameter = $D$ (mm)
- Allowable tensile stress of shell material = $\sigma_t$ (N/mm²)
- Joint efficiency (longitudinal or circumferential) = $\eta_j$
- Corrosion allowance = $c$ (mm)
- Hoop stress dominates (longitudinal stress is half of hoop, hence neglected for thickness design).

#### Step 1 — Imagine the Shell Cut in Half
Consider the cylindrical shell of internal diameter $D$ and length $L$ mm, subjected to internal pressure $P$. Imagine slicing the shell longitudinally along its top and bottom — we obtain a **half cylinder** of projected area $(D \times L)$.

$$
\text{Total bursting force acting on the half-cylinder} = P \times D \times L
$$

#### Step 2 — Identify the Resisting Force
The shell resists this bursting force along its two longitudinal cut edges. The total resisting cross-sectional area is:

$$
\text{Resisting area} = 2 \times t \times L
$$

Therefore the resisting force generated by the allowable stress $\sigma_t$ is:

$$
\text{Resisting force} = 2 \times t \times L \times \sigma_t
$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Force Balance (Equilibrium)
For the boiler to not burst:

$$
P \times D \times L \;=\; 2 \times t \times L \times \sigma_t
$$

#### Step 4 — Cancel Length and Rearrange

$$
P \cdot D = 2 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_t}
$$

This is the **ideal thickness for a seamless / fully efficient joint** ($\eta_j = 1$).

#### Step 5 — Introduce the Joint Efficiency Factor
In a riveted boiler, the longitudinal joint has a strength only $\eta_j$ times that of a seamless plate. Therefore the effective resisting stress is $\eta_j \cdot \sigma_t$. Substituting:

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \eta_j \cdot \sigma_t}
$$

#### Step 6 — Add Corrosion Allowance
To compensate for internal scaling and external corrosion over the boiler's lifetime, an extra thickness $c$ is added:

$$
\boxed{\,t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \eta_j \cdot \sigma_t} + c\,}
$$

#### Step 7 — Numerical Illustration (KTU Sample)
A locomotive boiler has:
- $D = 2000$ mm
- $P = 1.5$ N/mm²
- $\sigma_t = 120$ N/mm²
- $\eta_j = 0.85$
- $c = 2$ mm

$$
t = \frac{1.5 \times 2000}{2 \times 0.85 \times 120} + 2
$$

$$
t = \frac{3000}{204} + 2
$$

$$
t = 14.71 + 2
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 16.71 \text{ mm}}
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Boiler Efficiency (Combined First & Second Law)

#### Step 1 — Energy In
$$
Q_{in} = \dot{m}_{f} \times GCV \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

#### Step 2 — Energy Utilized (in steam)
$$
Q_{util} = \dot{m}_{s} \times (h_{g} - h_{f}) \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

#### Step 3 — Efficiency Definition
$$
\eta = \frac{Q_{util}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{s}(h_{g}-h_{f})}{\dot{m}_{f} \cdot GCV}
$$

#### Step 4 — Evaporation Ratio
The **equivalent evaporation** is defined as the kg of water evaporated from and at 100 °C per kg of coal:

$$
\text{Evaporation Ratio} = \frac{\dot{m}_{s}}{\dot{m}_{f}}
$$

#### Step 5 — Substituting
$$
\eta = \left(\frac{\dot{m}_{s}}{\dot{m}_{f}}\right) \times \frac{h_{g}-h_{f}}{GCV}
$$

For a typical locomotive boiler:
- $\dot{m}_{s}/\dot{m}_{f} \approx 6$ to 8 kg of steam / kg of coal
- $h_g - h_f \approx 2257$ kJ/kg (at 1 MPa)
- $GCV \approx 25000$ kJ/kg

$$
\eta = 7 \times \frac{2257}{25000} \approx 0.632 = 63.2 \%
$$

This matches the standard **60–70 %** range quoted in textbooks.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Why Fire-tube vs Water-tube)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is a high-yield KTU comparison question (often 7 marks).

| Parameter | **Locomotive Boiler (Fire-tube)** | **Water-tube Boiler** |
|---|---|---|
| Steam generation rate | Low (≈ 6000 kg/hr) | Very high (≥ 100 T/hr) |
| Operating pressure | Low–medium (10–20 bar) | High (up to 250 bar) |
| Time to raise steam | Slow (1–2 hours) | Fast (10–15 minutes) |
| Risk of explosion | Higher (large water volume) | Lower (small water volume) |
| Mobility / portability | Excellent (mounted on wheels) | Poor (fixed installation) |
| Maintenance | Easy (simple construction) | Difficult (complex tube banks) |
| Water treatment sensitivity | Low | High (scale on tubes) |
| Typical application | Railways, marine auxiliaries | Power stations, process plants |
| Floor area | Large | Compact |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **functional flow** rather than a literal engineering drawing — Mermaid cannot render true to-scale mechanical components, but it precisely captures the **cause-effect chain** of a working locomotive boiler.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coal Feed Hopper] --> B[Grate in Firebox]
    B -- Combustion --> C[Hot Flue Gas 1500C]
    C --> D[Refractory-Lined Firebox Walls]
    D -- Reflected Heat --> C
    C -- Forced by Blast Pipe Draft --> E[Combustion Chamber]
    E --> F[Fire Tubes 100-250]
    F -- Heat Transfer via Conduction+Convection --> G[Boiler Water in Shell]
    G -- Boils into --> H[Saturated Steam]
    H -- Rises due to Lower Density --> I[Steam Dome]
    I --> J[Superheater Tubes]
    J -- Reheats to 300-350C --> K[Superheated Steam]
    K --> L[Regulator Valve]
    L --> M[Locomotive Cylinders]
    M -- Expands and Pushes Piston --> N[Driving Wheels]
    M -- Exhaust Steam Diverted --> O[Blast Pipe]
    O -- High Velocity Jet --> P[Chimney]
    P -- Induces Draft --> B
    F -- Spent Flue Gas --> Q[Smoke Box]
    Q --> P
    G -- Make-up Feedwater --> R[Feed Check Valve]
    R -- From Injector or Pump --> G
    S[Pressure Gauge] -. Monitors .-> H
    T[Water Gauge Glass] -. Monitors .-> G
    U[Safety Valve] -. Releases at 1.25 Pdesign .-> H
    V[Fusible Plug] -. Melts at 250C crown burnout .-> B
    W[Ash Pan] -. Collects Residue .-> B
```

### 4.2 Component Cross-Reference Map (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FrontEnd["FRONT END of Locomotive"]
        A1[Chimney] --> A2[Blast Pipe]
        A2 --> A3[Smoke Box]
        A3 --> A4[Superheater Header]
    end

    subgraph MiddleSection["MIDDLE SECTION - Shell"]
        B1[Fire Tubes Bank] --> B2[Boiler Shell with Water]
        B2 --> B3[Steam Dome]
        B3 --> B4[Dry Pipe]
    end

    subgraph RearEnd["REAR END - Firebox"]
        C1[Refractory Firebox] --> C2[Grate]
        C2 --> C3[Ash Pan]
        C1 --> C4[Combustion Chamber]
    end

    subgraph Safety["SAFETY & CONTROL"]
        D1[Safety Valve] -.-> B3
        D2[Pressure Gauge] -.-> B2
        D3[Water Gauge Glass] -.-> B2
        D4[Fusible Plug] -.-> C1
        D5[Feed Check Valve] -.-> B2
        D6[Regulator Valve] -.-> B3
        D7[Blow Off Cock] -.-> B2
    end

    C4 --> B1
    A4 --> D6
```

### 4.3 Working Cycle (Sequential Process Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Coal Ignition on Grate] --> S2[Step 2: Combustion Produces Hot Gases 1500C]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Hot Gases Enter Fire Tubes]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Heat Transfer to Surrounding Water]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Water Converts to Saturated Steam]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Steam Accumulates in Steam Dome]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Steam Passes Through Superheater]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Superheated Steam Reaches Regulator]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Steam Expands in Cylinders Work Output]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Exhaust Steam Creates Chimney Draft]
    S10 --> S1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any **six** distinguishing features of a Locomotive Boiler.*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. Horizontal, multitubular, internally fired, fire-tube boiler.
2. Forced draft via blast-pipe principle.
3. Mounted on a movable chassis — high portability.
4. Working pressure 10–20 bar; capacity 2000–6000 kg/hr.
5. Contains a refractory-lined firebox and a steam dome for dry steam collection.
6. Fitted with fusible plug, safety valve, water gauge, pressure gauge, and regulator.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the function of the **fusible plug** in a locomotive boiler?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The fusible plug is a safety device fitted on the **crown sheet** of the firebox (the part of the firebox roof directly above the fire). It is made of a **lead-bismuth alloy** that melts at approximately **250 °C**. If the water level in the boiler drops dangerously low and exposes the crown sheet, the temperature of the crown sheet rises rapidly. The fusible plug then melts, allowing steam and water to rush into the firebox, **extinguishing the fire and preventing crown-sheet burnout** — a common cause of catastrophic boiler explosions in steam locomotives.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEMET631]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a Locomotive Boiler**. Clearly label **any 12 parts**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A locomotive boiler has an internal diameter of **1.8 m** and a working pressure of **1.6 MPa**. The efficiency of the longitudinal joint is **0.82** and the allowable tensile stress of the shell plate is **110 N/mm²**. Taking a corrosion allowance of **2.5 mm**, determine the **required shell plate thickness**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working** (7 marks)

*Construction:* The locomotive boiler consists of a **horizontal cylindrical shell** closed at both ends. The rear end houses a **rectangular firebox** with refractory firebrick lining, a **cast-iron grate**, and an **ash pan** below. The front end houses the **smoke box** and **chimney**. Connecting the firebox to the smoke box are **100 to 250 fire tubes** (smoke tubes) of 40–75 mm diameter, packed tightly through tube sheets. A **steam dome** rises from the top of the shell. Accessories include a **safety valve**, **pressure gauge**, **water gauge glass**, **fusible plug**, **feed check valve**, **regulator**, and **blow-off cock**.

*Working:* Coal is fed onto the grate. Primary air enters from beneath the grate. Combustion produces hot gases at ≈ 1500 °C. These gases pass through the combustion chamber into the fire tubes, transferring heat to the surrounding water — generating steam. The flue gases finally escape through the chimney. The blast-pipe principle (exhaust steam jet in the chimney) creates induced draft, sustaining combustion. The steam rises into the steam dome, gets dried, and then passes (optionally through a superheater) to the regulator valve and into the locomotive cylinders, where it expands and drives the pistons. **Working pressure = 1.6 MPa, capacity = 2000–6000 kg/hr, efficiency = 60–70 %.**

*(Sketch — examiner will accept a labelled block diagram if Mermaid is used.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution** (7 marks)

*Given:*
- $D = 1800$ mm
- $P = 1.6$ N/mm² (= 1.6 MPa)
- $\eta_j = 0.82$
- $\sigma_t = 110$ N/mm²
- $c = 2.5$ mm

*Formula:*

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \eta_j \cdot \sigma_t} + c
$$

*Substitution:*

$$
t = \frac{1.6 \times 1800}{2 \times 0.82 \times 110} + 2.5
$$

$$
t = \frac{2880}{180.4} + 2.5
$$

$$
t = 15.96 + 2.5
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 18.46 \text{ mm} \;\; (\text{say } 19 \text{ mm})}
$$

*Verification:*
- Factor of safety implicit in joint efficiency ≈ 1.22 (acceptable for locomotive boiler).
- Standard plate thickness available: 20 mm. Hence select 20 mm plate.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary, PEMET631]**

**(a)** Explain the **blast-pipe principle** and its role in producing forced draft in a locomotive boiler. How does it differ from a stationary boiler chimney? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A locomotive boiler consumes **750 kg of coal per hour** with a GCV of **28000 kJ/kg**. It produces **4500 kg of steam per hour** at an average enthalpy of **2756 kJ/kg** from a feedwater enthalpy of **420 kJ/kg**. Calculate: **(i)** the **rate of heat absorbed** by the boiler, **(ii)** the **thermal efficiency** of the boiler, and **(iii)** the **equivalent evaporation** per kg of coal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Blast-Pipe Principle** (7 marks)

In a stationary boiler, natural draft is generated by the buoyancy difference of hot flue gases vs. ambient air — the chimney acts as a passive stack. In contrast, a locomotive boiler cannot rely on natural draft because:

1. It moves at high velocity (so natural draft is insufficient and unstable).
2. Its short chimney has limited height.

**The blast-pipe principle works as follows:**
- A portion of the **exhaust steam from the locomotive cylinders** is diverted, at high velocity, through a tapered **blast pipe** whose outlet is located at the base of the chimney.
- The high-velocity steam jet entrains the surrounding flue gases in the smoke box, **pulling them upward into the chimney** (the *ejector* or *jet-pump* effect).
- This creates a **partial vacuum (low pressure) in the smoke box**, which in turn draws fresh air through the firebox grate.
- The pressure drop across the grate (≈ 25–35 mm of water column) sustains forced draft and hence continuous combustion.

**Advantage over natural draft:**
- Independent of locomotive speed (works even when stationary).
- Strong and stable draft with a short chimney.
- Power is "free" — drawn from exhaust steam that has already done work in the cylinders.

**Disadvantage:**
- Small loss of exhaust-steel kinetic energy to the jet.
- Cannot be used in non-reciprocating systems (e.g., turbines, marine boilers) — hence marine boilers use **induced-draft fans** instead.

**(b) Numerical Solution** (7 marks)

*Given:*
- $\dot{m}_f = 750$ kg/hr
- $GCV = 28000$ kJ/kg
- $\dot{m}_s = 4500$ kg/hr
- $h_g = 2756$ kJ/kg
- $h_f = 420$ kJ/kg

**(i) Rate of heat absorbed:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{abs} = \dot{m}_s \times (h_g - h_f)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{abs} = 4500 \times (2756 - 420) = 4500 \times 2336
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_{abs} = 1.0512 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/hr} \approx 10512 \text{ MJ/hr} \approx 2920 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(ii) Thermal efficiency:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times GCV = 750 \times 28000 = 2.1 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/hr}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{\dot{Q}_{abs}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{1.0512 \times 10^7}{2.1 \times 10^7}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta \approx 0.5006 = 50.06\%}
$$

*(Note: efficiency is below the 60–70 % range, suggesting wet coal or significant heat losses — examiner expects students to **comment on this**.)*

**(iii) Equivalent evaporation:**
"From and at 100 °C" latent heat $= 2257$ kJ/kg.

$$
\text{Evaporation ratio} = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{4500}{750} = 6.0 \; \text{kg steam / kg coal}
$$

Equivalent evaporation (steam converted to 100 °C basis):

$$
E_{eq} = \frac{\dot{m}_s \times (h_g - h_f)}{2257} \times \frac{1}{\dot{m}_f}
$$

$$
E_{eq} = \frac{4500 \times 2336}{750 \times 2257}
$$

$$
E_{eq} = \frac{1.0512 \times 10^7}{1.69275 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{eq} \approx 6.21 \text{ kg of water (from \& at 100 °C) per kg of coal}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks every semester:**
> 1. **Forgetting corrosion allowance $c$** in shell thickness derivation — KTU examiners explicitly deduct **1 mark** for this omission. Always add $c = 1–3$ mm.
> 2. **Mixing up hoop and longitudinal stress** — hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress. For shell thickness design, you must use the **hoop stress** formula.
> 3. **Converting pressure units incorrectly** — 1 MPa = 1 N/mm². Many students write $1.6$ MPa and substitute as $1.6 \times 10^6$ N/mm², which is wrong.
> 4. **Ignoring joint efficiency** — A common trap: tutors give you a "riveted boiler" but expect you to apply $\eta_j$ even if you assume welded construction. **Always read the question carefully** for the keyword "riveted" vs "welded."
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU valuation strictly deducts for unit-less final answers.
> 6. **Confusing "steam dome" with "smoke box"** — these are entirely different components at opposite ends of the boiler.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin this on your wall before the exam.**

- **Definition:** Horizontal, multitubular, internally fired, fire-tube, **mobile** boiler.
- **Working Pressure:** **10–20 bar (1–2 MPa)**.
- **Capacity:** **2000–6000 kg of steam / hour**.
- **Efficiency:** **60–70 %** (can be 80 % with superheater).
- **Tube count:** **100–250 fire tubes**, diameter **40–75 mm**.
- **Key Components (16 total):** Shell, Firebox, Grate, Ash-pan, Fire-tubes, Smoke-box, Chimney, Steam-dome, Dry-pipe, Safety-valve, Water-gauge, Pressure-gauge, Regulator, Feed-check-valve, Blow-off-cock, Fusible-plug.
- **Blast-pipe principle:** Exhaust-steam jet creates **forced induced draft**; essential because the locomotive moves (natural draft is insufficient).
- **Fusible plug material:** **Lead-bismuth alloy** melting at **≈ 250 °C**, fitted on the firebox crown sheet.
- **Steam dome purpose:** Collects **dry steam**, separates entrained water droplets.
- **Thin-shell thickness formula (must memorize):**
  $$t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \eta_j \cdot \sigma_t} + c$$
- **Birnie's equation (for thick shells, longitudinal stress considered):**
  $$P_{cr} = \frac{2.042 \cdot E \cdot t^2}{L \cdot D} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\nu^2}{1+\nu^2}\right)$$
- **Boiler efficiency formula:**
  $$\eta = \frac{\dot{m}_s (h_g - h_f)}{\dot{m}_f \cdot GCV}$$
- **Equivalent evaporation:** Steam per kg of coal, normalized to "from and at 100 °C" enthalpy of **2257 kJ/kg**.
- **FOS for boiler:** **4–6** (much higher than general pressure vessels because of catastrophic failure risk).
- **Draft pressure:** **25–35 mm of water column** for a typical locomotive boiler.
- **Steam dome height above shell crown:** Typically **300–450 mm** — sufficient to disengage water from steam.
- **Superheater location:** In the **smoke box** (high-temperature flue gas region).
- **Compare-fire vs water-tube boiler** — high-yield 7-mark question every KTU cycle.
- **Application:** **Railway traction** (modern: heritage steam locomotives; historic: 19th–20th century mainline railways).
- **Inventor / Pioneer:** **George Stephenson** (Rocket, 1829) — the canonical reference in Indian engineering textbooks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Babcock and Wilcox Boiler, Stirling Boiler, High Pressure Boilers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Babcock and Wilcox (B&W) Boiler

### Formal KTU Definition
The **Babcock and Wilcox (B&W) Boiler** is a **horizontal, stationary, externally fired, water-tube boiler** with a **bent-tube design**, consisting of a steam drum (upper) and a water drum (lower) connected by numerous bent water tubes. It was developed by George Herman Babcock and Stephen Wilcox in 1867. The bent tubes accommodate thermal expansion and contraction, allowing safe operation at higher pressures (**up to 17.5 bar**) and capacities (**up to 25,000 kg/hr**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The B&W boiler is the *gateway topic* for understanding modern water-tube boilers. Every subsequent high-pressure boiler concept (La-Mont, Benson, Loeffler) is built on this foundational understanding.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **vertical sandwich toaster**: heat enters from below (the furnace), but instead of a flat plate holding the bread, you have a series of curved metal pipes filled with water that zigzag through the hot combustion gases. As the gases rise, they "tickle" the bent tubes, boiling the water inside. The curved shape is critical — just like how a flexible drinking straw can bend without breaking, the curved tubes can expand when hot without cracking.

### Key Constructional Features
- **Steam Drum (Upper):** Collects saturated steam; fitted with internal moisture separators and feed check valves.
- **Mud Drum (Lower):** Collects sediments and impurities; fitted with a blow-down valve.
- **Bent Water Tubes:** Connect the two drums; the bent geometry provides **flexibility for thermal expansion**.
- **Headers:** Upper and lower headers distribute water and collect steam.
- **Superheater:** Located in the path of hot gases; raises steam temperature above saturation.
- **Economizer & Air Preheater:** Recuperative heat recovery components (in modern variants).
- **Safety valves, pressure gauges, water level indicators** for safe operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Metrics (Bold):**
> - Working Pressure: **up to 17.5 bar**
> - Steam Capacity: **up to 25,000 kg/hr**
> - Efficiency: **75–85%** (without ancillaries); **88–92%** (with economizer + air preheater)

---

## 1.2 Stirling Boiler

### Formal KTU Definition
The **Stirling Boiler** is a **horizontal, stationary, externally fired, three-drum, bent-tube water-tube boiler** developed by the Stirling Boiler Company. It uses a unique **bent-tube bank** between a large upper steam drum and two lower water drums (one on each side), making it ideally suited for **medium-pressure applications** with capacities ranging from **5,000 to 50,000 kg/hr**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture a **three-level kitchen shelf system**: the top shelf holds freshly boiled water vapor (steam drum), and the two bottom shelves (left and right water drums) hold the reserve water supply. Curved pipes connecting all three shelves act like a heated highway — water gets pumped up, steam rises to the top, and the cycle repeats. The three-drum arrangement gives the boiler remarkable stability and even heat distribution.

### Key Constructional Features
- **Upper Steam Drum (Large):** Houses steam output and feed inlet.
- **Two Lower Water Drums (Symmetric):** Ensure uniform water circulation.
- **Bent Tube Bank:** Connects the drums; the curving eliminates the need for thick tube walls.
- **Superheater Tubes:** Form part of the tube bank itself; hot gases superheat steam inside these tubes.
- **Chain Grate Stoker:** Common fuel feeding mechanism in Stirling boilers.
- **Furnace:** Refractory-lined chamber below the tube bank.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Metrics (Bold):**
> - Working Pressure: **up to 30 bar** (in some high-pressure variants)
> - Steam Capacity: **5,000 – 50,000 kg/hr**
> - Tubes Diameter: **75–100 mm**
> - Efficiency: **80–88%**

---

## 1.3 High Pressure Boilers — An Overview

### Formal KTU Definition
**High-Pressure Boilers** are advanced water-tube boilers designed to operate at pressures **above 100 bar** (and temperatures **above 500°C**), employing **forced circulation**, **once-through principles**, or **pressurized combustion** to achieve high thermal efficiency (**up to 90–92%**) and large specific capacities. They are essential in **central power stations** where every fraction of a percentage in efficiency translates to thousands of tonnes of coal saved annually.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of **high-pressure boilers as the Formula 1 cars of the steam world**. Just as F1 cars use every engineering trick — turbocharging, lightweight materials, optimized fuel flow — to squeeze maximum performance from every drop of fuel, high-pressure boilers use forced water circulation (La-Mont), no-drum design (Benson), or supercritical fluid behavior (modern units) to push efficiency to its physical limits.

### Classification of High-Pressure Boilers (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Major Types Studied in KTU Module 2:**
> 1. **La-Mont Boiler** — Forced circulation, separate steam and water drums.
> 2. **Benson Boiler** — Once-through, **no steam drum** at all.
> 3. **Loeffler Boiler** — Uses latent heat of vaporization for feedwater heating.
> 4. **Velox Boiler** — Pressurized furnace with gas turbine exhaust augmentation.
> 5. **Schmidt-Hartmann Boiler** — High-pressure, twin-drum design.

### Why High Pressure? — The Thermodynamic Driver
As boiler pressure rises, the **enthalpy of evaporation decreases** sharply, the **mean temperature of heat addition increases**, and consequently the **Rankine cycle efficiency** improves:

$$\eta_{Rankine} = 1 - \frac{T_{condenser}}{T_{boiler}}$$

Higher boiler temperature → higher $T_{boiler}$ → higher efficiency.

| Boiler Type | Pressure Range | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| Babcock & Wilcox | up to 17.5 bar | Bent tubes for thermal expansion |
| Stirling | up to 30 bar | Three-drum bent-tube stability |
| La-Mont | 100–170 bar | Forced circulation pump |
| Benson | up to 250 bar (supercritical) | Once-through, no drum |
| Loeffler | 100–140 bar | Latent-heat feed heating |
| Velox | up to 30 bar (pressurized) | Gas turbine exhaust coupling |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Babcock and Wilcox Boiler — Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

The B&W boiler follows a **natural circulation** cycle driven by density difference between hot water (in tubes) and cooler water (in drums).

**Step-by-step Operation:**

1. **Feedwater Entry:** Treated feedwater enters the **steam drum** through the feed check valve (typically pre-heated by an economizer).
2. **Downcomer Flow:** Cold, dense water descends from the steam drum through downcomer pipes to the **mud drum** / lower headers.
3. **Distribution:** Water rises into the **bent water tubes** connected to upper and lower headers.
4. **Heat Absorption:** Hot combustion gases from the furnace envelop the bent tubes; water absorbs heat and forms a **steam-water mixture** (the water-steam density differential drives natural circulation).
5. **Steam Separation:** The steam-water mixture rises back into the steam drum, where saturated steam separates from water via **internal cyclone separators** and **dry pipes**.
6. **Superheating (if fitted):** Saturated steam passes through the **superheater** tubes (placed in the hot gas path), becoming superheated.
7. **Combustion & Flue Gas:** Gases pass over superheater → economizer → air preheater → chimney.

> [!NOTE]
> **Natural Circulation Driving Head:**
> The circulation occurs because the density of the **steam-water mixture inside the tubes** ($\rho_{mix}$) is **less** than the density of the **saturated water in the downcomers** ($\rho_w$). This creates a hydrostatic pressure differential.

$$\Delta P_{driving} = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{mix})$$

where $H$ is the height between the steam drum centerline and the tube bank.

### Advantages of B&W Boiler
- **Higher pressure** capability than fire-tube boilers (up to 17.5 bar).
- **Larger capacity** (up to 25,000 kg/hr).
- **Bent tubes** accommodate thermal stress — reduces risk of tube failure.
- **Higher efficiency** due to better heat transfer area density.
- **Shorter downtime** — a single defective tube can be plugged or replaced without shutting down the entire boiler.

### Disadvantages of B&W Boiler
- **Higher initial cost** than simple fire-tube boilers.
- **Requires skilled operation** and water treatment.
- **Susceptible to scale formation** in tubes — water quality critical.

---

## 2.2 Stirling Boiler — Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

The Stirling boiler's three-drum design creates a **robust natural circulation loop** with superior steam-purity characteristics.

**Step-by-step Operation:**

1. **Feedwater Admission:** Feedwater enters the upper steam drum (it carries the feed valve and internal distribution pipe).
2. **Distribution to Lower Drums:** Water flows down through **downcomers** to the **two lower water drums**.
3. **Rise Through Bent Tubes:** Water is forced upward through the **bent tube bank** by natural convection.
4. **Steam Generation in Tubes:** The bent tubes act as the **evaporating surface** — water partially flashes to steam.
5. **Superheating:** Some bent tubes are dedicated to **superheating** — saturated steam from the upper drum is routed back through these special tubes, gaining temperature from the hot gases.
6. **Steam Collection:** Dry steam collects in the upper drum and exits to the steam main.
7. **Mud Collection:** Sediments settle in the lower drums and are periodically blown down.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Three Drums?**
> The two lower water drums provide **redundant water capacity**, **symmetrical heating**, and **balanced expansion** under varying load conditions. This makes the Stirling boiler exceptionally **stable under fluctuating steam demand** — a critical feature for industrial plants.

---

## 2.3 High Pressure Boilers — Comparative Analysis

### A. La-Mont Boiler

**Operating Principle:** A **circulation pump** forces water through the evaporator tubes at high velocity (**3–4 m/s**), ensuring positive circulation even at very high pressures where natural circulation fails.

**Key Components:**
- **Evaporator (Steam Generator):** Vertical tubes heated by combustion gases.
- **Circulation Pump:** Provides forced circulation of water through tubes.
- **Steam Separator / Drum:** Separates steam from water; much smaller than natural-circulation boilers.
- **Superheater, Economizer, Air Preheater.**

**Working Cycle:**
1. Feedwater (pre-heated in economizer) enters the **steam drum / separator**.
2. The **circulation pump** drives water through the evaporator tubes at high velocity.
3. Partial flashing occurs inside the evaporator tubes.
4. The steam-water mixture enters the separator; saturated steam goes to the superheater.
5. Saturated water recirculates back via the pump.

**Advantages:**
- Compact design for high capacity.
- High heat transfer coefficient due to forced circulation.
- Can use low-grade water (less scale sensitivity due to high velocity).

### B. Benson Boiler

**Operating Principle:** A **once-through boiler** — water enters, becomes steam, and exits in a **single pass** through progressively heated tubes. **No steam drum is required** because the conversion to steam is completed before the water exits the heating surface.

**Working Cycle:**
1. Feedwater enters the **economizer** at high pressure.
2. Passes through the **evaporator section** (radiant and convective zones) where it is heated to saturation and then completely vaporized.
3. The steam then passes through the **superheater** for further temperature rise.
4. Critical Point: At the boiler's working pressure (≥ **225.62 bar**, the critical pressure of water), there is **no distinction** between liquid and vapor phases — the fluid passes smoothly through the critical state.

**Advantages:**
- **No drum** → lighter, cheaper, no drum-level control issue.
- **Quick start-up** (no thermal inertia of a large water mass).
- **Suitable for supercritical pressures.**
- **Lower weight** per unit steam output.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Critical water treatment** required — any impurity concentrates and deposits on tubes.
- **Difficult load following** — narrow operating window near critical point.
- **Higher feed pump power** required.

### C. Loeffler Boiler

**Operating Principle:** Uses a unique **forced circulation loop** where a portion of the steam generated in the evaporator is bled off and mixed with feedwater in a **condenser-mixer**, transferring its latent heat to the incoming feed.

**Working Cycle:**
1. Feedwater enters the **economizer** and is pre-heated.
2. Pre-heated water enters the **condenser-mixer**, where it mixes with bled steam from the evaporator.
3. The mixture is then pumped by the **circulation pump** into the **evaporator** at very high pressure (~**140 bar**).
4. About **75%** of the water evaporates in the evaporator tubes (heated by hot gases).
5. The **saturated steam is divided:**
   - **~25%** goes to the superheater → delivered as useful steam output.
   - **~75%** is bled to the condenser-mixer → heats feedwater (recovers latent heat).

**Advantages:**
- **High efficiency** due to efficient feed heating via latent heat recovery.
- **No feedwater heater required separately** — built into the cycle.
- Compact and suitable for marine applications.

**Disadvantages:**
- Complex control system.
- Requires high-pressure circulation pump.

### D. Velox Boiler

**Operating Principle:** Operates with **pressurized combustion** (furnace pressure > atmospheric) — the combustion gas is routed through a **gas turbine** before exiting to the chimney. The gas turbine's exhaust is then used to pre-heat combustion air, achieving **very high heat release rates**.

**Working Cycle:**
1. Air is pressurized by the **gas turbine compressor**.
2. Combustion occurs at high pressure in the **water-tube boiler**.
3. High-pressure, high-temperature flue gases drive the **gas turbine** (which drives the compressor and an electric generator).
4. Turbine exhaust preheats incoming air via a **recuperator**.

**Advantages:**
- Extremely **compact** (10× smaller than atmospheric boilers of same capacity).
- Very high heat release rate.
- High overall thermal efficiency when coupled with gas turbine.

**Disadvantages:**
- Complicated sealing due to pressurized furnace.
- High capital cost.
- Limited capacity.

### E. Schmidt-Hartmann Boiler

**Operating Principle:** A **high-pressure, twin-drum** boiler with bent tubes connecting an upper and lower drum; the upper drum contains a built-in **superheater header**. Operates at pressures **up to 100 bar** with good response to load changes.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Natural Circulation Driving Head | $\Delta P = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{mix})$ | $H$ = height [m]; $g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$; densities in $kg/m^3$ |
| 2 | Rankine Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{Rankine} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cond}}{T_{boil}}$ | $T$ in Kelvin |
| 3 | Equivalent Evaporation | $m_e = m_s \cdot \dfrac{H - h_{f1}}{h_g - h_{f2}}$ | $H$ = enthalpy of 1 kg steam at 100°C from feed at 0°C (= **2257 kJ/kg**) |
| 4 | Factor of Evaporation | $F.E. = \dfrac{H - h_{f1}}{2257}$ | Dimensionless; typically **1.1 – 1.4** |
| 5 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct Method) | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_s (H - h_{f1})}{m_f \cdot GCV}$ | Mass basis; dimensionless |
| 6 | Boiler Efficiency (Heat Loss Method) | $\eta = 100 - (\% \text{ loss due to dry flue gas} + \% \text{ H}_2\text{O} + \text{radiation} + \text{CO})$ | Percentage sum |
| 7 | Heat Transfer in Boiler | $Q = m_s \cdot (H_{sup} - h_{fw})$ | $Q$ in kJ/hr |
| 8 | Steam Specific Volume (saturated) | $v_g = 0.4615 \cdot T / P$ for $T, P$ near saturation (approx) | $v_g$ in $m^3/kg$ |
| 9 | La-Mont Circulation Velocity | $v_{circ} = 3 \text{ to } 4 \ m/s$ | Forced circulation range |
| 10 | Critical Pressure of Water | $P_{cr} = 22.064 \ MPa = 220.64 \ bar$ | Above this, no phase distinction |
| 11 | Critical Temperature of Water | $T_{cr} = 374.15°C = 647.3 \ K$ | Highest $T$ at which liquid phase exists |
| 12 | Benson Boiler Operating Pressure | $P_{Benson} \geq P_{cr}$ (supercritical mode possible) | For subcritical Benson: 170–190 bar |
| 13 | Loeffler Steam Split Ratio | Steam to superheater / Total steam = **25% / 75%** | Typical operating ratio |
| 14 | Chimney (Stack) Height (KTU recommended) | $H = 9.6 \cdot Q^{0.5}$ (with site correction) | $Q$ = $SO_2$ emission rate |
| 15 | Air-Fuel Ratio (stoichiometric) | $A/F = \dfrac{C}{0.115} + \dfrac{S}{0.04} - \dfrac{O}{0.455}$ (mass basis, for coal) | Approximate empirical formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For KTU numerical problems, the most-tested formulas are **Equivalent Evaporation (#3)**, **Boiler Efficiency by Heat Loss Method (#6)**, and **Natural Circulation Head (#1)**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Boiler Type | Real-World Deployment | Application Sector |
|---|---|---|
| B&W Boiler | Legacy industrial plants, sugar mills, paper mills | Process steam |
| Stirling Boiler | Old textile, chemical, and paper industries | Process steam |
| La-Mont Boiler | Large thermal power stations (1950s–80s); some marine boilers | Central power, naval |
| Benson Boiler | **Modern supercritical & ultra-supercritical power plants** (≥ 600 MW) | Utility power |
| Loeffler Boiler | **Marine applications** (high-pressure naval propulsion) | Naval, marine |
| Velox Boiler | Compact peak-load plants; submarines | Defense, peak-load |
| Schmidt-Hartmann | European industrial plants; some power stations | Industrial & utility |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Evaporation (KTU Most-Tested Formula)

**Statement:** The mass of water that would be evaporated from a feedwater temperature of **100°C** to dry saturated steam at **100°C** (atmospheric) is called *equivalent evaporation*.

### Given / Assumed Conditions
- Actual steam generated: $m_s$ kg/hr at enthalpy $H$ (kJ/kg).
- Feedwater enters at temperature $T_{fw}$ with enthalpy $h_{f1}$.
- The reference equivalent evaporation uses water at 100°C → steam at 100°C.
- The latent heat at atmospheric pressure = **2257 kJ/kg**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Heat supplied per kg of actual steam in the boiler:

$$q_{actual} = H - h_{f1} \quad [\text{kJ/kg steam}]$$

where:
- $H$ = enthalpy of steam leaving the boiler (kJ/kg)
- $h_{f1}$ = enthalpy of feedwater at its entry temperature (kJ/kg)

**Step 2:** For equivalent evaporation, the same heat would convert water at 100°C into dry saturated steam at 100°C. The heat needed per kg:

$$q_{equivalent} = h_{g,\ 100°C} - h_{f,\ 100°C} = 2676 - 419 = 2257 \ kJ/kg$$

(The latent heat of vaporization at atmospheric pressure.)

**Step 3:** By the **principle of heat balance**, the total heat absorbed by the steam in the boiler equals the total heat that would evaporate the equivalent mass:

$$m_s \cdot (H - h_{f1}) = m_e \cdot 2257$$

**Step 4:** Solving for equivalent evaporation $m_e$:

$$m_e = m_s \cdot \frac{H - h_{f1}}{2257} \quad [\text{kg/hr}]$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for equivalent evaporation.

### Step 5 — Definition of Factor of Evaporation (F.E.)

The Factor of Evaporation expresses the ratio of actual heat absorbed per kg of steam to the latent heat at atmospheric conditions:

$$F.E. = \frac{H - h_{f1}}{2257}$$

Therefore:

$$m_e = m_s \cdot F.E.$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** A Factor of Evaporation of 1.2 means each kg of steam produced in this boiler does 1.2 times the work of an equivalent kg of "atmospheric" steam — typical for superheated steam at moderate pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Direct Method Boiler Efficiency

**Statement:** Boiler efficiency by the **direct (input-output) method** is the ratio of useful heat output (absorbed by steam) to heat input (from fuel).

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Heat output from boiler (per hour):

$$Q_{out} = m_s (H - h_{f1}) + m_{blown} (h_{f,\ sat} - h_{fw}) \ [\text{kJ/hr}]$$

For simplification (ignoring blowdown loss):

$$Q_{out} \approx m_s (H - h_{f1})$$

**Step 2:** Heat input from fuel combustion:

$$Q_{in} = m_f \cdot GCV \ [\text{kJ/hr}]$$

where:
- $m_f$ = mass of fuel burned per hour (kg/hr)
- $GCV$ = Gross Calorific Value of fuel (kJ/kg)

**Step 3:** Boiler efficiency:

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{m_s (H - h_{f1})}{m_f \cdot GCV}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{boiler} = \frac{m_s (H - h_{f1})}{m_f \cdot GCV}}$$

### Boundary Conditions / Validity
- Steady-state operation assumed.
- Blowdown losses neglected (or accounted for separately).
- $m_s$ measured at steam outlet; $m_f$ measured at the fuel feeder.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Loss Method (Indirect Method)

This is the alternate way to compute boiler efficiency without measuring steam output — used in KTU numerical problems when only flue gas analysis is given.

**Step 1:** Heat loss due to **dry flue gas** (sensible heat in exhaust gases):

$$L_{dry} = \frac{m_g \cdot c_{pg} \cdot (T_g - T_{air})}{GCV} \times 100 \ [\%]$$

where:
- $m_g$ = mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel (kg/kg fuel)
- $c_{pg}$ = specific heat of dry flue gas ≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- $T_g$ = flue gas temperature at chimney exit (°C or K)
- $T_{air}$ = ambient combustion air temperature

**Step 2:** Heat loss due to **moisture in fuel + moisture formed from H₂ combustion**:

$$L_{moisture} = \frac{m_{steam} \cdot [h_{fg} + c_{pv} (T_g - 100)]}{GCV} \times 100$$

where $h_{fg}$ at 100°C = **2257 kJ/kg** and $c_{pv}$ = steam specific heat ≈ **2.1 kJ/kg·K**.

**Step 3:** Heat loss due to **incomplete combustion** (CO formation):

$$L_{CO} = \frac{m_{CO} \cdot 5670}{GCV} \times 100$$

(The calorific value of CO = **5670 kJ/kg**.)

**Step 4:** Heat loss due to **radiation + convection** from boiler shell (typical value: 1–2%):

$$L_{rad} \approx 1.5\%$$

**Step 5:** Total efficiency:

$$\eta_{boiler} = 100 - (L_{dry} + L_{moisture} + L_{CO} + L_{rad})$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (Worked — KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A Babcock and Wilcox boiler generates **15,000 kg/hr** of steam at a pressure of **15 bar** and a temperature of **250°C** (superheated). The feedwater enters at **80°C**. Coal is burned at a rate of **1,800 kg/hr** with a GCV of **30,000 kJ/kg**. Compute:
(a) Equivalent evaporation
(b) Factor of evaporation
(c) Boiler efficiency by direct method

**Given Data:**
- $m_s = 15{,}000 \ kg/hr$
- $P = 15 \ bar$
- $T_{sup} = 250°C$ → from steam tables: $H \approx 2924 \ kJ/kg$
- $T_{fw} = 80°C$ → $h_{f1} \approx 335 \ kJ/kg$
- $m_f = 1800 \ kg/hr$
- $GCV = 30{,}000 \ kJ/kg$

### Part (a): Equivalent Evaporation

**Step 1:** Heat per kg of actual steam:

$$H - h_{f1} = 2924 - 335 = 2589 \ kJ/kg$$

**Step 2:** Apply formula:

$$m_e = m_s \cdot \frac{H - h_{f1}}{2257} = 15000 \cdot \frac{2589}{2257}$$

$$m_e = 15000 \times 1.1471 = 17{,}207 \ kg/hr$$

$$\boxed{m_e \approx 17{,}207 \ kg/hr}$$

### Part (b): Factor of Evaporation

$$F.E. = \frac{H - h_{f1}}{2257} = \frac{2589}{2257} = 1.147$$

### Part (c): Boiler Efficiency

**Step 1:** Heat output:

$$Q_{out} = m_s (H - h_{f1}) = 15000 \times 2589 = 3.884 \times 10^{7} \ kJ/hr$$

**Step 2:** Heat input:

$$Q_{in} = m_f \cdot GCV = 1800 \times 30000 = 5.4 \times 10^{7} \ kJ/hr$$

**Step 3:** Efficiency:

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{3.884 \times 10^{7}}{5.4 \times 10^{7}} = 0.7193 = 71.93\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{boiler} \approx 72\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** A B&W boiler without an economizer typically operates at 70–75% efficiency — our result of 72% lies exactly in this band, confirming the calculation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — High Pressure Boilers

| Feature | La-Mont | Benson | Loeffler | Velox | Schmidt-Hartmann |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure (bar)** | 100–170 | 170–250 (supercritical) | 100–140 | 20–30 | up to 100 |
| **Circulation** | Forced | Once-through | Forced (recirculation) | Forced | Natural |
| **Steam Drum** | Yes (small) | **No** | Yes | Yes | Yes (two drums) |
| **Start-up Time** | Medium | **Very fast** | Medium | Fast | Medium |
| **Water Quality Requirement** | Moderate | **Very high** | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Capacity Range** | High | Very high | High | Medium | Medium-high |
| **Capital Cost** | High | High | High | Very high | Medium |
| **Efficiency (%)** | 88–90 | 90–92 | 88–90 | 89–91 | 86–89 |
| **Load-following** | Good | Moderate | Good | Excellent | Good |
| **Typical Use** | Power | Modern power | Marine | Submarines | Industrial |
