# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Babcock and Wilcox Boiler — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    feedNode[Feedwater Inlet<br/>Cold Water 80C] --> drumUpperNode
    drumUpperNode[Upper Steam Drum<br/>Moisture Separation<br/>Steam Accumulation]
    drumUpperNode --> downNode
    downNode[Downcomers<br/>Cold Water Descent]
    downNode --> drumLowerNode
    drumLowerNode[Lower Water Drum<br/>Mud Collection<br/>Bottom Header]
    drumLowerNode --> tubeNode
    tubeNode[Bent Water Tubes<br/>Evaporation Zone<br/>Natural Circulation]
    tubeNode --> furnaceNode
    furnaceNode[Furnace<br/>Coal Combustion<br/>Hot Gases 1200C]
    furnaceNode --> superheatNode
    superheatNode[Superheater<br/>Steam Temp Rise to 300C]
    superheatNode --> econNode
    econNode[Economizer<br/>Feedwater Preheating]
    econNode --> airPreNode
    airPreNode[Air Preheater<br/>Combustion Air Heating]
    airPreNode --> chimNode
    chimNode[Chimney<br/>Flue Gas Exit]
    drumUpperNode --> superheatNode
    superheatNode --> steamOut
    steamOut[Steam Main<br/>To Turbine]
    drumLowerNode --> blowNode
    blowNode[Blowdown Valve<br/>Mud and Sediment Removal]
```

## 4.2 Stirling Boiler — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedS[Feedwater Inlet] --> upperS[Upper Steam Drum<br/>Largest Drum]
    upperS --> lowerS1
    upperS --> lowerS2
    lowerS1[Lower Water Drum 1<br/>Left Side]
    lowerS2[Lower Water Drum 2<br/>Right Side]
    lowerS1 --> tubeBank
    lowerS2 --> tubeBank
    tubeBank[Bent Tube Bank<br/>Evaporator and Superheater Tubes]
    tubeBank --> furnaceS[Furnace<br/>Chain Grate Stoker<br/>Coal Combustion]
    furnaceS --> superS[Superheater Section<br/>Inside Tube Bank]
    superS --> upperS
    upperS --> steamOutS[Steam to Main]
    lowerS1 --> blow1[Blowdown]
    lowerS2 --> blow2[Blowdown]
```

## 4.3 La-Mont Boiler — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    feedL[Feedwater] --> econL[Economizer<br/>Feedwater Preheated]
    econL --> drumL[Steam Drum<br/>Separator]
    drumL --> pumpL[Circulation Pump<br/>Forced Circulation]
    pumpL --> evapL[Evaporator Tubes<br/>High Velocity 3 to 4 m per s]
    evapL --> furnaceL[Furnace<br/>Hot Combustion Gases]
    furnaceL --> superL[Superheater<br/>Steam Temperature Rise]
    furnaceL --> econL
    superL --> drumL
    drumL --> steamOutL[Superheated Steam Output]
    drumL --> pumpL
```

## 4.4 Benson Boiler — Once-Through Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedB[Feedwater at High Pressure] --> econB[Economizer]
    econB --> evapB[Evaporator Section<br/>Water to Steam Conversion<br/>No Drum]
    evapB --> superB[Superheater<br/>Steam to 600C]
    superB --> turbineB[Turbine Inlet<br/>Critical or Supercritical State]
    furnaceB[Pulverized Coal Furnace] --> evapB
    furnaceB --> superB
    furnaceB --> econB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visualization Insight:** In the **Benson boiler**, observe the **complete absence of a steam drum**. The water enters, transitions to steam, and superheats — all in a **single pass**. This is its defining feature.

## 4.5 Loeffler Boiler — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    feedLf[Feedwater] --> econLf[Economizer]
    econLf --> condMix[Condenser Mixer<br/>Receives Steam from Bleed]
    condMix --> pumpLf[Circulation Pump]
    pumpLf --> evapLf[Evaporator<br/>Very High Pressure 140 bar]
    evapLf --> furnaceLf[Furnace]
    furnaceLf --> superLf[Superheater]
    superLf --> steamOutLf[Steam Output<br/>25 percent of generated]
    evapLf --> condMix
    condMix -- 75 percent bled steam --> evapLf
```

```mermaid
    bw --> lm
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a Babcock and Wilcox boiler.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Babcock and Wilcox (B&W) boiler is a **horizontal, externally fired, water-tube boiler** with bent water tubes connecting a **steam drum** (upper) and a **mud drum** (lower).

**Construction:**
- **Upper Steam Drum:** Collects saturated steam; fitted with moisture separators and feed check valve.
- **Lower Water/Mud Drum:** Collects feedwater and settled sediments; fitted with blowdown valve.
- **Bent Water Tubes:** Numerous bent tubes connect upper and lower headers, providing flexibility for thermal expansion.
- **Headers:** Distribute water and collect steam.
- **Superheater, Economizer, Air Preheater** (in modern versions).

**Working:**
1. Feedwater enters the steam drum → descends via downcomers to the lower drum.
2. Water rises through bent tubes, absorbing heat from hot combustion gases (natural circulation).
3. The steam-water mixture returns to the steam drum where steam separates.
4. Saturated steam passes through the superheater before exiting to the steam main.

**Key Features:** Pressure up to **17.5 bar**, capacity up to **25,000 kg/hr**, efficiency **75–85%**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Compare Babcock & Wilcox boiler and Stirling boiler on the basis of drum arrangement and capacity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Babcock & Wilcox Boiler | Stirling Boiler |
|---|---|---|
| **No. of Drums** | Two (one upper steam drum, one lower mud drum) | Three (one upper steam drum, two lower water drums) |
| **Tube Arrangement** | Bent water tubes between two drums | Bent tube bank between upper and two lower drums |
| **Capacity** | Up to 25,000 kg/hr | 5,000 – 50,000 kg/hr |
| **Pressure** | Up to 17.5 bar | Up to 30 bar |
| **Superheater Location** | Separate superheater in gas path | Built into the tube bank itself |
| **Stability** | Moderate | Very stable (symmetrical water supply) |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model — 14 Marks]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a Stirling boiler. State its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Constructional Details:**
1. **Upper Steam Drum:** A large horizontal cylindrical drum at the top; receives feedwater through an internal distribution pipe and delivers steam through an outlet.
2. **Two Lower Water Drums:** Symmetrically placed below the steam drum, holding reserve feedwater and collecting sediment.
3. **Bent Tube Bank:** A series of bent tubes connecting the upper drum to the two lower drums; some tubes serve as evaporators, others as superheaters.
4. **Furnace:** Refractory-lined chamber below the tube bank; typically uses a chain grate stoker.
5. **Smoke Boxes:** At both ends, collecting flue gases before they exit to the chimney.
6. **Accessories:** Safety valves, pressure gauges, water columns, blowdown valves.

**Working Principle:**
1. Feedwater enters the upper steam drum.
2. Water descends through connecting pipes to the two lower drums.
3. Water rises through the bent tubes by natural circulation, where combustion gases heat it.
4. Partial evaporation occurs in the evaporator tubes; the steam-water mixture returns to the upper drum.
5. Steam is separated and passes through the **integrated superheater tubes** (within the tube bank).
6. Dry superheated steam exits to the steam main.
7. Sediments in lower drums are periodically blown down.

**Advantages:**
- Three-drum design gives excellent stability under fluctuating loads.
- High steam capacity (up to 50,000 kg/hr).
- Built-in superheater in tube bank → compact design.
- Better steam purity due to secondary water separation.
- Symmetrical expansion under heat → lower thermal stress.

**Limitations:**
- More complex construction than B&W.
- Higher capital cost.
- Requires regular cleaning of bent tubes.
- Limited to ~30 bar pressure (cannot match La-Mont/Benson).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Students often confuse the Stirling boiler with a 3-drum B&W variant. The **key distinction** is that in the Stirling boiler, the **superheater tubes form an integral part of the bent tube bank**, whereas in B&W, the superheater is a separate component placed in the hot gas duct. Failing to mention this loses **2 marks**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A boiler generates 20,000 kg/hr of steam at 14 bar absolute with 2.5% moisture content. The feedwater enters at 60°C. The coal consumption is 2,400 kg/hr with a GCV of 28,000 kJ/kg. Calculate: (i) Equivalent evaporation, (ii) Factor of evaporation, (iii) Boiler efficiency by direct method.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $m_s = 20{,}000 \ kg/hr$
- $P = 14 \ bar$
- Moisture content = 2.5% → Dryness fraction $x = 1 - 0.025 = 0.975$
- $T_{fw} = 60°C$ → $h_{f1} = 251.1 \ kJ/kg$ (from steam tables)
- At 14 bar saturated: $h_f = 830.1 \ kJ/kg$, $h_{fg} = 1958.5 \ kJ/kg$, $h_g = 2788.6 \ kJ/kg$
- $m_f = 2400 \ kg/hr$
- $GCV = 28{,}000 \ kJ/kg$

**Step 1:** Enthalpy of actual steam (wet steam):
$$H = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} = 830.1 + 0.975 \times 1958.5$$

$$H = 830.1 + 1909.54 = 2739.64 \ kJ/kg$$

**Step 2:** Heat per kg of steam:
$$H - h_{f1} = 2739.64 - 251.1 = 2488.54 \ kJ/kg$$

**Part (i) — Equivalent Evaporation:**
$$m_e = m_s \cdot \frac{H - h_{f1}}{2257} = 20000 \times \frac{2488.54}{2257}$$

$$m_e = 20000 \times 1.1026 = 22{,}053 \ kg/hr$$

$$\boxed{m_e \approx 22{,}053 \ kg/hr}$$

**Part (ii) — Factor of Evaporation:**
$$F.E. = \frac{2488.54}{2257} = 1.103$$

$$\boxed{F.E. \approx 1.10}$$

**Part (iii) — Boiler Efficiency (Direct Method):**
$$Q_{out} = 20000 \times 2488.54 = 4.977 \times 10^7 \ kJ/hr$$

$$Q_{in} = 2400 \times 28000 = 6.72 \times 10^7 \ kJ/hr$$

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{4.977 \times 10^7}{6.72 \times 10^7} = 0.7406$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{boiler} \approx 74.06\%}$$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model — 14 Marks]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a La-Mont boiler. State its advantages over natural circulation boilers.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
1. **Economizer:** Heats feedwater using waste flue gas heat.
2. **Steam Separator / Drum:** Smaller than natural-circulation boiler; receives steam-water mixture and separates.
3. **Circulation Pump:** Provides forced circulation of water through evaporator tubes (velocity 3–4 m/s).
4. **Evaporator Tubes:** Vertical water tubes heated by combustion gases; steam generation occurs here.
5. **Superheater:** Raises saturated steam temperature.
6. **Air Preheater:** Preheats combustion air using flue gases.
7. **Furnace:** Pulverized coal or oil-fired combustion chamber.

**Working:**
1. Feedwater enters the economizer and is preheated.
2. It then enters the steam separator/drum.
3. The **circulation pump** forces water at high velocity through the evaporator tubes.
4. Partial vaporization occurs; the steam-water mixture returns to the separator.
5. Saturated steam passes through the superheater and exits to the turbine.
6. Unvaporized water is recirculated through the pump.

**Advantages over Natural Circulation Boilers:**
- **Smaller steam drum** → compact design.
- **Can operate at very high pressures** (100–170 bar) where natural circulation is inadequate.
- **Higher heat transfer coefficient** due to forced velocity.
- **Faster response to load changes.**
- **Less scale formation** due to high tube velocity (impurities don't settle).
- **Suitable for poor-quality water.**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the working of a Benson boiler with a neat sketch. Why is it called a "once-through" boiler? What are its advantages and limitations?**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
1. **Economizer:** Initial feedwater heating using flue gas.
2. **Radiant Evaporator Section:** Tubes exposed to furnace radiation; water vaporizes here.
3. **Convective Evaporator Section:** Tubes in the convective gas path where remaining vaporization completes.
4. **Superheater:** Final superheating of steam.
5. **Furnace:** Pulverized coal-fired combustion chamber.
6. **Critical components only — no steam/water drum.**

**Working (Once-Through Principle):**
1. Feedwater enters at very high pressure (≥ **170 bar**, often supercritical at **>225.64 bar**).
2. It flows through the **economizer** → **radiant evaporator** → **convective evaporator** → **superheater** in a **single, continuous pass**.
3. The water's state changes progressively: **compressed liquid → saturated liquid → two-phase mixture → saturated vapor → superheated vapor**.
4. **No drum is required** because the steam-water separation happens implicitly (the fluid is either fully liquid or fully vapor by the end).

**Why "Once-Through"?** Because feedwater passes through the entire heat-absorbing circuit **only once** before becoming superheated steam — no recirculation loop.

**Advantages:**
- **No drum** → lighter, cheaper, no drum-level control.
- **Rapid start-up and shutdown** (no large water inventory to heat/cool).
- **Suitable for supercritical operation** (above 225.64 bar) for highest efficiency.
- **Lower weight per kg of steam** → ideal for marine and large utility applications.
- Higher efficiency (90–92%).

**Limitations:**
- **Strict water quality requirement** — any impurity deposits on tubes (concentrates).
- **Narrow operating range near critical point** — difficult load-following.
- **Higher feed pump power** required.
- **Higher capital cost** for water treatment plant.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students incorrectly state that the Benson boiler "**does not require any feedwater treatment**." This is **completely wrong** and costs full marks. The Benson boiler requires the **most stringent feedwater treatment** of all boiler types, because any scale or impurity remains in the tubes permanently and can cause catastrophic failure. Write clearly: *"Benson boiler requires very high-quality feedwater (low TDS, deaerated, demineralized)."*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

### Babcock & Wilcox Boiler
- ✅ Two drums: upper steam drum + lower mud drum; bent water tubes connect via headers.
- ✅ Pressure: **up to 17.5 bar**; Capacity: **up to 25,000 kg/hr**.
- ✅ Bent tubes accommodate thermal expansion → main reason for higher pressure capability.
- ✅ Natural circulation driven by density difference between water and steam-water mixture.

### Stirling Boiler
- ✅ **Three drums**: one large upper + two symmetric lower water drums.
- ✅ **Superheater tubes are integrated** into the bent tube bank — this is its key distinction.
- ✅ Pressure: **up to 30 bar**; Capacity: **5,000 – 50,000 kg/hr**.
- ✅ Symmetrical design → stable under load fluctuations.

### La-Mont Boiler
- ✅ **Forced circulation** via centrifugal pump (velocity 3–4 m/s).
- ✅ Pressure: **100–170 bar**; smaller drum than natural-circulation boilers.
- ✅ Suitable where natural circulation fails at high pressure.

### Benson Boiler
- ✅ **Once-through** — no steam drum.
- ✅ Pressure: **170–250 bar** (subcritical) or **>225.64 bar** (supercritical).
- ✅ **Critical pressure of water = 22.064 MPa = 220.64 bar**.
- ✅ **Critical temperature of water = 374.15°C**.
- ✅ Fastest start-up among major boiler types.

### Loeffler Boiler
- ✅ Forced circulation; **~75% of generated steam bled** to heat feedwater in condenser-mixer.
- ✅ **~25%** delivered as final steam output.
- ✅ Primarily a **marine boiler**; uses latent heat of vaporization for feed heating.

### Velox Boiler
- ✅ **Pressurized furnace**; combustion gas drives a gas turbine.
- ✅ Very compact — used in submarines and peak-load plants.

### Must-Memorize KTU Formulas
- ✅ **Equivalent Evaporation:** $m_e = m_s \cdot \dfrac{H - h_{f1}}{2257}$
- ✅ **Factor of Evaporation:** $F.E. = \dfrac{H - h_{f1}}{2257}$
- ✅ **Boiler Efficiency (Direct):** $\eta = \dfrac{m_s (H - h_{f1})}{m_f \cdot GCV}$
- ✅ **Natural Circulation Head:** $\Delta P = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{mix})$
- ✅ **Latent heat at 100°C** = **2257 kJ/kg** (the *magic number* in boiler problems)

### Examiner's Red Flags to Avoid
- ❌ Don't confuse **Benson (no drum)** with **La-Mont (small drum)**.
- ❌ Don't claim **Stirling = Stirling Engine** — they are completely different concepts.
- ❌ Always mention **thermal expansion accommodation** as the reason for bent tubes in B&W.
- ❌ Always state **water quality requirement** explicitly for Benson boiler — examiner will check.
- ❌ Don't write "no circulation in Benson" — there IS flow, but it's **once-through**, not zero.

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Power Plant Engineering (PEMET631, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the **"Effect of Friction"** refers to the study of energy losses and pressure drops that occur when a working fluid (water, steam, condensate, or combustion gases) flows through the piping networks, headers, ducts, and heat exchange surfaces of a thermal power plant. These friction-induced losses degrade the thermodynamic availability of the fluid, reduce the net power output of turbines, increase pumping/compression work, and directly influence the design efficiency, economics, and auxiliary power consumption of the plant.

Mathematically, the effect of friction is quantified as the **head loss** $\,h_f\,$ per unit weight of fluid, the **pressure drop** $\,\Delta P_f\,$ across a flow path, and the **friction power loss** $\,P_f = \rho g Q h_f\,$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic is anchored under **Module 2** of PEMET631 and connects directly to **Boiler Feed Pump (BFP) sizing, Condensate piping, Economiser/Superheater tube circuits, Cooling Water circuits, and Draught systems (FD/ID fans)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Real-World Intuition"

Imagine you are drinking a thick milkshake through a long, narrow straw.

- **No-friction ideal flow** → A slippery, frictionless straw (like a greased glass tube). The milkshake flows easily and reaches your mouth with its full energy.
- **With friction** → The straw walls are rough. The milkshake molecules rub against the walls AND against each other. Some of the *kinetic energy* you applied gets converted into **heat** and dissipated. You have to **suck harder** to push the same volume through.

In a power plant, the "straw" is kilometres of boiler tubes, condenser water lines, and steam pipes. The "milkshake" is the working fluid. The harder the plant must "suck" (i.e., the more pump or fan power consumed), the **less net electricity** reaches the grid. This lost energy is the **Effect of Friction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Friction is the silent tax on every power plant. Typical BFP friction losses in a 500 MW plant can account for **2–4 %** of gross generation — enough to power thousands of homes lost forever as heat in the pipe walls.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following parameters are universally used in KTU board questions on this topic:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\,\rho\,$ | Density of fluid | **kg/m³** |
| $\,\mu\,$ | Dynamic viscosity | **Pa·s (or N·s/m²)** |
| $\,\nu\,$ | Kinematic viscosity | **m²/s**, with $\nu = \mu/\rho$ |
| $\,g\,$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $\,f\,$ | Darcy friction factor | dimensionless |
| $\,Re\,$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| $\,k_s\,$ | Equivalent sand-grain roughness | **mm or m** |
| $\,\varepsilon\,$ | Relative roughness $\varepsilon = k_s/D$ | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> Always remember: $\nu$ for water at 20 °C ≈ **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**; for steam condensate it varies from **0.2 × 10⁻⁶** (hot) to **0.65 × 10⁻⁶** (cold) m²/s.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Frictional Losses — Why and How

When a real (viscous) fluid flows inside a pipe, the no-slip boundary condition forces the fluid layer adjacent to the wall to be stationary. Successive layers slide over one another, generating **viscous shear stresses** $\tau_w$ at the wall.

**The 'Why':** Molecules in the bulk move faster than those near the wall → velocity gradient → momentum transfer → dissipation of mechanical energy into heat.

**The 'How':** This dissipation manifests as a continuous **drop in static pressure** along the flow direction, equivalent to a loss of mechanical head $h_f$.

### 2.1.1 The Three Flow Regimes (Reynolds Classification)

| Regime | Reynolds Range | Friction Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** | $Re < 2000$ | $f \propto 1/Re$ (Hagen–Poiseuille) |
| **Transitional** | $2000 < Re < 4000$ | Unstable — KTU assumes turbulent design |
| **Turbulent** | $Re > 4000$ | $f$ weakly dependent on $Re$, strongly on $\varepsilon$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** Most power-plant flows (BFP discharge, HP/IP/LP steam lines, ID/FD fan ducts, cooling water) are **fully turbulent**. Always verify $Re > 4000$ before applying turbulent formulas.

## 2.2 The Darcy–Weisbach Equation — The Master Equation

The head loss due to friction in a pipe of length $L$, diameter $D$, carrying fluid with mean velocity $V$, is:

$$\boxed{h_f \;=\; f \,\frac{L}{D}\,\frac{V^{2}}{2g}}$$

where:
- $h_f$ = head loss due to friction (m of fluid)
- $f$ = **Darcy friction factor** (4 × Fanning friction factor)
- $L$ = length of pipe (m)
- $D$ = internal diameter (m)
- $V$ = mean flow velocity (m/s)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

### 2.2.1 Alternative Forms of the Same Equation

**Pressure-drop form:**
$$\Delta P_f \;=\; f \,\frac{L}{D}\,\frac{\rho V^{2}}{2}$$

**Energy-loss form (per unit mass):**
$$\Delta e_f \;=\; f \,\frac{L}{D}\,\frac{V^{2}}{2}$$

**Power-loss form:**
$$P_f \;=\; \rho \, g \, Q \, h_f \;=\; f \,\frac{L}{D}\,\frac{\rho Q V^{2}}{2}$$

## 2.3 The Friction Factor $f$ — Empirical Correlations

### 2.3.1 Laminar Flow (Hagen–Poiseuille)

$$f \;=\; \frac{64}{Re}$$

**Why:** Derived analytically from the parabolic velocity profile. The loss depends **only** on $Re$ — pipe roughness is irrelevant in laminar flow.

### 2.3.2 Turbulent Flow — Colebrook–White Equation

The most accurate implicit correlation for commercial pipes:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} \;=\; -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$$

where $\varepsilon = k_s / D$ is the **relative roughness**.

### 2.3.3 Smooth-Pipe Limit (Blasius — for $Re < 10^5$)

$$f \;=\; \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$$

### 2.3.4 Fully-Rough Turbulent Limit ($Re \to \infty$)

$$f \;=\; \frac{1}{\left[-2\log_{10}(\varepsilon/3.7)\right]^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Moody Diagram:** The graphical solution of $f$ vs $Re$ for various $\varepsilon$ lines. KTU board questions frequently reference it qualitatively even when the numerical value is given.

## 2.4 Friction in Power-Plant Auxiliaries — Engineering Utility

| Power-Plant Component | Typical $f$ Range | Engineering Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **BFP discharge pipe** | 0.018 – 0.022 | Determines pump head rating |
| **HP steam line** | 0.012 – 0.015 | Reduces turbine inlet pressure |
| **Condensate line** | 0.018 – 0.025 | Affects deaerator pump sizing |
| **Cooling water (CW) pipe** | 0.020 – 0.028 | Increases CW pump power |
| **ID/FD fan duct** | 0.014 – 0.020 | Fan power penalty, fan law sizing |
| **Economiser tubes** | 0.015 – 0.020 | Reduces feed-water pressure at outlet |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_f = f(L/D)(V^2/2g)$ | Darcy–Weisbach head loss | m |
| 2 | $\Delta P_f = f(L/D)(\rho V^2/2)$ | Pressure drop | Pa |
| 3 | $P_f = \rho g Q h_f$ | Pump/fan power penalty | W |
| 4 | $Re = VD/\nu = \rho V D / \mu$ | Flow regime check | — |
| 5 | $f = 64/Re$ | Laminar | — |
| 6 | $f = 0.316 / Re^{0.25}$ | Turbulent smooth (Blasius) | — |
| 7 | $f = 1/\left[-2\log(\varepsilon/3.7)\right]^2$ | Fully rough turbulent | — |
| 8 | $Q = A \cdot V = (\pi D^2 / 4) V$ | Continuity | m³/s |
| 9 | $\varepsilon = k_s / D$ | Relative roughness | — |
| 10 | $h_{eq} = K V^2/2g$ | Minor losses (fittings) | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the use of `\vert` for absolute/division separation in the formula sheet above to keep the markdown table safe — never use `|` directly inside table cells.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Darcy–Weisbach Equation (from Energy Balance)

We apply the **steady-flow energy equation** between two sections (1) and (2) of a horizontal pipe of constant cross-section:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$

### Step-by-Step Reduction

**Step 1:** Apply horizontal-flow assumption $\Rightarrow z_1 = z_2$.

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + h_f$$

**Step 2:** Apply constant-area (incompressible) continuity $\Rightarrow V_1 = V_2$.

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + h_f$$

**Step 3:** Therefore the friction head loss equals the piezometric drop:

$$h_f \;=\; \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} \;=\; \frac{\Delta P_f}{\rho g}$$

**Step 4:** Now relate $\Delta P_f$ to the **wall shear stress** $\tau_w$ via a force balance on a fluid element of length $L$:

$$\Delta P_f \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \;=\; \tau_w \cdot \pi D \cdot L$$

Solving for $\Delta P_f$:

$$\Delta P_f \;=\; \frac{4 \tau_w L}{D}$$

**Step 5:** Define the **Darcy friction factor** such that $\tau_w$ is proportional to the dynamic pressure $\rho V^2 / 2$:

$$\tau_w \;=\; f \,\frac{\rho V^{2}}{8}$$

(The factor of 8 (or 1/4 of $\rho V^2/2$) is the standard definition that makes $f$ dimensionless.)

**Step 6:** Substitute into $\Delta P_f$:

$$\Delta P_f \;=\; \frac{4 L}{D} \cdot f \,\frac{\rho V^{2}}{8} \;=\; f \,\frac{L}{D} \,\frac{\rho V^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 7:** Divide by $\rho g$ to obtain the head form:

$$\boxed{h_f \;=\; f \,\frac{L}{D} \,\frac{V^{2}}{2g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation is complete. KTU examiners award full marks only when each algebraic transition is justified. Skipping Step 5 → loss of **2 marks** on a 14-mark question.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Laminar Friction Factor $f = 64/Re$

Start from the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for laminar flow in a circular pipe:

$$\Delta P_f \;=\; \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^{2}}$$

**Step 1:** Equate with the Darcy–Weisbach form:

$$f \,\frac{L}{D} \,\frac{\rho V^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Cancel $L$ on both sides:

$$f \,\frac{1}{D} \,\frac{\rho V^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{32 \mu V}{D^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by $2D^2/(\rho V^2)$:

$$f \,\cdot\, D \;=\; \frac{64 \mu}{\rho V}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to recognise $Re = \rho V D / \mu$:

$$f \;=\; \frac{64 \mu}{\rho V D} \;=\; \frac{64}{Re}$$

$$\boxed{f \;=\; \frac{64}{Re}} \quad \text{(laminar)}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 500 MW thermal power plant has a Boiler Feed Pump (BFP) discharge line of diameter $D = 0.30$ m and length $L = 80$ m. The mean velocity of water is $V = 3.2$ m/s. Take kinematic viscosity $\nu = 0.65 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s and equivalent roughness $k_s = 0.046$ mm. Compute the head loss, pressure drop, and friction power loss if the discharge flow rate is $Q = 0.226$ m³/s.

### Step 1 — Reynolds Number

$$Re \;=\; \frac{V D}{\nu} \;=\; \frac{3.2 \times 0.30}{0.65 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$Re \;=\; \frac{0.96}{0.65 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 1.477 \times 10^{6}$$

### Step 2 — Relative Roughness

$$\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{k_s}{D} \;=\; \frac{0.046 \times 10^{-3}}{0.30} \;=\; 1.533 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 3 — Friction Factor via Colebrook Equation

For $Re = 1.477 \times 10^{6}$ and $\varepsilon = 1.533 \times 10^{-4}$, iterate:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} \;=\; -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.533 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{1.477 \times 10^{6} \sqrt{f}}\right)$$

Trial 1: $f_0 = 0.018$ → $1/\sqrt{f} = 7.453$
RHS → $-2\log_{10}(4.143\times 10^{-5} + 2.07 \times 10^{-6}/7.453) = -2\log_{10}(4.350\times 10^{-5}) = 8.361$

Trial 2: $1/\sqrt{f_1} = 8.361$ → $f_1 = 0.01430$
Iterate again → $f \approx 0.0143$

For board exams, the equivalent result from the **Moody chart** is acceptable: $f \approx 0.014$.

### Step 4 — Head Loss

$$h_f \;=\; f \,\frac{L}{D} \,\frac{V^{2}}{2g} \;=\; 0.014 \times \frac{80}{0.30} \times \frac{(3.2)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f \;=\; 0.014 \times 266.67 \times \frac{10.24}{19.62}$$

$$h_f \;=\; 0.014 \times 266.67 \times 0.5219 \;=\; 1.948 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5 — Pressure Drop

$$\Delta P_f \;=\; \rho g h_f \;=\; 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.948$$

$$\Delta P_f \;=\; 19{,}110 \text{ Pa} \;\approx\; 19.11 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 6 — Friction Power Loss

$$P_f \;=\; \rho g Q h_f \;=\; 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.226 \times 1.948$$

$$P_f \;=\; 4318 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 4.32 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 4.32 kW continuous penalty — small per unit, but across **8 feedwater lines, 24 hours/day, 365 days/year**, multiplied by an electricity cost of ₹6/kWh, this single friction loss costs the plant **₹2.27 lakh/year**. Multiply by hundreds of friction points in a real plant → crores lost annually.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Choose the Right Formula

| Given Quantity | Unknown Quantity | Direct Formula to Use |
|---|---|---|
| $L, D, V, f$ | $h_f$ | $f(L/D)(V^2/2g)$ |
| $h_f, V, D, g$ | $f$ | $f = 2gDh_f/(LV^2)$ |
| $V, D, \nu$ | $Re$ | $Re = VD/\nu$ |
| $Re$, regime | $f$ | $64/Re$ or Blasius or Moody |
| $h_f, Q, \rho$ | $P_f$ | $\rho g Q h_f$ |
| $\Delta P_f, L, D, V$ | $f$ | $f = 2D\Delta P_f/(\rho L V^2)$ |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**"State and explain the Darcy–Weisbach equation for head loss due to friction in a pipe. Define each term with its SI unit."**

**Model Answer:**
The Darcy–Weisbach equation gives the head loss $h_f$ (in metres) due to friction when fluid flows through a pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ at mean velocity $V$:

$$h_f \;=\; f \,\frac{L}{D} \,\frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$

where $f$ is the dimensionless Darcy friction factor, $L$ is pipe length in m, $D$ is internal diameter in m, $V$ is mean velocity in m/s, and $g = 9.81$ m/s². It is applicable to laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow regimes, with $f$ determined from the Reynolds number and pipe roughness.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for correct equation, 1 mark for all symbols/units, 1 mark for applicability.)*

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**"Distinguish between laminar and turbulent flow in pipes with respect to the friction factor. State the relevant equations."**

**Model Answer:**
In laminar flow ($Re < 2000$), the friction factor depends solely on the Reynolds number:

$$f \;=\; \frac{64}{Re}$$

The velocity profile is parabolic and losses are due to molecular viscosity only — pipe roughness has no effect. In turbulent flow ($Re > 4000$), $f$ is empirically obtained from the Colebrook–White, Moody chart, or Blasius ($f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ for smooth pipes) relations and depends on **both** $Re$ and the relative roughness $\varepsilon = k_s/D$. The velocity profile is flatter (logarithmic) and losses are dominated by eddy viscosity.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for each regime equation, 1 mark for dependence on roughness.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — **(14 Marks)**

> **A(a)** Derive the Darcy–Weisbach equation for head loss due to friction in a pipe starting from a force balance on the fluid element. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **A(b)** A 250 mm diameter steel pipe ($k_s = 0.046$ mm) carries water at a flow rate of $0.06$ m³/s over a length of 200 m. Take $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Calculate the **(i)** Reynolds number, **(ii)** Darcy friction factor using the Colebrook–White equation, **(iii)** head loss, and **(iv)** power lost due to friction. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**A(a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Assume steady, incompressible, fully-developed flow in a horizontal pipe of constant cross-section $A = \pi D^2/4$.

**Step 1 — Force balance:** Consider a cylindrical fluid element of length $L$. Pressure force on left = $P_1 A$; on right = $P_2 A$. Wall shear force opposing flow = $\tau_w \pi D L$.

For equilibrium (in steady flow):

$$P_1 A \;-\; P_2 A \;-\; \tau_w \pi D L \;=\; 0$$

$$(P_1 - P_2) A \;=\; \tau_w \pi D L$$

**Step 2 — Replace $A$:** $A = \pi D^2/4$.

$$(P_1 - P_2) \,\frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \;=\; \tau_w \pi D L$$

$$\Delta P_f \;=\; P_1 - P_2 \;=\; \frac{4 \tau_w L}{D}$$

**Step 3 — Define friction factor:** $\tau_w = f \rho V^2 / 8$.

$$\Delta P_f \;=\; \frac{4 L}{D} \cdot \frac{f \rho V^{2}}{8} \;=\; f \,\frac{L}{D} \,\frac{\rho V^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to head:** $h_f = \Delta P_f / (\rho g)$.

$$\boxed{h_f \;=\; f \,\frac{L}{D} \,\frac{V^{2}}{2g}} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

**Assumptions:** (i) Steady, incompressible flow; (ii) Constant pipe diameter; (iii) Uniform velocity profile; (iv) Friction factor $f$ constant along length; (v) No minor (fitting) losses considered separately.

*(Marks: 2 for force balance, 2 for substitution, 1 for friction-factor definition, 1 for head form, 1 for assumptions.)*

**A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute mean velocity:**

$$V \;=\; \frac{Q}{A} \;=\; \frac{0.06}{\pi (0.250)^2 / 4} \;=\; \frac{0.06}{0.04909} \;=\; 1.222 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re \;=\; \frac{V D}{\nu} \;=\; \frac{1.222 \times 0.250}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 3.055 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 3 — Relative roughness:**

$$\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{k_s}{D} \;=\; \frac{0.046 \times 10^{-3}}{0.250} \;=\; 1.84 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Friction factor via Colebrook–White (iterative):**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} \;=\; -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.84 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{3.055 \times 10^{5} \sqrt{f}}\right)$$

Iteration 1: assume $f = 0.020 \Rightarrow 1/\sqrt{f} = 7.071$
RHS = $-2\log_{10}(4.973 \times 10^{-5} + 2.85 \times 10^{-5}/7.071)$
$= -2\log_{10}(5.376 \times 10^{-5}) = 7.539$
$\Rightarrow f = 0.0176$

Iteration 2: $f = 0.0176 \Rightarrow 1/\sqrt{f} = 7.539$
RHS = $-2\log_{10}(4.973 \times 10^{-5} + 1.769 \times 10^{-5}) = 7.561$
$\Rightarrow f = 0.01749 \approx 0.0175$ (converged)

$$\boxed{f \;\approx\; 0.0175}$$

**Step 5 — Head loss:**

$$h_f \;=\; 0.0175 \times \frac{200}{0.250} \times \frac{(1.222)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f \;=\; 0.0175 \times 800 \times \frac{1.4933}{19.62}$$

$$h_f \;=\; 0.0175 \times 800 \times 0.07611 \;=\; 1.066 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Power lost:**

$$P_f \;=\; \rho g Q h_f \;=\; 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.06 \times 1.066$$

$$P_f \;=\; 627.4 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 0.627 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answers:**
(i) $Re = 3.055 \times 10^{5}$
(ii) $f = 0.0175$
(iii) $h_f = 1.066$ m
(iv) $P_f = 627.4$ W

*(Marks: 1 for V, 1 for Re, 1 for ε, 2 for Colebrook iteration, 1 for h_f, 1 for P_f.)*

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]` — **(14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **B(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the concept of **major** and **minor losses** in pipe flow. Why are minor losses often neglected in long industrial piping? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **B(b)** A cooling-water pipeline of diameter 350 mm and length 500 m conveys $0.30$ m³/s of water at 30 °C. The pipe is of cast iron ($k_s = 0.26$ mm) with $\nu = 0.80 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Calculate the **head loss** and the **annual pumping cost** if the pump motor efficiency is 88 % and electricity cost is ₹6.50/kWh, operating 7200 hours per year. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**B(a) — Conceptual (7 Marks):**

**Major losses ($h_f$):** These are the head losses due to **friction along the length of the pipe**, given by Darcy–Weisbach:

$$h_f \;=\; f \,\frac{L}{D} \,\frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$

**Minor losses ($h_m$):** These are losses at fittings, bends, valves, sudden expansions/contractions, and entries/exits:

$$h_m \;=\; K \,\frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$

where $K$ is the loss coefficient (e.g., $K = 0.5$ for sudden contraction, $K = 1.0$ for standard elbow, $K = 10$ for fully open globe valve).

**Why minor losses are often neglected:** In **long industrial pipelines** (e.g., feed water mains, cooling water lines of length $> 500$ m), the ratio of length to diameter is very large ($L/D > 1000$). Minor loss coefficients sum to a few units while $fL/D$ typically equals hundreds — so minor losses are usually $< 1\%$ of total loss. Hence engineers **add a margin of 5–10 %** to the major loss instead of computing each fitting individually.

*(Marks: 2 for definitions, 2 for formulae, 2 for comparison, 1 for practical reasoning.)*

**B(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Velocity:**

$$A \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{\pi (0.350)^{2}}{4} \;=\; 0.0962 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$V \;=\; \frac{Q}{A} \;=\; \frac{0.30}{0.0962} \;=\; 3.118 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re \;=\; \frac{3.118 \times 0.350}{0.80 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 1.364 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 3 — Relative roughness:**

$$\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{0.26 \times 10^{-3}}{0.350} \;=\; 7.43 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Friction factor (Colebrook):**

Iterate:
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} \;=\; -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{7.43 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{1.364 \times 10^{6} \sqrt{f}}\right)$$

Trial $f = 0.020$: RHS = $-2\log_{10}(2.008 \times 10^{-4} + 9.20 \times 10^{-5}) = 7.097 \Rightarrow f = 0.0199$
Converged: $f \approx 0.0200$.

**Step 5 — Head loss:**

$$h_f \;=\; 0.020 \times \frac{500}{0.350} \times \frac{(3.118)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f \;=\; 0.020 \times 1428.57 \times \frac{9.722}{19.62}$$

$$h_f \;=\; 0.020 \times 1428.57 \times 0.4955 \;=\; 14.16 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Hydraulic power:**

$$P_{\text{hyd}} \;=\; \rho g Q h_f \;=\; 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.30 \times 14.16$$

$$P_{\text{hyd}} \;=\; 41{,}667 \text{ W} \;=\; 41.67 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Electrical input power (with $\eta_m = 0.88$):**

$$P_{\text{elec}} \;=\; \frac{41.67}{0.88} \;=\; 47.35 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8 — Annual energy and cost:**

$$E \;=\; 47.35 \times 7200 \;=\; 340{,}920 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$\text{Cost} \;=\; 340{,}920 \times 6.50 \;=\; \text{₹} 22{,}15{,}980/\text{year} \;\approx\; \text{₹} 22.16 \text{ lakh/year}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Head loss $h_f \approx 14.16$ m
- Annual pumping cost $\approx$ ₹22.16 lakh/year

*(Marks: 1 for V, 1 for Re, 1 for ε, 1 for f, 1 for h_f, 1 for P_hyd, 1 for annual cost.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $k_s$ from mm to m** before computing $\varepsilon$. Cost: **1 mark** lost.
> 2. **Using the Fanning factor ($f_f = f/4$)** instead of the Darcy factor. The two are NOT interchangeable — KTU always uses Darcy. Cost: **2 marks** lost.
> 3. **Stopping Colebrook iteration after one trial** — at least 2 iterations are expected. Cost: **1 mark** lost.
> 4. **Computing $P_f$ instead of $P_{\text{hyd}}$ when asked for "pump power"** — must divide by motor efficiency if electrical input is asked. Cost: **1 mark** lost.
> 5. **Using $g = 9.8$ vs $9.81$** inconsistently across the same problem. Cost: **0.5 mark** per inconsistency.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert hours → kWh** in cost calculations. Cost: **1 mark**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Darcy–Weisbach equation:** $h_f = f(L/D)(V^2/2g)$ — the **single most important** equation on this topic.
- ⭐ **Friction factor** $f$ is **Darcy** (NOT Fanning) in all KTU problems unless explicitly stated otherwise.
- ⭐ **Laminar:** $f = 64/Re$ (analytical, no roughness needed).
- ⭐ **Turbulent smooth:** Blasius $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ for $Re < 10^5$.
- ⭐ **General turbulent:** Colebrook–White implicit equation; iterate or read from **Moody diagram**.
- ⭐ **Relative roughness:** $\varepsilon = k_s/D$ — always convert mm → m before dividing.
- ⭐ **Power loss:** $P_f = \rho g Q h_f$ — multiply by operating hours and electricity rate for cost.
- ⭐ **Typical pipe roughness values (memorise):** drawn tubing $k_s \approx 0.0015$ mm; commercial steel $0.046$ mm; cast iron $0.26$ mm; concrete $0.3$–$3.0$ mm.
- ⭐ **Regime thresholds:** $Re < 2000$ laminar; $2000 < Re < 4000$ transitional (avoid designing here); $Re > 4000$ turbulent.
- ⭐ **Power-plant application map:** BFP discharge → friction sets pump head; HP steam line → friction drops turbine inlet pressure; cooling water → friction sets CW pump power; ID/FD ducts → friction sets fan rating.
- ⭐ **Quick sanity check:** $h_f$ in metres is typically 1–20 m for power-plant piping; if you get 200 m, recheck the diameter.
- ⭐ **Energy economics:** A 1 % friction loss in a 500 MW plant = 5 MW continuous loss = ~₹24 crore/year at ₹6/kWh.
- ⭐ **Minor vs major loss decision:** Use $L/D > 100$ as a quick test; if yes, neglect minor losses with a 5 % safety margin.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Super Saturated Flow, Steam Turbines, Impulse and Reaction Turbines
Velocity Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Power Plant Engineering** (specifically steam and gas turbines), a **Velocity Diagram** (also called a **Velocity Triangle**) is a graphical vector representation that illustrates the relationship between the absolute velocity of the working fluid, the velocity of the rotating turbine blades, and the relative velocity of the fluid with respect to the moving blades at both the **inlet** and **outlet** of a turbine stage.

It is the fundamental analytical tool used to determine the **work output**, **blade efficiency**, **degree of reaction**, and **blade angles** in impulse and reaction turbines, forming the mathematical backbone of KTU Module 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Velocity diagrams are essential for the design and analysis of single-stage and multi-stage impulse turbines, and form the basis for solving Part B (14-mark) numerical problems in KTU university examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the back of a **moving open pickup truck** and throwing a ball forward. A person standing on the road sees the ball moving with the sum of the truck's velocity and your throw velocity (**absolute velocity**). However, *from your perspective on the truck*, the ball leaves your hand with only the speed of the throw (**relative velocity**). The truck itself represents the **blade velocity**.

Now, if you draw arrows for:
- The truck's motion (Blade velocity, $u$)
- The ball's path as seen by the road observer (Absolute velocity, $V$)
- The ball's path as seen by you (Relative velocity, $V_r$)

...you will get a closed **triangle of vectors**. This is exactly what a velocity diagram represents in a turbine stage. The working steam (or gas) behaves like the ball, and the rotating blades behave like the moving truck.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used in Velocity Diagrams:**
> - **Rotational speed of shaft:** $N$ (in rpm) → typically **3000 rpm** for 50 Hz AC systems
> - **Mean blade diameter:** $D_m$ (in metres)
> - **Blade speed (mean):** $u = \frac{\pi D_m N}{60}$ (in m/s)
> - **Steam/gas flow rate:** $m$ (in kg/s) — relevant for power calculations

## 1.3 Fundamental Vector Components

A complete velocity diagram comprises **three primary vectors** and **three primary angles**:

| Symbol | Vector Name | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | **Absolute Velocity** | Velocity of fluid with respect to a stationary (ground) observer |
| $u$ | **Blade Velocity** | Linear velocity of the blade at the mean radius |
| $V_r$ | **Relative Velocity** | Velocity of fluid with respect to the moving blade |

| Symbol | Angle Name | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | **Nozzle Angle** (Guide angle) | Angle made by $V$ with the plane of rotation |
| $\beta$ | **Blade Angle** | Angle made by $V_r$ with the plane of rotation |
| $\theta$ | **Nozzle Outlet Angle** (for fixed nozzles) | Same as $\alpha$ for a single-stage turbine |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vector Relationship — The Heart of the Velocity Diagram

The fundamental kinematic relationship governing every velocity diagram is the **vector triangle equation**:

$$\vec{V} = \vec{u} + \vec{V_r}$$

This means: if you start at the tail of $\vec{u}$ (on the blade) and add $\vec{V_r}$ head-to-tail, you arrive at the tip of $\vec{V}$. Conversely:

$$\vec{V_r} = \vec{V} - \vec{u}$$

This is applied at **inlet** (subscript 1) and **outlet** (subscript 2) of every blade row.

## 2.2 Components of Absolute Velocity

The absolute velocity $V$ is resolved into two orthogonal components:

- **Whirl component (tangential):** $V_w = V \cos\alpha$ — the component that actually does work on the blade
- **Flow component (axial):** $V_f = V \sin\alpha$ — the component that pushes fluid through the blade passage without doing work

$$V^2 = V_w^2 + V_f^2$$

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Construction Logic

1. **Draw a horizontal reference line** representing the plane of rotation of the blades.
2. **Mark the blade velocity vector** $\vec{u}$ along the horizontal line (direction of rotation).
3. **From the head of $\vec{u}$**, draw the absolute velocity vector $\vec{V}$ at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal.
4. **Close the triangle** by drawing $\vec{V_r}$ from the tail of $\vec{u}$ to the tip of $\vec{V}$. This is the relative velocity at **inlet**.
5. **Repeat for outlet** using subscript 2.
6. **Whirl direction conventions:** By KTU convention, velocities causing rotation in the direction of blade motion are **positive**; those opposing it are **negative**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all formulas that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce and apply in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** questions. Memorize this cheat sheet thoroughly.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Mean blade speed | m/s |
| 2 | $V_w = V \cos\alpha$ | Whirl (tangential) component of $V$ | m/s |
| 3 | $V_f = V \sin\alpha$ | Flow (axial) component of $V$ | m/s |
| 4 | $V^2 = V_w^2 + V_f^2$ | Vector magnitude of absolute velocity | m²/s² |
| 5 | $V_r^2 = (V_w - u)^2 + V_f^2$ | Relative velocity magnitude | m²/s² |
| 6 | $W = u \cdot (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})$ | Work done per kg of fluid per stage | J/kg |
| 7 | $P = m \cdot W$ | Power developed by the stage | W (Watts) |
| 8 | $\eta_b = \frac{\text{Work done on blade}}{\text{K.E. supplied by nozzle}} = \frac{u(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{V_1^2/2}$ | Blade (isentropic) efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $R = \frac{\Delta K.E._{relative}}{\Delta K.E._{absolute}}$ | Degree of reaction | dimensionless |
| 10 | $R = 1 - \frac{V_{w1} \pm V_{w2}}{2 u} \cdot \frac{\cos\beta_2}{\cos\alpha_1}$ | Reaction expressed via velocity triangles | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention for Work Done formula:** Use **$+$** sign if the outlet whirl component $V_{w2}$ is in the **same direction** as blade motion (under-expanded exit). Use **$-$** sign if $V_{w2}$ is **opposite** to blade motion (most common — "ideal exit, no whirl").

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Velocity diagrams are not merely academic drawings — they directly govern:
- **Blade Profile Design** — Choosing the correct blade angle $\beta$ so that steam enters the blade passage without shock.
- **Stage Efficiency Maximization** — Optimal blade speed ratio $u/V_1$ (around 0.45–0.5 for single impulse stage) is derived from the velocity triangle geometry.
- **Power Output Sizing** — Used by BHEL, Siemens, and GE to design utility-scale turbines ranging from 1 MW to 1000 MW.
- **Nozzle Selection** — Determines whether a single nozzle, multi-nozzle, or convergent-divergent (de Laval) nozzle is needed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Work Done per kg of Fluid

**Starting premise:** Work done per unit time on a rotating blade row = (Tangential force) × (blade velocity at that radius).

**Step 1 — Mass flow consideration:**
Mass of fluid striking per second = $m$ (kg/s).
Change in tangential momentum per second = $m \cdot (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})$.

**Step 2 — Force on blades (Newton's 2nd Law):**
Tangential force $F_t$ on the blades equals the rate of change of tangential momentum:

$$F_t = m \cdot (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})$$

**Step 3 — Work done per second (Power):**
Work done per second (Power) = Force × velocity in the direction of force.

$$P = F_t \cdot u = m \cdot u \cdot (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})$$

**Step 4 — Work done per kg of fluid:**
Dividing the power by the mass flow rate $m$:

$$W = \frac{P}{m} = u \cdot (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2}) \quad \text{[J/kg]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation completed.** This single equation is the most-tested formula in KTU Module 2 numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation: Blade (Diagram) Efficiency

**Step 1 — Energy supplied by the nozzle:**
The nozzle converts the enthalpy drop $\Delta h$ into kinetic energy of the jet:

$$\text{K.E. supplied} = \frac{V_1^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Energy actually utilized on the blade:**
From the velocity triangle, only the whirl components $V_{w1}$ and $V_{w2}$ interact with the rotating blade to produce work:

$$\text{K.E. utilized} = u(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})$$

**Step 3 — Ratio (definition of efficiency):**

$$\eta_b = \frac{\text{K.E. utilized}}{\text{K.E. supplied}} = \frac{u(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{V_1^2 / 2}$$

**Step 4 — Ideal case (no exit whirl, $V_{w2} = 0$):**
Substituting and using the inlet triangle relation $V_{w1} = V_1 \cos\alpha$:

$$\eta_b = \frac{2 u V_1 \cos\alpha}{V_1^2} = \frac{2 u \cos\alpha}{V_1}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum efficiency condition:**
For a given nozzle angle $\alpha$, taking $\frac{d\eta_b}{du} = 0$ yields the optimum blade speed ratio:

$$\frac{u}{V_1} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{2}$$

Substituting this optimum back into the efficiency expression gives the **maximum blade efficiency**:

$$\eta_{b,\text{max}} = \cos^2\alpha$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For $\alpha = 20°$ (typical), $\eta_{b,\text{max}} \approx 0.883$ (88.3%)** — this is the theoretical upper limit for a single impulse stage and is a frequent 3-mark KTU question.

## 3.3 Derivation: Degree of Reaction from the Velocity Diagram

**Step 1 — Kinetic energy balance:**
The total enthalpy drop across a stage splits into a kinetic energy change in the relative frame (blades) and the absolute frame:

$$\Delta h = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2} + \frac{V_{r2}^2 - V_{r1}^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Definition:**
Degree of reaction $R$ is defined as the fraction of total enthalpy drop that occurs in the **rotor (moving blade)** portion:

$$R = \frac{V_{r2}^2 - V_{r1}^2}{V_1^2 - V_2^2}$$

**Step 3 — For a symmetric stage** (where $V_1 = V_2$ and $V_{r1} = V_{r2}$ — Parsons-type reaction stage):

$$R = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

**Step 4 — For an impulse stage** (pressure drop occurs only in nozzle, $\Delta h_{\text{rotor}} = 0$):

$$R = 0$$

**Step 5 — Conversion to velocity-triangle angles:**

$$R = 1 - \frac{(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})\cos\beta_2}{2 u \cos\alpha_1}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Special Velocity Diagram Cases

| Case | Condition | Work Done | Efficiency Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure Impulse** | $R = 0$, $\Delta p$ only in nozzle | $W = u V_{w1}$ (if $V_{w2}=0$) | Max $= \cos^2\alpha$ |
| **Parsons (50% Reaction)** | $R = 0.5$, symmetric triangles | $W = u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})$ | Highest stage efficiency |
| **Parson Stage with $\alpha = \beta$** | Symmetric | $W = 2 u V_w$ | $V_{w1} = V_{w2}$ |
| **Outlet whirl considered** | $V_{w2}$ in same direction as $u$ | $W = u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})$ | Used in Curtis stage |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Velocity Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> A velocity triangle is a geometric figure. Since Mermaid cannot render angled physical triangles, the following **block-level functional flow** represents the equivalent vector relationships, blade angles, and decision paths used in KTU numerical problem solving.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start: Given V1, alpha, D, N"] --> calcU["Compute u = pi times D times N divided by 60"]
    calcU --> resolveV1["Resolve V1 into Vw1 and Vf1"]
    resolveV1 --> inletTri["Construct INLET velocity triangle"]
    inletTri --> inletTriA["Vector u along horizontal"]
    inletTri --> inletTriB["Vector V1 at angle alpha above u"]
    inletTri --> inletTriC["Vector Vr1 closes the triangle"]
    inletTriC --> assumeOut["Assume outlet condition: ideal exit, Vw2 = 0, OR given beta2"]
    assumeOut --> outletTri["Construct OUTLET velocity triangle"]
    outletTri --> outletTriA["Vector u along horizontal"]
    outletTri --> outletTriB["Vector Vr2 at angle beta2 to horizontal"]
    outletTri --> outletTriC["Vector V2 closes the triangle"]
    outletTriC --> computeW["Compute Work W per kg = u times Vw1 plus or minus Vw2"]
    computeW --> computePower["Compute Power P = m times W"]
    computePower --> computeEta["Compute blade efficiency eta_b = 2uVw1 divided by V1 squared"]
    computeEta --> computeR["Compute degree of reaction R from velocity triangles"]
    computeR --> resultNode["Result: W, P, eta_b, R for the stage"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Single-Stage Impulse Turbine with Velocity Triangles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nozzleBlock["NOZZLE: Converts enthalpy to kinetic energy. Steam leaves at V1, angle alpha."] --> inletTri["INLET VELOCITY TRIANGLE: V1, u, Vr1, angles alpha and beta1"]
    inletTri --> bladeRow["MOVING BLADE ROW: Rotating at u. Steam changes direction inside blade passage."]
    bladeRow --> outletTri["OUTLET VELOCITY TRIANGLE: V2, u, Vr2, angles beta2 and alpha2"]
    outletTri --> workExt["WORK EXTRACTION: Tangential force on blade x blade velocity"]
    workExt --> outputNode["OUTPUT: Shaft power P = m times u times Vw1 plus or minus Vw2"]
    nozzleBlock -. pressure drop .-> nozzleBlock
    bladeRow -. ideally no pressure drop .-> bladeRow
```

## 4.3 Labelled Vector Architecture — Inlet vs Outlet

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InletSide["INLET TRIANGLE Block A"]
        A1["Vw1 = V1 cos alpha  —  TANGENTIAL COMPONENT"]
        A2["Vf1 = V1 sin alpha  —  AXIAL COMPONENT"]
        A3["Vr1 — RELATIVE VELOCITY at blade inlet"]
    end

    subgraph OutletSide["OUTLET TRIANGLE Block B"]
        B1["Vw2 = V2 cos alpha2  —  EXIT WHIRL"]
        B2["Vf2 = V2 sin alpha2  —  EXIT FLOW"]
        B3["Vr2 — RELATIVE VELOCITY at blade outlet"]
    end

    InletSide -->|same blade u| OutletSide
    A1 --> work["W = u (Vw1 plus or minus Vw2) per kg"]
    B1 --> work
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix for KTU Velocity Diagram Problems

| Given in Question | Use This Triangle Relation |
|---|---|
| $V_1, \alpha, D, N$ only | Ideal exit assumed → $V_{w2} = 0$ |
| $\beta_1, \beta_2, u$ given | Construct from blade angles directly |
| $V_1, u, \alpha, \beta_2$ given | Construct inlet, then transfer $V_{r2}$ for outlet |
| Reaction $R$ given | Use $R$ equation to find outlet angles |
| "Symmetric" mentioned | $V_1 = V_2$, $V_{r1} = V_{r2}$, $R = 0.5$ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define a velocity diagram. What are the three fundamental vectors represented in it?

**Model Answer:**
A velocity diagram is a vector triangle that represents the relationship between the absolute velocity $V$ of the fluid, the blade velocity $u$, and the relative velocity $V_r$ of the fluid with respect to the moving blade.

The three fundamental vectors are:
1. **Absolute velocity $V$** — velocity of fluid relative to a stationary observer.
2. **Blade velocity $u$** — linear velocity of the blade at the mean radius ($u = \pi D N / 60$).
3. **Relative velocity $V_r$** — velocity of fluid relative to the moving blade.

They satisfy the vector relation $\vec{V} = \vec{u} + \vec{V_r}$.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is meant by the degree of reaction? State its value for a pure impulse stage and a 50% reaction stage.

**Model Answer:**
The degree of reaction $R$ is defined as the ratio of the enthalpy drop occurring in the moving blades (rotor) to the total enthalpy drop in the stage:

$$R = \frac{\Delta h_{\text{rotor}}}{\Delta h_{\text{stage}}}$$

- For a **pure impulse stage**, $R = 0$ (all pressure drop occurs in the nozzle).
- For a **50% reaction (Parsons) stage**, $R = 0.5$ (pressure drop is equally split between stator and rotor).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q (a).** A single-stage impulse turbine has a mean blade diameter of **1.2 m** and runs at **3000 rpm**. The nozzle angle is **20°** and the absolute velocity of steam leaving the nozzle is **350 m/s**. Assuming ideal exit (no whirl at outlet) and an isentropic heat drop of **70 kJ/kg**, find:
(i) The work done per kg of steam.
(ii) The blade efficiency.
(iii) The axial thrust on the blades.
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Blade velocity:**
$$u = \frac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 3000}{60} = 60 \pi = 188.496 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Whirl component at inlet:**
$$V_{w1} = V_1 \cos\alpha = 350 \times \cos 20° = 350 \times 0.9397 = 328.89 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Flow component at inlet:**
$$V_{f1} = V_1 \sin\alpha = 350 \times \sin 20° = 350 \times 0.3420 = 119.7 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Work done per kg (ideal exit, $V_{w2} = 0$):**
$$W = u \cdot V_{w1} = 188.496 \times 328.89 = 61{,}989 \text{ J/kg} \approx 61.99 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Blade efficiency:**
$$\eta_b = \frac{2 W}{V_1^2} = \frac{2 \times 61{,}989}{350^2} = \frac{123{,}978}{122{,}500} = 1.012$$

Since this exceeds 1.0, the assumption is physically unrealistic. The optimum blade speed $u_{opt} = V_1 \cos\alpha / 2 = 164.45$ m/s is far less than 188.5 m/s — meaning the blades are **over-speeding** and the actual efficiency is less. We must redo with **$\cos\beta_2 = 0$** assumption corrected (blade angle chosen to give zero exit whirl only if $u = V_1 \cos\alpha / 2$).

For $V_{w2} \neq 0$, we need a blade angle assumption. Assuming symmetric blade $\beta_1 = \beta_2$ such that $V_{w2} = u - V_1 \cos\alpha$ (exit whirl opposite to motion):

$$V_{w2} = 188.496 - 328.89 = -140.39 \text{ m/s (opposite to motion)}$$

$$W = u(V_{w1} - V_{w2}) = 188.496 \times (328.89 - (-140.39)) = 188.496 \times 469.28 = 88{,}461 \text{ J/kg} \approx 88.46 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_b = \frac{2 \times 88{,}461}{122{,}500} = 1.444 \text{ (unrealistic — would imply negative losses)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to apply the **sign convention** for $V_{w2}$. If $V_{w2}$ is **opposite** to blade motion, it is **negative** in the work formula, and the correct expression becomes $W = u(V_{w1} + \vert V_{w2} \vert)$. Always check direction of exit whirl before substituting.

**Step 6 — Axial thrust:**
Axial thrust $= m \cdot (V_{f1} - V_{f2}) = m \cdot V_{f1}$ (assuming $V_{f2} = V_{f1}$ for symmetrical blades) = $m \times 119.7$ N.

If $m$ is not given, leave in symbolic form: **Axial thrust = $m \times 119.7$ Newtons**.

**Q (b).** Explain the concept of a **compound velocity diagram** as used in multi-stage (Curtis) impulse turbines. How does it improve efficiency compared to a single-stage impulse turbine? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **compound velocity diagram** represents the complete flow path of steam through **two or more rows of moving blades** mounted on the same wheel, with **fixed guide blades (or reversing blades)** in between. The first row of moving blades receives steam from the nozzle, partially expands, and discharges it. A row of fixed blades reverses the direction of the remaining steam and feeds it into the second row of moving blades on the same rotor.

**Working principle:**
- In the **first moving row**, the steam has absolute velocity $V_1$ at angle $\alpha$ and relative velocity $V_{r1}$ at angle $\beta_1$.
- The steam exits the first row with absolute velocity $V_2'$, enters the **fixed reversing blade**, and is redirected with the same magnitude (ideally) but reversed direction.
- It then enters the **second moving row** with a fresh velocity triangle.

**Key advantages:**
1. **Velocity is split across multiple blade rows** → no single row has to handle the full $V_1$. This reduces the relative velocity and hence **frictional losses**.
2. **Higher work extraction per stage** — useful when a single row cannot absorb the full kinetic energy without excessive blade speed.
3. **Reduces exit kinetic energy** losses since each row can be designed to have near-zero exit whirl.
4. **Compact design** — fewer pressure stages needed for the same overall pressure drop.

**Comparison table:**

| Parameter | Single-Stage Impulse | Curtis (Compound) Impulse |
|---|---|---|
| Number of moving blade rows | 1 | 2 or 3 |
| Nozzle pressure drop | Full | Full |
| Blade speed required | Higher | Lower (for same output) |
| Efficiency | ~85% (max $\cos^2\alpha$) | ~88–90% (better) |
| Complexity | Low | Higher |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q (a).** With the help of a velocity diagram, derive an expression for the **blade efficiency** of a single-stage impulse turbine. State the condition for maximum blade efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw the velocity triangle** at inlet with $V_1$ at angle $\alpha$ to the blade velocity $u$, and $V_{r1}$ closing the triangle.

**Step 2 — K.E. supplied by nozzle** per kg:
$$\text{K.E. supplied} = \frac{V_1^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Work done per kg** (assuming ideal exit, $V_{w2} = 0$):
$$W = u \cdot V_{w1} = u \cdot V_1 \cos\alpha$$

**Step 4 — Define blade efficiency** as the ratio of work done to the K.E. supplied:
$$\eta_b = \frac{W}{V_1^2 / 2} = \frac{u V_1 \cos\alpha}{V_1^2 / 2} = \frac{2 u \cos\alpha}{V_1}$$

**Step 5 — Condition for maximum efficiency:**
Treating $\eta_b$ as a function of $u$ for a given $V_1$ and $\alpha$, the efficiency is linear in $u$. But the upper limit of $u$ is constrained by the **physical requirement that $V_{r1}$ must be real and non-negative** and that the blade angle $\beta_1$ is finite.

The maximum theoretical efficiency occurs when the blade is symmetric ($\beta_1 = \beta_2$) and exit whirl is zero. Substituting $V_{w1} = u + V_{r1}\cos\beta_1$ and eliminating variables using the inlet triangle:

$$\eta_{b,\text{max}} = \cos^2\alpha$$

**Condition for maximum blade efficiency:**
$$\frac{u}{V_1} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{2} \quad \text{(optimum blade-speed ratio)}$$

For $\alpha = 20°$, $\eta_{b,\text{max}} = 0.883$ or **88.3%**.

**Q (b).** In a **50% reaction turbine (Parsons stage)**, the mean blade speed is **120 m/s**, the blade angle at outlet is **30°**, and the flow velocity is constant. If the absolute velocity at exit makes an angle of **15°** with the axial direction, find:
(i) The work done per kg of steam.
(ii) The degree of reaction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Flow velocity (constant across stage):**
$$V_f = V_{f1} = V_{f2}$$

**Step 2 — From exit geometry:**
$V_{f2} = V_2 \sin 15°$, and $V_{w2} = V_2 \cos 15°$.

**Step 3 — From outlet blade angle:**
$$V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 = 120 - V_{r2} \cos 30°$$

Also, $V_{f2} = V_{r2}\sin\beta_2 = V_{r2}\sin 30° = 0.5 V_{r2}$.

**Step 4 — Since $V_{f1} = V_{f2}$ (constant flow velocity), and for a 50% reaction stage, $V_{r1} = V_{r2}$ (symmetric relative velocity), we have $V_{f1} = V_{f2}$.**

From the exit triangle: $V_{f2} = V_{r2} \sin 30° = 0.5 V_{r2}$.

Using the outlet velocity triangle: $V_{w2} = 120 - V_{r2}\cos 30°$.

**Step 5 — Also from outlet:** $V_{w2} = V_2 \cos 15°$ and $V_{f2} = V_2 \sin 15°$.

So $\frac{V_{w2}}{V_{f2}} = \cot 15°$ and $\frac{V_{r2}\cos\beta_2}{V_{r2}\sin\beta_2} = \cot\beta_2$.

Using vector relation at outlet: $V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta_2$, and $V_{f2} = V_{r2}\sin\beta_2$.

$$\cot 15° = \frac{120 - V_{r2}\cos 30°}{V_{r2}\sin 30°}$$

$$3.732 = \frac{120 - 0.866 V_{r2}}{0.5 V_{r2}}$$

$$1.866 V_{r2} = 120 - 0.866 V_{r2}$$

$$2.732 V_{r2} = 120 \implies V_{r2} = 43.92 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — $V_{w2}$:**
$$V_{w2} = 120 - 43.92 \times 0.866 = 120 - 38.04 = 81.96 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7 — For a 50% reaction stage (symmetric), $V_{w1} = V_{w2} = 81.96$ m/s** (with $V_{w1}$ in same direction as $u$).

**Step 8 — Work done per kg:**
$$W = u(V_{w1} + V_{w2}) = 120 \times (81.96 + 81.96) = 120 \times 163.92 = 19{,}670 \text{ J/kg} \approx 19.67 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 9 — Degree of reaction:** By the definition of a 50% reaction (Parsons) stage:
$$R = 0.5 \text{ or } 50\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — 50% Reaction Assumption:** Students often forget that in a true **50% reaction turbine**, the velocity triangles are **symmetric**, meaning $V_{w1} = V_{w2}$ and $V_{r1} = V_{r2}$. This symmetry lets you skip several algebraic steps. Always check the problem statement for keywords like *"symmetric"*, *"Parsons stage"*, or *"50% reaction"* before solving.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Velocity Diagrams (Module 2)**

- The **velocity triangle** is built from three vectors: $V$, $u$, $V_r$ — satisfying $\vec{V} = \vec{u} + \vec{V_r}$.
- **Mean blade speed** is computed as $u = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$ — always the first step in any numerical problem.
- **Whirl component** $V_w = V \cos\alpha$ (does work on the blade). **Flow component** $V_f = V \sin\alpha$ (passes axially through the blade).
- **Work done per kg:** $W = u(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})$ — use **+** for under-expanded exit (same direction whirl), **−** for ideal exit (no whirl).
- **Power developed:** $P = m \cdot W$ (in Watts, with $m$ in kg/s and $W$ in J/kg).
- **Blade efficiency** $\eta_b = \frac{2 u (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{V_1^2}$ — maximum value is $\cos^2\alpha$ when $u/V_1 = \cos\alpha / 2$.
- **Degree of reaction** $R$ = fraction of enthalpy drop in the rotor. $R = 0$ for impulse, $R = 0.5$ for Parsons, $R = 1$ for pure reaction.
- **Compound (Curtis) stage** uses multiple moving blade rows on the same wheel — improves efficiency by splitting velocity.
- **Sign convention:** $V_{w2}$ in the direction of blade motion → positive; opposite → negative. Always check before substituting.
- **Symmetric triangles** imply $V_1 = V_2$, $V_{r1} = V_{r2}$, $\alpha_1 = \beta_2$, $\beta_1 = \alpha_2$ — a key property of 50% reaction (Parsons) stages.
- **Axial thrust** on blades $= m(V_{f1} - V_{f2})$; if flow velocity is constant, axial thrust is zero.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always label your velocity diagram clearly in the answer sheet — the examiner awards marks for the *correct diagram* even if the final numerical answer is wrong. Never skip the figure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compounding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Compounding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compounding** is defined as the process of expanding steam in a reciprocating steam engine (or turbine) in **multiple successive stages** (HP → IP → LP) rather than in a single large cylinder, in order to limit the physical size of the cylinders, reduce cylinder condensation losses, and extract maximum work from the steam.

In a **simple (single-stage) expansion engine**, the entire pressure drop from boiler pressure $P_1$ to condenser pressure $P_3$ occurs inside *one* cylinder. This is mechanically inefficient for high pressure ratios. **Compounding** resolves this by splitting the expansion across two or more cylinders, each handling a fraction of the total pressure drop.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Staircase" of Steam:**
> Imagine releasing a fully pressurized fire extinguisher all at once through one large nozzle — most of the energy is wasted as uncontrolled turbulence. Now imagine releasing it through **three progressively larger nozzles** in a row. Each nozzle handles a *portion* of the drop, the flow stays smoother, and you extract more useful thrust.
>
> That is compounding: a *staged, controlled descent* of steam pressure — from high → intermediate → low — using cylinders of progressively **larger volume but lower pressure rating**.

### 1.3 Why Compounding is Needed (Syllabus Highlight)

A single-cylinder engine with a high expansion ratio (e.g., $P_1/P_3 = 80$) would require an impractically large cylinder. The **physical constants and metrics** involved are:

- **Expansion Ratio** $r = P_1 / P_3$ — typically **10 to 100** in steam plants.
- **Cylinder Volume** scales **linearly** with the expansion ratio.
- A 10× pressure ratio requires a cylinder roughly **10× larger** in swept volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The *physical cylinder size*, *mechanical balance*, and *condensation losses* (reheating of expanded steam by hot cylinder walls) are the three engineering drivers for compounding.

### 1.4 Types of Compounding (Overview)

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies compounding by **mechanical arrangement**:

| Type | Description | Key Feature |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Tandem Compound** | HP and LP cylinders on the *same* crankshaft, in line | Single shaft, compact |
| **Cross Compound** | HP and LP on *separate* crankshafts | Flexible, balanced |
| **Receiver Type** | An intermediate reservoir between HP and LP | Allows pressure equalization |
| **Reheating (Regenerative)** | Steam is re-heated between stages | Improves thermal efficiency |
| **Three-Stage** | HP + IP + LP cylinders | Used for very high pressure ratios |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step by Step

The compounding process follows a controlled, staged expansion:

- **Step 1 — High Pressure (HP) Stage:** Steam from the boiler at pressure $P_1$ and temperature $T_1$ enters the **HP cylinder** and expands down to the **intermediate pressure** $P_2$. The HP cylinder is *small* and built for high pressure.
- **Step 2 — Receiver / Inter-stage Vessel:** The partially expanded steam (now at $P_2$) is collected in a **receiver**. This dampens pressure fluctuations from the HP cylinder's pulsating exhaust.
- **Step 3 — Low Pressure (LP) Stage:** Steam from the receiver enters the **LP cylinder**, which is *physically larger* (more volume) but rated for lower pressure. It expands from $P_2$ to the condenser pressure $P_3$.
- **Step 4 — Exhaust:** Steam leaves the LP cylinder at $P_3$ and is dumped into the **condenser**.

### 2.2 The Core 'Why' Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why a smaller HP cylinder?** Because high-pressure cylinders are mechanically stressed; smaller diameters keep wall thickness manageable.
> - **Why a larger LP cylinder?** Because the steam has expanded and occupies a larger volume — a *larger* swept volume is needed to fully utilize the steam.
> - **Why a receiver?** To act as a *buffer* — HP exhaust is pulsating; the receiver smooths the flow into the LP cylinder.

### 2.3 Cylinder Ratio — The Key Design Parameter

The **cylinder ratio** is the most important design quantity in compounding:

$$R_c = \frac{V_{LP}}{V_{HP}}$$

For a **two-stage compound engine with equal pressure ratios per stage** (the KTU standard assumption):

$$R_c = \sqrt{r} = \sqrt{\frac{P_1}{P_3}} = \sqrt{\frac{V_3}{V_1}}$$

This is derived from the principle of **equal work distribution** between the two stages. For an *n*-stage engine:

$$R_{c,n} = r^{1/n}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the exam-ready formula set. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Total expansion ratio | $r = P_1 / P_3$ | dimensionless | Always $\geq 4$ in practice |
| 2 | Intermediate pressure (equal ratio) | $P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_3}$ | bar or kPa | Geometric mean |
| 3 | Cylinder ratio (2-stage) | $R_c = \sqrt{r}$ | dimensionless | LP is $\sqrt{r}$ times larger |
| 4 | Cylinder ratio (n-stage) | $R_c = r^{1/n}$ | dimensionless | Generalization |
| 5 | HP stage work (isothermal / $pV=$ const) | $W_{HP} = P_1 V_1 \ln(r)$ | kJ | For hyperbolic expansion |
| 6 | LP stage work | $W_{LP} = P_2 V_2 \ln(r_2)$ | kJ | $r_2 = P_2 / P_3$ |
| 7 | Total work per kg steam | $W_{tot} = W_{HP} + W_{LP}$ | kJ/kg | Sum of stage works |
| 8 | Work ratio per stage | $W_{HP} : W_{LP} = 1 : 1$ | — | Equal work assumption |
| 9 | Mean Effective Pressure (HP) | $\text{MEP}_{HP} = \dfrac{W_{HP}}{V_1}$ | kPa | $V_1$ is HP swept volume |
| 10 | Indicated Power per cylinder | $\text{IP} = \dfrac{P_{mean} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N}{60}$ | kW | $N$ in rpm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Cylinder ratio directly determines the **physical dimensions** of LP and HP cylinders in any compound steam engine. Modern thermal power stations use this principle in **reheat turbines** (HP + IP + LP turbine stages), which are the rotational analog of compounded reciprocating engines.

### 2.5 Comparison of Compounding Arrangements

| Feature | Tandem Compound | Cross Compound |
|---------|-----------------|----------------|
| Crankshaft | Common | Separate |
| Footprint | Compact | Larger |
| Mechanical Balance | Poor (large unbalanced forces) | Excellent |
| Maintenance | Difficult (one shaft) | Easy (isolated cylinders) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Use Case | Marine engines, locomotives | Large power plants |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Intermediate Pressure for Equal Pressure Ratios

**Given:** Two-stage compound engine. $P_1$ = initial pressure, $P_3$ = exhaust pressure.

**Goal:** Find $P_2$ such that the pressure ratio in HP equals the pressure ratio in LP.

**Assumption (KTU standard):** Equal work and equal pressure ratio per stage.

**Step 1 — Define the two stage pressure ratios:**

$$r_1 = \frac{P_1}{P_2} \quad \text{and} \quad r_2 = \frac{P_2}{P_3}$$

**Step 2 — Total expansion ratio:**

$$r = \frac{P_1}{P_3} = \frac{P_1}{P_2} \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_3} = r_1 \cdot r_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply equal ratio condition $r_1 = r_2$:**

$$r_1 = r_2 = \sqrt{r}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_2$:**

$$P_2 = \frac{P_1}{r_1} = \frac{P_1}{\sqrt{r}} = \frac{P_1}{\sqrt{P_1/P_3}} = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_3}$$

$$\boxed{P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The intermediate pressure is the **geometric mean** of $P_1$ and $P_3$. This is the same form as the root-mean-square, and it is the KTU board's most-tested formula on compounding.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cylinder Ratio for Equal Work Distribution

**Given:** Two-stage compound engine, hyperbolic expansion ($pV = \text{const}$), equal work per stage.

**Step 1 — Work done in HP cylinder** (expansion from $P_1$ to $P_2$ with volume ratio $V_1/V_2$):

$$W_{HP} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = P_1 V_1 \ln(r_1)$$

**Step 2 — Work done in LP cylinder:**

$$W_{LP} = P_2 V_2 \ln(r_2)$$

**Step 3 — Apply continuity of mass flow** (same steam passes through both cylinders):

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{P_2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_2$ into the LP work equation:**

$$W_{LP} = P_2 \cdot \frac{P_1 V_1}{P_2} \cdot \ln(r_2) = P_1 V_1 \ln(r_2)$$

**Step 5 — Apply equal work condition $W_{HP} = W_{LP}$:**

$$P_1 V_1 \ln(r_1) = P_1 V_1 \ln(r_2)$$

$$\ln(r_1) = \ln(r_2) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad r_1 = r_2$$

**Step 6 — This confirms $r_1 = r_2 = \sqrt{r}$.** The cylinder volume ratio:

$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{P_1}{P_2} = r_1 = \sqrt{r}$$

$$\boxed{R_c = \frac{V_{LP}}{V_{HP}} = \sqrt{\frac{P_1}{P_3}} = \sqrt{r}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Work Done in Two-Stage Compound Engine

**Step 1 — Total work is the sum of stage works:**

$$W_{tot} = W_{HP} + W_{LP}$$

**Step 2 — Using equal work per stage:**

$$W_{HP} = W_{LP} = \frac{W_{tot}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Total work (with hyperbolic expansion throughout):**

$$W_{tot} = P_1 V_1 \ln(r_1) + P_2 V_2 \ln(r_2)$$

**Step 4 — Since $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ and $r_1 = r_2$:**

$$W_{tot} = 2 \cdot P_1 V_1 \ln(\sqrt{r}) = P_1 V_1 \ln(r)$$

**Step 5 — Note that this is *identical* to a single-stage expansion** of the same steam from $P_1$ to $P_3$. The thermodynamic total work is conserved; compounding only **redistributes** it between stages.

$$W_{tot} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_3}\right) = P_1 V_1 \ln(r)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Compounding does **not** increase the total work of expansion (thermodynamics is unchanged), but it **reduces cylinder size**, **reduces condensation losses**, and **improves mechanical balance**. This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question.

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (For Internal Practice)

**Problem:** A two-stage compound steam engine receives steam at **15 bar** and exhausts to a condenser at **0.2 bar**. Find (a) intermediate pressure, (b) cylinder ratio, (c) ratio of work done in each cylinder.

**Given:** $P_1 = 15 \text{ bar}$, $P_3 = 0.2 \text{ bar}$.

**Step (a) — Intermediate pressure:**

$$P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_3} = \sqrt{15 \times 0.2} = \sqrt{3.0}$$

$$P_2 = 1.732 \text{ bar}$$

**Step (b) — Total expansion ratio:**

$$r = \frac{P_1}{P_3} = \frac{15}{0.2} = 75$$

**Step (c) — Cylinder ratio:**

$$R_c = \sqrt{r} = \sqrt{75} = 8.66$$

**Step (d) — Work ratio per stage:** By equal-work assumption, $W_{HP} : W_{LP} = 1 : 1$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference

| Parameter | HP Cylinder | LP Cylinder |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Inlet Pressure | $P_1$ | $P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 P_3}$ |
| Exit Pressure | $P_2$ | $P_3$ |
| Pressure Ratio | $r_1 = \sqrt{r}$ | $r_2 = \sqrt{r}$ |
| Swept Volume | $V_1$ | $V_2 = \sqrt{r} \cdot V_1$ |
| Work Done | $W_{HP}$ | $W_{LP} = W_{HP}$ |
| Physical Size | Small, thick walls | Large, thin walls |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Tandem Compound Steam Engine

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below represents a **Tandem Compound Engine** where the HP and LP cylinders share a single crankshaft.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["BOILER P1 15 bar"] -->|Steam Inlet| B["HP CYLINDER Small High Pressure"]
    B -->|Exhaust P2 1.7 bar| C["RECEIVER Intermediate Vessel"]
    C -->|Steam Inlet| D["LP CYLINDER Large Low Pressure"]
    D -->|Exhaust P3 0.2 bar| E["CONDENSER Vacuum"]
    B -.->|Tandem Mount| F["COMMON CRANKSHAFT"]
    D -.->|Tandem Mount| F
    F -->|Output| G["LOAD Generator or Pump"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cross-Compound Steam Engine

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["BOILER P1"] -->|Steam Inlet| B["HP CYLINDER"]
    B -->|Exhaust P2| C["RECEIVER"]
    C -->|Steam Inlet| D["LP CYLINDER"]
    D -->|Exhaust P3| E["CONDENSER"]
    B -->|Drive Shaft 1| F["HP CRANKSHAFT"]
    D -->|Drive Shaft 2| G["LP CRANKSHAFT"]
```
