```mermaid
    F -->|Gear Coupling| H["COMBINED LOAD"]
    G -->|Gear Coupling| H
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multi-Stage Compounding

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph stage1["STAGE 1 HIGH PRESSURE"]
        A1["Boiler Inlet P1 T1"] --> A2["Throttle Valve"]
        A2 --> A3["HP Cylinder Expansion to P2"]
    end
    subgraph stage2["STAGE 2 INTERMEDIATE RECEIVER"]
        B1["Receiver Vessel Volume VR"] --> B2["Pressure Stabilization"]
    end
    subgraph stage3["STAGE 3 LOW PRESSURE"]
        C1["LP Cylinder Expansion to P3"] --> C2["Exhaust to Condenser"]
    end
    A3 -->|Steam at P2| B1
    B2 -->|Smoothed Steam| C1
    C1 -->|Spent Steam P3| C2
```

### 4.4 Schematic Logic Flow — Design Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["Start: Given P1 and P3"] --> stepB["Compute r = P1 divided by P3"]
    stepB --> stepC["Compute P2 = sqrt of P1 times P3"]
    stepC --> stepD["Compute Rc = sqrt of r"]
    stepD --> stepE["Assume swept volume V1"]
    stepE --> stepF["Compute V2 = Rc times V1"]
    stepF --> stepG["Verify W HP equals W LP"]
    stepG -->|Pass| stepH["End: Design Complete"]
    stepG -->|Fail| stepI["Adjust V1 and Reiterate"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "compounding" as applied to steam engines. List the different types of compounding with a brief note on each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Compounding is the process of expanding steam in a reciprocating steam engine in **two or more successive stages** (HP, IP, LP) instead of a single cylinder, in order to limit cylinder dimensions, reduce condensation losses, and improve mechanical balance.

**Types (1.5 marks):**
- **Tandem compound** — HP and LP cylinders on a common crankshaft, in line.
- **Cross compound** — HP and LP on separate crankshafts coupled through gearing.
- **Receiver type** — includes a pressure-equalizing vessel between stages.
- **Reheating type** — steam is re-superheated between stages.
- **Three-stage** — HP + IP + LP for very high expansion ratios.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State any **four** advantages of compounding steam engines over simple expansion engines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Cylinder size is reduced** — high and low pressures are handled in separate, physically smaller cylinders.
2. **Reduced cylinder condensation losses** — smaller temperature range per cylinder.
3. **Better mechanical balance** — forces are distributed across multiple cranks (esp. cross compound).
4. **Improved steam economy** — closer approach to isothermal expansion.
5. **Larger expansion ratios are practically feasible.**
6. **Steadier turning moment** due to staged expansion.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain with neat block diagrams the construction and working of:
(i) a **Tandem Compound** steam engine, and
(ii) a **Cross-Compound** steam engine.

Highlight the key mechanical differences and typical applications.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A two-stage compound steam engine receives steam at **12 bar** and exhausts to a condenser at **0.15 bar**. Determine:
1. The intermediate pressure $P_2$ assuming equal pressure ratios.
2. The cylinder ratio $R_c$.
3. The ratio of work done in HP and LP cylinders, assuming hyperbolic expansion and equal work distribution.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**(i) Tandem Compound Engine (3.5 marks):**
- **Construction:** HP and LP cylinders are mounted **in line** on a **common piston rod and crankshaft**. The HP cylinder is smaller in diameter and built for high pressure; the LP cylinder is larger in diameter.
- **Working:** Steam from the boiler enters the HP cylinder, expands to the intermediate pressure, and exhausts into a **receiver**. From the receiver, it enters the LP cylinder, expands to condenser pressure, and is exhausted.
- **Application:** Marine engines, locomotives — wherever a **compact axial layout** is needed.

**(ii) Cross-Compound Engine (3.5 marks):**
- **Construction:** HP and LP cylinders are mounted on **separate crankshafts**, typically with cylinders at right angles or side-by-side. The two shafts are coupled through **gears or chains** to drive a common load.
- **Working:** Same thermodynamic sequence as tandem, but mechanically decoupled.
- **Application:** Large stationary power plants — where **mechanical balance and accessibility** for maintenance are priorities.

**Key Differences Table (to be drawn by student):**

| Feature | Tandem | Cross-Compound |
|---------|--------|----------------|
| Crankshaft | One | Two |
| Footprint | Compact | Larger |
| Balance | Moderate | Excellent |
| Maintenance | Difficult | Easy |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P_1 = 12 \text{ bar}$, $P_3 = 0.15 \text{ bar}$.

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Total expansion ratio:**

$$r = \frac{P_1}{P_3} = \frac{12}{0.15} = 80$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Intermediate pressure:**

$$P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_3} = \sqrt{12 \times 0.15} = \sqrt{1.8}$$

$$P_2 = 1.342 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Cylinder ratio:**

$$R_c = \sqrt{r} = \sqrt{80} = 8.944$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Work ratio:**

For hyperbolic expansion with equal work assumption:

$$\frac{W_{HP}}{W_{LP}} = 1 : 1$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **forget units** on $P_1$ and $P_3$ — they must be in the *same* units (both bar or both kPa). Mixed units = **0 marks** for the numerical.
> - The intermediate pressure formula $P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 P_3}$ is valid **only** under the equal-pressure-ratio assumption. Stating this assumption is worth 1 mark.
> - For the cylinder ratio, do **not** invert: $R_c = V_{LP}/V_{HP} = \sqrt{r}$, not $1/\sqrt{r}$.

---

### **QUESTION B (Alternative) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss in detail the **reasons for compounding** steam engines. How does compounding address each limitation of simple expansion?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain the difference between a **receiver-type** compound engine and a **reheating-type** compound engine. Under what conditions is each preferred?

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Reasons for Compounding (7 marks):**

**Limitation 1 (2 marks) — Excessive Cylinder Volume:**
In a simple expansion engine, the cylinder must be large enough to accommodate the *fully expanded* steam at low pressure. For high pressure ratios ($r > 10$), this requires impractically large bore. *Compounding splits the expansion into smaller, manageable stages.*

**Limitation 2 (2 marks) — Cylinder Condensation Losses:**
When high-temperature, high-pressure steam enters a cold cylinder wall, the wall temperature is significantly lower. The steam **partially condenses** on the walls during admission, then re-evaporates during expansion — a cyclic loss. *Compounding limits the temperature drop per cylinder, reducing this loss.*

**Limitation 3 (1.5 marks) — Mechanical Forces:**
A large single cylinder produces large reciprocating forces and uneven turning moment. *Multiple smaller cylinders distribute the load.*

**Limitation 4 (1.5 marks) — Cost & Manufacturing:**
A single large cylinder thick enough to resist high pressure is expensive. *Smaller, optimized cylinders are cheaper to manufacture and maintain.*

---

**Part (b) — Receiver vs Reheating Type (7 marks):**

| Feature | Receiver Type (3.5 marks) | Reheating Type (3.5 marks) |
|---------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Intermediate Equipment | A simple **pressure-stabilizing vessel** | A **reheater** (heat exchanger) |
| Steam Condition in LP | Saturated or wet | **Re-superheated**, dry |
| Purpose | Smooth out pressure pulsations | Restore temperature & dryness |
| Thermal Efficiency | Moderate | Higher (reheat cycle benefit) |
| Complexity & Cost | Low | Higher (reheater required) |
| Preferred When | Moderate pressure ratios, cost-sensitive | High pressure ratios, efficiency-critical |

**Reheater working:** Exhaust steam from the HP cylinder passes through a **reheater** (often bled steam or live steam from the boiler) before entering the LP cylinder. This brings the steam back to a higher temperature, increasing its enthalpy and the available work in the LP stage.

**Selection Criterion:** Receiver type is preferred for **small to medium** plants where capital cost dominates. Reheating type is preferred for **large central power stations** where thermal efficiency justifies the additional investment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **conflate receiver with reheater** — they are *not* the same. A receiver only stabilizes pressure; a reheater transfers heat.
> - In Part (a), students forget to *quantify* the cylinder condensation problem. Mentioning the **temperature swing per cylinder** is a 2-mark trigger.
> - In Part (b), drawing **both schematics** earns 2 bonus marks under KTU's "neat sketch" policy.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Compounding (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Multi-stage expansion of steam across HP → IP/LP cylinders.
- **Primary driver:** Limit cylinder size and reduce condensation losses.
- **Total expansion ratio:** $r = P_1 / P_3$.
- **Intermediate pressure (equal ratio):** $P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_3}$ (geometric mean).
- **Cylinder ratio (2-stage):** $R_c = V_{LP} / V_{HP} = \sqrt{r}$.
- **Generalized (n-stage):** $R_{c,n} = r^{1/n}$.
- **Work per stage (equal-work assumption):** $W_{HP} = W_{LP} = P_1 V_1 \ln(\sqrt{r})$.
- **Total work conserved:** $W_{tot} = P_1 V_1 \ln(r)$ — **same as a simple expansion** (compounding only *redistributes* work).
- **Tandem compound:** HP + LP on **same** crankshaft (compact, marine use).
- **Cross compound:** HP + LP on **separate** crankshafts (balanced, large power plants).
- **Receiver type:** Intermediate vessel for pressure smoothing.
- **Reheating type:** Re-superheats steam between stages for higher efficiency.
- **Three-stage:** Used when $r > 100$ (rare in reciprocating engines, common in turbines).
- **KTU board tip:** Always state the *equal-pressure-ratio* assumption before using $P_2 = \sqrt{P_1 P_3}$.
- **Unit discipline:** Keep all pressures in the *same* unit (bar or kPa) — no mixing.
- **Examiner trap:** The total work is **unchanged** by compounding — do not claim otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reheat Factor, Blade Height
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Reheat Factor, Blade Height ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Reheat Factor (RF) **Formal KTU Definition:** The **Reheat Factor (RF)** of a multi-stage steam turbine is defined as the ratio of the **cumulative sum of the actual heat drops in all the stages** to the **isentropic heat drop available across the entire turbine**. Mathematically: $$RF = \frac{\sum (\text{Actual heat drop in each stage})}{(\text{Isentropic heat drop of the whole turbine})} = \frac{\sum \Delta H_{actual}}{\Delta H_{isentropic}}$$ It is a dimensionless factor that quantifies the internal thermodynamic inefficiencies inside a multi-stage turbine. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** > [!NOTE] > Imagine you are walking down a long staircase (the turbine) from the 10th floor to the ground floor. In an ideal (isentropic) world, every step you take drops you by exactly the same height — your total descent equals the sum of all individual steps. However, in reality, some steps are slippery, some are worn out, and you lose energy to friction, heat, and bouncing. To compensate for these losses, your actual total descent on the staircase becomes **greater** than the ideal net drop. The Reheat Factor is exactly that "extra descent" multiplier — it's always greater than 1 because real steam "reheats" internally inside the turbine due to friction and leakage losses. **Key Physical Insight — Why RF > 1:** - Inside the turbine, the working steam suffers from **internal friction, blade windage losses, leakage past clearances, and partial admission losses**. - These losses generate heat, which is **absorbed back by the steam itself** (reheating effect). - This reheating causes the steam to expand **beyond** the isentropic point in later stages, increasing the actual enthalpy drop per stage beyond what pure isentropic theory predicts. - Typical RF values: **Curtis stage ≈ 1.02–1.04**, **Rateau stage ≈ 1.04–1.06**, **Parsons (reaction) stage ≈ 1.06–1.08**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Reheat Factor is *not* the same as "Reheating in a power plant cycle." It is a *purely internal, stage-by-stage phenomenon* that occurs without any external heat supply. --- ### 1.2 Blade Height **Formal KTU Definition:** **Blade Height (l)** of a turbine stage is the **mean radial length of the moving blades** measured from the root (at the rotor surface) to the tip (at the casing inner surface). It is one of the most critical geometric parameters in turbine design, directly determining the **annular flow area** through which steam passes. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** > [!NOTE] > Think of a turbine stage as a giant circular "fan" inside a cylindrical pipe. The blades are like the fan blades sticking out from the central shaft. **Blade height** is simply *how long these blades are*. Longer blades = bigger "mouth" = more steam can pass through per second. As steam expands from high pressure (small volume) to low pressure (large volume), the blades must grow progressively taller in the LP (Low Pressure) stages — just like how a megaphone gets wider toward the exit to handle the larger volume of sound waves. **Fundamental Continuity Equation:** Mass flow rate ($\dot{m}$) remains constant through every stage: $$\dot{m} = \frac{A \cdot C_a}{v} = \frac{\pi \cdot D_m \cdot l \cdot C_a}{v}$$ Where: - $D_m$ = Mean diameter of the blade ring - $l$ = Blade height - $C_a$ = Axial (flow) velocity of steam - $v$ = Specific volume of steam at that stage - $A = \pi D_m l$ = Annular flow area Solving for blade height: $$l = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D_m \cdot C_a}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** As steam moves from HP (High Pressure) to LP stages, its **specific volume (v) increases enormously** (often by a factor of 100–500). Therefore, **blade height grows dramatically** in the rear stages. This is why LP turbine blades in a 500 MW unit can be **1 meter tall**, while HP blades may be only **20–50 mm**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Reheat Factor — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown The Reheat Factor arises due to three principal irreversible losses inside the turbine: 1. **Stage Friction Losses** — Friction of steam with stationary and moving blades generates heat, partially absorbed by steam (reheating). 2. **Leakage Losses** — Steam leaking past the diaphragm and gland clearances causes additional heat absorption by the main steam. 3. **Partial Admission / Disc Windage Losses** — In Curtis stages, when only a fraction of the circumference admits steam, residual heat is added back. **Effect on the h-s (Mollier) Diagram:** - In an **ideal isentropic expansion** (1→2s), steam drops in enthalpy by $\Delta H_{isentropic}$. - In an **actual expansion with reheat** (1→2), the actual path lies **above the isentropic line**. - For a multi-stage turbine, each stage has its own small irreversibility, and the steam enters the next stage at a *higher actual enthalpy* than the ideal. **Velocity Triangle Relationship:** The actual work done per stage = $\frac{C_{w1} \pm C_{w2}}{g} \cdot U$ (velocity triangle-driven), and summation across all stages gives the total actual work. --- ### 2.2 Blade Height — Theoretical Framework **Why Blade Height Increases in LP Stages:** The continuity equation shows that **$l \propto v$** (specific volume). Since $v$ grows as pressure drops (according to $Pv = RT$ for superheated steam), the LP blades must be correspondingly longer. **Design Considerations for Blade Height:** | Parameter | Effect on Blade Height | |---|---| | ↑ Specific volume ($v$) | ↑ Blade height | | ↑ Mass flow ($\dot{m}$) | ↑ Blade height | | ↑ Mean diameter ($D_m$) | ↓ Blade height | | ↑ Axial velocity ($C_a$) | ↓ Blade height | **Constraints on Maximum Blade Height:** - **Mechanical strength** — Longer blades experience high centrifugal stress: $\sigma = \rho \cdot \omega^2 \cdot l \cdot D_m$ - **Tip clearance losses** — Very long blades suffer more leakage at tips - **Vibration and aeroelasticity** — Tall slender blades risk flutter **Practical limit:** Modern LP blades reach up to **1016 mm (40 inches)** in large 1000 MW units. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $RF = \frac{\sum \Delta h_{actual}}{\Delta h_{isentropic}}$ | Reheat Factor definition | Dimensionless | | 2 | $\Delta h_{stage} = \frac{C_{w1} \pm C_{w2}}{g} \cdot U$ | Actual stage enthalpy drop | J/kg | | 3 | $\dot{m} = \frac{\pi D_m l C_a}{v}$ | Mass flow continuity | kg/s | | 4 | $l = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v}{\pi D_m C_a}$ | Blade height | m | | 5 | $v_{stage} = \frac{v_{in} + v_{out}}{2}$ | Mean specific volume in a stage | m³/kg | | 6 | $\sigma_{centrifugal} = \rho_b \cdot \omega^2 \cdot l \cdot (D_m + l)$ | Centrifugal stress in blade | N/m² | | 7 | $RF_{Parsons} \approx 1.06 - 1.08$ | Typical for 50% reaction turbines | — | | 8 | $RF_{Curtis} \approx 1.02 - 1.04$ | Typical for single-stage Curtis | — | | 9 | $l \propto v \propto \frac{1}{P}$ (approx.) | Specific volume grows as P falls | m | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Reheat Factor from First Principles **Given:** A multi-stage turbine with $n$ stages. Steam enters stage $i$ at enthalpy $h_{i-1}$ and exits at enthalpy $h_i$. **Step 1: Actual heat drop in stage $i$** $$\Delta h_{actual, i} = h_{i-1} - h_i$$ **Step 2: Sum across all stages** $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta h_{actual, i} = (h_0 - h_1) + (h_1 - h_2) + \dots + (h_{n-1} - h_n) = h_0 - h_n$$ This is the **total actual enthalpy drop** for the turbine. **Step 3: Isentropic heat drop for whole turbine** The isentropic exit enthalpy is $h_s$ (at exit pressure $P_{exit}$ but starting from inlet entropy $s_0$). $$\Delta h_{isentropic} = h_0 - h_s$$ **Step 4: Definition of Reheat Factor** $$RF = \frac{h_0 - h_n}{h_0 - h_s}$$ Since $h_n < h_s$ (actual exit enthalpy is lower than isentropic — see Mollier diagram), the numerator is **larger** than the denominator: $$h_0 - h_n > h_0 - h_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad RF > 1$$ **Step 5: Alternative Stage-wise Expression** From velocity triangles, the work done per kg of steam per stage is: $$W_{stage} = \frac{U}{g}(C_{w1} \pm C_{w2})$$ Summing across all stages gives the **actual** work, while the isentropic work is: $$W_{isentropic} = \Delta h_{isentropic} = h_0 - h_s$$ Therefore: $$RF = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{U_i}{g}(C_{w1,i} \pm C_{w2,i})}{h_0 - h_s}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Blade Height from Continuity Equation **Step 1: Annular flow area** For a ring of mean diameter $D_m$ and blade height $l$, the flow area (cross-section perpendicular to flow) is: $$A = \pi D_m \cdot l$$ **Step 2: Mass conservation** Mass flow rate is constant at every station: $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot C_a = \frac{A \cdot C_a}{v}$$ **Step 3: Substituting A** $$\dot{m} = \frac{\pi D_m l C_a}{v}$$ **Step 4: Solving for $l$** $$l = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v}{\pi D_m C_a}$$ **Step 5: Mean specific volume in a stage** Since specific volume changes across the stage, we use the average: $$v_{stage} = \frac{v_{inlet} + v_{outlet}}{2}$$ **Step 6: Substituting into blade height formula** $$l_{stage} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot \left(\frac{v_{in} + v_{out}}{2}\right)}{\pi \cdot D_m \cdot C_a}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** Because $v_{out} \gg v_{in}$ in later stages, designers use the **specific volume at the mean radius at exit conditions**, accounting for temperature, pressure, and quality (in wet steam regions). --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern) **Problem:** A 5-stage turbine receives steam at $h_0 = 3200$ kJ/kg, $s_0 = 6.7$ kJ/kg·K. The isentropic exit enthalpy (at condenser pressure) is $h_s = 2200$ kJ/kg. The actual stage-wise heat drops (in kJ/kg) are: Stage 1 = 180, Stage 2 = 175, Stage 3 = 170, Stage 4 = 165, Stage 5 = 160. Find the Reheat Factor. **Step 1: Total actual heat drop** $$\sum \Delta h_{actual} = 180 + 175 + 170 + 165 + 160 = 850 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2: Isentropic heat drop** $$\Delta h_{isentropic} = h_0 - h_s = 3200 - 2200 = 1000 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3: Compute RF** $$RF = \frac{850}{1000} = 0.85$$ **Step 4: Interpretation** Since RF < 1, this would mean the turbine is **under-performing** (in practice RF > 1 always). This numerical pattern is purely academic. In real turbines: $$h_n < h_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad (h_0 - h_n) > (h_0 - h_s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad RF > 1$$ A realistic example: If actual total drop = 1050 kJ/kg and isentropic drop = 1000 kJ/kg, then **RF = 1.05**. --- ### 3.4 Blade Height Worked Example **Given:** - Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 100$ kg/s - Mean diameter at stage $D_m = 1.5$ m - Axial velocity $C_a = 80$ m/s - Specific volume at this stage $v = 0.30$ m³/kg **Step 1: Apply the formula** $$l = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D_m \cdot C_a}$$ **Step 2: Substitute values** $$l = \frac{100 \times 0.30}{\pi \times 1.5 \times 80}$$ $$l = \frac{30}{376.99}$$ $$l \approx 0.0796 \text{ m} \approx 79.6 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3: Interpretation** This is consistent with an **HP or IP stage** (short blades). For an LP stage where $v \approx 5$ m³/kg: $$l_{LP} = \frac{100 \times 5}{\pi \times 1.5 \times 80} \approx 1.327 \text{ m}$$ Demonstrating how dramatically blade height increases in LP regions. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mollier (h-s) Diagram Showing Reheat Effect ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Point 1: Turbine Inlet
h0, s0, P0 high"] -->|"Isentropic Expansion (Ideal)"| B["Point 2s
hs, s0, Pexit"] A -->|"Actual Expansion with Reheat"| C["Point 2
h2, s2, Pexit"] D["Isentropic Heat Drop = h0 - hs
(Numerator's theoretical equivalent)"] -.-> B E["Actual Heat Drop = h0 - h2
(Greater than isentropic)"] -.-> C F["Reheat Factor RF = h0-h2 / h0-hs
> 1 (always)"] -.-> G["Process Insight:
Reheating raises actual
work output per stage"] C --> G B --> G style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px ``` **Caption:** *The actual expansion path (1→2) lies to the right of the isentropic path (1→2s) due to internal reheating, making the actual enthalpy drop larger than the isentropic drop — hence RF > 1.* --- ### 4.2 Multi-Stage Turbine Architecture with Increasing Blade Heights ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HP["HIGH PRESSURE SECTION"] H1["Stage 1
Blade height: 25 mm
Pressure: 150 bar"] H2["Stage 2
Blade height: 30 mm"] H3["Stage 3
Blade height: 38 mm"] end subgraph IP["INTERMEDIATE PRESSURE SECTION (After Reheat)"] I1["Stage 4
Blade height: 60 mm"] I2["Stage 5
Blade height: 90 mm"] I3["Stage 6
Blade height: 150 mm"] end subgraph LP["LOW PRESSURE SECTION"] L1["Stage 7
Blade height: 250 mm"] L2["Stage 8
Blade height: 450 mm"] L3["Stage 9
Blade height: 850 mm"] L4["Last Stage
Blade height: 1016 mm
(39.4 inches max)"] end H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 C["Steam Inlet
3200 kJ/kg
600 C"] --> H1 L4 --> EX["Exhaust to Condenser
0.05 bar"] style HP fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style IP fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style LP fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006600 style EX fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333333 ``` **Caption:** *Visual schematic showing progressive blade height growth across HP → IP → LP sections of a large utility turbine. Notice the dramatic increase in size due to specific volume expansion.* --- ### 4.3 Blade Height Computation Flow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["INPUT DATA
Mass flow m-dot
Mean diameter Dm
Axial velocity Ca"] --> B["Determine Steam State
at Stage Inlet and Outlet"] B --> C["Compute Specific Volumes
vin and vout
via Steam Tables"] C --> D["Average Specific Volume
v_stage = vin + vout / 2"] D --> E["Apply Continuity Equation
l = m-dot x v_stage
------
pi x Dm x Ca"] E --> F["Check Mechanical Limits
Centrifugal Stress
Tip Clearance Loss"] F --> G{"Stress within
Allowable?"} G -->|"Yes"| H["ACCEPT Blade Height l"] G -->|"No"| I["Reduce l or Increase Dm
or use Twisted Blade"] I --> F style A fill:#e1f5ff style H fill:#90EE90 style I fill:#FFB6C1 ``` **Caption:** *Engineering workflow for designing blade height in a turbine stage — from inputs to validation.* ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define Reheat Factor. Why is its value always greater than unity?** **Model Answer:** Reheat Factor (RF) is the ratio of the cumulative actual heat drop in all stages to the isentropic heat drop of the entire turbine. $$RF = \frac{\sum \Delta h_{actual, i}}{\Delta h_{isentropic}}$$ Its value is greater than unity because of internal irreversibilities — friction, leakage, and disc windage losses — which generate heat that is reabsorbed by the working steam. This internal reheating causes the actual enthalpy drop per stage to exceed the isentropic prediction, making $\sum \Delta h_{actual} > \Delta h_{isentropic}$, hence **RF > 1**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Why are the blades in the low-pressure (LP) section of a steam turbine much longer than those in the high-pressure (HP) section?** **Model Answer:** From the continuity equation $\dot{m} = \frac{\pi D_m l C_a}{v}$, blade height is given by $l = \frac{\dot{m} v}{\pi D_m C_a}$. Since $l \propto v$ (specific volume), and $v$ increases as pressure drops, the LP stages have much larger specific volumes (since $Pv \approx RT$ for superheated steam). Therefore, the LP blades must be proportionally longer to accommodate the increased volumetric flow rate of steam while maintaining the same mass flow and reasonable axial velocity. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a)** Define Reheat Factor and explain the various losses that contribute to it. Discuss how the type of turbine (Curtis, Rateau, Parsons) affects its value. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The Reheat Factor is defined as: $$RF = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta h_{actual, i}}{\Delta h_{isentropic}}$$ **Losses contributing to RF:** 1. **Blade Friction Losses** — Friction between steam and the blade surfaces generates thermal energy that is partially absorbed by the steam. 2. **Leakage Losses** — Steam leaking past the diaphragm (stationary blade) clearances and gland packings causes additional internal energy redistribution. 3. **Partial Admission Losses** — In Curtis single-stage turbines, steam enters only a fraction of the circumference at high pressure, causing pulsation and dissipation. 4. **Disc Windage Losses** — Friction between the rotating discs and surrounding steam. 5. **Nozzle and Diaphragm Losses** — Non-isentropic flow through nozzles. **Effect of Turbine Type on RF:** | Turbine Type | Typical RF | Reason | |---|---|---| | Curtis (single pressure stage) | 1.02 – 1.04 | Large single drop → small relative reheating | | Rateau (simple impulse, many stages) | 1.04 – 1.06 | Moderate reheat effect across multiple stages | | Parsons (50% reaction) | 1.06 – 1.08 | Maximum reheat effect due to distributed losses | The Parsons turbine has the highest RF because losses are distributed across many stages, with each stage contributing some reheating. The Curtis has the lowest RF because the entire pressure drop occurs in one or two stages. --- **(b)** A 6-stage turbine has stage-wise actual heat drops (in kJ/kg): 145, 140, 138, 135, 132, 130. The inlet enthalpy is 3050 kJ/kg and the isentropic exit enthalpy (from h-s diagram) is 2120 kJ/kg. Calculate the Reheat Factor and comment on the result. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Total actual heat drop** $$\sum \Delta h_{actual} = 145 + 140 + 138 + 135 + 132 + 130 = 820 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2: Isentropic heat drop** $$\Delta h_{isentropic} = h_0 - h_s = 3050 - 2120 = 930 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3: Reheat Factor** $$RF = \frac{820}{930} = 0.8817$$ **Step 4: Comment** This calculated value (RF < 1) is **physically unrealistic** in a real turbine because RF must always exceed 1 due to internal reheating. This indicates either (a) an error in problem data, or (b) the values represent ideal isentropic stage drops rather than actual ones. In a properly designed turbine, the actual heat drops should sum to *more* than the isentropic drop. For a real Parsons-type turbine of this configuration, a typical RF would be **1.05 – 1.07**. The corrected interpretation: If we assume the stage drops above are *ideal stage drops*, then the *actual* stage drops (with reheat effect) would be larger, giving RF > 1. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Derive the expression for blade height of a turbine stage from first principles using the continuity equation. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady, one-dimensional flow through the stage. 2. Axial entry of steam (no pre-swirl at inlet). 3. Constant mean diameter across the stage. 4. Uniform axial velocity across the annulus. 5. Mass flow rate is constant through the stage (no leakage considered in this basic derivation). **Derivation:** **Step 1: Annular Flow Area** For a ring of mean diameter $D_m$ and blade height $l$: $$A = \pi D_m \cdot l$$ **Step 2: Mass Flow Rate via Continuity** $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot C_a$$ Since $\rho = \frac{1}{v}$: $$\dot{m} = \frac{A \cdot C_a}{v}$$ **Step 3: Substituting A** $$\dot{m} = \frac{\pi D_m \cdot l \cdot C_a}{v}$$ **Step 4: Solving for Blade Height** $$\boxed{l = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D_m \cdot C_a}}$$ **Step 5: Mean Specific Volume** In practice, the specific volume varies across the stage. Using the arithmetic mean: $$v_{mean} = \frac{v_{inlet} + v_{outlet}}{2}$$ Therefore: $$l = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v_{mean}}{\pi \cdot D_m \cdot C_a}$$ --- **(b)** In a particular stage of a steam turbine, the following data is given: Mass flow rate = 250 kg/s, mean diameter = 1.8 m, axial velocity = 75 m/s. The specific volumes at inlet and outlet of the stage are 0.45 m³/kg and 0.65 m³/kg respectively. Calculate the blade height. If this were an LP stage with specific volume 4.5 m³/kg, what would be the blade height, assuming all other parameters remain the same? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (i): HP/IP Stage** **Step 1: Mean specific volume** $$v_{mean} = \frac{0.45 + 0.65}{2} = 0.55 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Step 2: Apply blade height formula** $$l = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v_{mean}}{\pi \cdot D_m \cdot C_a} = \frac{250 \times 0.55}{\pi \times 1.8 \times 75}$$ $$l = \frac{137.5}{424.115} = 0.3242 \text{ m} \approx 324 \text{ mm}$$ **Part (ii): LP Stage** **Step 1:** New mean specific volume (assuming outlet value 4.5 m³/kg with inlet ≈ 4.0 m³/kg for typical LP): $$v_{mean,LP} = \frac{4.0 + 4.5}{2} = 4.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ (If problem assumes $v = 4.5$ directly, use that value.) **Step 2:** Apply formula: $$l_{LP} = \frac{250 \times 4.25}{\pi \times 1.8 \times 75} = \frac{1062.5}{424.115} = 2.505 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3: Interpretation** The LP blade height (~2.5 m) is about **7.7 times** the HP blade height (324 mm). This confirms the principle that blade height grows dramatically in LP stages due to specific volume expansion — a key reason why utility turbines have such physically large LP sections. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Reheat Factor with Cycle Reheating** — Students often mistakenly relate RF to the Rankine cycle reheat process (external reheat between HP and IP). RF is purely an **internal** phenomenon. > 2. **Forgetting to average specific volume** — In blade height calculations, students frequently use only the outlet specific volume, ignoring inlet conditions. **Always use $v_{mean}$**. > 3. **Units inconsistency** — Specific volume must be in m³/kg, mass flow in kg/s, diameter in m, velocity in m/s. Mixing units leads to wrong answers. > 4. **Wrong interpretation of RF < 1** — Some problems give numerical data that yields RF < 1. This is a **trap**; recognize it as physically unrealistic and explain. > 5. **Forgetting mean diameter** — Students sometimes use root or tip diameter instead of mean diameter $D_m$, which is the standard reference for blade height calculations. > 6. **Ignoring centrifugal stress limits** — Blade height is not just a flow equation outcome; it must satisfy mechanical strength constraints. Long blades require stronger materials or twisted (3D) blade profiles. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** > > **Reheat Factor (RF):** > - ✅ Definition: $RF = \frac{\sum \Delta h_{actual}}{\Delta h_{isentropic}}$ > - ✅ Always **> 1** (between 1.02 and 1.08 typically) > - ✅ Caused by **internal friction, leakage, partial admission losses** > - ✅ Highest in Parsons (reaction) turbines; lowest in Curtis single-stage turbines > - ✅ Independent of overall turbine efficiency — it describes the *internal expansion path*, not the cycle efficiency > - ✅ Related to the area under the expansion curve on the Mollier (h-s) diagram > > **Blade Height (l):** > - ✅ Definition: mean radial length from root to tip of moving blades > - ✅ Formula: $l = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v}{\pi D_m C_a}$ > - ✅ **$l \propto v$** — directly proportional to specific volume > - ✅ **$l \propto 1/P$** approximately — inversely proportional to steam pressure > - ✅ Increases dramatically in LP stages (sometimes >1 meter tall) > - ✅ Constrained by **centrifugal stress, tip clearance, vibration limits** > - ✅ Modern LP blades: up to ~1016 mm (40 inches) in 1000 MW units > - ✅ Twisted (3D) blade profiles used in long LP blades for uniform stress distribution > > **Key Numerical Values to Memorize:** > - RF (Curtis) ≈ 1.02–1.04 > - RF (Rateau) ≈ 1.04–1.06 > - RF (Parsons) ≈ 1.06–1.08 > - Typical axial velocity $C_a$ = 60–120 m/s > - Maximum practical blade height ≈ 1.0–1.1 m

topics.title: Cogeneration and Combined Cycle Power Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cogeneration and Combined Cycle Power Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cogeneration — Formal KTU Definition

**Cogeneration (also called Combined Heat and Power, CHP)** is the simultaneous production of two forms of useful energy — **electrical (or mechanical) energy** and **useful thermal energy (process heat)** — from a single primary fuel source within a single integrated system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Cogeneration is the sequential or simultaneous generation of electrical power and process heat from a single fuel input. It is classified as either a *Topping Cycle* (power first, then heat) or a *Bottoming Cycle* (heat first, then power).

The thermodynamic premise of cogeneration is based on the **Second Law of Efficiency** — conventional power plants reject **50–70%** of fuel energy as low-grade waste heat. Cogeneration captures a substantial portion of this rejected energy to meet thermal demands of industries, thereby pushing the **overall fuel utilization efficiency to 70–90%**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Stove Intuition

Imagine a domestic gas stove:

- A **conventional power plant** is like a stove used *only* to boil water for tea. The heat that warms the kitchen is wasted.
- A **cogeneration system** is like a stove used simultaneously to *boil water for tea* (process heat) AND *warm the kitchen* (useful work) — **two useful outputs from one flame**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric:**
> - **Energy Utilization Factor (EUF)** for a conventional plant ≈ **35–45%**
> - **EUF** for a cogeneration plant ≈ **70–90%**
> - The fuel savings can reach **30–40%** compared to separate heat and power generation.

## 1.3 Combined Cycle Power Generation — Formal Definition

A **Combined Cycle Power Plant (CCPP)** is a power generation arrangement in which **two or more thermodynamic cycles** operate in series, such that the **waste heat rejected from one cycle becomes the input energy for the next cycle**. The most common configuration is the **Brayton–Rankine combined cycle** (Gas Turbine + Steam Turbine).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A combined cycle uses the **hot exhaust gases of a gas turbine (Brayton cycle)** to generate steam in a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG), which then drives a steam turbine (Rankine cycle). This cascading energy usage can push overall plant efficiency to **55–62%**, the highest among commercial thermal power plants.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Exhaust-Powered Booster

Think of a car engine:
- The **main engine** (gas turbine) does the primary work but exhausts hot gases.
- A **turbocharger** (steam turbine) uses the energy of those exhaust gases to extract *additional* work.
- The combined output (engine + turbocharger) gives **far more useful work from the same fuel**.

Combined cycles work on the same principle: **never let hot exhaust go to waste**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Cogeneration Systems

### A. Topping Cycle Cogeneration

In a **topping cycle**, the fuel is first used to **produce electrical/mechanical power**, and the **waste heat** from this process is then recovered to supply thermal/process heat to the user.

**Working Sequence:**

$$\text{Fuel} \rightarrow \text{Prime Mover (Power)} \rightarrow \text{Waste Heat Recovery (Heat)}$$

**Common Configurations:**

| Configuration | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Back-Pressure Turbine** | Steam exhausted at >1 atm used for process heat | Sugar, paper, chemical plants |
| **Extraction-Condensing Turbine** | Steam partly extracted at intermediate pressure | District heating, large industries |
| **Gas Turbine with Heat Recovery** | GT exhaust → HRSG → process steam | Oil refineries, steel plants |
| **Diesel Engine + Heat Recovery** | Engine jacket + exhaust heat recovered | Small industrial units, hospitals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Topping Cycle HPR (Heat-to-Power Ratio):** Generally **> 1**, meaning the plant is **heat-dominated**.

### B. Bottoming Cycle Cogeneration

In a **bottoming cycle**, the fuel is first used to produce **high-temperature process heat** (e.g., in furnaces, kilns), and the **waste heat from the process** is then used to generate power via a waste heat boiler.

**Working Sequence:**

$$\text{Fuel} \rightarrow \text{High-Temp Process} \rightarrow \text{Waste Heat Boiler (Power)}$$

**Typical Applications:**
- Cement kilns
- Iron and steel blast furnaces
- Glass and ceramic furnaces
- Incinerators (municipal solid waste)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bottoming Cycle HPR:** Generally **< 1**, meaning the plant is **power-dominated**.

## 2.2 Performance Parameters of Cogeneration

### A. Energy Utilization Factor (EUF) / Utilization Efficiency

The **overall fuel utilization efficiency** of a cogeneration system is:

$$\text{EUF} \; \eta_u \;=\; \frac{\text{Net Useful Power Output} \;+\; \text{Useful Process Heat Delivered}}{\text{Total Fuel Energy Input}}$$

$$\eta_u \;=\; \frac{W_{net} + Q_{process}}{Q_{fuel}}$$

**Range:** 70% to 90% for well-designed cogeneration systems.

### B. Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR)

$$\text{HPR} \;=\; \frac{\text{Useful Process Heat}}{\text{Net Power Output}} \;=\; \frac{Q_{process}}{W_{net}}$$

- **HPR > 1** → Topping cycle (heat-load dominant)
- **HPR < 1** → Bottoming cycle (power-load dominant)
- **HPR = 1** → Balanced cycle

### C. Heat-to-Power Ratio for the Process (HPR_process)

$$\text{HPR}_{process} \;=\; \frac{\text{Maximum Process Heat Demand}}{\text{Maximum Power Demand}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a feasible cogeneration design:** The cycle's HPR must **match** the process HPR. The KTU exam often asks: *“Is the chosen cogeneration cycle suitable for the process?”* — verify by comparing both HPRs.

### D. Fuel Savings (FS)

$$\text{Fuel Savings (FS)} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{1}{\eta_u} \quad \text{(for comparison vs. separate production)}$$

Where $\eta_u$ is the **utilization efficiency** of the cogeneration plant.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy Utilization Factor | $\eta_u = \dfrac{W_{net} + Q_{process}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | dimensionless | 70–90% typical |
| 2 | Heat-to-Power Ratio | $HPR = \dfrac{Q_{process}}{W_{net}}$ | dimensionless | > 1 topping, < 1 bottoming |
| 3 | Fuel Savings | $FS = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\eta_u}$ | dimensionless | Compared to separate heat + power |
| 4 | Combined Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{cc} = \eta_g + (1-\eta_g)\eta_{b}\eta_s$ | dimensionless | Brayton + Rankine |
| 5 | Approx. Combined Efficiency | $\eta_{cc} \approx 1 - (1-\eta_g)(1-\eta_s)$ | dimensionless | Quick estimation |
| 6 | Gas Turbine Power | $W_g = m_a c_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | kW | Brayton cycle |
| 7 | Steam Turbine Power | $W_s = m_s (h_5 - h_6)$ | kW | Rankine cycle |
| 8 | HRSG Steam Generation | $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{\dot{m}_g c_{pg}(T_4 - T_5)}{h_g - h_f}$ | kg/s | Energy balance on HRSG |
| 9 | Specific Steam Output | $SSO = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_g}$ | kg steam / kg gas | HRSG performance |
| 10 | Net Plant Efficiency | $\eta_{net} = \dfrac{W_{net,total}}{Q_{fuel,total}}$ | dimensionless | HHV basis typically |

> **Notation Key:** $\eta_g$ = gas turbine cycle efficiency, $\eta_s$ = steam cycle efficiency, $\eta_b$ = HRSG effectiveness, $T_3$ = TIT (turbine inlet temperature), $T_4$ = turbine exhaust temperature, $T_5$ = stack temperature.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sugar industries** (India's cogeneration hotspot) use back-pressure turbines with **HPR ≈ 2 to 4**.
- **District heating in Scandinavian countries** uses extraction-condensing turbines with **HPR ≈ 1 to 2**.
- **Modern combined cycle plants** in Kerala/Karnataka (e.g., CCGT plants) achieve **η_cc > 55%** with single-shaft and multi-shaft configurations.
- **Repowering** of old Rankine plants by adding a gas turbine topping cycle can boost efficiency from 35% to 45% without replacing existing steam infrastructure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Combined Cycle Plant Efficiency (Fundamental Energy Balance)

We derive the overall efficiency of a **Brayton–Rankine combined cycle** by performing an energy balance on the entire plant.

**Step 1 — Identify the energy flows entering and leaving the plant boundaries.**

The fuel (typically natural gas) supplies chemical energy $Q_{fuel}$ in the combustion chamber of the gas turbine cycle. The plant outputs useful work from *two* turbines.

$$\text{Total Useful Work} \;=\; W_{gas\;turbine} \;+\; W_{steam\;turbine}$$

**Step 2 — Express the net work output of the gas turbine cycle.**

The net work of the gas turbine cycle (after subtracting compressor work) is:

$$W_{net,g} \;=\; \eta_{Brayton} \cdot Q_{fuel,gas}$$

where $Q_{fuel,gas}$ is the fuel energy supplied to the gas turbine combustion chamber.

**Step 3 — Determine the waste heat available in the gas turbine exhaust.**

By the First Law applied to the gas turbine cycle, the rejected heat in the exhaust (entering the HRSG) is:

$$Q_{exhaust} \;=\; Q_{fuel,gas} - W_{net,g} \;=\; (1 - \eta_g) \cdot Q_{fuel,gas}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the HRSG effectiveness.**

The HRSG (Heat Recovery Steam Generator) does not transfer 100% of the exhaust heat to the steam cycle. Let $\eta_{b}$ be the HRSG thermal effectiveness. The heat actually delivered to the steam cycle is:

$$Q_{to\;steam} \;=\; \eta_{b} \cdot Q_{exhaust} \;=\; \eta_{b} (1 - \eta_g) Q_{fuel,gas}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the net work output of the steam turbine cycle.**

The net work from the steam Rankine cycle is:

$$W_{net,s} \;=\; \eta_{Rankine} \cdot Q_{to\;steam} \;=\; \eta_s \cdot \eta_b (1 - \eta_g) Q_{fuel,gas}$$

**Step 6 — Write the total net work output of the combined cycle.**

$$W_{net,total} \;=\; W_{net,g} + W_{net,s} \;=\; \eta_g Q_{fuel,gas} + \eta_s \eta_b (1 - \eta_g) Q_{fuel,gas}$$

Factoring out $Q_{fuel,gas}$:

$$W_{net,total} \;=\; Q_{fuel,gas} \left[ \eta_g + \eta_s \eta_b (1 - \eta_g) \right]$$

**Step 7 — Define the overall combined cycle efficiency.**

$$\eta_{combined} \;=\; \frac{W_{net,total}}{Q_{fuel,gas}} \;=\; \eta_g + \eta_s \eta_b (1 - \eta_g)$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{cc} \;=\; \eta_g + \eta_s \, \eta_b \, (1 - \eta_g)\;}$$

**Step 8 — Simplify for the case of 100% HRSG effectiveness ($\eta_b = 1$).**

If HRSG effectiveness is unity (ideal):

$$\eta_{cc,\,ideal} \;=\; \eta_g + \eta_s (1 - \eta_g) \;=\; \eta_g + \eta_s - \eta_g \eta_s$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{cc,\,ideal} \;=\; 1 - (1 - \eta_g)(1 - \eta_s)\;}$$

This elegant identity shows the combined cycle is **always more efficient** than either cycle alone.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Utilization Factor of a Topping Cycle Cogeneration Plant

Consider a **back-pressure steam turbine** cogeneration plant with the following data:

- Fuel input rate: $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/s), calorific value: $CV$ (kJ/kg)
- Net electrical power output: $W_e$ (kW)
- Process heat delivered: $Q_{process}$ (kW)

**Step 1 — Write the total fuel energy input rate.**

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} \;=\; \dot{m}_f \cdot CV$$

**Step 2 — Write the total useful energy output rate.**

The plant delivers *two* useful outputs:

$$\dot{Q}_{useful} \;=\; W_e + Q_{process}$$

**Step 3 — Form the EUF ratio.**

$$\eta_u \;=\; \frac{W_e + Q_{process}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the heat-to-power ratio.**

$$\text{HPR} \;=\; \frac{Q_{process}}{W_e}$$

**Step 5 — Express the equivalent "saved" fuel.**

If the same power and heat were produced *separately* (a conventional power plant with $\eta_{conv}$ and a separate boiler with $\eta_{boiler}$):

$$\text{Fuel separately} \;=\; \frac{W_e}{\eta_{conv}} + \frac{Q_{process}}{\eta_{boiler}}$$

$$\text{Fuel in cogen} \;=\; \frac{W_e + Q_{process}}{\eta_u}$$

The **fuel saving ratio** is:

$$\text{FS} \;=\; 1 - \frac{\dot{m}_{f,cogen}}{\dot{m}_{f,separate}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{\eta_u} \cdot \frac{W_e + Q_{process}}{\dfrac{W_e}{\eta_{conv}} + \dfrac{Q_{process}}{\eta_{boiler}}}$$

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

> **Problem:** A cogeneration plant uses a back-pressure steam turbine. The plant produces **4 MW** of electrical power and **30 MW** of process heat. The fuel input is natural gas with $CV = 50$ MJ/kg, supplied at a rate of **1.1 kg/s**. Calculate (a) Energy Utilization Factor, (b) Heat-to-Power Ratio, (c) Identify the cycle type, (d) Fuel savings if the same energy were produced separately with $\eta_{conv} = 0.35$ and $\eta_{boiler} = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — Compute fuel energy input rate.**

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} \;=\; \dot{m}_f \cdot CV \;=\; 1.1 \times 50 \;=\; 55 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total useful energy output.**

$$\dot{Q}_{useful} \;=\; W_e + Q_{process} \;=\; 4 + 30 \;=\; 34 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Energy Utilization Factor.**

$$\eta_u \;=\; \frac{34}{55} \;=\; 0.6182$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_u \;\approx\; 61.82\%\;}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Heat-to-Power Ratio.**

$$\text{HPR} \;=\; \frac{30}{4} \;=\; 7.5$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{HPR} \;=\; 7.5\;}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the cycle type.**

Since **HPR = 7.5 > 1**, this is a **TOPPING CYCLE** cogeneration plant, with **heat-load dominance** (typical of sugar/paper mills).

**Step 6 — Compute the fuel consumption for separate production.**

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel,sep} \;=\; \frac{W_e}{\eta_{conv}} + \frac{Q_{process}}{\eta_{boiler}}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel,sep} \;=\; \frac{4}{0.35} + \frac{30}{0.85}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel,sep} \;=\; 11.4286 + 35.2941 \;=\; 46.7227 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the fuel savings.**

$$\text{FS} \;=\; 1 - \frac{55}{46.7227} \;=\; 1 - 1.1773$$

Wait — this gives a *negative* fuel savings, meaning the cogeneration as designed is worse than separate production. **This is the KTU examiner's trap!**

**Step 8 — Re-examine: The EUF must be compared to the weighted average efficiency of separate production.**

$$\eta_{sep,weighted} \;=\; \frac{4 + 30}{11.4286 + 35.2941} \;=\; \frac{34}{46.7227} \;=\; 0.7276$$

So $\eta_{sep,weighted} = 72.76\%$, while $\eta_u = 61.82\%$. The cogeneration plant is **less efficient** in this design — the EUF must be **improved** (e.g., by better boiler efficiency) for it to be competitive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** A high EUF *does not automatically* mean fuel savings — the comparison must be made against the **weighted average** of separate production efficiencies. This is a very common 14-mark question pattern.

## 3.4 Combined Cycle Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A combined cycle plant has a gas turbine (Brayton) with $\eta_g = 0.38$ and a steam turbine (Rankine) with $\eta_s = 0.40$ (based on HRSG input heat). The HRSG effectiveness is $\eta_b = 0.85$. If the fuel input is 100 MW (HHV), calculate the overall combined cycle efficiency and net power output.

**Step 1 — Apply the combined cycle efficiency formula.**

$$\eta_{cc} \;=\; \eta_g + \eta_s \eta_b (1 - \eta_g)$$

$$\eta_{cc} \;=\; 0.38 + (0.40)(0.85)(1 - 0.38)$$

$$\eta_{cc} \;=\; 0.38 + (0.34)(0.62)$$

$$\eta_{cc} \;=\; 0.38 + 0.2108$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{cc} \;=\; 0.5908 \;\text{or}\; 59.08\%\;}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the net power output.**

$$W_{net,total} \;=\; \eta_{cc} \times Q_{fuel} \;=\; 0.5908 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\;W_{net,total} \;=\; 59.08 \text{ MW}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with either cycle alone.**

$$\text{Gas turbine alone: } W_g = 38 \text{ MW (efficiency 38\%)}$$
$$\text{Steam turbine alone: needs separate fuel, so this is a stacking benefit}$$

**Efficiency gain = 59.08% − 38% = 21.08 percentage points** from the integration — a powerful illustration of the combined cycle principle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Topping Cycle Cogeneration (Back-Pressure Steam Turbine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fuelA[Natural Gas / Coal] --> boilerA[Fire Tube Boiler]
    boilerA -->|High Pressure Steam| turbineA[Back Pressure Steam Turbine]
    turbineA -->|Electrical Power| generatorA[Electrical Generator]
    turbineA -->|Exhaust Steam Low Pressure| processA[Process Heat User]
    processA -->|Condensed Water Returns| feedwaterA[Feedwater Tank]
    feedwaterA -->|Feed Pump| boilerA
    generatorA -->|Electrical Output| gridA[Plant Grid]

    style fuelA fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style boilerA fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style turbineA fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style generatorA fill:#277da1,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style processA fill:#e63946,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style feedwaterA fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style gridA fill:#577590,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The fuel → boiler → turbine path produces power first (generator), and the *exhaust* steam from the back-pressure turbine (not condensed) is used directly as process heat. This is the classic topping cycle.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Bottoming Cycle Cogeneration (Waste Heat Recovery)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fuelB[High Temperature Fuel] --> furnaceB[Industrial Furnace / Kiln]
    furnaceB -->|High Temp Process Heat| productB[Process Output]
    furnaceB -->|Hot Flue Gas 800C to 1000C| whrb[Waste Heat Recovery Boiler]
    whrb -->|Generated Steam| turbineB[Steam Turbine]
    turbineB --> generatorB[Electrical Generator]
    turbineB -->|Condenser| coolingB[Cooling Tower]
    coolingB --> feedB[Condensate Pump]
    feedB --> whrb

    style fuelB fill:#d62828,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style furnaceB fill:#f77f00,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style productB fill:#fcbf49,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style whrb fill:#003049,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style turbineB fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style generatorB fill:#277da1,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style coolingB fill:#9d4edd,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style feedB fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Fuel first serves the high-temperature process (e.g., cement kiln at 1400°C), and the *waste* flue gas is recovered in a waste heat boiler to generate steam for power generation. This is a bottoming cycle.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Combined Cycle Power Plant (Brayton + Rankine)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    airIn[Atmospheric Air] --> comp[Air Compressor]
    comp -->|High Pressure Air| comb[Combustion Chamber]
    fuelIn[Natural Gas Fuel] --> comb
    comb -->|Hot Gas 1300C to 1500C| gTurb[Gas Turbine]
    gTurb -->|Shaft Power| gGen[Gas Turbine Generator]
    gTurb -->|Exhaust Gas 500C to 600C| hrsg[Heat Recovery Steam Generator HRSG]
    hrsg -->|Superheated Steam| sTurb[Steam Turbine]
    hrsg -->|Feedwater In| feedP[Feedwater Pump]
    feedP --> hrsg
    sTurb -->|Condenser 0.05 bar| cond[Condenser]
    cond --> feedP
    sTurb -->|Shaft Power| sGen[Steam Turbine Generator]
    gGen --> bus[Electrical Bus]
    sGen --> bus
    hrsg -->|Stack Gas 100C to 150C| stack[Stack to Atmosphere]

    style airIn fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style comp fill:#457b9d,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style comb fill:#e63946,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style fuelIn fill:#f1faee,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style gTurb fill:#f4a261,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style gGen fill:#e76f51,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style hrsg fill:#264653,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style sTurb fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style sGen fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style cond fill:#6a4c93,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style feedP fill:#1982c4,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style bus fill:#8ac926,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style stack fill:#6c757d,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The **air → compressor → combustor → gas turbine** path is the Brayton cycle. The **hot exhaust → HRSG → steam turbine → condenser** path is the Rankine cycle. The HRSG is the *bridge* between the two cycles. Both generators feed a common electrical bus. The total efficiency can exceed 55%.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Cogeneration Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ[Need for both power and process heat] --> q1{Which is the PRIMARY demand?}
    q1 -->|Heat is primary| q2{HPR ratio}
    q1 -->|Power is primary| q3{HPR ratio}
    q2 -->|HPR greater than 1| top[Topping Cycle Back Pressure Turbine]
    q2 -->|HPR approx 1| ext[Extraction Condensing Turbine]
    q3 -->|HPR less than 1| bot[Bottoming Cycle Waste Heat Boiler]
    q3 -->|HPR much less than 1| cc[Combined Cycle Plant]

    top --> appA[Sugar Paper Chemical Plants]
    ext --> appB[District Heating Large Industries]
    bot --> appC[Cement Steel Glass Industries]
    cc --> appD[Utility Scale CCPP]

    style startQ fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style q1 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style q2 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style q3 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style top fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style ext fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style bot fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style cc fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style appA fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style appB fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style appC fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style appD fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Use this decision tree in the KTU exam to justify your choice of cogeneration configuration based on the heat-to-power ratio of the consumer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define cogeneration. Distinguish between topping and bottoming cycles with examples.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Cogeneration** is the simultaneous generation of electrical (or mechanical) energy and useful thermal/process heat from a **single fuel source** in an integrated system.

| Feature | Topping Cycle | Bottoming Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Output | Power (first), then heat | Heat (first), then power |
| HPR | > 1 (heat dominant) | < 1 (power dominant) |
| Example | Back-pressure turbine in sugar mill | Waste heat boiler in cement kiln |
| Typical η_u | 70–85% | 60–75% |

**(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for distinction with example)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Define Energy Utilization Factor (EUF) for a cogeneration system. State its typical range.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Energy Utilization Factor (EUF)** is the ratio of the total useful energy output (electrical power + process heat) to the total fuel energy input:

$$\eta_u \;=\; \frac{W_{net} + Q_{process}}{Q_{fuel}}$$

- **Typical Range:** 70% to 90% for well-designed cogeneration systems
- **Conventional plant reference:** 30% to 45%
- A higher EUF indicates better utilization of fuel energy.

**(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for range, 1 mark for significance)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice — 2 × 14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A cogeneration plant produces **5 MW** of electrical power and **40 MW** of process heat. The fuel (natural gas, CV = 50 MJ/kg) is supplied at **1.2 kg/s**. Determine:
   (i) Energy Utilization Factor (EUF) (3 marks)
   (ii) Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR), and identify the cycle type (2 marks)
   (iii) Fuel savings, if the same energy were produced separately with $\eta_{conv} = 0.38$ and $\eta_{boiler} = 0.88$ (2 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **combined cycle power plant** using a **gas turbine (Brayton cycle) and steam turbine (Rankine cycle)**. Show that the combined cycle efficiency is given by $\eta_{cc} = \eta_g + \eta_s \eta_b (1 - \eta_g)$. State the typical efficiency range of modern CCPPs. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Part (i) — EUF Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} = 1.2 \times 50 = 60 \text{ MW}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{useful} = 5 + 40 = 45 \text{ MW}$$

$$\eta_u = \frac{45}{60} = 0.75 = \mathbf{75\%}$$

**Part (ii) — HPR & Cycle Type (2 marks):**

$$\text{HPR} = \frac{40}{5} = 8$$

Since **HPR = 8 > 1**, the plant is a **TOPPING CYCLE** (heat-load dominant). Suitable for **sugar or paper industry**.

**Part (iii) — Fuel Savings (2 marks):**

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel,sep} = \frac{5}{0.38} + \frac{40}{0.88} = 13.1579 + 45.4545 = 58.6124 \text{ MW}$$

$$\text{Fuel Savings} = 1 - \frac{60}{58.6124} = 1 - 1.0237 = -0.0237$$

**Interpretation:** The fuel savings is **negative (−2.37%)**, meaning the cogeneration plant, as designed, is *not* saving fuel. The EUF (75%) is less than the weighted average of separate production efficiencies (76.8%). To make this design competitive, **process heat utilization must be increased** or **boiler efficiency improved**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often assume "high EUF = always good". They miss the weighted average comparison. Always check if the cogeneration plant is *actually* saving fuel versus the conventional baseline. A negative FS is a common trap!

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Working Principle (5 marks):**

1. **Air Intake:** Atmospheric air is drawn into the **axial-flow compressor** of the gas turbine, where it is compressed to high pressure (typically 10–30 bar).

2. **Combustion:** The compressed air enters the **combustion chamber**, where natural gas (or liquid fuel) is burned at constant pressure, raising the gas temperature to **1300–1500°C** (TIT — Turbine Inlet Temperature).

3. **Gas Turbine Expansion:** The hot, high-pressure gas expands through the gas turbine, producing shaft work. The turbine drives both the compressor and an electrical generator. The exhaust gas leaves at **500–600°C** (still containing significant enthalpy).

4. **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG):** The gas turbine exhaust is routed through the **HRSG**, which acts as the boiler for the bottoming steam cycle. The exhaust gas transfers heat to water/steam in successive **economizer, evaporator, and superheater** sections.

5. **Steam Turbine:** The superheated steam expands through a **steam turbine** driving a separate generator. The exhaust steam is condensed in a surface condenser and the condensate is pumped back to the HRSG.

**Mathematical Derivation (1 mark):**

By energy balance on the entire plant:

$$\eta_{cc} = \eta_g + \eta_s \eta_b (1 - \eta_g)$$

**Typical Efficiency Range (1 mark):**

Modern combined cycle plants achieve **η_cc = 55% to 62%** (LHV basis), with the most advanced F-class and H-class turbines reaching up to 64% in combined configuration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (Internal Choice)

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **back-pressure steam turbine cogeneration system** used in a sugar industry. Derive the expression for EUF and HPR. Compare its EUF with a condensing turbine. (7 marks)

**(b)** A combined cycle plant uses natural gas as fuel. The gas turbine produces **80 MW** at an efficiency of 38%. The exhaust at 580°C is fed to an HRSG that produces steam at the rate of **55 kg/s** at 80 bar, 500°C. The steam expands in a turbine to 0.05 bar with an isentropic efficiency of 88%. The HRSG effectiveness is 0.86. The fuel calorific value is 50 MJ/kg. Calculate:
   (i) Net combined cycle efficiency (4 marks)
   (ii) Net steam cycle power output (2 marks)
   (iii) HPR if the steam cycle exhaust heat is fully used for district heating (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Working of Back-Pressure Turbine Cogeneration (3 marks):**

1. High-pressure steam is generated in a boiler using bagasse (sugar industry) or coal as fuel.
2. The steam expands in a **back-pressure turbine** (exhaust pressure typically 2–10 bar instead of condenser vacuum).
3. The turbine drives an electrical generator producing power.
4. The **entire exhaust steam** from the turbine (which still has significant enthalpy) is sent to the process plant (e.g., evaporators, juice heaters in a sugar mill).
5. The condensed water is returned to the boiler, completing the cycle.

**EUF and HPR Derivation (2 marks):**

Let $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel rate, $CV$ = calorific value, $W_e$ = electrical output, $Q_p$ = process heat:

$$\eta_u = \frac{W_e + Q_p}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$$

$$\text{HPR} = \frac{Q_p}{W_e}$$

**Comparison with Condensing Turbine (2 marks):**

- **Condensing turbine:** All exhaust is condensed, and the latent heat is rejected to cooling water. EUF = $W_e / Q_{fuel}$ ≈ 25–35%.
- **Back-pressure turbine:** Exhaust steam fully used as process heat. EUF = 60–80%.
- The back-pressure turbine has a **higher EUF** but is *less flexible* — its power output is tied to the process heat demand.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given Data:**
- Gas turbine: $W_g = 80$ MW, $\eta_g = 0.38$
- HRSG: $\eta_b = 0.86$, exhaust $T_4 = 580°C$
- Steam: 80 bar, 500°C, $\dot{m}_s = 55$ kg/s
- Steam turbine: $\eta_{s,isen} = 0.88$, condenser 0.05 bar
- CV = 50 MJ/kg

**Part (i) — Net Combined Cycle Efficiency (4 marks):**

Fuel input rate:
$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \frac{W_g}{\eta_g} = \frac{80}{0.38} = 210.526 \text{ MW}$$

Exhaust heat entering HRSG:
$$Q_{exhaust} = \dot{Q}_{fuel} - W_g = 210.526 - 80 = 130.526 \text{ MW}$$

Heat transferred to steam (with HRSG effectiveness):
$$Q_{to\;steam} = \eta_b \times Q_{exhaust} = 0.86 \times 130.526 = 112.253 \text{ MW}$$

From steam tables:
- $h_5$ at 80 bar, 500°C ≈ 3399 kJ/kg
- $h_{6s}$ at 0.05 bar (saturated mixture, $s_5 = 6.7266$ kJ/kg·K, $s_f$ at 0.05 bar = 0.4764, $s_{fg}$ = 7.3727)

$$x_{6s} = \frac{s_5 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7266 - 0.4764}{7.3727} = 0.8476$$

$$h_{6s} = h_f + x_{6s} \cdot h_{fg} = 137.82 + 0.8476 \times 2415.9 = 137.82 + 2047.7 = 2185.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Actual exit enthalpy:
$$h_6 = h_5 - \eta_{s,isen}(h_5 - h_{6s}) = 3399 - 0.88 \times (3399 - 2185.5) = 3399 - 0.88 \times 1213.5$$

$$h_6 = 3399 - 1067.88 = 2331.12 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Steam turbine power:
$$W_s = \dot{m}_s (h_5 - h_6) = 55 \times (3399 - 2331.12) = 55 \times 1067.88 = 58.733 \text{ MW}$$

Steam cycle efficiency (based on HRSG input):
$$\eta_s = \frac{W_s}{Q_{to\;steam}} = \frac{58.733}{112.253} = 0.5232 = 52.32\%$$

Combined cycle efficiency:
$$\eta_{cc} = \eta_g + \eta_s \eta_b (1 - \eta_g)$$

$$\eta_{cc} = 0.38 + 0.5232 \times 0.86 \times (1 - 0.38)$$

$$\eta_{cc} = 0.38 + 0.5232 \times 0.86 \times 0.62$$

$$\eta_{cc} = 0.38 + 0.2789 = \mathbf{0.6589 = 65.89\%}$$

**Part (ii) — Net Steam Cycle Power Output (2 marks):**

$$W_s = 58.733 \text{ MW} \approx \mathbf{58.73 \text{ MW}}$$

**Part (iii) — HPR with District Heating (1 mark):**

If all turbine exhaust heat (latent + sensible) is used for district heating:

$$Q_{process} = \dot{m}_s \times (h_6 - h_{cond}) \approx 55 \times 2331.12 = 128.21 \text{ MW}$$

$$\text{HPR} = \frac{Q_{process}}{W_{net,total}} = \frac{128.21}{80 + 58.73} = \frac{128.21}{138.73} = \mathbf{0.924}$$

Since HPR < 1, this is a **power-dominated combined cycle with district heating integration** (technically a "**CHP combined cycle**" or **CCCHP**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Steam Tables:** The 14-mark question will require accurate steam table interpolation. Use **interpolation for superheated steam at 80 bar, 500°C** and **saturated tables at 0.05 bar** carefully. Skipping this step costs 3–4 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Bulletins for KTU Module 2 — Cogeneration & Combined Cycle**

- **Cogeneration (CHP):** Simultaneous production of **power + process heat** from a single fuel.
- **Topping Cycle:** Power first → heat later. HPR > 1. Example: back-pressure turbine in **sugar mill**.
- **Bottoming Cycle:** Heat first → power later. HPR < 1. Example: waste heat boiler in **cement industry**.
- **EUF (Energy Utilization Factor):** $\eta_u = \dfrac{W_{net} + Q_{process}}{Q_{fuel}}$ — typical 70–90%.
- **HPR (Heat-to-Power Ratio):** $\text{HPR} = \dfrac{Q_{process}}{W_{net}}$. Use this to *select* the cogeneration configuration.
- **Fuel Savings Formula:** Always compare with **weighted average** of separate production, not with a single efficiency.
- **Combined Cycle:** Brayton (gas turbine) + Rankine (steam turbine) integrated via HRSG.
- **Combined Cycle Efficiency:** $\eta_{cc} = \eta_g + \eta_s \eta_b (1 - \eta_g)$ — simplified to $\eta_{cc} \approx 1 - (1-\eta_g)(1-\eta_s)$.
- **Modern CCPP efficiency:** 55–62% (LHV), F-class and H-class turbines can reach **64%**.
- **HRSG Effectiveness:** The fraction of exhaust heat actually transferred to the steam cycle; typically **0.80–0.90**.
- **Repowering:** Adding a gas turbine topping cycle to an existing Rankine plant to boost efficiency from 35% → 45%.
- **Key Steam Tables to Memorize for KTU:**
  - $h$ at 80 bar, 500°C ≈ 3399 kJ/kg
  - $h_f$ at 0.05 bar ≈ 137.82 kJ/kg
  - $h_{fg}$ at 0.05 bar ≈ 2415.9 kJ/kg
  - $s_f$ at 0.05 bar ≈ 0.4764 kJ/kg·K
  - $s_{fg}$ at 0.05 bar ≈ 7.3727 kJ/kg·K
- **Comparison Table (must memorize for KTU viva):**

| Parameter | Conventional Plant | Cogeneration | Combined Cycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Useful Outputs | Power only | Power + Heat | Power only (higher η) |
| η | 35–40% | 70–90% (EUF) | 55–62% |
| Capital Cost | Low | Moderate | High |
| Fuel Type | Any | Any | Mostly natural gas |
| Best For | Grid power | Industrial heat + power | Utility scale power |

- **KTU Module 2 Weightage:** This topic typically carries **14–20 marks** in the End Semester Exam, with at least one full 14-mark question guaranteed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Power Plants, General Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Thermal Power Plants, General Layout # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Thermal Power Plant (Steam Power Station):** A thermal power plant is an installation that converts the chemical energy stored in fossil fuels (coal, oil, or natural gas) into electrical energy by utilizing a thermodynamic Rankine/steam cycle. The fuel is combusted to produce high-pressure, high-temperature steam in a boiler; the steam expands through a steam turbine, which drives an alternator (synchronous generator) coupled on the same shaft, producing electricity. ### KTU 2024 Nomenclature Mapping | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **TPP** | Thermal Power Plant | | **BFP** | Boiler Feed Pump | | **CEP** | Condensate Extraction Pump | | **ID Fan** | Induced Draft Fan | | **FD Fan** | Forced Draft Fan | | **ESP** | Electrostatic Precipitator | | **DM Water** | De-Mineralized Water | | **CHP** | Coal Handling Plant | | **AHP** | Ash Handling Plant | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Giant Tea Kettle" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a massive industrial tea kettle the size of a building. You put coal (the "tea leaves' fuel") into the furnace (the "stove"). Water boils inside pipes (the "kettle body"). The high-pressure steam rushes out like steam from a pressure cooker and pushes the blades of a giant fan (the turbine). That fan is connected by a long shaft to another machine (the generator) that produces electricity. After the steam pushes the blades, it loses energy and becomes water again, which is cooled in a giant radiator (the cooling tower) and sent back to the kettle. The cycle repeats millions of times per day. **Key Insight:** The only difference between your kitchen kettle and a 500 MW thermal power station is **scale**, **pressure (≈ 250 bar)**, **temperature (≈ 600°C)**, and **continuous recycling** of water. ## 1.3 Why Thermal Power Plants Dominate the Indian Energy Mix > [!IMPORTANT] > India generated approximately **73–75 %** of its total electricity from thermal (coal, gas, and oil-based) power stations as of 2024–2025. KTU specifically emphasizes this topic because Kerala and the wider Indian grid rely heavily on pit-head and coastal thermal stations (e.g., NTPC Kayamkulam, NTPC Ramagundam) to meet base-load demand. **Typical Rating Range:** Indian thermal units range from **50 MW** (old small sets) to **800 MW (super-critical)** and now **660 MW / 800 MW ultra-super-critical** units at NTPC plants. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Thermal Power Plants Thermal power plants are broadly classified based on the working fluid and prime mover: ### A. Based on Fuel Used 1. **Coal-Based (Steam) Power Plant** — Most common; uses pulverized coal. 2. **Oil-Based (Diesel) Power Plant** — Uses diesel engine as prime mover; used for peaking or remote loads. 3. **Gas-Based Power Plant** — Uses natural gas in a Gas Turbine (Brayton cycle) or combined cycle. ### B. Based on Steam Pressure (Modern Classification) 1. **Sub-critical** — Boiler pressure < 221 bar (typical 150–170 bar). 2. **Supercritical** — Boiler pressure > 221.2 bar (typical 250 bar), no phase change at saturation. 3. **Ultra-Supercritical** — Pressure 280–300 bar, temperatures 600°C/620°C. ## 2.2 Major Systems of a Modern Thermal Power Plant (Block-by-Block Logic) ### Step 1 — Fuel Handling System (Coal Handling Plant - CHP) - **Why?** Coal arrives by rail wagons (BOXN), ships, or trucks in lumps up to **300 mm**. - **How?** Coal is crushed by crushers (primary → secondary), screened to **≤ 20 mm** for PF (Pulverized Fuel) firing, then conveyed to the bunker via belt conveyors. ### Step 2 — Pulverizing (Pulverizer/Mill) - **Why?** Fine coal particles (≈ **75 microns**, 70 % passing 200 mesh) burn faster, completely, and uniformly. - **How?** Ball mills or vertical spindle mills grind coal using hot primary air from FD fan. ### Step 3 — Combustion (Boiler / Steam Generator) - **Why?** Convert chemical energy of fuel into thermal energy of steam. - **How?** Pulverized coal is blown into the furnace. Combustion air is supplied in stages (PA → FD). Combustion gases pass over the water tubes, transferring heat to water/steam. ### Step 4 — Steam Generation & Superheating - **Why?** Wet steam would damage turbine blades due to erosion; superheated steam gives higher Rankine cycle efficiency. - **How?** Saturated steam from the boiler passes through superheaters (convective + radiant) reaching **540–600 °C**, then to the High-Pressure (HP) turbine. ### Step 5 — Steam Turbine (Prime Mover) - **Why?** Convert thermal/pressure energy of steam into mechanical rotational energy. - **How?** Multi-stage impulse-reaction turbine: HP → IP (Intermediate Pressure) → LP (Low Pressure). Pressure drops from **250 bar to 0.05 bar** (condenser vacuum). ### Step 6 — Electrical Generator (Alternator) - **Why?** Convert mechanical rotation to electrical energy. - **How?** Three-phase synchronous generator; rotor field excited at **3000 RPM (50 Hz)** with DC excitation. ### Step 7 — Condenser & Cooling System - **Why?** Reduce back-pressure on the turbine; recover condensate. - **How?** Steam exhaust is condensed using cooling water from a cooling tower or river/sea. Vacuum maintained at **0.04–0.08 bar absolute**. ### Step 8 — Feed Water Heating & Boiler Feed Pump (BFP) - **Why?** Improve cycle efficiency by regenerative feed heating using bled steam. - **How?** Multi-stage centrifugal BFP raises condensate pressure from condenser level back to **≈ 270 bar** (for a 250 bar boiler). ### Step 9 — Ash & Flue Gas Handling - **Why?** Comply with emission norms (PM ≤ 30 mg/Nm³). - **How?** ESP captures fly ash; bottom ash goes to ash pond; flue gas exits via chimney after FGD (Flue Gas Desulfurization). ### Step 10 — Switchyard & Transformer - **Why?** Step-up voltage from generator **21 kV to 400 kV/220 kV** for long-distance transmission. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Typical Value | |---|---------------------|------------|-------|----------------| | 1 | Plant Heat Rate | $H = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{\text{overall}}}$ | kJ/kWh | 9000–10500 kJ/kWh | | 2 | Overall Plant Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{plant}} = \dfrac{860}{\text{Heat Rate (kcal/kWh)}}$ | % | 33–42 % | | 3 | Specific Coal Consumption | $s_c = \dfrac{3600 \times 1000}{\eta_{\text{plant}} \times \text{GCV}}$ | kg/kWh | 0.55–0.65 kg/kWh | | 4 | Specific Oil Consumption | $s_o = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{\text{gen}} \times \text{GCV}_{\text{oil}}}$ | kg/kWh | 0.20–0.25 kg/kWh | | 5 | Cycle (Rankine) Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Rankine}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | % | 38–42 % | | 6 | Steam Rate (Turbine) | $m_s = \dfrac{3600 \times P}{h_1 - h_2}$ | kg/kWh | 3.0–4.5 kg/kWh | | 7 | Condenser Vacuum (absolute) | $P_c$ | bar | 0.05–0.10 bar | | 8 | Boiler Drum Pressure | $P_d$ | bar | 150–250 bar | | 9 | Reheat Steam Temp | $T_{\text{rh}}$ | °C | 540–600 °C | | 10 | Alternator Frequency | $f = \dfrac{P \cdot N}{120}$ | Hz | 50 Hz (P=2, N=3000) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Table:** The vertical bar in $h_1 - h_2$ (which is a difference, not an absolute value) is safe; however, if absolute values appear anywhere in this sheet, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown rendering. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Thermal power plants form the **base-load backbone** of the Indian grid because they: - Provide **24×7 dispatchable** power (unlike solar/wind). - Allow **bulk storage** of fuel (15–30 days coal stock). - Can be ramped at **2–5 % per minute** in modern units. > [!NOTE] > **Modern Trend:** India's push toward **supercritical and ultra-supercritical** units is driven by the need to reduce CO₂ emissions per kWh. Going from sub-critical (38 % η) to ultra-supercritical (45 % η) reduces coal consumption by ~ **18 %** for the same power output. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Working Principle — Rankine Cycle in a Steam Power Plant The thermal power plant operates on the **Ideal Rankine Cycle** with practical modifications (reheat and regenerative feed heating). ### The Four Ideal Processes $$P_1 \rightarrow P_2: \quad \text{Pump (isentropic compression of condensate)}$$ $$P_2 \rightarrow P_3: \quad \text{Boiler (constant pressure heat addition)}$$ $$P_3 \rightarrow P_4: \quad \text{Turbine (isentropic expansion)}$$ $$P_4 \rightarrow P_1: \quad \text{Condenser (constant pressure heat rejection)}$$ ### Step-by-Step Energetic Derivation **Step 1 — Define Net Work Output per kg of steam** The turbine produces work per kg equal to the enthalpy drop of steam: $$W_T = h_3 - h_4 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$ The feed water pump consumes work to raise condensate to boiler pressure: $$W_P = h_2 - h_1 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$ Net work output per kg of circulating fluid: $$W_{\text{net}} = W_T - W_P = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)$$ This equation uses the algebraic difference of enthalpies across each component. **Conversion Logic:** Enthalpy is a state function (kJ/kg); work is calculated as the difference between outlet and inlet enthalpy, which is equivalent to the area under the process on an h-s diagram. **Step 2 — Define Heat Supplied per kg in the Boiler** Heat input in the boiler per kg of steam: $$Q_{\text{in}} = h_3 - h_2 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$ This represents the energy absorbed by the working fluid at constant pressure during phase change (saturated → vapor) and superheating. **Step 3 — Define Thermal Efficiency of the Rankine Cycle** $$\eta_{\text{Rankine}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$$ ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A 250 MW thermal power plant operates with the following enthalpy data: $$h_1 = 140 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad h_2 = 150 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad h_3 = 3200 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad h_4 = 2100 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Find:** 1. Turbine work per kg. 2. Pump work per kg. 3. Net work per kg. 4. Heat supplied per kg. 5. Rankine efficiency. **Solution:** **(a)** Turbine work per kg: $$W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 3200 - 2100 = 1100 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(b)** Pump work per kg: $$W_P = h_2 - h_1 = 150 - 140 = 10 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(c)** Net work per kg: $$W_{\text{net}} = 1100 - 10 = 1090 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(d)** Heat supplied per kg: $$Q_{\text{in}} = h_3 - h_2 = 3200 - 150 = 3050 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(e)** Rankine efficiency: $$\eta_{\text{Rankine}} = \dfrac{1090}{3050} = 0.3574 = 35.74\,\%$$ This matches typical Indian sub-critical thermal plants. ## 3.2 Derivation of Plant Heat Rate and Specific Fuel Consumption ### Step 1 — Plant Heat Rate Heat rate is the inverse of overall efficiency.
