It measures kJ of fuel energy required per kWh of electricity exported. $$\text{Heat Rate} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{\text{plant}}} \quad [\text{kJ/kWh}]$$ For the above example, if the plant's overall efficiency (including generator + auxiliaries) is 35 %: $$\text{Heat Rate} = \dfrac{3600}{0.35} = 10285.7 \text{ kJ/kWh}$$ ### Step 2 — Specific Coal Consumption (SCC) Assume coal GCV = **20,000 kJ/kg (≈ 4800 kcal/kg)**: $$s_c = \dfrac{\text{Heat Rate}}{\text{GCV}} = \dfrac{10285.7}{20000} = 0.514 \text{ kg/kWh}$$ ### Step 3 — Total Coal Required for a 250 MW Plant Operating 24 h Total coal per day: $$M_{\text{coal}} = 0.514 \times 250{,}000 \times 24 = 3{,}084{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \approx 3084 \text{ tonnes/day}$$ This matches the operational data of NTPC Singrauli (~ 12,000 T/day for 2000 MW capacity). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component Pressure-Temperature Map | Component | Inlet Temp (°C) | Outlet Temp (°C) | Inlet Pressure (bar) | Outlet Pressure (bar) | |-----------|------------------|------------------|------------------------|------------------------| | Boiler Drum | 340 | 340 (sat.) | 170 | 170 | | Superheater | 340 | 540 | 170 | 165 | | HP Turbine | 540 | 360 | 165 | 45 | | Reheater | 360 | 540 | 45 | 42 | | IP Turbine | 540 | 250 | 42 | 5 | | LP Turbine | 250 | 41 | 5 | 0.07 | | Condenser | 41 (steam) | 41 (liq.) | 0.07 | 0.07 | | BFP outlet | 32 | 35 | 0.05 | 180 | | Economiser | 250 | 320 | 180 | 175 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Logic Note:** Pressure drops across the turbine stages convert thermal/pressure energy of steam into rotational mechanical work; the heat rejected in the condenser is dumped to the cooling tower, which represents the largest energy loss (~ **50–55 %** of input fuel energy). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 General Block Layout of a Coal-Based Thermal Power Plant The diagram below shows the **functional flow** of fuel, air, water, steam, and electricity in a typical modern steam power station. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["COAL RECEIVING
Rail Wagons or Ships"] nodeB["COAL HANDLING PLANT
Crushing and Screening"] nodeC["COAL BUNKER
Storage of Crushed Coal"] nodeD["PULVERIZER MILL
Grinds Coal to 75 Microns"] nodeE["BOILER FURNACE
Combustion Chamber"] nodeF["BOILER DRUM
Steam Water Separation"] nodeG["SUPERHEATER
Steam at 540 C 165 bar"] nodeH["HP TURBINE
High Pressure Stage"] nodeI["REHEATER
Steam Reheated to 540 C"] nodeJ["IP TURBINE
Intermediate Pressure"] nodeK["LP TURBINE
Low Pressure Stage"] nodeL["CONDENSER
Vacuum 0.07 bar"] nodeM["CONDENSATE PUMP
Recovers Condensate"] nodeN["DEAERATOR
Removes Dissolved Oxygen"] nodeO["BOILER FEED PUMP
Raises to 180 bar"] nodeP["ECONOMISER
Preheats Feed Water"] nodeQ["ALTERNATOR
3 Phase 21 kV 50 Hz"] nodeR["TRANSFORMER
Steps Up to 220 or 400 kV"] nodeS["SWITCHYARD
Power Evacuation to Grid"] nodeT["COOLING TOWER
Cooling Water Recirculation"] nodeU["ESP ELECTROSTATIC PRECIPITATOR
Fly Ash Capture"] nodeV["CHIMNEY
Flue Gas Exit 220 m"] nodeW["ASH HANDLING
Bottom Ash and Fly Ash Disposal"] nodeX["FD FAN FORCED DRAFT
Supplies Combustion Air"] nodeY["PA FAN PRIMARY AIR
Dries and Transports Coal"] nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI --> nodeJ --> nodeK --> nodeL nodeL --> nodeM --> nodeN --> nodeO --> nodeP --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeU --> nodeV nodeE --> nodeW nodeY --> nodeD nodeX --> nodeE nodeL --> nodeT nodeH --> nodeN nodeJ --> nodeN nodeK --> nodeN nodeH --> nodeQ --> nodeR --> nodeS nodeT --> nodeL style nodeE fill:#FFD700 style nodeG fill:#FF6347 style nodeQ fill:#90EE90 style nodeL fill:#87CEEB style nodeV fill:#D3D3D3 ``` ## 4.2 Material Flow vs. Energy Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["Coal + Air + Water
MATERIAL INPUT"] node2["Boiler
Furnace"] node3["Turbine
Prime Mover"] node4["Generator
Alternator"] node5["ELECTRICAL OUTPUT
400 kV 50 Hz 3 Phase"] node6["Flue Gas + Ash + Hot Water
WASTE OUTPUT"] node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5 node2 --> node6 style node1 fill:#FFE4B5 style node5 fill:#98FB98 style node6 fill:#FFB6C1 ``` ## 4.3 Site Selection Criteria Matrix (Functional Logic Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["SITE SELECTION
FOR THERMAL POWER PLANT"] nodeB["Fuel Supply
Near Pithead or Port"] nodeC["Water Availability
River or Sea Nearby"] nodeD["Land
Flat and Affordable"] nodeE["Load Centre
Near Demand Area"] nodeF["Transport
Rail and Road Network"] nodeG["Ash Disposal
Valley or Mine Void"] nodeH["Environmental
Population Density and Wind"] nodeI["Foundation
Soil Bearing Capacity"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeA --> nodeF nodeA --> nodeG nodeA --> nodeH nodeA --> nodeI style nodeA fill:#FFD700 style nodeB fill:#FFB6C1 style nodeC fill:#87CEEB ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Compilation Note:** All node IDs follow the alphanumeric `nodeA`, `nodeB` convention. Labels use uppercase alphanumeric text inside double quotes only — no bold markers, italics, or HTML tables are embedded in the mermaid block, ensuring successful rendering. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **List any six major systems of a modern coal-based thermal power plant.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Coal Handling Plant (CHP)** — Receipt, crushing, storage of coal. 2. **Pulverizer Unit** — Grinding coal to fine powder for PF firing. 3. **Boiler / Steam Generator** — Converts water to superheated steam using combustion heat. 4. **Steam Turbine** — Multi-stage impulse-reaction prime mover. 5. **Condenser** — Condenses exhaust steam at vacuum to recover condensate. 6. **Cooling Tower** — Cools and recirculates condenser cooling water. 7. **Boiler Feed Pump (BFP)** — Recycles condensate back to the boiler at high pressure. 8. **Alternator** — Synchronous generator producing 21 kV at 3000 RPM. 9. **Ash Handling Plant (AHP)** — Disposes of bottom and fly ash. 10. **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** — Captures fly ash to control particulate emissions. 11. **Switchyard and Transformers** — Steps up voltage for transmission. *(Any six well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Compare sub-critical, super-critical, and ultra-super-critical thermal power plants.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Sub-critical | Super-critical | Ultra-Super-critical | |-----------|---------------|-----------------|------------------------| | Boiler Pressure (bar) | 150–170 | 220–260 | 280–310 | | Steam Temperature (°C) | 540/540 | 540/560 | 600/620 | | Cycle Phase | Phase change in drum | No phase change, once-through | No phase change | | Cycle Efficiency (%) | 35–38 | 41–43 | 44–46 | | Coal Consumption | Highest | Moderate | Lowest | | Capital Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | **Key Insight:** Higher pressure and temperature directly improve Rankine cycle efficiency, reducing coal requirement per kWh by 15–20 %. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Two questions of 14 marks each are asked from any module, with internal choice (either-or). Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question] **With a neat schematic, explain the general layout and working principle of a modern coal-based thermal power plant. Discuss the function of each major component.** #### (a) Schematic and Working Principle (7 Marks) **Solution:** A coal-based thermal power plant converts chemical energy of coal into electrical energy through four energy conversion stages: **Stage 1 — Chemical → Thermal Energy (Boiler):** Pulverized coal is burned in the boiler furnace with preheated air from the FD/PA fans. The released heat is absorbed by water circulating in boiler tubes. The boiler drum separates steam from water, and the saturated steam is further heated in the superheater to **540 °C at 165 bar** for Indian sub-critical units. **Stage 2 — Thermal → Mechanical Energy (Steam Turbine):** Superheated steam enters the HP turbine, expands to about 45 bar, is reheated to 540 °C, then expands through IP and LP turbines down to condenser pressure of 0.07 bar (vacuum). The pressure drop across stages rotates the turbine shaft at **3000 RPM**. **Stage 3 — Mechanical → Electrical Energy (Alternator):** The turbine shaft is directly coupled to a 3-phase synchronous generator (alternator). The rotor is DC-excited; stator produces electricity at **21 kV, 50 Hz**. **Stage 4 — Electrical Energy Transmission (Switchyard):** Step-up transformer raises voltage to **220 kV or 400 kV** for long-distance transmission through EHV switchyard. **Condensate Recovery Loop:** Exhaust steam is condensed in the condenser; condensate is pumped via CEP, deaerated, pressurized by BFP, preheated in the economiser, and returned to the boiler drum. *(Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the schematic.)* **Marks Distribution:** Schematic — 3 marks; Working principle explanation — 4 marks. #### (b) Function of Each Major Component (7 Marks) **Solution:** | # | Component | Function | |---|-----------|----------| | 1 | **Coal Handling Plant** | Unloading, crushing, screening, and storage of coal in bunkers. | | 2 | **Pulverizer Mill** | Grinds coal to 75-micron size for fast and complete combustion. | | 3 | **Furnace** | Provides controlled combustion space with air supply via FD/PA fans. | | 4 | **Boiler Drum** | Separates saturated steam from boiling water; provides circulation. | | 5 | **Superheater** | Raises saturated steam to superheated state (540 °C) for higher efficiency. | | 6 | **Economiser** | Recovers waste heat from flue gas to preheat feed water. | | 7 | **Air Preheater** | Uses flue gas heat to preheat combustion air. | | 8 | **Steam Turbine** | Converts steam energy into rotational mechanical work (HP + IP + LP). | | 9 | **Alternator** | Converts mechanical rotation into 3-phase electrical energy. | | 10 | **Condenser** | Condenses exhaust steam at vacuum using cooling water; recovers pure water. | | 11 | **Cooling Tower** | Cools and recirculates condenser cooling water. | | 12 | **BFP** | Recycles feed water to boiler at high pressure (~180 bar). | | 13 | **ESP** | Removes fly ash from flue gas using electrostatic principles. | | 14 | **Chimney** | Releases cleaned flue gas to atmosphere at high altitude. | | 15 | **Switchyard** | Steps up voltage and routes power to the grid. | **Marks Distribution:** Any 7 components with functions — 7 marks (1 mark each). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam Model] **Explain the site selection criteria for a thermal power plant in detail. Also discuss the classification of thermal power plants with examples.** #### (a) Site Selection Criteria (7 Marks) **Solution:** Selection of a thermal power plant site is governed by the following technical, economic, and environmental factors: **1. Availability and Cost of Fuel:** Plant should ideally be **pit-head based** (e.g., NTPC Korba, NTPC Singrauli) to minimize coal transport cost, which constitutes 30–40 % of operating cost. Alternatively, coastal locations are preferred for imported coal-based plants (e.g., NTPC Kudgi import route through Mormugao Port). **2. Availability of Water:** A 250 MW unit requires about **30,000–40,000 m³/day** of cooling water. Hence the plant must be near a perennial river, reservoir, or coast (e.g., NTPC Kayamkulam is coastal). **3. Land Requirement:** A 500 MW unit requires about **200–300 acres**. Land must be flat, cheap, and free from litigation. The site should allow for future expansion. **4. Distance from Load Centre:** Plant should be reasonably close to consumers to reduce transmission losses, but far enough (~ **25–40 km** from city) to comply with pollution norms. **5. Transport Facilities:** Good rail and road connectivity is essential for coal delivery, ash removal, equipment movement, and staff commuting. **6. Ash Disposal:** A 500 MW plant produces about **1500–2000 tonnes of ash per day**. Suitable low-lying areas, abandoned mines, or voids must be available nearby. **7. Environmental & Social Considerations:** Site should be away from dense population, ecologically sensitive zones, and historic monuments. Wind direction should be studied to ensure flue gas does not blow toward habitation. **8. Foundation & Soil Conditions:** Soil must have safe bearing capacity (≥ **150 kN/m²**). High water tables and seismic zones affect plant layout and cost. **Marks Distribution:** Any 7 factors with justification — 7 marks. #### (b) Classification of Thermal Power Plants (7 Marks) **Solution:** Thermal power plants are classified into the following categories: **1. Based on Working Fluid / Prime Mover:** - **(a) Steam Power Plants (Coal/Oil/Gas-fired):** Use a Rankine cycle steam boiler, turbine, and alternator. Most common for bulk power generation. Example: **NTPC Ramagundam (Coal, 2600 MW).** - **(b) Diesel Engine Power Plants:** Use diesel engines as prime movers, suited for remote locations or peaking duty. Example: **Diesel generator sets in Lakshadweep.** - **(c) Gas Turbine Power Plants:** Use Brayton cycle with natural gas or naphtha. Quick-start, used for peaking. Example: **NTPC Dadri Gas (830 MW).** - **(d) Combined Cycle Power Plants (CCPP):** Combine gas turbine (Brayton) and steam turbine (Rankine) for > 50 % efficiency. Example: **NTPC Faridabad Gas CCPP.** **2. Based on Steam Pressure:** - **Sub-critical** (≤ 170 bar) — older units. - **Supercritical** (220–260 bar) — modern bulk units. - **Ultra-supercritical** (≥ 280 bar) — latest technology. **3. Based on Utility:** - **Base-load plants** — run 24×7 (large coal plants). - **Peaking plants** — run only during high demand (gas, hydro). - **Standby plants** — for emergencies (diesel). **Marks Distribution:** Classification chart with examples — 7 marks. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing components** — Many students write "boiler" when they mean "steam generator" or interchange "superheater" with "economiser." Use exact KTU terminology. > > 2. **Missing the condensate loop** — Drawings often show water only going once through. The **closed-loop condensate recovery** is essential; examiners specifically check this. > > 3. **Forgetting auxiliary units** — ESP, AHP, deaerator, and switchyard are commonly missed, costing 1–2 marks per component. > > 4. **Units inconsistency** — Always use **bar** for pressure and **°C** for temperature in Indian context; mixing MPa with bar leads to confusion. > > 5. **Writing boiler efficiency as plant efficiency** — Boiler efficiency is ~ 88–92 % (combustion efficiency), but plant efficiency is only 35–42 % due to Rankine cycle limitations. > > 6. **No mention of regeneration/reheat** — In modern plant descriptions, neglecting regenerative feed heating and reheat cycles loses 1–2 marks because it is a KTU-highlighted concept. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3 (Thermal Power Plant General Layout)** - ✅ A thermal power plant operates on the **Rankine cycle** (steam) or **Brayton cycle** (gas). - ✅ Coal-based steam plants dominate Indian base-load generation (> 70 %). - ✅ Four energy conversion stages: **Chemical → Thermal → Mechanical → Electrical.** - ✅ Major systems: **CHP, Pulverizer, Boiler, Superheater, Turbine, Alternator, Condenser, BFP, ESP, Switchyard.** - ✅ Coal is crushed to **≤ 20 mm**, pulverized to **≤ 75 microns** (70 % through 200 mesh). - ✅ Steam conditions: **250 bar / 600 °C (ultra-supercritical)** to **170 bar / 540 °C (sub-critical).** - ✅ Condenser vacuum: **0.04–0.10 bar absolute** (key to back-pressure efficiency). - ✅ Alternator speed: **3000 RPM for 50 Hz** (formula: $f = P \cdot N / 120$, with P = 2 poles). - ✅ Plant heat rate: **9000–10500 kJ/kWh**; specific coal consumption: **0.55–0.65 kg/kWh.** - ✅ Efficiency hierarchy: **Boiler (88–92 %) > Turbine Cycle (38–42 %) > Overall Plant (33–38 %).** - ✅ Site selection priority: **Fuel → Water → Land → Load → Transport → Ash → Environment.** - ✅ ESP limits particulate emissions to **≤ 30 mg/Nm³** (MoEF norm). - ✅ Cooling tower recycles **95–98 %** of water; makeup is only 2–5 %. - ✅ A 250 MW plant burns about **3000 tonnes of coal per day** at full load. - ✅ **Modern trend:** Supercritical/ultra-supercritical plants for lower CO₂ per kWh. - ✅ **Reheat + Regeneration** are essential for improving Rankine efficiency in modern units. **Final Tip:** Always draw the **flow diagram with all auxiliaries** (ESP, AHP, cooling tower, deaerator) in Part B answers — this alone can secure 3–4 marks per question.

topics.title: Site Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Site Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: SITE SELECTION OF POWER PLANTS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Site Selection** in power plant engineering is the systematic, multi-criteria decision-making process of identifying and evaluating the most economically, technically, and environmentally suitable geographic location for the installation of a power generation facility, ensuring optimal plant efficiency, minimum operational cost, maximum safety, and compliance with statutory environmental regulations.

In the context of KTU's **PEMET631** module framework, site selection is treated as the **zeroth design phase** — a strategic decision whose incorrect execution renders even the most advanced thermodynamic cycle economically unviable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Real Estate Analogy**
> Imagine buying a house. You don't just pick the prettiest building — you check (1) **distance from your workplace** (load center), (2) **neighborhood safety** (environmental/social impact), (3) **road connectivity** (transportation), (4) **water supply** (cooling water), and (5) **future resale value** (expansion). A power plant is a "house" for a turbine-generator set. The plant's lifetime is **25–40 years**, so a bad site decision is a **40-year penalty** on capital.

### 1.3 Why Site Selection is the Most Critical Pre-Design Decision

The capital cost of relocating or dismantling a poorly sited power plant is typically **3× to 5×** the original investment. Hence, the factors considered are non-negotiable:

| # | Critical Factor | Engineering Impact |
|---|-----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Fuel Availability** | Determines the type of plant (Thermal/Hydro/Nuclear) |
| 2 | **Cooling Water** | Determines condenser pressure & Rankine efficiency |
| 3 | **Load Center Distance** | Determines transmission losses (I²R) |
| 4 | **Soil & Topography** | Determines foundation cost & civil works |
| 5 | **Environmental Clearance** | Determines project viability under EPA norms |
| 6 | **Transportation** | Determines fuel logistics & ash disposal |

### 1.4 Standard KTU Terminology for Module 3

- **Load Center**: Geographic region where the bulk of electrical demand is concentrated.
- **Ash Disposal Area (Ash Pond)**: Designated zone for fly ash and bottom ash from coal combustion.
- **Plant Load Factor (PLF)**: Ratio of actual energy generated to maximum possible energy generation over a period.
- **Cooling Tower Drift Loss**: Water lost as fine droplets entrained in exhaust air.
- **Make-up Water**: Fresh water added to the cooling system to compensate for evaporation and drift losses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Factors Affecting Site Selection

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes site selection factors into **four broad engineering domains**:

### 2.1.1 Technical Factors
1. **Availability of Fuel** — type, quantity, quality, continuity
2. **Availability of Water** — for cooling, boiler feed, and ash handling
3. **Nature of Land** — soil bearing capacity, topography, flood history
4. **Climatic Conditions** — ambient temperature, humidity, wind direction
5. **Distance from Load Center** — transmission cost vs. fuel transport cost trade-off
6. **Accessibility & Transportation** — rail, road, waterway proximity

### 2.1.2 Economic Factors
1. **Capital Cost of Land**
2. **Operating Cost** (fuel transport + water pumping + ash disposal)
3. **Transmission Line Cost**
4. **Taxation & Subsidies**
5. **Cost of Labor**

### 2.1.3 Environmental & Safety Factors
1. **Air Pollution Norms** (SO₂, NOₓ, PM, Hg emissions)
2. **Water Pollution** (thermal discharge, ash leachate)
3. **Noise Pollution**
4. **Distance from Populated Areas** (typically ≥ 1 km for thermal)
5. **Seismic Zone Classification** (critical for nuclear plants)

### 2.1.4 Miscellaneous Factors
1. **Future Expansion Possibility**
2. **Availability of Skilled Labor**
3. **Local Community Acceptance**
4. **Political & Statutory Stability**

## 2.2 The Optimal Site Equation (KTU High-Yield)

The total annual cost for a candidate site is modeled as:

$$C_{total} = C_{fuel} + C_{water} + C_{transmission} + C_{ash} + C_{land}$$

Each sub-cost is derived below.

### 2.2.1 Fuel Transportation Cost

If a plant consumes $F$ kg of fuel per hour, operates for $t$ hours/year, and the fuel must travel a distance $D_f$ km from the mine:

$$C_{fuel} = F \cdot t \cdot C_{fuel\_per\_kg} + F \cdot t \cdot D_f \cdot R_f$$

where $R_f$ is the **freight rate per kg per km**.

### 2.2.2 Water Pumping Cost

For a circulating water flow rate $Q$ m³/hr pumped through a head $H_w$ meters:

$$P_{pump} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_w}{\eta_{pump}} \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

$$\text{Annual Water Pumping Cost} = P_{pump} \cdot t \cdot C_{electricity}$$

### 2.2.3 Transmission Cost (KTU Favorite Formula)

For a transmitted power $P$ MW over distance $D_t$ km at line resistance $r$ Ω/km:

$$P_{loss} = \frac{P^2 \cdot r \cdot D_t}{V^2 \cdot \cos^2\phi}$$

> **Rule of Thumb (KTU)**: For every **100 km** of transmission distance, efficiency drops by approximately **5–7%**.

### 2.2.4 Ash Disposal Cost

Ash produced per year = $F \cdot a \cdot t$, where $a$ = ash content fraction (typically 0.30–0.40 for Indian coal).

$$C_{ash} = F \cdot a \cdot t \cdot D_a \cdot R_a$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Governing Equation |
|---|-----------|--------|------|--------------------|
| 1 | Plant Capacity | $P$ | MW | Fixed by demand |
| 2 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $sfc$ | kg/kWh | $3600 / \eta_{th} \cdot GCV$ |
| 3 | Annual Fuel Cost | $C_f$ | ₹/yr | $sfc \cdot P \cdot t \cdot p_f \cdot D_f$ |
| 4 | Water Pumping Power | $P_{pump}$ | kW | $\rho g Q H_w / \eta$ |
| 5 | Transmission Loss | $P_L$ | MW | $I^2 R \cdot D_t$ |
| 6 | Ash Quantity | $M_{ash}$ | tonnes/yr | $F \cdot a \cdot t$ |
| 7 | Land Area (rule) | $A_{land}$ | acres | $0.4 \cdot P$ (thermal), $2.5 \cdot P$ (hydro) |
| 8 | Specific Water Consumption | $swc$ | m³/MWh | 3.5 (tower), 1.0 (once-through) |
| 9 | Plant Load Factor | $PLF$ | % | $\text{Actual Units} / \text{Max Possible Units}$ |
| 10 | Auxiliary Consumption | $AUX$ | % | 8–12% (thermal), 1–2% (hydro) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the Indian power sector context (KTU relevance), site selection directly determines:
- Whether a **Super Thermal Power Plant** (like NTPC Vindhyachal, 4760 MW) can sustain 85%+ PLF.
- The **economic dispatch** capability within the regional grid.
- Compliance with **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)** norms under the **Environment Protection Act, 1986**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Distance Between Fuel Source and Load Center

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU derivation** — frequently asked for 7-mark questions.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A thermal power plant is to be located between a coal mine (distance $x$ km from mine side) and a load center (distance $L - x$ km). Determine the optimum value of $x$ that minimizes total transport cost.

### 3.1.2 Given Data

- Plant capacity: $P$ MW
- Specific fuel consumption: $sfc$ kg/kWh
- Operating hours per year: $t = 8760 \cdot PLF$
- Freight rate for coal: $c_1$ ₹/tonne/km
- Freight rate for electricity transmission: $c_2$ ₹/kWh/km

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Annual coal consumption**

$$M_{coal} = sfc \cdot P \cdot 1000 \cdot t \quad \text{(kg/year, since } P \text{ is in MW)}$$

Dividing by 1000 to convert kg → tonnes:

$$M_{coal} = \frac{sfc \cdot P \cdot 1000 \cdot t}{1000} = sfc \cdot P \cdot t \quad \text{(tonnes/year)}$$

**Step 2: Annual cost of coal transport**

Distance from mine = $x$ km. Freight rate = $c_1$ ₹/tonne/km.

$$C_{coal\_transport} = M_{coal} \cdot x \cdot c_1$$

**Step 3: Annual energy transmitted**

Energy generated per year = $P \cdot t \cdot 1000$ kWh (since P in MW = 1000 kW).

Energy lost in auxiliaries (8% typical):

$$E_{transmitted} = 0.92 \cdot P \cdot t \cdot 1000 \quad \text{kWh}$$

**Step 4: Annual cost of electricity transmission**

Distance from load center = $L - x$ km. Freight rate = $c_2$ ₹/kWh/km.

$$C_{elec\_transmission} = E_{transmitted} \cdot (L - x) \cdot c_2$$

**Step 5: Total annual cost**

$$C_{total} = M_{coal} \cdot x \cdot c_1 + 0.92 \cdot P \cdot t \cdot 1000 \cdot (L - x) \cdot c_2$$

**Step 6: Minimize $C_{total}$ with respect to $x$**

$$\frac{dC_{total}}{dx} = M_{coal} \cdot c_1 - 0.92 \cdot P \cdot t \cdot 1000 \cdot c_2 = 0$$

**Step 7: Solve for $x_{optimum}$**

$$M_{coal} \cdot c_1 = 0.92 \cdot P \cdot t \cdot 1000 \cdot c_2$$

Substituting $M_{coal} = sfc \cdot P \cdot t$:

$$sfc \cdot P \cdot t \cdot c_1 = 920 \cdot P \cdot t \cdot c_2$$

$$\boxed{x_{optimum} = \frac{920 \cdot c_2}{sfc \cdot c_1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight**: The optimum location is **INDEPENDENT of the total distance $L$** between mine and load center! It depends only on the fuel consumption rate and the relative freight rates.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cooling Water Make-up Requirement

### 3.2.1 Given Data

- Plant capacity: $P = 500$ MW
- Condenser heat rejected: $Q_{rej}$ MW
- Cooling tower evaporation loss: $e = 1\%$ of circulating water
- Drift loss: $d = 0.2\%$ of circulating water
- Blowdown: $b = 2\%$ of circulating water
- Range: $\Delta T = 10°\text{C}$
- Approach: $5°\text{C}$

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate circulating water flow**

Heat rejected: $Q_{rej} = P / \eta_{th} \cdot (1 - \eta_{th}) = 500/0.38 \cdot 0.62 = 815.8$ MW

$$Q_{rej} = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\dot{m}_w = \frac{815.8 \times 10^6}{4.186 \times 10^3 \times 10} = 19488 \text{ kg/s}$$

In m³/hr: $\dot{V}_w = 19488 \times 3.6 = 70157$ m³/hr

**Step 2: Calculate evaporation loss**

$$\dot{V}_{evap} = 0.01 \times 70157 = 701.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 3: Calculate drift loss**

$$\dot{V}_{drift} = 0.002 \times 70157 = 140.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 4: Calculate blowdown**

$$\dot{V}_{blowdown} = 0.02 \times 70157 = 1403.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 5: Total make-up water**

$$\dot{V}_{makeup} = \dot{V}_{evap} + \dot{V}_{drift} + \dot{V}_{blowdown} = 2245 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Specific Water Consumption} = \frac{2245 \times 24}{500} = 107.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{day per MW}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ash Pond Area Calculation

### 3.3.1 Given Data

- Coal consumption: $F = 4500$ tonnes/day
- Ash content: $a = 35\%$
- Ash density in pond: $\rho_{ash} = 0.8$ tonnes/m³
- Pond depth: $h = 5$ m
- Storage years: $n = 10$ years

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Daily ash generation**

$$M_{ash\_daily} = 4500 \times 0.35 = 1575 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 2: Annual ash generation**

$$M_{ash\_annual} = 1575 \times 365 = 574875 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 3: Total ash over 10 years**

$$M_{total} = 574875 \times 10 = 5.749 \times 10^6 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4: Volume required**

$$V_{ash} = \frac{M_{total}}{\rho_{ash}} = \frac{5.749 \times 10^6}{0.8} = 7.186 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Pond area**

$$A_{pond} = \frac{V_{ash}}{h} = \frac{7.186 \times 10^6}{5} = 1.437 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$$

Converting to acres (1 acre = 4047 m²):

$$\boxed{A_{pond} = 355 \text{ acres}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Site Selection by Plant Type

| Selection Factor | Thermal (Coal) | Hydro | Nuclear | Diesel | Gas Turbine |
|------------------|----------------|-------|---------|--------|-------------|
| **Primary fuel** | Coal (transport critical) | Water head | Uranium (stored on-site) | HSD/LDO | Natural gas |
| **Cooling water** | 3.5 m³/MWh (tower) | Minimal | 3.0 m³/MWh (critical) | 1.5 m³/MWh | 1.0 m³/MWh |
| **Distance from load** | 50–200 km | Often remote | 50–100 km | <50 km | <50 km |
| **Land area** | 0.4 acres/MW | 2.5 acres/MW | 0.6 acres/MW | 0.1 acres/MW | 0.05 acres/MW |
| **Population distance** | ≥ 1 km (EPA) | Catchment area | ≥ 5 km (exclusion zone) | <1 km | <1 km |
| **Foundation type** | Raft on hard soil | Rock anchor | Seismic base isolators | Simple | Simple |
| **Construction time** | 4–6 years | 6–10 years | 8–12 years | 1–2 years | 6 months–1 year |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Decision Flow for Site Selection (KTU Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Start: Demand Assessment] --> node2{Plant Type Decided?}
    node2 -- Yes --> node3[Identify Candidate Sites]
    node2 -- No --> node3
    node3 --> node4[Apply Technical Filters]
    node4 --> node5{Fuel Available?}
    node5 -- No --> node6[Eliminate Site]
    node5 -- Yes --> node7{Water Source Nearby?}
    node7 -- No --> node6
    node7 -- Yes --> node8{Soil Bearing OK?}
    node8 -- No --> node6
    node8 -- Yes --> node9[Apply Economic Filters]
    node9 --> node10{Land Cost Affordable?}
    node10 -- No --> node6
    node10 -- Yes --> node11{Transport Cost Low?}
    node11 -- No --> node6
    node11 -- Yes --> node12[Apply Environmental Filters]
    node12 --> node13[Clearance from MoEFCC]
    node13 --> node14{Approved?}
    node14 -- No --> node6
    node14 -- Yes --> node15[Final Site Selected]
    node6 --> node3
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Site Selection Factors Interrelationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXT[External Inputs]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: List any six factors affecting the site selection of a thermal power plant.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **Availability of fuel (coal)** at economical transport distance.
2. **Availability of cooling water** in adequate quantity throughout the year.
3. **Distance from load center** to minimize transmission losses.
4. **Nature of land and soil bearing capacity** for foundation design.
5. **Transportation facilities** — rail/road access for coal delivery and ash removal.
6. **Environmental considerations** — distance from populated areas, dispersion of pollutants.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Why is a hydro power plant usually located far from the load center?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A hydro power plant must be located at a site with adequate **hydraulic head** and **water flow**, which is naturally found in **hilly/mountainous regions** where load centers (cities) are typically absent. Although this increases transmission cost, the **zero fuel cost** and **renewable nature** of hydro power more than compensate for transmission losses over the plant lifetime. Also, **reservoir construction** requires valley topography, which is rarely near urban load centers.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q: Discuss in detail the various factors affecting the site selection of a steam (thermal) power plant. Compare it with the site selection criteria for a nuclear power plant. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a): Site Selection Factors for Thermal Power Plant (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The site selection of a thermal power plant involves the systematic evaluation of the following factors:

**1. Availability of Fuel (Coal)**
- The plant should be located near coal mines to reduce transportation cost, as coal accounts for **60–70%** of operating cost.
- Pit-head plants (e.g., NTPC Singrauli) minimize fuel logistics cost.

**2. Availability of Water**
- Required for condenser cooling, boiler feed, and ash handling.
- Once-through cooling needs a **large river/lake**; cooling towers allow inland siting.
- Specific water consumption: **3.5 m³/MWh** (tower) or **100 m³/MWh** (once-through).

**3. Distance from Load Center**
- Should be within **50–200 km** to limit transmission losses.
- Far locations increase both capital (line cost) and operational (I²R loss) cost.

**4. Land and Soil Conditions**
- Flat terrain with high soil bearing capacity (≥ 200 kN/m²) reduces foundation cost.
- Avoid flood-prone zones and earthquake-prone areas.

**5. Transportation Facilities**
- Railway siding for coal delivery.
- Road network for men, material, and ash disposal.

**6. Environmental Considerations**
- Distance from populated areas: minimum **1 km** (MoEFCC norm).
- Adequate stack height for dispersion: **220 m for 500 MW, 275 m for 1000 MW**.
- Provision for ash pond (≈ 0.4 acres/MW).

**7. Labor and Local Amenities**
- Availability of skilled and unskilled labor.
- Proximity to township for staff accommodation.

#### Part (b): Comparison with Nuclear Power Plant Site Selection (7 marks)

| Factor | Thermal Plant | Nuclear Plant |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Fuel source | Coal transported in | Uranium stored on-site (long-life) |
| Cooling water | 3.5 m³/MWh (tower) | 3.0 m³/MWh (critical) |
| Exclusion zone | 1 km from population | **5 km exclusion + 16 km emergency planning zone** |
| Seismic design | Zone II–III capable | Must withstand **Zone V** (highest) |
| Foundation | Raft on hard soil | Seismic base isolators; rock anchoring |
| Land area | 0.4 acres/MW | 0.6 acres/MW (more safety buffer) |
| Environmental clearance | Single ministry | Multiple agencies (AERB, DAE, MoEFCC) |
| Distance from load | Can be far (pit-head) | Typically closer for grid stability |
| Construction time | 4–6 years | 8–12 years |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students often write generic "factors" without linking each factor to its **economic or environmental impact metric**. Always quantify (e.g., "3.5 m³/MWh", "5 km exclusion zone"). **Unquantified answers lose 2–3 marks.**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Derive the expression for the optimum location of a thermal power plant between a coal mine and a load center. A 200 MW thermal power plant operates at PLF 80% with a specific fuel consumption of 0.6 kg/kWh. The freight rate for coal is ₹2/tonne/km and for electricity transmission is ₹0.005/kWh/km. The total distance between mine and load center is 100 km. Calculate the optimum distance from the coal mine. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a): Derivation (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given**: $P = 200$ MW, $PLF = 0.8$, $sfc = 0.6$ kg/kWh, $c_1 = ₹2/$tonne/km, $c_2 = ₹0.005/$kWh/km$, $L = 100$ km.

**Step 1: Operating hours per year**
$$t = 8760 \times 0.8 = 7008 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step 2: Annual coal consumption**
$$M_{coal} = sfc \times P \times 1000 \times t = 0.6 \times 200 \times 1000 \times 7008 = 8.41 \times 10^8 \text{ kg/year}$$
$$M_{coal} = 8.41 \times 10^5 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 3: Annual cost of coal transport (distance $x$ from mine)**
$$C_{coal} = M_{coal} \times x \times c_1 = 8.41 \times 10^5 \times x \times 2$$

**Step 4: Annual energy transmitted**
$$E_{trans} = 0.92 \times P \times t \times 1000 = 0.92 \times 200 \times 7008 \times 1000 = 1.289 \times 10^9 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5: Annual cost of transmission (distance $L-x$)**
$$C_{trans} = E_{trans} \times (L-x) \times c_2 = 1.289 \times 10^9 \times (100-x) \times 0.005$$

**Step 6: Total cost**
$$C_{total} = 1.682 \times 10^6 \cdot x + 6.445 \times 10^6 \cdot (100-x)$$

**Step 7: Differentiate and equate to zero**
$$\frac{dC_{total}}{dx} = 1.682 \times 10^6 - 6.445 \times 10^6 = 0$$

This shows that when freight rates and sfc are constant, **$x_{optimum}$ is independent of $L$**.

**Step 8: Apply general formula**
$$x_{optimum} = \frac{920 \times c_2}{sfc \times c_1} = \frac{920 \times 0.005}{0.6 \times 2}$$

$$x_{optimum} = \frac{4.6}{1.2} = 3.83 \text{ km from the coal mine}$$

#### Part (b): Numerical Interpretation (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Since $x_{optimum} = 3.83$ km from coal mine, the plant should be located **3.83 km from the mine and 96.17 km from the load center**.

**Interpretation:**
- The high cost of transporting electricity (₹0.005/kWh/km) relative to coal (₹2/tonne/km) means the plant should be closer to the coal mine.
- The ratio $\frac{c_2}{c_1 \cdot sfc} = 0.005 / (2 \times 0.6) = 0.00417$ km/(kWh/tonne) determines this preference.
- **Verification**: At $x = 3.83$ km, $C_{total}$ is minimum; for any $x > 3.83$, transmission cost dominates; for $x < 3.83$, coal transport cost dominates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> 1. **Unit mismatch**: Students forget that $P$ is in MW, so kWh requires multiplication by **1000**. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Auxiliary consumption**: Students forget the **8% auxiliary loss factor** (multiplied by 0.92). Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
> 3. **PLF usage**: Students use 8760 instead of $8760 \times PLF$. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Final answer units**: Must be in **km**, not dimensionless. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist for Site Selection**

- ✅ **Cooling water** is the SINGLE most critical factor — without water, no plant operates. For thermal: 3.5 m³/MWh (tower), 100 m³/MWh (once-through).
- ✅ **Pit-head plants** (e.g., NTPC Singrauli, NTPC Korba) are sited near coal mines because coal transport cost > electricity transmission cost.
- ✅ **Nuclear plants** need 5 km exclusion zone + 16 km emergency planning zone (EPZ) — non-negotiable under Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB).
- ✅ **Stack height** rule (KTU favorite): $H = 14 \cdot Q^{0.3}$ where $Q$ is SO₂ emission in kg/hr — applies for thermal plants.
- ✅ **Optimum plant location formula**: $x_{opt} = 920 \cdot c_2 / (sfc \cdot c_1)$ — independent of total distance $L$.
- ✅ **Ash pond area**: Approx **0.4 acres per MW** for thermal plants (rule of thumb).
- ✅ **Hydro plants** need **2.5 acres/MW** of land — much larger due to reservoir.
- ✅ **Diesel/Gas plants** have shortest construction time (6 months–1 year) — used for peaking/emergency.
- ✅ **Soil bearing capacity** ≥ 200 kN/m² is ideal for thermal; rock strata preferred for nuclear.
- ✅ **Ambient temperature** affects condenser pressure: a 1°C rise in cooling water temperature reduces Rankine efficiency by ~0.5%.
- ✅ **Wind direction** must be studied — stack gases should NOT blow toward the city.
- ✅ **Flood level data** of past 100 years must be analyzed before final site approval.
- ✅ **Transmission efficiency** drops by ~5–7% per 100 km — a key economic trade-off.
- ✅ **Future expansion**: Always reserve **30–40%** of land for future unit addition.
- ✅ **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** is mandatory under EIA Notification 2006 for plants > 50 MW.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chimney Draught, Nuclear Power Plants, Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Chimney Draught (Natural Draught)

### Formal Definition
**Chimney Draught** (also called **Natural Draught** or **Stack Effect**) is the negative pressure created at the base of a furnace or boiler due to the difference in density between the hot flue gases inside the chimney and the cooler atmospheric air outside. This pressure differential causes the combustion gases to flow upward through the heat exchange zones and exit through the chimney, without the need for any mechanical fan or blower.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Chimney draught is the ONLY type of draught that does **not consume any external work**. It relies purely on the buoyancy principle governed by the equation of hydrostatic equilibrium and the gas laws.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine blowing air into a balloon underwater. The air (less dense) rushes upward while the surrounding water (more dense) pushes down. The chimney works exactly like this, but using **hot air (flue gas)** and **cold air (atmosphere)**.

- Hot gases rise because they are **lighter** (lower density).
- The cold, denser atmospheric air pushes from below to fill the void left by the rising hot gases.
- This continuous push is the **draught** (a suction force measured in mm of water column).

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Atmospheric pressure (standard):** **101.325 kPa** or **760 mm Hg**
- **Universal Gas Constant (R̄):** **8.314 kJ/kmol·K**
- **Molecular weight of dry air:** **28.97 kg/kmol**
- **Molecular weight of flue gases (approx.):** **29.0 to 31.0 kg/kmol**
- **Draught measurement unit:** **mm of water column (mm wc)** — 1 mm wc ≈ **9.81 Pa**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "mm of water column"?** Pressure in low-pressure boiler draught systems is extremely small. Measuring in Pascals yields large unwieldy numbers. Engineers use a water column manometer where 1 cm height difference ≈ 98.1 Pa, making it very convenient.

---

## 1.2 Nuclear Power Plants — Overview

### Formal Definition
A **Nuclear Power Plant (NPP)** is a thermal power station in which the **heat source** is a **nuclear reactor** where controlled nuclear fission of isotopes such as **Uranium-235 (²³⁵U)**, **Plutonium-239 (²³⁹Pu)**, or **Uranium-233 (²³³U)** takes place. The liberated thermal energy is used to generate steam, which drives a steam turbine coupled to an electrical generator.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a nuclear reactor as a **highly controlled "atomic campfire"**:
- A campfire uses wood (chemical energy stored in carbon bonds) to release heat.
- A reactor uses **U-235** (nuclear energy stored in atomic nuclei) to release heat.
- The difference: 1 kg of U-235 releases the energy of approximately **2,500,000 kg of coal** (≈ **2 × 10⁷ kWh/kg**).

### Standard Metrics in Nuclear Engineering
- **Energy of 1 Atomic Mass Unit (u):** **931.5 MeV** (Einstein's mass-energy equivalence)
- **Fission energy per U-235 atom:** ≈ **200 MeV** (≈ 3.2 × 10⁻¹¹ J)
- **Specific energy of U-235:** ≈ **8.2 × 10¹³ J/kg** (82 TJ/kg)
- **Moderators used:** **Light Water (H₂O)**, **Heavy Water (D₂O)**, **Graphite (C)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** A common definition students miss in the exam is that a nuclear plant is a *thermal* power plant — only the **heat source** changes. The Rankine cycle remains identical to coal-based plants.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Power Plants

### Formal Definition
**Classification of Power Plants** refers to the systematic grouping of power-generating stations based on the **primary energy source**, **prime mover**, **load-handling capability**, or **service function** in the grid.

### Primary Classification Table (KTU Syllabus-Standard)

| S. No. | Classification Basis | Category |
|--------|--------------------|----------|
| 1 | **Source of Energy** | Conventional (Fossil, Hydro, Nuclear) and Non-Conventional (Solar, Wind, Tidal, Geothermal, Biomass) |
| 2 | **Prime Mover** | Steam Turbine, Gas Turbine, Hydraulic Turbine, Diesel Engine, Wind Turbine |
| 3 | **Load Service** | Base Load Plant, Peak Load Plant, Standby Plant |
| 4 | **Fuel Used** | Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Nuclear Fuel, Biofuel |
| 5 | **Application** | Public Utility, Captive (Industrial), Emergency, Mobile |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Out of these, the examiner frequently asks the **Source-based** and **Load-based** classifications. Memorize Base Load vs Peak Load distinction — it appears in Part A almost every year.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Chimney Draught

### Principle of Operation
The chimney draught is governed by **Archimedes' Principle of Buoyancy** applied to gas columns. The pressure at the base of any fluid/gas column is:

$$P_{base} = P_{top} + \rho \cdot g \cdot H$$

Where:
- $P_{base}$ = pressure at chimney base (Pa)
- $P_{top}$ = pressure at chimney exit (≈ atmospheric, Pa)
- $\rho$ = density of gas (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $H$ = chimney height (m)

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Cold air column** (outside the chimney) has density $\rho_a$ at ambient temperature $T_a$.
2. **Hot flue gas column** (inside the chimney) has density $\rho_g$ at higher temperature $T_g$.
3. Since $T_g > T_a \Rightarrow \rho_g < \rho_a$.
4. The cold column therefore weighs **more** than the hot column for the same height $H$.
5. This creates a **net upward suction force** at the chimney base.
6. The resultant pressure difference drives combustion air through the boiler furnace.

### The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula No. | Equation | Description | Units |
|:-----------:|----------|-------------|-------|
| **1** | $\Delta P = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_a - \rho_g)$ | Theoretical chimney draught pressure | Pa |
| **2** | $\rho = \frac{P}{R \cdot T}$ | Ideal gas density | kg/m³ |
| **3** | $\rho_a = \frac{353}{T_a}$ | Density of air at 1 atm (using $R_a = 287$ J/kg·K) | kg/m³ |
| **4** | $\rho_g = \frac{353 \cdot M_a}{T_g \cdot M_g}$ | Density of flue gas (where $M_a, M_g$ are molecular masses) | kg/m³ |
| **5** | $\Delta P_{wc} = 353 \cdot H \cdot g \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g}\right)$ | **Draught in terms of temperature** (most-used form) | Pa |
| **6** | $\Delta P_{mm} = 0.0342 \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g}\right)$ | **Draught in mm of water column** (multiplied by 1000/ρ_water·g) | mm wc |
| **7** | $H_{mass} = \frac{1}{D_{mass}} \cdot C$ | Height of chimney from mass flow rate $C$ | m |
| **8** | $D_{mass} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g H \cdot \left(\rho_a - \rho_g\right)}$ | Theoretical mass flow rate of flue gas | kg/s |
| **9** | $H = 0.0342 \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g}\right)$ (corrected) | **With friction and velocity factors** (multiply by ~1.2) | mm wc |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA FOR KTU BOARD EXAMS:**
> $$\boxed{\Delta P = 0.0342 \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g}\right) \text{ mm of water column}}$$
> The constant **0.0342** comes from: $\frac{P_{atm}}{R \cdot g} \cdot \frac{1}{\rho_{water}}$ evaluated at standard atmosphere. **This single formula is worth 7 marks** in any Part B question.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Power plant chimney design** in coal, oil, and biomass thermal stations.
- **Industrial furnace ventilation** (steel plants, cement kilns, glass furnaces).
- **Building HVAC systems** where the **stack effect** influences high-rise ventilation.
- **Boiler efficiency calculations** — draught is the silent force that must overcome **flue gas flow resistance** of the entire heat exchange surface.

---

## 2.2 Nuclear Power Plant — Theoretical Foundation

### Components & Their Functions
1. **Nuclear Reactor Core** — Houses fuel rods (U-235, Pu-239). The **heart** of the system where controlled fission occurs.
2. **Moderator** — Slows down fast neutrons to thermal energies (~0.025 eV) so that they can sustain the chain reaction. Examples: H₂O, D₂O, Graphite, Beryllium.
3. **Control Rods** — Made of neutron-absorbing materials (Boron Carbide B₄C, Cadmium Cd, Hafnium Hf). Inserted or withdrawn to regulate the **multiplication factor (k)**.
4. **Coolant** — Transfers fission heat from core to steam generator. Examples: H₂O, D₂O, CO₂, Liquid Sodium (Na), Helium.
5. **Steam Generator** — Heat exchanger that transfers heat from primary coolant to secondary feed water, producing steam. *Not present in Boiling Water Reactors (BWR)*.
6. **Reactor Shielding** — Massive concrete + lead + water barrier to contain radiation.
7. **Containment Structure** — Dome-shaped (typically 1 m thick pre-stressed concrete) building to prevent radioactive release during accidents.
8. **Turbine, Condenser, Cooling Tower** — Standard Rankine cycle components.

### Key Equations
| Parameter | Formula | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Multiplication Factor | $k = \frac{\text{neutrons in one generation}}{\text{neutrons in previous generation}}$ | $k=1$: Critical, $k<1$: Subcritical, $k>1$: Supercritical |
| Mass-Energy Equivalence | $E = \Delta m \cdot c^2$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Energy per Fission | $E_f \approx 200 \text{ MeV/atom}$ | 1 MeV = $1.602 \times 10^{-13}$ J |
| Reactor Power | $P = \dot{n}_f \cdot E_f$ | Where $\dot{n}_f$ = fissions per second |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Chimney Draught Equation

### Derivation — Pressure Inside vs Outside the Chimney

Consider a chimney of height $H$ open to atmosphere at the top. Let:
- $T_a$ = absolute temperature of atmospheric air (K)
- $T_g$ = absolute temperature of flue gas inside chimney (K)
- $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure at top = pressure at chimney exit (Pa)
- $R_a, R_g$ = characteristic gas constants for air and flue gas
- $M_a = 28.97$ kg/kmol, $M_g ≈ 30$ kg/kmol (approximate)

**Step 1: Pressure at chimney base due to cold air column (OUTSIDE)**

The pressure at ground level due to the outside atmospheric column of height $H$ is:

$$P_{cold} = P_{atm} + \rho_a \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 2: Pressure at chimney base due to hot gas column (INSIDE)**

The pressure at ground level due to the hot flue gas column of height $H$ is:

$$P_{hot} = P_{atm} + \rho_g \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 3: Theoretical Draught Definition**

The theoretical draught is the **pressure difference** that pushes air into the furnace:

$$\Delta P = P_{cold} - P_{hot}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta P = (P_{atm} + \rho_a \cdot g \cdot H) - (P_{atm} + \rho_g \cdot g \cdot H)$$

$$\Delta P = \rho_a \cdot g \cdot H - \rho_g \cdot g \cdot H$$

Factoring $g \cdot H$:

$$\Delta P = g \cdot H \cdot (\rho_a - \rho_g)$$

This is the **master equation (Formula 1)**.

**Step 4: Expressing densities using Ideal Gas Law**

For air: $\rho_a = \frac{P_a}{R_a \cdot T_a}$ where $R_a = 287$ J/kg·K

For flue gas: $\rho_g = \frac{P_g}{R_g \cdot T_g}$ where $R_g = \frac{\bar{R}}{M_g}$

**Step 5: Substitution into master equation**

$$\Delta P = g \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{P_a}{R_a \cdot T_a} - \frac{P_g}{R_g \cdot T_g}\right)$$

Assuming both columns have the same mean pressure (≈ $P_{atm}$) and using $R = \frac{\bar{R}}{M}$:

$$\Delta P = g \cdot H \cdot P_{atm} \cdot \left(\frac{M_a}{T_a} - \frac{M_g}{T_g}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\bar{R}}$$

**Step 6: Numerator/denominator adjustment for M_a ≈ M_g (≈ 29 kg/kmol)**

For typical boiler flue gas $M_a / M_g ≈ 1$ (closely equal molecular masses), so:

$$\Delta P = \frac{P_{atm} \cdot g \cdot H \cdot M}{\bar{R}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g}\right)$$

**Step 7: Converting to mm of water column**

Dividing by $\rho_{water} \cdot g = 1000 \cdot 9.81 = 9810$ N/m³ per metre of water:

$$\Delta P_{mm\,wc} = \frac{P_{atm} \cdot M}{\bar{R} \cdot \rho_{water}} \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g}\right)$$

Plugging values: $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa, $M = 29$ kg/kmol, $\bar{R} = 8314$ J/kmol·K, $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³:

$$\frac{101325 \cdot 29}{8314 \cdot 1000} = 0.3534$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta P_{mm\,wc} = 0.3534 \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g}\right) \cdot 1000 / 9.81$$

After further numerical reduction and conversion:

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{mm\,wc} = 0.0342 \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant 0.0342 emerges from $\frac{P_{atm} \cdot M}{\bar{R} \cdot \rho_{water} \cdot g}$ where the division by $g$ converts the unit from metres of water to mm of water. The **/1000** for the mm conversion is also absorbed.

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A power plant chimney is 60 m tall. The atmospheric temperature is 27°C and the average flue gas temperature inside the chimney is 247°C. Calculate the theoretical draught produced in:
(a) Pa
(b) mm of water column

**Solution:**

Given:
- $H = 60$ m
- $T_a = 27 + 273 = 300$ K
- $T_g = 247 + 273 = 520$ K
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa

**Part (a) — In Pascals**

Using $\Delta P = 0.0342 \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g}\right) \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1000$:

$$\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{520} = 0.003333 - 0.001923 = 0.001410 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta P = 0.0342 \cdot 60 \cdot 0.001410 \cdot 9810$$

$$\Delta P = 0.0342 \cdot 60 \cdot 0.001410 \cdot 9810 = 28.42 \text{ Pa}$$

**Part (b) — In mm of water column**

Converting 1 mm wc = 9.81 Pa:

$$\Delta P_{mm\,wc} = \frac{28.42}{9.81} = 2.90 \text{ mm of water column}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Pattern:** Most students lose 1-2 marks by forgetting to convert °C to K. Always add **273** (or 273.15 for precision) to the Celsius value before applying the formula.

### Analytical Matrix — Draught Losses in Real Chimneys

| Loss Component | Typical Magnitude | Cause |
|----------------|-------------------|-------|
| Friction in chimney | 10-15% | Wall roughness, gas viscosity |
| Acceleration of gas | 5-10% | Velocity head at exit |
| Shock at bends/entries | 5-8% | Sudden area changes |
| Heat loss through walls | 5-8% | Reduces effective ΔT |

**Total efficiency factor (η_chimney)** ≈ 0.7 to 0.8 of theoretical.

Actual available draught: $D_{actual} = \eta \cdot D_{theoretical}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Chimney Draught Pressure Visualization (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Atmospheric Air Column H] -->|Pressure Pa = atm + rho_a gH| nodeB[Chimney Base External Pressure]
    nodeC[Hot Flue Gas Column H] -->|Pressure Pg = atm + rho_g gH| nodeD[Chimney Base Internal Pressure]
    nodeB -->|Pressure Difference| nodeE[Theoretical Draught deltaP]
    nodeD -->|Pressure Difference| nodeE
    nodeE -->|Multiplied by eta| nodeF[Available Draught at Furnace]
    nodeF -->|Drives| nodeG[Combustion Air through Boiler]
    nodeF -->|Overcomes| nodeH[Flue Gas Flow Resistance in Heat Exchangers]
    nodeG -->|Produces| nodeI[Steam in Boiler Drums]
    nodeH -->|Exits via| nodeJ[Electrostatic Precipitator]
    nodeI -->|Drives| nodeK[Steam Turbine and Generator]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Module 3)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define draught in a steam power plant. Why is it required?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Draught is the small pressure difference (typically 5–25 mm of water column) created at the base of the furnace to overcome the resistance of the fuel bed, grate, economizer, superheater, and air preheater, in order to flow the combustion air through them and remove the products of combustion. **It is required** because natural convection alone cannot supply sufficient air for complete combustion and cannot carry away the large volume of hot flue gases produced. Without adequate draught, the boiler will suffer incomplete combustion, back-pressure on the fire side, reduced heat transfer, and unsafe operating conditions. (3 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List any THREE differences between Base Load and Peak Load power plants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Base Load Plant | Peak Load Plant |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Operating hours | 24 × 7, continuous (~85% PLF) | Few hours per day during peaks |
| Capacity factor | High (>70%) | Low (5–15%) |
| Start-up time | Slow (hours) — e.g., coal, nuclear | Fast (minutes) — e.g., gas turbine, hydro |
| Operating cost | Low per kWh | High per kWh |
| Example | Coal, Nuclear, Large Hydro | Diesel, Gas Turbine, Pumped Storage |
| Capital cost per kW | Lower | Higher |

(3 marks — 1 for each valid difference)

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the theoretical draught produced by a chimney in terms of its height, atmospheric temperature, and flue gas temperature. State the assumptions made. **— 7 marks**

**(b)** A chimney serves a coal-fired boiler with the following data:
- Height of chimney, H = 80 m
- Atmospheric temperature, Tₐ = 293 K
- Average flue gas temperature inside chimney, T_g = 553 K
- Mass of flue gases, C = 25 kg/s
- Mean gas velocity in chimney, V_g = 10 m/s
- Coefficient of discharge, C_d = 0.65
- Density of air at 0°C, ρ_ao = 1.293 kg/m³

Calculate:
1. The theoretical draught in **mm of water column**.
2. The mass flow rate of flue gases actually induced (using the given C_d).
3. Comment on whether the available draught is sufficient for normal boiler operation. **— 7 marks**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions:** (1 mark)
- Mean molecular mass of air ≈ mean molecular mass of flue gases (≈ 29 kg/kmol)
- Pressure is uniform throughout both columns (≈ atmospheric)
- Steady-state, one-dimensional flow
- Ideal gas behaviour
- No heat loss through chimney walls (isothermal)

**Derivation** (already shown exhaustively in Section 3, but key steps for exam):

The pressure at the base due to the outside cold air column of height H:

$$P_{cold} = P_{atm} + \rho_a \cdot g \cdot H$$

The pressure at the base due to the inside hot gas column of height H:

$$P_{hot} = P_{atm} + \rho_g \cdot g \cdot H$$

Net theoretical draught:

$$\Delta P = P_{cold} - P_{hot} = g \cdot H \cdot (\rho_a - \rho_g) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Using ideal gas law $\rho = \frac{P \cdot M}{\bar{R} \cdot T}$ and simplifying:

$$\Delta P = \frac{P_{atm} \cdot g \cdot H \cdot M}{\bar{R}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g}\right) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Converting to mm of water column (dividing by $\rho_w \cdot g / 1000$):

$$\boxed{\Delta P = 0.0342 \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g}\right) \text{ mm of wc}} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

Where 0.0342 = $\frac{P_{atm} \cdot M}{1000 \cdot \bar{R}}$ (with $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa, $M = 29$, $\bar{R} = 8314$ J/kmol·K). **(3 marks for derivation steps, 2 marks for final boxed equation + units, 1 mark for assumptions)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** H = 80 m, Tₐ = 293 K, T_g = 553 K, C = 25 kg/s, V_g = 10 m/s, C_d = 0.65, ρ_ao = 1.293 kg/m³

**1. Theoretical draught in mm wc:**

$$\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{1}{293} - \frac{1}{553} = 0.003413 - 0.001808 = 0.001605 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta P = 0.0342 \cdot 80 \cdot 0.001605 = 4.39 \text{ mm of water column}$$

**In Pascals:** $\Delta P = 4.39 \times 9.81 = 43.07$ Pa **(2 marks)**

**2. Mass flow rate of flue gases actually induced:**

First compute $\rho_g$ at T_g = 553 K (assuming M_a = M_g):

$$\rho_g = \rho_{ao} \cdot \frac{273}{T_g} = 1.293 \cdot \frac{273}{553} = 0.638 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Cross-sectional area of chimney (derived from V_g and C):

$$A = \frac{C}{\rho_g \cdot V_g} = \frac{25}{0.638 \cdot 10} = 3.92 \text{ m}^2$$

Equivalent velocity head due to draught (Pa):

$$\Delta P_{actual} = 43.07 \text{ Pa} \Rightarrow h_{velocity} = \frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_g} = \frac{2 \cdot 43.07}{0.638} = 135.05 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$v_{eff} = \sqrt{135.05} = 11.62 \text{ m/s}$$

Actual mass flow rate induced:

$$\dot{m}_{actual} = C_d \cdot \rho_g \cdot A \cdot v_{eff} = 0.65 \cdot 0.638 \cdot 3.92 \cdot 11.62 = 18.92 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{actual} \approx 18.92 \text{ kg/s}} \quad \text{...(3 marks)}$$

**3. Comment on sufficiency:**

The actual induced flow (18.92 kg/s) is **less** than the required flue gas flow (25 kg/s). This means the chimney is **insufficient** by itself; an **induced draught fan (ID fan)** must supplement the natural draught. The boiler operation will not be satisfactory at full load with this chimney alone. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR)** nuclear power plant. List its main components and state the function of each. **— 7 marks**

**(b)** Classify power plants on the basis of (i) source of energy, and (ii) load service. Compare a **Nuclear Power Plant** and a **Coal-based Thermal Power Plant** on at least six parameters. **— 7 marks**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — PWR Working (7 marks)

A **Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR)** is the most widely used commercial nuclear reactor (~70% of world NPPs). In a PWR, the **primary coolant (light water) is kept under high pressure (~155 bar)** to prevent it from boiling, even at the high operating temperature (~325°C). Heat from the primary loop is transferred to a **secondary loop** through a steam generator, where steam is produced at lower pressure (~65 bar) and sent to the turbine.

**Block Diagram** (drawn as per Section 4.2 mermaid block).

**Main Components & Functions** (1 mark each, 6 components × ~1 mark):

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **1. Reactor Core** | Houses U-235 fuel rods in zircaloy cladding; sustained fission chain reaction takes place here. |
| **2. Control Rods (B₄C / Cd / Hf)** | Inserted to absorb excess neutrons and maintain k = 1 (critical state). |
| **3. Moderator (Light Water H₂O)** | Slows fast neutrons (~2 MeV) to thermal energies (~0.025 eV) for sustained fission. |
| **4. Primary Coolant Pump** | Circulates pressurized water (~225 kg/cm²) through core → steam generator → back to core. |
| **5. Steam Generator** | Shell-and-tube heat exchanger; transfers heat from primary loop to secondary feed water. |
| **6. Pressurizer** | Maintains primary loop pressure above saturation, preventing primary coolant boiling. |
| **7. Containment Building** | Pre-stressed concrete dome (1 m thick) that prevents release of radioactive material in case of accident. |

#### Part (b) — Classification and Comparison (7 marks)

**Classification of Power Plants** (3 marks):

**(i) Source of Energy:**
- **Conventional** — Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Hydro, Nuclear.
- **Non-Conventional (Renewable)** — Solar, Wind, Tidal, Geothermal, Biomass, OTEC, Wave.

**(ii) Load Service:**
- **Base Load Plants** — High capacity factor, run continuously (e.g., coal, nuclear, large hydro).
- **Peak Load Plants** — Operate only during peak demand hours, quick start (e.g., diesel, gas turbine, pumped storage hydro).
- **Standby Plants** — Kept ready for emergencies (e.g., DG sets in hospitals, data centers).

**Comparison Table: Nuclear vs Coal-based Thermal Power Plant** (4 marks — 6 parameters @ 0.5–1 mark each):

| Parameter | Nuclear Power Plant | Coal-based Thermal Plant |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| 1. Fuel | U-235 / Pu-239 (~10 g/day per MW) | Coal (~5000 tonnes/day per 1000 MW) |
| 2. Fuel cost per kWh | Very low (high energy density) | Higher & volatile (market-driven) |
| 3. Capital cost | Very high | Lower |
| 4. Operating cost | Low | High (coal handling, ash disposal) |
| 5. Efficiency | 32–40% (Rankine cycle) | 35–42% (modern subcritical) |
| 6. Environmental impact | No CO₂; low air pollution, but **radioactive waste** | High CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ, fly-ash, mercury |
| 7. Site location | Can be located near load centres | Must be near coal source & water |
| 8. Construction time | 8–10 years | 4–5 years |
| 9. Refuelling interval | Every 1–2 years | Continuous |
| 10. Safety concerns | Reactor meltdown, radiation leak | Boiler explosion, fire, ash ponds |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Disaster:** In chimney draught problems, 90% of students forget to convert °C → K. This **single mistake loses 2 marks** out of 7.
> 2. **Confusing R̄ with Rₐ:** The universal gas constant is 8.314 kJ/kmol·K. Using 287 J/kg·K (specific for air) in the wrong place will yield a wrong constant (0.0342 vs 0.0117) and a numerical error.
> 3. **Forgetting Assumptions:** In derivations, the examiner expects explicit **assumptions** (isothermal column, equal molecular weights, etc.). Missing this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing Theoretical vs Actual Draught:** The formula gives **theoretical** draught. The **actual available draught is only 70–80%** of theoretical due to friction, acceleration, and heat losses. Students who equate them are marked down.
> 5. **PWR vs BWR Confusion:** Many students incorrectly draw a steam generator inside a BWR. Remember: **BWR (Boiling Water Reactor) has NO steam generator** — steam is generated directly in the reactor pressure vessel.
> 6. **Multiplication Factor (k):** Never write $k = 1$ in a working reactor; write **k ≈ 1.0001 (just above critical)** because the actual operating point is slightly supercritical to overcome neutron poisons and leakage.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Equation:** $\Delta P = 0.0342 \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g}\right)$ mm of water column — memorize the **0.0342 constant** and its origin.
- **Conversion:** 1 mm of water column = **9.81 Pa**; always convert °C → K by adding 273 (or 273.15).
- **Chimney Draught Principle:** Hot flue gas column weighs less than cold air column → buoyancy creates suction at the furnace base.
- **Chimney Draught = Natural Draught = No External Work**; supplemented in large plants by **FD (Forced Draught)**, **ID (Induced Draught)**, or **Balanced Draught** fans.
- **Power Plant Classification (must-know categories):** Source-based, Prime-mover-based, Load-based, Fuel-based.
- **Base Load vs Peak Load:** Capacity factor, run-time, start-up time — know at least 3 differences.
- **Nuclear Power Plant = Thermal Power Plant with Nuclear Heat Source**; the **Rankine cycle is identical**, only the boiler is replaced.
- **Fuel, Moderator, Coolant, Control Rods, Shielding** — the 5 essential elements of any nuclear reactor.
- **PWR** (most common, ~70% world share) uses **two separate loops**; **BWR** uses **a single loop** (no steam generator).
- **Energy release per fission ≈ 200 MeV/atom of U-235**; **1 kg U-235 ≈ 2,500,000 kg of coal** in energy equivalent.
- **Multiplication Factor:** k=1 critical, k<1 subcritical, k>1 supercritical; controlled using control rods of B₄C/Cd/Hf.
- **Always state assumptions** in derivations: equal molecular weights, isothermal column, ideal gas, steady-state.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
