topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Safety Measures in a Power Plant?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Safety Measures** in a Power Plant refer to the integrated set of engineered safeguards, operational protocols, protective devices, administrative controls, and personnel protective equipment (PPE) implemented to prevent accidents, mitigate hazards, and protect human life, machinery, and the environment from the inherent risks associated with steam, fuel, ash, electrical, and chemical processes in power generation.

A power plant is one of the most hazard-dense industrial environments in existence. It handles:
- **High-pressure steam** (up to **250 bar** and **600 °C** in supercritical units)
- **Combustible fuels** (coal, oil, natural gas)
- **Toxic chemicals** (hydrazine, ammonia, chlorine)
- **High-voltage electricity** (up to **400 kV** for EHV transmission)
- **Hot ash and molten slag** (above **1000 °C**)
- **Rotating machinery** at high speeds (3000/3600 rpm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Household Kitchen" Metaphor 🍳**
> Think of a power plant like a giant, industrial-strength kitchen. Just as a home cook wears oven mitts (PPE), keeps a fire extinguisher nearby (fire safety), installs a smoke detector (alarm system), and turns off the gas when leaving (lock-out/tag-out), a power plant operator follows the same layered logic but at a million-fold scale. The stove is the boiler, the gas line is the fuel line, the kitchen fan is the draft system, and the smoke alarm is the Burner Management System (BMS). **Safety = Layered Defense (the Swiss Cheese Model)**.

## 1.2 Classification of Hazards in a Power Plant

| Hazard Category | Typical Source | Severity Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal** | Steam pipes, hot surfaces, molten ash | High |
| **Mechanical** | Rotors, turbines, conveyors, crushers | High |
| **Electrical** | Generators, transformers, switchyards | Very High |
| **Chemical** | Water treatment, flue gas desulfurization | Medium–High |
| **Fire/Explosion** | Coal dust, oil, hydrogen (generator cooling) | Very High |
| **Ergonomic** | Noise, vibration, confined spaces | Medium |
| **Environmental** | Effluents, fly ash, NOₓ, SO₂ | High |

## 1.3 The Hierarchy of Controls (KTU Board-Favorite Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Controls (Most Effective → Least Effective)**
> 1. **Elimination** — Remove the hazard entirely (e.g., use low-sulfur coal to eliminate SO₂ hazard)
> 2. **Substitution** — Replace with a less hazardous option (e.g., water-based cleaning instead of solvent)
> 3. **Engineering Controls** — Physical safeguards (relief valves, interlocks, fencing)
> 4. **Administrative Controls** — SOPs, permits, training, signage
> 5. **PPE** — Helmets, respirators, earplugs (last line of defense)

## 1.4 Statutory and Regulatory Framework (India-Specific)

- **Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR), 1950** — Governs boiler design, manufacture, and inspection.
- **Factories Act, 1948** — Workplace safety obligations.
- **Electricity Act, 2003** — Electrical safety compliance.
- **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB)** — For nuclear power plants.
- **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations** — Technical standards for grid-connected plants.
- **IS / IEC Standards** — e.g., **IS 14656** for PPE, **IEC 61508** for Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Defense — The Swiss Cheese Model

The **Swiss Cheese Model** (James Reason, 1990) is the globally accepted accident-causation framework that KTU examiners love to reference.

> [!NOTE]
> Each defensive layer (policy, training, equipment, PPE) is like a slice of Swiss cheese. Each slice has holes (latent failures + active failures). An accident only occurs when **all holes align** across every layer. Therefore, safety strategy = **add more layers + shrink the holes**.

## 2.2 Safety Measures in Major Power Plant Sub-Systems

### A. Coal Handling Plant (CHP) Safety

1. **Dust Suppression Systems** — Water spray nozzles, dry fog systems, and dust extraction cyclones.
2. **Fire Detection & Suppression** — Infrared (IR) flame detectors, linear heat sensing cables (LHS cable), and CO₂ / foam flooding systems in conveyors and bunkers.
3. **Bunker Protection** — Coal bunker level indicators + **CO monitors** (coal self-heating begins at **~70 °C** and self-ignites near **~300 °C**).
4. **Conveyor Safety** — Pull-cord switches (both sides), belt sway switches, zero-speed switches, and magnetic separators to remove tramp metal.
5. **Crushing & Pulverizing** — Enclosed mills with explosion vents (NFPA 654 compliant).

### B. Boiler Safety

1. **Safety Valves** — Spring-loaded and rupture-disc combinations to relieve overpressure.
2. **Burner Management System (BMS)** — Prevents light-off without all permissive conditions (purge, ignition, fuel pressure, FD/ID fan running).
3. **Flame Scanners** — UV / IR detectors; if flame fails within **1 second** (for gas) or **3 seconds** (for oil), the BMS initiates a **Master Fuel Trip (MFT)**.
4. **Drum Level Control** — Three-element control (drum level, steam flow, feedwater flow); low-low level triggers MFT to prevent furnace explosion.
5. **Sentinel & Tell-Tale Valves** — Mounted on safety valves; leak indicates seat damage.
6. **Boiler Light-Up & Purge** — Pre-ignition purge of **5 volume changes** with FD/ID fan before fuel admission.
7. **Soot Blower Safety** — Automatic retraction and over-travel interlocks.

### C. Turbine & Generator Safety

1. **Overspeed Trip (OST)** — Mechanical flyball governor + hydraulic trip at **110% of rated speed** (e.g., **3300 rpm** for a 3000 rpm unit).
2. **Thrust Bearing Wear Monitor** — Sudden axial shift trips the unit.
3. **Vibration Monitoring** — Proximity probes on each bearing; trip threshold typically **≥ 12 mm/s RMS** (ISO 10816-3).
4. **Hydrogen Cooling Safety** — H₂ purity maintained above **98%** to prevent explosive mixtures (LEL of H₂ = 4% in air). CO₂ is used as a purge medium to displace H₂ during shut-down.
5. **Lube Oil System** — Two AC pumps + one DC emergency pump; low oil pressure trips.
6. **Turning Gear** — Prevents rotor bowing during cool-down.
7. **Condenser Vacuum Trip** — Prevents overspeed from steam flash evaporation.
8. **Earthing & Lightning Protection** — Grounding grid with earth resistance below **1 Ω**.

### D. Electrical Safety

1. **Earth Leakage Circuit Breakers (ELCB)** — Trip at **30 mA** for personnel protection.
2. **Insulation Monitoring Devices (IMD)** for ungrounded (IT) systems.
3. **Arc Flash Protection** — Arc-resistant switchgear, PPE rated at **≥ 4 cal/cm²**.
4. **Lock-Out / Tag-Out (LOTO)** — Mandatory before any maintenance.
5. **Safe Approach Distances** — **1 m** for 11 kV, **2 m** for 33 kV, **3.7 m** for 400 kV (per Indian Electricity Rules).
6. **Step & Touch Potential Control** — Equipotential bonding mats in switchyards.

### E. Ash Handling Safety

1. **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) Safety** — High-voltage interlocks, rapping interlocks, access doors with earth switches.
2. **Bottom Ash Hopper Hydration** — Continuous water sprays to cool ash below **60 °C** before evacuation.
3. **Fly Ash Silos** — Fluidization air pads to prevent arching; explosion vents for silo overpressure.
4. **Pneumatic Conveying** — Pressure relief valves, leak detection.

### F. Water/Steam Cycle Safety

1. **Deaerator Level Control** — Low-level trip to prevent pump cavitation.
2. **Chemical Cleaning** — Inhibited hydrochloric / citric acid with neutralizing pits.
3. **Condensate Polisher Regeneration** — Acid/alkali handling with eyewash + emergency shower within **10 m**.
4. **HP/LP Heater Drain Level** — High level trips to protect feedwater pump.

### G. Fire Safety (Common to All Plants)

1. **Detection** — Smoke, heat, IR, flame detectors (addressable analog systems).
2. **Suppression** — Water spray, sprinklers, foam, CO₂, dry chemical powder (DCP), Inergen (for IT rooms).
3. **Fire Walls** — **2-hour fire-rated** separation between cable galleries and turbine hall.
4. **Fire Hydrant Network** — Ring main with **≥ 7 kg/cm²** pressure at the farthest hydrant.
5. **Evacuation** — Clearly marked exits, emergency lighting on UPS, assembly points.

### H. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

| Body Part | PPE | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Head | Hard hat (Type-II Class-E) | IS 2925 |
| Eyes/Face | Safety goggles / face shield | IS 5983 |
| Hearing | Ear plugs / ear muffs (≥ 25 dB attenuation) | IS 6229 |
| Respiratory | Half-face / full-face respirator with HEP/OV cartridges | IS 14746 |
| Hands | Heat-resistant gloves, electrical gloves | IS 4770 |
| Feet | Steel-toe safety shoes (ISI marked) | IS 15298 |
| Body | Flame-retardant coveralls, high-visibility vest | NFPA 2112 |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Topic | Formula / Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overpressure relief | $P_{sv} = P_{design} \times 1.05$ to $1.10$ | bar |
| 2 | Boiler purge volume | $V_{purge} = N \times V_{furnace}$, with $N \geq 5$ | m³ |
| 3 | Pre-ignition purge time | $t_{purge} = \dfrac{N \times V}{Q_{FD}}$ | s |
| 4 | MFT response (gas) | $t_{MFT,gas} \leq 1.0$ | s |
| 5 | MFT response (oil) | $t_{MFT,oil} \leq 3.0$ | s |
| 6 | Overspeed trip threshold | $N_{trip} = 1.10 \times N_{rated}$ | rpm |
| 7 | Hydrogen explosion LEL | $LFL_{H_2} = 4\%$ (by volume in air) | % |
| 8 | Safe H₂ purity in generator | $y_{H_2} \geq 98\%$ | % |
| 9 | Touch potential (IEEE 80) | $V_{touch} = (1000 + 1.5 \cdot \rho_s \cdot C_s) \cdot \dfrac{0.116}{\sqrt{t_s}}$ | V |
| 10 | Step potential (IEEE 80) | $V_{step} = (1000 + 6 \cdot \rho_s \cdot C_s) \cdot \dfrac{0.116}{\sqrt{t_s}}$ | V |
| 11 | Grounding resistance | $R_{earth} \leq 1.0$ (power station); $\leq 5.0$ (general) | Ω |
| 12 | ELCB trip current (personnel) | $I_{\Delta} \leq 30 \times 10^{-3}$ | A |
| 13 | Arc-flash incident energy | $E = 4.184 \cdot C_f \cdot E_n \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{0.2}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{610}{D}\right)^x$ | cal/cm² |
| 14 | LEL % for coal dust | $LFL_{coal} \approx 50 \text{ g/m}^3$ | g/m³ |
| 15 | Noise exposure limit (8 h) | $L_{ex,8h} \leq 85$ | dBA |
| 16 | Burner turndown ratio | $TR = \dfrac{Q_{max}}{Q_{min}}$ | – |
| 17 | Hydraulic lock-out pressure | $P_{trip} = \dfrac{F_{spring}}{A_{piston}}$ | bar |
| 18 | SF₆ gas pressure (GIS) | $P_{SF_6} \approx 0.5 \text{ MPa @ 20 °C}$ | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: These formulas are not just academic. For example, the IEEE 80 touch/step potential equations are used to design grounding grids in 400 kV switchyards. A value exceeding **437 V** (IEEE 80 limit for body weight 50 kg) triggers redesign. In KTU exam questions, you will most often encounter boiler purge, MFT logic, overspeed trip, and H₂ purity calculations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pre-Ignition Purge Time for a Boiler

The purpose of purge is to flush out any combustible fuel-air mixture that could explode on light-off. The standard rule is to provide **at least 5 volume changes** with the FD fan.

**Given:**
- Furnace volume: $V_f = 800 \text{ m}^3$
- Required purge volume changes: $N = 5$
- FD fan flow rate: $Q_{FD} = 40 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ (at purge condition)

**Step 1 — Total purge volume required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{purge} &= N \times V_f \\
&= 5 \times 800 \\
&= 4000 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Time to achieve this volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{purge} &= \dfrac{V_{purge}}{Q_{FD}} \\
&= \dfrac{4000}{40} \\
&= 100 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add 10% safety margin (engineering practice):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{actual} &= 1.10 \times t_{purge} \\
&= 1.10 \times 100 \\
&= 110 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result**: The BMS must hold the fuel admission for **at least 110 s** after FD fan run command. If the air flow falls below **25% of full load** during purge, the timer resets.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overspeed Trip Threshold for a 3000 rpm Steam Turbine

**Given:**
- Rated speed: $N_r = 3000 \text{ rpm}$
- Mechanical overspeed trip setting: **110%** of rated
- Emergency overspeed trip: **115%** (hydraulic)

**Step 1 — Calculate 110% trip speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{trip,mech} &= 1.10 \times N_r \\
&= 1.10 \times 3000 \\
&= 3300 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate 115% trip speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{trip,hyd} &= 1.15 \times N_r \\
&= 1.15 \times 3000 \\
&= 3450 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Margin between mechanical and hydraulic trips:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta N &= 3450 - 3300 \\
&= 150 \text{ rpm} \;(\text{i.e., } 5\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of two-trip system**: The mechanical (flyball) trip is the **first line**; the hydraulic trip is the **backup**. The 5% gap ensures the hydraulic trip does not spuriously activate during normal transients.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydrogen Purity Check in a Generator

**Given (inert purge sequence):**
- Initial gas in generator: Air (79% N₂ + 21% O₂)
- After N₂ purge: 95% N₂ + 5% O₂
- After H₂ purge (target): **98% H₂ + 2% impurity**

**Step 1 — Verify LEL safety:**

The Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) of H₂ in air is **4%**. With 98% H₂, the only oxidizer present is the residual **2% (mostly N₂)**. Since no oxygen is available, the mixture is **inert and safe**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{H_2} &= 98\% \\
y_{O_2} &\leq 0.5\% \; (\text{by design}) \\
\text{Product} &= y_{H_2} \times y_{O_2} \\
&= 0.98 \times 0.005 = 0.0049 < 0.0049 \text{ (limit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Confirm against NFPA 68:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{H_2, actual} &= 98\% \\
y_{H_2, NFPA \ limit} &= \geq 95\% \\
\text{Safe?} &= \text{YES} \; (98\% > 95\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: The purge sequence is always **Air → N₂ → CO₂ → H₂** (during commissioning) and **H₂ → CO₂ → Air** (during shutdown). CO₂ acts as the "inert divider" because it is denser than both air and H₂, forming an effective barrier.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Touch Potential for a 220 kV Switchyard

**Given (per IEEE 80):**
- Surface soil resistivity: $\rho_s = 200 \; \Omega \cdot m$
- Surface layer resistivity (gravel): $\rho_s' = 3000 \; \Omega \cdot m$
- Surface layer thickness: $h_s = 0.10 \text{ m}$
- Fault duration: $t_s = 0.5 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Calculate surface layer derating factor $C_s$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_s &= 1 - \dfrac{0.09 \cdot \left(1 - \rho_s / \rho_s'\right)}{2 h_s + 0.09} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{0.09 \cdot (1 - 200/3000)}{2(0.10) + 0.09} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{0.09 \cdot (1 - 0.0667)}{0.29} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{0.09 \cdot 0.9333}{0.29} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{0.0840}{0.29} \\
&= 1 - 0.2897 \\
&= 0.7103
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Touch potential limit (IEEE 80, Eq. 27):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{touch,lim} &= (1000 + 1.5 \cdot \rho_s \cdot C_s) \cdot \dfrac{0.116}{\sqrt{t_s}} \\
&= (1000 + 1.5 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.7103) \cdot \dfrac{0.116}{\sqrt{0.5}} \\
&= (1000 + 213.1) \cdot \dfrac{0.116}{0.7071} \\
&= 1213.1 \cdot 0.1641 \\
&\approx 199.1 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Body weight 50 kg reference:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{touch,lim}(70 \text{ kg}) &= (1000 + 1.5 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.7103) \cdot \dfrac{0.157}{\sqrt{0.5}} \\
&\approx 269.4 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The calculated touch potential is **199 V**, well below the IEEE 80 limit of **437 V** for a 50 kg body. The grounding grid is therefore **safe by design**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table of Safety Logic

| Trip / Interlock | Initiating Condition | Action | Response Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MFT** | Loss of flame, drum low-low, air flow low | Cut all fuel, trip FD/ID | < 1 s |
| **OST** | Speed > 110% rated | Close ESVs + trip TBV | < 0.2 s |
| **Condenser Vacuum Trip** | Vacuum < 600 mmHg | Trip turbine | < 2 s |
| **Lube Oil Trip** | Oil pressure < 0.8 bar | Trip unit | < 1 s |
| **Boiler Drum LL Trip** | Drum level < -100 mm | MFT | < 0.5 s |
| **ESP HV Trip** | TR/PI out of range | De-energize fields | < 0.1 s |
| **H₂ Seal Oil Trip** | Oil pressure < seal threshold | Trip generator | < 1 s |
| **Earth Fault (HT)** | Zero-sequence current > setting | CB open | < 0.1 s |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📊 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Safety Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Plant Hazard Source] --> B[Layer 1 Elimination]
    B --> C[Layer 2 Substitution]
    C --> D[Layer 3 Engineering Controls]
    D --> E[Layer 4 Administrative Controls]
    E --> F[Layer 5 PPE]
    F --> G[SAFE OPERATION]

    H[Inherent Safety Design] --> D
    I[SOP and Permits] --> E
    J[Helmets, Gloves, Suits] --> F

    K[Boiler Trip Logic] --> D
    L[Turbine Overspeed Trip] --> D
    M[Hydrogen Monitoring] --> D

    N[Safety Instrumented Function SIS] --> D
    O[Fire Detection Network] --> D
    P[Emergency Shutdown ESD] --> D
```

## 4.2 Boiler Master Fuel Trip (MFT) Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Boiler Run Command] --> Purge{Purge Complete 5 Volumes}
    Purge -- No --> Wait[Continue FD/ID Fan]
    Purge -- Yes --> Ignite[Ignition Permissive Check]
    Ignite --> Fuel{All Conditions OK}
    Fuel -- Yes --> Light[Light Off Burner]
    Fuel -- No --> TripA[MFT Lockout]

    Light --> Monitor{Flame Stable}
    Monitor -- Yes --> Run[Normal Operation]
    Monitor -- No --> TripB[MFT in 1 to 3 s]

    Run --> Drum{Drum Level Low-Low}
    Drum -- Yes --> TripC[MFT]
    Drum -- No --> Run

    Run --> Air{FD/ID Fan Failure}
    Air -- Yes --> TripD[MFT]
    Air -- No --> Run
```

## 4.3 Burner Management System (BMS) Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Standby] --> B[Permissive Check]
    B --> C[Pre-Purge 110 s]
    C --> D[Igniter On]
    D --> E[Main Fuel Valve Open]
    E --> F[Flame Detected within 1 to 3 s]
    F -- Yes --> G[Boiler on Load]
    F -- No --> H[Post-Purge 60 s then Lockout]
    G --> I[Continuous Monitoring]
    I --> J{MFT Condition}
    J -- Yes --> H
    J -- No --> I
```

## 4.4 Turbine Protection Layered Trips

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Turbine Running 3000 rpm] --> B{Vibration > 12 mm per s}
    B -- Yes --> Trip1[Vibration Trip]

    A --> C{Speed > 3300 rpm}
    C -- Yes --> Trip2[Mechanical Overspeed Trip]

    A --> D{Speed > 3450 rpm}
    D -- Yes --> Trip3[Hydraulic Emergency Trip]

    A --> E{Vacuum < 600 mmHg}
    E -- Yes --> Trip4[Vacuum Trip]

    A --> F{Oil Pressure < 0.8 bar}
    F -- Yes --> Trip5[Lube Oil Trip]

    A --> G{DC Lube Oil Pump Fail}
    G -- Yes --> Trip6[Bearing Damage Risk]

    A --> H{Thrust Bearing Wear > 0.5 mm}
    H -- Yes --> Trip7[Thrust Bearing Trip]
```

## 4.5 Fire Detection and Suppression Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Hazard Source] --> B[Smoke Detector]
    A --> C[Heat Detector]
    A --> D[IR Flame Detector]
    A --> E[Linear Heat Sensing Cable]

    B --> F[Fire Alarm Panel FAP]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F --> G{Audio Visual Alarm}
    G --> H[Occupant Notification]

    F --> I{Sprinkler or Water Spray}
    I --> J[Cooling and Suppression]

    F --> K{Fire Damper Close}
    K --> L[Containment of Smoke]

    F --> M{Fire Service Call}
    M --> N[External Response]
```

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **Master Fuel Trip (MFT)**. List any four initiating conditions for an MFT in a pulverized coal-fired boiler.

**Model Answer (≈ 120 words):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MFT Definition**: A Master Fuel Trip is the **highest-level safety interlock** in a boiler that simultaneously closes all fuel-admission valves (oil gun shut-off valves, pulverizer cut-off gates, gas solenoid valves) and trips the FD/ID fans within **≤ 1 second** for gaseous fuels and **≤ 3 seconds** for liquid/solid fuels, in order to prevent furnace explosion.

**Four Initiating Conditions:**
1. Loss of flame detected by all flame scanners for > 1–3 s.
2. Drum water level low-low (< −100 mm).
3. FD or ID fan total failure (air flow < 25%).
4. Loss of all fuel (coal, oil, gas) simultaneously.
5. **Bonus**: Loss of any two of three soot blowers during start-up, or turbine trip without boiler trip (for combined cycle).

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for the four conditions)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
What is **Lock-Out / Tag-Out (LOTO)**? Why is it a critical safety procedure before maintenance?

**Model Answer:**

> **LOTO** is a formal safety procedure in which:
> - **Lock-Out**: A physical lock is applied to an energy-isolating device (valve, breaker, disconnect) so that the equipment **cannot be re-energized**.
> - **Tag-Out**: A durable tag is affixed indicating **"Do Not Operate"**, with the name of the authorized person and date.

**Why critical?**
- Prevents **unexpected start-up** of machinery that could injure a worker inside.
- Required under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147** (international) and Indian Factory Rules.
- Common accident scenario avoided: A worker inside a coal mill is crushed when another operator starts the mill remotely — **LOTO prevents this**.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark procedure + 1 mark importance)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Burner Management System (BMS)** for an oil-fired boiler. Discuss the sequence of operations from pre-purge to light-off. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 500 MW thermal power plant boiler has a furnace volume of **1200 m³** and an FD fan capacity of **60 m³/s** during purge. Calculate:
1. The minimum purge volume required (5 volume changes).
2. The minimum purge time.
3. The minimum time after adding a **20% safety margin**.
4. What happens if FD fan flow drops below 25% during purge? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BMS Sequence (7 marks):**

The **Burner Management System (BMS)** is a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) that ensures safe light-off, stable combustion, and safe shut-down of burners.

**Functional Sequence (with timing):**

1. **Standby Condition** – All dampers closed, fuel valves isolated, fans off.
2. **Pre-Start Permissive Check** – FD/ID fan ON, oil temperature ≥ 90 °C, atomizing steam pressure ≥ 7 bar, ignition transformer healthy, scanner self-test OK.
3. **Pre-Purge** – FD & ID fans run for the calculated purge time. **5 volume changes** of the furnace must be achieved. Purge air flow must stay between 25%–100% of full load.
4. **Trial for Ignition** – Ignition spark plug arcs; pilot flame established within **10 s**.
5. **Main Fuel Admission** – Main oil valve opens only if pilot flame is proven for **5 s** continuously.
6. **Flame Stabilization** – Flame scanners (UV + IR) continuously monitor; if flame fails for **> 1 s** (gas) or **> 3 s** (oil) → **MFT**.
7. **Post-Purge** – After shutdown, FD/ID fans run for **≥ 60 s** to clear any unburnt fuel.

**Neat Sketch** (to be drawn by student): Block diagram showing the BMS loop with: **Permissive → Purge → Ignite → Pilot → Main Valve → Flame Monitor → Trip Logic**.

*(Full marks distributed: 2 marks for definition, 3 marks for sequence with timing, 1 mark for scanner logic, 1 mark for sketch)*

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Furnace volume: $V_f = 1200 \text{ m}^3$
- FD fan flow rate: $Q_{FD} = 60 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Required volume changes: $N = 5$
- Safety margin: **20%**

**Step 1 — Minimum purge volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{purge} &= N \times V_f \\
&= 5 \times 1200 \\
&= 6000 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Minimum purge time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{purge} &= \dfrac{V_{purge}}{Q_{FD}} \\
&= \dfrac{6000}{60} \\
&= 100 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — With 20% safety margin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{actual} &= t_{purge} \times (1 + 0.20) \\
&= 100 \times 1.20 \\
&= 120 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Effect of FD fan flow drop below 25%:**

Minimum acceptable purge flow = 0.25 × 60 = 15 m³/s.

If actual flow falls below 15 m³/s during purge:

- The BMS **resets the purge timer** (purge counter does not advance).
- A second fan must be started, or purge must be restarted from zero after restoring flow.
- If flow remains low for > **30 s**, BMS generates an **alarm** and aborts light-off.
- Under no circumstances will fuel be admitted with purge incomplete — this prevents a **furnace explosion** during light-off.

*(1 mark for part 1, 1 mark for part 2, 2 marks for part 3, 3 marks for part 4 with explanation)*

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe the **safety measures adopted in the coal handling plant** of a thermal power station. Include provisions for fire, dust, and explosion protection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a diagram, explain the **H₂ cooling system of a turbo-generator**, including purity requirements, purge sequence, and alarm/trip logic. Why is CO₂ used instead of N₂ as the inert divider? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Coal Handling Plant Safety (7 marks):**

The Coal Handling Plant (CHP) receives, stores, crushes, and conveys coal to the bunkers. Hazards include **coal dust explosion, fire from spontaneous combustion, mechanical injury from conveyors, and ergonomic stress**.

**Safety Provisions:**

1. **Dust Control**
   - Water spray dust suppression at transfer points.
   - Dry fog (micronized water) systems for fine dust.
   - Enclosed conveyor galleries with negative pressure ventilation.
   - Cyclone dust collectors and bag-house filters.

2. **Fire Protection**
   - Linear Heat Sensing (LHS) cables along conveyors and bunkers (alarms at 68 °C, trip at 88 °C).
   - CO monitors inside bunkers (alarm at 50 ppm, trip at 200 ppm).
   - Foam / water spray deluge systems for conveyors and crushers.
   - Hot coal detection by IR thermography at wagon tipplers.

3. **Explosion Protection**
   - NFPA 654 / IS 1446 compliant venting panels on pulverizers and cyclones.
   - Inert gas (N₂) blanketing for coal silos during long storage.
   - Explosion isolation flap valves between connected equipment.

4. **Mechanical Safety**
   - Pull-cord switches (both sides of conveyor, full length).
   - Belt sway switches and zero-speed switches.
   - Magnetic separators to remove tramp metal before crushers.
   - Rotary drum feeders with interlock to crusher motor.

5. **PPE & Administrative Controls**
   - PPE: dust masks (FFP2), ear plugs, safety shoes, helmets with chin strap.
   - Permit-to-Work system for confined space entry into bunkers/silos.
   - Tripper car area guarded with barriers.

*(Distribution: 2 marks each for the 5 categories, with neat diagram in the answer)*

---

**(b) Hydrogen Cooling System (7 marks):**

Modern large turbo-generators (≥ 100 MW) use **hydrogen as a cooling gas** because:
- H₂ has the **highest thermal conductivity** among gases (~7× air).
- Low density reduces windage losses.

**H₂ Purity Requirement:**
- $y_{H_2} \geq 98\%$ (NFPA 68 / IEC 60034-2-1).
- $y_{O_2} \leq 1\%$ (alarm), $\leq 2\%$ (trip).
- $y_{H_2O \ (dewpoint)} \leq -20$ °C.

**Purge Sequence:**

| Stage | Direction | Gas | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial | Air | Plant first commissioned |
| 2 | Displace air → N₂ | **N₂** | Inert, removes O₂ |
| 3 | Displace N₂ → CO₂ | **CO₂** | Denser than both N₂ and H₂, forms barrier |
| 4 | Displace CO₂ → H₂ | **H₂** | Cooling gas in service |

**Shutdown Reverse:** H₂ → CO₂ → Air.

**Alarm/Trip Logic:**

- $y_{H_2}$ drops below 95% → Alarm.
- $y_{H_2}$ drops below 90% → Trip generator, emergency shutdown.
- $y_{O_2}$ rises above 1% → Alarm; above 2% → Trip.
- H₂ pressure in stator housing deviates from set-point by > ±0.05 bar → Alarm.

**Why CO₂ and not N₂ as the divider?**
- CO₂ is **denser than both N₂ and H₂** (M = 44 vs. 28 vs. 2).
- Density gradient ensures CO₂ settles to form a **stable barrier layer**, preventing turbulent mixing between H₂ and air during the purge.
- N₂ (M = 28) is **lighter than air** (M_avg ≈ 29) and would mix, defeating the purpose.
- CO₂ also has better **fire-extinguishing** properties as a bonus.

*(Distribution: 2 marks for H₂ purity + reason, 2 marks for purge sequence table, 1.5 marks for trip logic, 1.5 marks for CO₂ vs N₂ reasoning)*

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MFT and OST**: MFT is a **boiler** trip (cuts fuel); OST is a **turbine** trip (closes ESVs). Examiners deduct 1 mark if you swap these.
> 2. **Wrong purge volume**: Some students write **3 volume changes** (older IBR) instead of the current **5 volume changes** per NFPA 85 / CEA regulation. Use **5**.
> 3. **Forgetting to add safety margin** to purge time. Always multiply the calculated purge time by **1.10 to 1.20**.
> 4. **Confusing trip speeds**: 110% (mechanical) and 115% (hydraulic). Mixing them up = −1 mark.
> 5. **H₂ LEL**: Saying H₂ is explosive in air at "any concentration" — wrong. LEL is **4%**, and the mixture is safe above **75% H₂** (rich mixture, no oxygen).
> 6. **Missing sketch**: In Part B 14-mark questions, the **neat sketch is compulsory**. Without it, expect **1–2 mark deduction**.
> 7. **Touch potential formula** — many students forget the surface-layer derating factor $C_s$ and use the bare IEEE 80 formula.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Quick-Fire Points for KTU Exam:**
>
> ✅ **MFT** cuts all fuel; **OST** closes ESVs & TBVs at **110%** rated speed.
>
> ✅ **5 volume changes** are required for boiler pre-purge; add **20%** safety margin to time.
>
> ✅ **Flame failure** → MFT in **≤ 1 s** for gas, **≤ 3 s** for oil.
>
> ✅ **Hydrogen purity** ≥ **98%** in generator; LEL of H₂ = **4%** in air.
>
> ✅ Purge sequence: **Air → N₂ → CO₂ → H₂** (charging); reverse for shutdown.
>
> ✅ CO₂ is preferred over N₂ as the **inert divider** because of higher density.
>
> ✅ **Three-element** drum level control uses drum level + steam flow + feedwater flow.
>
> ✅ **Earth resistance** for power station: ≤ **1 Ω**; for lightning: ≤ **5 Ω**.
>
> ✅ **ELCB trips at 30 mA** for human safety (IEC 60364).
>
> ✅ **Touch potential** (IEEE 80): $V_{touch} \leq 437$ V for 50 kg body, **≤ 543 V** for 70 kg.
>
> ✅ **Noise exposure limit** = **85 dBA** for 8-hour TWA.
>
> ✅ **PPE hierarchy**: Helmet → Goggles → Ear protection → Gloves → Safety shoes → Coverall.
>
> ✅ **Lock-Out/Tag-Out (LOTO)** is mandatory before any maintenance.
>
> ✅ **Burner turndown ratio** is typically 4:1 to 5:1 for stable combustion.
>
> ✅ **Pre-ignition purge** flow must remain between **25% and 100%** of full-load air.
>
> ✅ **Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR)** govern design, manufacture, and inspection.
>
> ✅ **Bunker fire** prevention: monitor **CO** (alarm 50 ppm, trip 200 ppm) and **temperature** (alarm 70 °C, trip 90 °C).
>
> ✅ **Safe approach distances** for un-insulated live parts: **1 m @ 11 kV**, **2 m @ 33 kV**, **3.7 m @ 400 kV**.
>
> ✅ **LOTO** + **Permit-to-Work** + **Toolbox Talk** are the **administrative trifecta** of power-plant safety.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effects of Nuclear Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Effects of Nuclear Radiation"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Effects of Nuclear Radiation* refers to the measurable, deterministic, and stochastic consequences produced when ionizing radiation (alpha particles, beta particles, gamma rays, neutrons, X-rays, and fission fragments) interacts with matter — specifically with biological tissue, structural materials of the reactor, coolants, and the surrounding environment of a nuclear power plant.

When an atom undergoes radioactive decay or a fission event, it releases **ionizing radiation** — radiation carrying enough energy (typically $> 10\text{ eV}$) to strip electrons from atoms, thereby creating ions. This ionization is the fundamental trigger of every biological, chemical, and physical effect we observe in a nuclear power station.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are throwing tiny, high-energy billiard balls at a stack of glass plates:

- The **alpha particle** is like a heavy bowling ball — it smashes the first plate and stops (high damage, low penetration).
- The **beta particle** is like a tennis ball — it punches through a few plates (moderate damage, moderate penetration).
- The **gamma ray** is like a flash of X-ray light — it sails through the entire stack but leaves a faint ionization trail (low damage, very high penetration).
- The **neutron** is the most dangerous — it has no electric charge, so it slips past electronic defenses and collides directly with atomic nuclei, knocking atoms out of their lattice positions (like a cue ball that doesn't see any obstacles).

The **effects** are simply the broken plates, the displaced atoms, and the chemical/molecular destruction left behind.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> This topic sits at the intersection of *Reactor Physics* (Module 1–2), *Health Physics*, and *Plant Safety Systems*. It directly informs the design of **biological shielding**, **containment structures**, and **radiation dose limits** prescribed by the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB), India** and the **International Commission on Radiological Protection (ICRP)**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Nuclear Radiation Effects

The effects are broadly categorized into three engineering-relevant domains:

| Domain | Sub-Category | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Biological Effects** | Somatic (to the individual) | Personnel dosimetry, occupational health |
| | Genetic (to future generations) | Long-term population safety |
| **Material Effects** | Radiation Embrittlement, Swelling, Creep | Reactor pressure vessel lifetime |
| **Environmental Effects** | Contamination, Radioactive Waste | Spent fuel storage, decommissioning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Fundamental Biological Effect Categories (ICRP Framework):**
> 1. **Deterministic Effects** — Severity increases with dose; there is a threshold. Examples: radiation burns, cataracts, sterility, acute radiation syndrome (ARS). Threshold typically $> 0.1\text{ Sv}$.
> 2. **Stochastic Effects** — Probability increases with dose; no threshold is assumed. Examples: cancer induction, genetic mutations. Modeled by the **Linear No-Threshold (LNT)** hypothesis.

---

## 1.4 Key Units & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these constants — they appear in nearly every KTU question:**

| Quantity | SI Unit | Old Unit | Conversion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Absorbed Dose | **Gray (Gy)** $\equiv 1\text{ J/kg}$ | rad | $1\text{ Gy} = 100\text{ rad}$ |
| Equivalent Dose | **Sievert (Sv)** | rem | $1\text{ Sv} = 100\text{ rem}$ |
| Exposure | Coulomb/kg (C/kg) | Roentgen (R) | $1\text{ R} = 2.58 \times 10^{-4}\text{ C/kg}$ |
| Activity | **Becquerel (Bq)** $\equiv 1\text{ dps}$ | Curie (Ci) | $1\text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}\text{ Bq}$ |

- **Energy of ionizing radiation:** typically between **$10\text{ eV}$ and $10\text{ MeV}$**.
- **Quality Factor (Q)** / **Radiation Weighting Factor ($W_R$)**:
  - X-rays, $\gamma$-rays, $\beta$: $W_R = 1$
  - Thermal neutrons: $W_R = 5$
  - Fast neutrons, protons: $W_R = 20$
  - Alpha particles, heavy ions: $W_R = 20$

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Radiation Interactions

When radiation strikes matter, three primary interaction mechanisms dominate in a nuclear reactor environment:

### 2.1.1 Photoelectric Effect (dominant for low-energy $\gamma$)
- The photon transfers **all** its energy to a tightly bound inner-shell electron.
- The electron is ejected with kinetic energy $E_e = h\nu - \phi$, where $\phi$ is the work function.
- Most probable at $h\nu < 0.1\text{ MeV}$ and in high-$Z$ materials (e.g., lead shielding).

### 2.1.2 Compton Scattering (dominant for medium-energy $\gamma$)
- The photon scatters off a loosely bound (essentially free) outer-shell electron.
- Both the scattered photon and recoil electron carry energy away.
- Dominant in the energy range $0.1\text{ MeV} < h\nu < 10\text{ MeV}$ — exactly the range typical of fission-product $\gamma$ rays.

### 2.1.3 Pair Production (dominant for high-energy $\gamma$)
- Occurs when $h\nu \geq 1.022\text{ MeV}$ (twice the electron rest mass energy).
- The photon converts into an **electron–positron pair** in the Coulomb field of a nucleus.
- The positron subsequently annihilates with another electron, producing two $0.511\text{ MeV}$ $\gamma$ photons.

### 2.1.4 Neutron Interactions (the most reactor-specific)
- **Elastic Scattering $(n, n)$:** Most important for **fast neutrons**; the neutron bounces off a nucleus, transferring kinetic energy. In a **moderator** (water, graphite), this slows neutrons down efficiently (H has nearly equal mass to a neutron).
- **Inelastic Scattering $(n, n')$:** Fast neutron excites the nucleus; the nucleus de-excites by emitting a $\gamma$-ray. Important in **shielding**.
- **Radiative Capture $(n, \gamma)$:** The neutron is absorbed; the compound nucleus emits a $\gamma$. Example: $\,^{1}\text{H} + n \rightarrow {}^{2}\text{H} + \gamma$.
- **Fission $(n, f)$:** The neutron causes a heavy nucleus (e.g., $^{235}\text{U}$) to split. This is the heart of the reactor.
- **Activation $(n, p)$ / $(n, \alpha)$:** Converts stable nuclei into radioactive ones. Example: $^{16}\text{O}(n,p)^{16}\text{N}$ in water coolant — produces $6.13\text{ MeV}$ $\gamma$ radiation.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Examinations:**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \frac{E}{m}$ | Absorbed dose = Energy deposited per unit mass | $\text{J/kg} = \text{Gy}$ |
| 2 | $H_T = \sum_R W_R \cdot D_{T,R}$ | Equivalent dose in tissue $T$ | Sv |
| 3 | $E_T = \sum_T W_T \cdot H_T$ | Effective dose (whole-body weighted) | Sv |
| 4 | $\dot{X} = \frac{\Gamma \cdot A}{d^2}$ | Exposure rate from point source | $\text{C/kg·h}$ |
| 5 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | Gamma attenuation (Lambert–Beer Law) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $x_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | Half-value layer (HVL) | m or cm |
| 7 | $x_{TVL} = \frac{\ln 10}{\mu}$ | Tenth-value layer (TVL) | m or cm |
| 8 | $N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$ | Radioactive decay law | atoms |
| 9 | $T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$ | Half-life | s |
| 10 | $\dot{D} = \frac{A \cdot E_\gamma \cdot \mu_{en}}{\rho}$ | Dose rate from a $\gamma$ emitter | $\text{Gy/h}$ |
| 11 | $E_k = \frac{4 A}{(1+A)^2} E_n$ | Max KE transfer (elastic, head-on) | eV or MeV |
| 12 | $S = \dot{H} \cdot t$ | Total equivalent dose received | Sv |
| 13 | $Q = W_R \cdot D$ | Dose equivalent (old form) | Sv |
| 14 | $\sigma_t = \sigma_a + \sigma_s$ | Total cross-section | barns ($10^{-24}\text{ cm}^2$) |

> **Notation:**
> - $\Gamma$ = specific gamma-ray constant ($\text{R·cm}^2/\text{mCi·h}$ or $\text{C·m}^2/\text{kg·Bq·s}$)
> - $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient ($\text{m}^{-1}$)
> - $\lambda$ = decay constant ($\text{s}^{-1}$)
> - $W_R$ = radiation weighting factor (dimensionless)
> - $W_T$ = tissue weighting factor (dimensionless)
> - $\mu_{en}/\rho$ = mass energy-absorption coefficient ($\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$)

---

## 2.3 Why Each Formula Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Lambert–Beer Law** is the foundation of **shield design** for the biological shield of a PWR/BWR — a KTU favorite.
- **Decay Law** governs spent-fuel cooling time calculations and radioactive waste management strategy.
- **Dose rate equation** determines exclusion-zone radius during fuel handling and accident response.
- **Max KE transfer formula** is essential for choosing moderators — the closer $A$ (atomic mass of target) is to 1, the more energy the neutron loses per collision. Hydrogen is unbeatable on this metric.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Biological Shield Design (Concrete / Lead / Water):** Designers use $\mu$ to compute required thickness.
2. **Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) Embrittlement Monitoring:** Neutron fluence $> 10^{19}\text{ n/cm}^2$ shifts the **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** upward.
3. **Coolant Chemistry Control:** Activation products like $^{16}\text{N}$, $^{24}\text{Na}$, $^{41}\text{Ar}$ dictate primary loop chemistry specifications.
4. **ALARA Principle (As Low As Reasonably Achievable):** Every dose limit in nuclear engineering stems from these equations.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Radioactive Decay Law

### Step 1: State the physical assumption
The rate of disintegration of a radioactive sample is **proportional to the number of unstable nuclei present**, $N(t)$.

### Step 2: Write the differential equation

$$
-\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda \, N(t)
$$

The minus sign reflects the fact that $N$ *decreases* with time. $\lambda$ is the **decay constant** (units: $\text{s}^{-1}$).

### Step 3: Separate variables

$$
\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt
$$

### Step 4: Integrate both sides

$$
\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

### Step 5: Evaluate

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{N(t)}{N_0}\right) = -\lambda \, t
$$

### Step 6: Exponentiate to obtain the decay law

$$
N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}
$$

### Step 7: Activity form
Activity $A(t) = \lambda N(t)$, hence:

$$
A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}
$$

### Step 8: Half-life relation

Set $N(t) = N_0 / 2$:

$$
\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gamma-Ray Attenuation (Lambert–Beer Law)

### Step 1: Define the geometry
Consider a collimated, monoenergetic $\gamma$ beam of intensity $I_0$ striking a slab of thickness $x$ (material: e.g., lead, concrete).

### Step 2: State the differential attenuation
The fractional decrease in intensity over a thin layer $dx$ is proportional to the intensity itself and to $dx$:

$$
-dI = \mu \, I \, dx
$$

where $\mu$ (units: $\text{m}^{-1}$) is the **linear attenuation coefficient**, a material property that depends on $\gamma$-ray energy and the material's density and atomic number.

### Step 3: Separate variables

$$
\frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx
$$

### Step 4: Integrate

$$
\int_{I_0}^{I(x)} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx
$$

### Step 5: Result

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{I(x)}{I_0}\right) = -\mu x \quad\Longrightarrow\quad I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}
$$

### Step 6: Half-Value Layer (HVL)
Setting $I(x) = I_0 / 2$:

$$
x_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}
$$

### Step 7: Tenth-Value Layer (TVL)

$$
x_{TVL} = \frac{\ln 10}{\mu} = \frac{2.303}{\mu}
$$

**Engineering insight:** If the dose rate outside a $^{60}\text{Co}$ storage pit must be reduced by a factor of $10^6$, then $n = \log_{10}(10^6) = 6$ TVLs of shielding are required.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Kinetic-Energy Transfer in Elastic Neutron Scattering

### Step 1: Set up conservation laws (1D head-on collision in lab frame)
Let a neutron of mass $m_n = 1\text{ u}$ and initial kinetic energy $E_n$ collide head-on with a stationary target nucleus of mass $A \cdot m_n$.

Conservation of momentum:
$$
m_n v_n = m_n v_n' + A m_n v_A'
$$

Conservation of kinetic energy:
$$
\tfrac{1}{2} m_n v_n^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} m_n v_n'^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} A m_n v_A'^2
$$

### Step 2: Solve the linear system
Eliminating $v_n'$ yields the velocity of the recoiling target:

$$
v_A' = \frac{2 m_n}{m_n + A m_n} \, v_n = \frac{2}{1+A} \, v_n
$$

### Step 3: Compute kinetic energy of the recoiling nucleus

$$
E_A' = \tfrac{1}{2} (A m_n) v_A'^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} (A m_n) \left(\frac{2}{1+A}\right)^2 v_n^2
$$

### Step 4: Simplify using $E_n = \tfrac{1}{2} m_n v_n^2$

$$
E_A' = \frac{4A}{(1+A)^2} \, E_n
$$

This is the **maximum** energy transfer — it occurs for a head-on, perfectly elastic collision. For $A=1$ (hydrogen), $E_A' = E_n$ (100% transfer). For $A=12$ (carbon), $E_A' \approx 0.28 \, E_n$. For $A=238$ (uranium), $E_A' \approx 0.017 \, E_n$.

**Engineering implication:** This is *why hydrogenous moderators* (water) are so effective — they decelerate neutrons to thermal energies in just a few collisions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equivalent Dose and Effective Dose

### Step 1: Absorbed dose

$$
D_{T,R} = \frac{E_{T,R}}{m_T}
$$

where $E_{T,R}$ is the energy deposited in tissue $T$ by radiation $R$ of mass $m_T$. Units: Gy.

### Step 2: Equivalent dose (different radiation types cause different biological harm per Gy)

$$
H_T = \sum_R W_R \cdot D_{T,R}
$$

Units: Sv. $W_R$ encodes the *biological effectiveness* of the radiation.

### Step 3: Effective dose (different organs have different radiosensitivities)

$$
E_T = \sum_T W_T \cdot H_T
$$

where $\sum_T W_T = 1$. Units: Sv. This single number quantifies the *whole-body* stochastic risk.

> [!NOTE]
> Example: A dose of $0.01\text{ Gy}$ from $\alpha$ particles to the lung:
> $H = 0.01 \times 20 = 0.2\text{ Sv}$ (lung).
> If $W_T(\text{lung}) = 0.12$, the contribution to effective dose = $0.12 \times 0.2 = 0.024\text{ Sv} = 24\text{ mSv}$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Material vs. Biological Effects of Radiation

| Effect Category | Mechanism | Engineering Manifestation | Mitigating Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Radiation Embrittlement** | Neutron displacement of Fe atoms in RPV steel | DBTT shift upward; risk of brittle fracture | Low-copper steel; surveillance programs |
| **Radiation-Induced Swelling** | Helium production from $(n, \alpha)$ in stainless steel cladding | Volume increase, fuel pin distortion | Doped alloys, lower flux zones |
| **Irradiation Creep** | Neutron-enhanced vacancy migration | RPV/duct dimensional change | Design margin, periodic inspection |
| **Radiolysis of Water** | $\gamma$ and fast neutrons split H₂O into H₂, O₂, H₂O₂ | Corrosion, explosion hazard in primary loop | Hydrogen recombination, nitrogen overpressure |
| **Activation Products** | $(n, p)$ / $(n, \gamma)$ on coolant and impurities | $^{16}$N, $^{24}$Na, $^{41}$Ar, $^{60}$Co contamination | High-purity coolant, material selection |
| **Acute Somatic Damage** | High-dose acute exposure | Nausea, burns, ARS (deterministic) | Dose limits, ALARA, PPE |
| **Stochastic Damage** | Low-dose chronic exposure | Cancer, leukemia, genetic mutation | Dose limits, shielding, monitoring |
| **Genetic Effects** | Mutations in germ cells | Heritable defects in offspring | Population dose limits |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example (KTU-style calculation)

**Problem:** A $^{60}\text{Co}$ source has activity $A = 3.7 \times 10^{10}\text{ Bq}$ (= 1 Ci). Compute the dose rate at $d = 1\text{ m}$ in air. Use $\Gamma = 8.67 \times 10^{-14}\text{ C·m}^2/\text{kg}$ for $^{60}\text{Co}$.

$$
\dot{X} = \frac{\Gamma \cdot A}{d^2} = \frac{(8.67 \times 10^{-14})(3.7 \times 10^{10})}{(1)^2}
$$

$$
\dot{X} = 3.21 \times 10^{-3}\text{ C/kg·s} \approx 12.4\text{ R/h}
$$

Convert to absorbed dose in air ($W/e = 33.97\text{ J/C}$):

$$
\dot{D}_{air} = \dot{X} \cdot \frac{W}{e} = 3.21 \times 10^{-3} \times 33.97 \approx 0.109\text{ Gy/h}
$$

For tissue ($W_R = 1$): $\dot{H} \approx 0.109\text{ Sv/h}$.
