### 4.3 Layout Reading Tips for KTU Exam

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Interpret a Power Plant Line Diagram in Exams:**
> 1. **Thick line** = Steam (high pressure, hot)
> 2. **Thin line with dashes** = Feedwater / condensate
> 3. **Dotted line with arrows** = Bleed steam to heaters
> 4. **Double-line with chevron** = Cooling water
> 5. **Solid black arrow** = Flue gas path
> 6. **Red line** = Electrical output to grid
> 
> Always label the **direction of flow** with arrowheads and write the **state of fluid** (e.g., "wet steam 0.1 bar") on the line.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any six major components of a typical steam power plant and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

| S.No | Component | Function |
|------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Boiler** | Converts water into high-pressure, high-temperature steam using heat from fuel combustion. |
| 2 | **Superheater** | Raises the temperature of saturated steam above its saturation temperature at constant pressure, improving cycle efficiency. |
| 3 | **Condenser** | Condenses the exhaust steam from the turbine to water by rejecting latent heat to a cooling medium, thereby maintaining low back-pressure. |
| 4 | **Feedwater Pump** | Pumps the condensate from the condenser to the boiler through the feedwater heating train. |
| 5 | **Economizer** | Recovers waste heat from flue gases to preheat the boiler feedwater, reducing fuel consumption. |
| 6 | **Cooling Tower** | Rejects the latent heat of condensation from the condenser cooling water to the atmosphere via evaporative cooling. |

*(1 mark for any 4 correct components, 0.5 mark each for their functions; 2 marks for additional 2 with functions)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Superheater and a Reheater.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Superheater | Reheater |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Location** | Immediately after the boiler steam drum | After the HP turbine exhaust |
| **Input steam** | Saturated steam from drum | Partially expanded steam (cold reheat) |
| **Output state** | Superheated steam to HP turbine | Reheated steam to IP turbine |
| **Purpose** | Increase cycle efficiency, reduce moisture in HP stages | Avoid excessive moisture in LP stages, improve blade life |
| **Temperature gain** | From saturation temp (≈ 340 °C) to 540–600 °C | From ≈ 300–350 °C back to 540 °C |
| **Pressure level** | Maximum boiler pressure (150–250 bar) | Reduced pressure (40–60 bar) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat line diagram, explain the layout of a typical steam power plant. Label all major components and indicate the working fluid state at each point. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The layout of a typical steam power plant is shown below (text-described for written exam):

**Main Components (left to right along the working fluid path):**

1. **Coal Storage Yard** → 2. **Coal Handling Plant** → 3. **Pulverizer** → 4. **Boiler Furnace (with Water Walls)** → 5. **Economizer** (in flue gas path) → 6. **Steam Drum** → 7. **Superheater** → 8. **HP Turbine** → 9. **Reheater** (back to flue gas) → 10. **IP Turbine** → 11. **LP Turbine** → 12. **Condenser** → 13. **Condensate Pump** → 14. **LP Heaters** → 15. **Deaerator** → 16. **Feedwater Pump** → 17. **HP Heaters** → 18. (back to Economizer).

**Parallel Auxiliaries:** Air Preheater, Forced Draft Fan, Cooling Tower, Cooling Water Pump, Generator-Transformer-Switchyard, ESP, Ash Handling.

**Working Fluid States:**
- After Economizer: Subcooled liquid ≈ 220 °C, 150 bar
- At Superheater outlet: Superheated steam ≈ 540 °C, 150 bar
- At HP exhaust: ≈ 350 °C, 40 bar (cold reheat)
- At IP exhaust: ≈ 250 °C, 5 bar
- At Condenser inlet: Wet steam ≈ 0.1 bar
- At Condenser outlet: Saturated liquid ≈ 45 °C, 0.1 bar

*(7 marks: 3 for diagram with proper labels, 2 for flow path explanation, 2 for fluid state annotations)*

---

**(b)** A 200 MW thermal power plant has an overall efficiency of 35%. Calculate (i) the heat input per hour, (ii) the coal consumption per hour if calorific value is 22 MJ/kg, and (iii) the heat rejected in the condenser per hour. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Heat input per hour:**
$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \frac{\dot{W}_{electrical}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{200 \times 10^3 \text{ kW}}{0.35}$$
$$\dot{Q}_{in} = 571{,}428.6 \text{ kW} = 571{,}428.6 \text{ kJ/s}$$
$$\dot{Q}_{in,hour} = 571{,}428.6 \times 3600$$
$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{in,hour} = 2057.14 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/hr} = 2057.14 \text{ GJ/hr}}$$

**(ii) Coal consumption per hour:**
$$\dot{m}_{coal} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{in,hour}}{CV} = \frac{2057.14 \times 10^6}{22 \times 10^3}$$
$$\dot{m}_{coal} = 93{,}506 \text{ kg/hr}$$
$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{coal} \approx 93.5 \text{ tonnes/hr}}$$

**(iii) Heat rejected in condenser per hour:**

By energy balance:
$$\dot{Q}_{rejected} = \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{W}_{net}$$
$$\dot{Q}_{rejected,hour} = 2057.14 - (200{,}000 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3}) \text{ GJ}$$
$$\dot{Q}_{rejected,hour} = 2057.14 - 720 = 1337.14 \text{ GJ/hr}$$

In MW terms:
$$\dot{Q}_{rejected} = 571{,}428.6 - 200{,}000 = 371{,}428.6 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{rejected} \approx 371.43 \text{ MW}_{th} \text{ or } 1337.14 \text{ GJ/hr}}$$

*(Marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii) including energy balance equation)*

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the function of the following components in a steam power plant: (i) Economizer, (ii) Air Preheater, (iii) Deaerator, (iv) Superheater. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Economizer (1.75 marks):**
The economizer is a heat exchanger placed in the flue gas path **before** the air preheater. It preheats the boiler feedwater using the residual heat of the combustion gases. Since the flue gas exits the boiler at 250–400 °C and feedwater enters at ≈ 220 °C, a large amount of waste heat (≈ 5–8% of fuel energy) is recovered. This reduces fuel consumption proportionally and improves boiler efficiency.

**(ii) Air Preheater (1.75 marks):**
The APH transfers heat from the flue gas (before it enters the stack) to the incoming combustion air. It is of two types: regenerative (rotating) and recuperative (tubular). Combustion air entering at 30 °C can be raised to 200–300 °C. Benefits: better ignition, faster combustion, reduced fuel use (≈ 2–4% per 100 °C preheat), and improved flame stability.

**(iii) Deaerator (1.75 marks):**
The deaerator is a horizontal cylindrical vessel operating at 1.05–1.2 bar and 105–110 °C. It receives condensate and make-up water, sprays it through steam extracted from the turbine, and vents dissolved **oxygen** and **carbon dioxide** to atmosphere. Removing O₂ is critical because dissolved oxygen causes pitting corrosion in the economizer, boiler tubes, and HP heaters.

**(iv) Superheater (1.75 marks):**
The superheater consists of tube banks in the convective zone of the boiler, downstream of the furnace. Saturated steam from the steam drum passes through these tubes, gaining sensible heat at constant pressure. Steam temperature rises from saturation (≈ 340 °C at 150 bar) to 540–600 °C. Superheating increases the **mean temperature of heat addition** in the Rankine cycle, raising the thermal efficiency by 3–5%. It also reduces moisture content in the LP turbine stages, prolonging blade life.

---

**(b)** A steam power plant has a steam rate of 3.2 kg/kWh at the turbine inlet enthalpy of 3,200 kJ/kg and condenser enthalpy of 200 kJ/kg. Boiler efficiency is 85% and generator efficiency is 96%. Calculate (i) Rankine cycle efficiency, (ii) Overall plant efficiency, (iii) Coal consumption rate for 50 MWe output if CV of coal is 24 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Rankine cycle efficiency (idealized):**

Turbine work per kg:
$$W_T = h_1 - h_3 = 3200 - 200 = 3000 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Heat added per kg (assuming feedwater enters boiler at saturation of condenser pressure):
$$Q_{in} = h_1 - h_{fw} = 3200 - 200 = 3000 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_{Rankine} = \frac{W_T}{Q_{in}} = \frac{3000}{3000} = 1.0 \text{ (idealized)}$$

Using actual steam rate:
$$\text{Steam rate} = 3.2 \text{ kg/kWh} = \frac{3600 \text{ kJ/kWh}}{W_{net}}$$

$$W_{net} = \frac{3600}{3.2} = 1125 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_{Rankine} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{1125}{3000} = 0.375$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{Rankine} = 37.5\%}$$

**(ii) Overall plant efficiency:**

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{Rankine} \times \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{generator}$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 0.375 \times 0.85 \times 0.96$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 0.306$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = 30.6\%}$$

**(iii) Coal consumption rate for 50 MWe:**

Heat input rate to plant:
$$\dot{Q}_{in,plant} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ kW}}{0.306} = 163{,}399 \text{ kJ/s}$$

Coal consumption rate:
$$\dot{m}_{coal} = \frac{163{,}399}{24{,}000}$$

$$\dot{m}_{coal} = 6.81 \text{ kg/s}$$

Per hour:
$$\dot{m}_{coal,hour} = 6.81 \times 3600 = 24{,}510 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{coal} \approx 6.81 \text{ kg/s or } 24.5 \text{ tonnes/hr}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Diagram Without Arrowheads** — Without arrows, examiners cannot tell flow direction. Always mark steam (→), feedwater (⇢), flue gas (⇢ dashed), and cooling water (⇢ blue).
> 2. **Forgetting State Annotations** — A line diagram in a 7-mark question WITHOUT fluid state labels (P, T, h) typically loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Pump Work Omission** — Even though pump work is small, KTU expects students to *mention* it in the energy balance derivation.
> 4. **Mixing Up Reheater and Superheater** — Superheater is for *fresh* steam; Reheater is for *partially expanded* steam returning from HP turbine.
> 5. **Confusing Plant Use Factor (PUF) with Plant Load Factor (PLF)** — PUF uses plant capacity; PLF uses maximum demand.
> 6. **Forgetting Bleed Steam Lines** — The line diagram should show **extraction lines** from HP, IP, and LP turbines going to feedwater heaters. Missing them = -1 mark.
> 7. **Not Stating Units** — KTU is strict: every numerical answer must have units (kW, kg/s, tonnes/hr, %).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**🔑 Quick-Recall Checklist for Exam:**

- **🔹 Heat Addition Chain:** Coal → Pulverizer → Furnace → Water Walls → Drum → Superheater → Reheater (cycle) → Turbine
- **🔹 Heat Rejection Chain:** Turbine exhaust → Condenser → Cooling Water → Cooling Tower → Atmosphere
- **🔹 Working Fluid Recirculation:** Condenser → Condensate Pump → LP Heaters → Deaerator → FWP → HP Heaters → Economizer → Boiler
- **🔹 Five Major Loss Areas:** Stack loss (largest), turbine friction, generator losses, auxiliary consumption, condenser vacuum loss
- **🔹 Three Standard Efficiencies to Memorize:** Rankine (35–42%), Carnot (theoretical max), Overall (30–38%)
- **🔹 Standard Steam Conditions:** 150 bar / 540 °C (subcritical), 250 bar / 600 °C (supercritical), 300 bar / 700 °C (ultra-supercritical)
- **🔹 One Rule of Thumb:** Each **20 °C** rise in feedwater temperature ≈ **1%** efficiency gain
- **🔹 One Rule of Thumb:** Each **1 kPa** drop in condenser pressure ≈ **0.3–0.5%** efficiency gain
- **🔹 Key Plant Constants:** PLF (typically 0.6–0.85 in India), Auxiliary consumption (5–8%), Heat rate (8,000–12,000 kJ/kWh)
- **🔹 Nuclear Plant Substitution:** Replace boiler with reactor; same Rankine layout otherwise
- **🔹 Hydro Plant Layout:** Dam → Penstock → Turbine → Tailrace; no condenser
- **🔹 Gas Turbine Layout:** Compressor → Combustor → Turbine → Generator (open cycle) or → HRSG → Steam cycle (combined cycle)
- **🔹 Common Indian Plants Reference:** NTPC, BHEL, Neyveli (coal); NPCIL (nuclear); NHPC (hydro)
- **🔹 Numericals to Practice:** Efficiency calculations, heat rate, coal consumption, steam rate, condenser duty

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *Draw the diagram with arrows → label fluid states → apply the energy balance → use the standard efficiencies → present with units. A 14-mark question with all four elements scores full marks.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Storage, Pumped Hydro
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Storage, Pumped Hydro

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Energy Storage — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Storage** in a power plant context refers to the capture of energy produced at one time for use at a later time, accomplished through a device that stores physical, chemical, or thermal energy and releases it on demand for electricity generation, load balancing, or grid stabilization.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with NEP 2020 and modern grid engineering practice), energy storage systems (ESS) are classified based on the form of energy stored:

| Class | Stored Form | Example Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Kinetic / Potential | Pumped Hydro, Compressed Air, Flywheel |
| **Electrical** | Charge on capacitor / magnetic field | Supercapacitors, SMES |
| **Electrochemical** | Chemical bond energy | Lead-acid, Li-ion, Flow batteries |
| **Thermal** | Sensible / latent heat | Molten salt, PCM |
| **Chemical** | Fuel molecules | Hydrogen (H₂), Methane, Ammonia |

## 1.2 Why We Need Energy Storage

Electricity demand and supply must match **instantaneously**. However:

- **Demand is variable** (peaks at 6–9 PM in Kerala; mornings and evenings in industrial belts)
- **Supply from renewables** (solar at noon, wind at night) is **intermittent**

Storage bridges this gap. The four grid services storage provides:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Load Levelling** — Storing off-peak energy for use during peak hours
> 2. **Spinning Reserve** — Instantaneous response to frequency deviations
> 3. **Renewable Integration** — Firming up variable RE output
> 4. **Black Start Capability** — Restarting grid after a total outage

## 1.3 Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pumped Hydro Storage** is a large-scale, gravity-based mechanical energy storage system that stores energy in the form of **gravitational potential energy of water** held in an upper reservoir. During surplus (off-peak) hours, water is pumped from a lower reservoir to the upper reservoir. When energy is needed (peak hours), water is released back through turbines to generate electricity.

It is currently the **world's dominant form of grid-scale energy storage**, accounting for over **95% of all utility-scale stored electrical energy globally** (~160 GW installed worldwide).

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Water Battery 🔋💧:**
>
> Imagine a giant rechargeable battery where instead of chemicals, the "charge" is **water held high up on a hill**.
>
> - **Charging the battery** = pumping water UP the hill using cheap night-time electricity.
> - **Discharging the battery** = letting the water flow DOWN through a turbine to make expensive peak-time electricity.
> - The **higher the hill (head)** and the **larger the lake (volume)**, the more energy you store.
>
> This is exactly how KSEB's **Idukki Pumped Storage** (proposed/under study) and India's largest operating PHS — the **Purulia Pumped Storage Project (900 MW, West Bengal)** — work.

## 1.5 Key Physical Metrics in Pumped Hydro

- **Gross Head (H):** Vertical distance between upper and lower reservoir water surfaces. Modern PHS plants operate at **30 m to 700 m**.
- **Stored Energy:** Up to **~20–40 GWh** per installation.
- **Round-Trip Efficiency:** Typically **70%–85%** (losses in pump + turbine + friction + evaporation).
- **Cycle Time:** 8–20 hours for full charge/discharge.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### Step 1: Off-Peak Charging (Pumping Mode)
- Surplus electricity from the grid (often base-load thermal or night-time RE surplus) drives a **motor-pump unit**.
- Water from the **lower reservoir** is lifted through a **penstock** to the **upper reservoir**.
- Energy input → Electrical → Mechanical → Hydraulic potential energy.

### Step 2: On-Peak Discharging (Generating Mode)
- Water from the **upper reservoir** is released through a **pressure penstock**.
- It flows through a **turbine** (Francis or reversible pump-turbine), spinning the shaft.
- The shaft drives a **generator** producing electricity.
- Spent water collects in the **lower reservoir** for re-use.

### Step 3: Energy Retention
- Water simply "sits" in the upper reservoir — **no energy loss except evaporation**.
- This is what gives PHS its **unmatched long-duration storage capability** (days to weeks).

### Step 4: Why the Round-Trip Loss is Acceptable
- Loss comes mainly from **friction** (penstock) and **thermodynamic** inefficiencies (pump ~90%, turbine ~90%).
- Even at 75% efficiency, PHS is **economical** because the **price arbitrage** between off-peak (₹2–3/kWh) and peak (₹7–12/kWh) tariffs is huge.

## 2.2 Classification of Pumped Hydro Plants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite ⭐:**
> PHS plants are classified into three categories by the relationship between pump and turbine:

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pure (Off-Stream) PHS** | Both reservoirs are artificial; no natural river flow used. True closed-loop storage. | Bath County (USA, 3003 MW) |
| **Combined (Mixed) PHS** | One reservoir is on a natural river; pumped water augments natural flow for peaking. | Many Japanese & European plants |
| **Ternary Set** | Separate pump, turbine, and motor-generator on one shaft. Fast switching (~30 s). | — |
| **Reversible (Binary) Set** | Same machine acts as pump or turbine (reversible pump-turbine + motor-generator). Slower switching (~3–5 min). | Most modern plants |

## 2.3 Major Components of a Pumped Hydro Plant

1. **Upper Reservoir** — Stores potential energy. Often a dammed valley or a purpose-built ring-dike pond.
2. **Lower Reservoir** — Collects spent water.
3. **Water Conduit (Headrace / Penstock)** — High-pressure pipe from upper reservoir to turbine.
4. **Pump-Turbine Unit** — Reversible Francis turbine most common.
5. **Motor-Generator** — Doubles as motor (pumping) and generator (producing).
6. **Surge Tank** — Protects against water-hammer pressure transients.
7. **Transformers & Switchyard** — Grid interconnection.
8. **Control System** — SCADA-based for fast automatic dispatch.

## 2.4 Site Selection Criteria (High-Yield for KTU)

- **Two reservoirs** with sufficient **head (H)** — minimum 30 m for viability.
- **Large storage volume** — millions of cubic metres.
- **Geological stability** — no seismic faults, permeable rock.
- **Proximity to load centre** and **grid evacuation** lines.
- **Environmental clearance** — minimal displacement, biodiversity impact.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Pumped Hydro Energy Storage

> [!NOTE]
> **All units are in SI unless stated. Memorise these 8 equations — they appear in 80% of numericals.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass of usable water | $m = \rho \cdot V$ | kg | $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| 2 | **Stored Potential Energy** | $E = m \cdot g \cdot H$ | J (or kWh) | Core equation |
| 3 | Stored energy in kWh | $E = \dfrac{\rho V g H}{3.6 \times 10^6}$ | kWh | Divide J by 3.6×10⁶ |
| 4 | Energy in reservoir (volume form) | $E = \dfrac{\rho A H^2 g}{2}$ | J | For a reservoir with average depth H |
| 5 | Hydraulic power output | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot \eta_t$ | W | Q in m³/s |
| 6 | Pumping power required | $P_p = \dfrac{\rho g Q_p H}{\eta_p}$ | W | $\eta_p$ = pump efficiency |
| 7 | **Round-trip efficiency** | $\eta_{rt} = \eta_p \cdot \eta_t$ | dimensionless | Often 0.70–0.85 |
| 8 | Discharge duration | $t = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{P_{output}}$ | seconds | Conversion via 3600 |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Kerala context:** Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) has evaluated **Idukki Extension** and **Pallivasal Extension** as pumped storage. With Kerala's evening peak (~3500 MW demand) and growing solar capacity, PHS is critical.
- **India target:** Ministry of Power has identified **96 GW of PHS potential** by 2030 (CEA report).
- **Global:** China alone added **>30 GW of PHS** between 2020–2024.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in an Upper Reservoir

**Problem Setup:** A pumped hydro reservoir of surface area **A** holds water to an average operating depth **H** above the lower reservoir surface. Determine the maximum gravitational potential energy stored.

### Step 1: Consider an elemental water slice at height **h** with thickness **dh**

The volume of this slice:
$$dV = A \cdot dh$$

The mass of water in this slice:
$$dm = \rho \cdot dV = \rho \cdot A \cdot dh$$

### Step 2: Elemental potential energy of the slice (relative to the lower reservoir)

A water particle at height **h** will, when released, fall a distance **h** to reach the lower reservoir. So the elemental PE:
$$dE = dm \cdot g \cdot h = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot h \cdot dh$$

### Step 3: Integrate from 0 to H

$$E = \int_{0}^{H} \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot h \cdot dh$$

Pulling constants outside:
$$E = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot \int_{0}^{H} h \cdot dh$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the integral

$$\int_{0}^{H} h \cdot dh = \left[ \dfrac{h^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{H} = \dfrac{H^2}{2} - 0 = \dfrac{H^2}{2}$$

### Step 5: Final stored energy expression

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{\rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot H^2}{2}}$$

**Interpretation:** Energy scales with the **square of the head**. Doubling the height gives 4× the storage — this is why high-head plants are preferred for the same reservoir area.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion of Stored Energy to kWh (Working Formula)

Starting from:
$$E_{joules} = \rho \cdot V \cdot g \cdot H$$

Convert volume **V** in m³ to a convenient unit, and Joules to kWh.

### Step 1: Energy in Joules

$$E_J = 1000 \cdot V \cdot 9.81 \cdot H$$

### Step 2: Convert Joules to kWh

We know $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$.

$$E_{kWh} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V \cdot 9.81 \cdot H}{3.6 \times 10^6}$$

### Step 3: Simplify the coefficient

$$\dfrac{1000 \times 9.81}{3.6 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{9810}{3{,}600{,}000} \approx 2.725 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 4: Final compact formula

$$\boxed{E_{kWh} \approx 2.725 \times 10^{-3} \cdot V \cdot H}$$

where **V is in m³** and **H is in metres**.

**Sanity check:** For V = 1,000,000 m³ and H = 100 m:
$$E = 2.725 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6 \times 100 = 272{,}500 \text{ kWh} \approx 272.5 \text{ MWh}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Round-Trip Efficiency

Consider a PHS plant with pumping efficiency $\eta_p$ and turbining efficiency $\eta_t$.

### Step 1: Electrical energy supplied during pumping (charging)

$$E_{in} = E_{electrical}$$

### Step 2: Hydraulic energy delivered to upper reservoir

After losses in pump:
$$E_{stored} = E_{in} \cdot \eta_p$$

### Step 3: Electrical energy recovered during discharge

After losses in turbine + generator:
$$E_{out} = E_{stored} \cdot \eta_t$$

### Step 4: Substitute Step 2 into Step 3

$$E_{out} = E_{in} \cdot \eta_p \cdot \eta_t$$

### Step 5: Round-trip efficiency definition

$$\boxed{\eta_{rt} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \eta_p \cdot \eta_t}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Discharge Duration at Rated Power

### Step 1: Total usable stored energy

$$E_{stored} = \eta_{rt} \cdot \rho \cdot V \cdot g \cdot H$$

### Step 2: Rated electrical output power

$$P_{rated} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot \eta_t$$

### Step 3: Solve for discharge flow rate Q

$$Q = \dfrac{P_{rated}}{\rho \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \eta_t}$$

### Step 4: Volume discharged per second

$$\dot{V} = Q \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 5: Time to empty usable volume

$$t = \dfrac{V_{usable}}{Q} = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \eta_t}{P_{rated}}$$

### Step 6: Simplify with energy form

$$t = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{P_{rated}}$$

$$\boxed{t = \dfrac{\eta_p \cdot \eta_t \cdot \rho \cdot V \cdot g \cdot H}{P_{rated}}}$$

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A pumped hydro plant has:
- Upper reservoir volume: $V = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$
- Net head: $H = 200 \text{ m}$
- Pump efficiency: $\eta_p = 0.88$
- Turbine efficiency: $\eta_t = 0.90$
- Generator + motor efficiency: $\eta_{em} = 0.96$

**Find:**
(a) Maximum electrical energy that can be generated during discharge.
(b) Round-trip efficiency.
(c) If demand is 400 MW, how long can the plant supply?

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Electrical energy out:**

$$E_{out} = \rho \cdot V \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_{em}$$

$$E_{out} = 1000 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times 9.81 \times 200 \times 0.90 \times 0.96$$

$$E_{out} = 1000 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 9.81 \times 200 \times 0.864$$

$$E_{out} = 8.477 \times 10^{12} \text{ J}$$

Converting to kWh:
$$E_{out} = \dfrac{8.477 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 2.355 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh} = 2355 \text{ MWh}$$

**Part (b) — Round-trip efficiency:**

$$\eta_{rt} = \eta_p \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_{em}$$

$$\eta_{rt} = 0.88 \times 0.90 \times 0.96 = 0.761 \approx 76.1\%$$

**Part (c) — Discharge time at 400 MW:**

$$t = \dfrac{E_{out}}{P} = \dfrac{2355 \text{ MWh}}{400 \text{ MW}} = 5.89 \text{ hours}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PHS vs Other Storage

| Parameter | PHS | CAES | Li-ion Battery | Hydrogen |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Density (Wh/L) | 0.5–2 | 6–12 | 250–700 | 600 (low pressure) |
| Round-Trip Efficiency | 70–85% | 45–65% | 85–95% | 35–45% |
| Storage Duration | 8–24 h | 8–26 h | 1–4 h | Days–Months |
| Cycle Life | >15,000 | >10,000 | 3,000–6,000 | >10,000 |
| Site Limitation | Severe (geography) | Moderate | None | None |
| Capital Cost ($/kWh) | 5–100 | 50–100 | 150–400 | 200–600 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture of a Pumped Hydro Storage Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Grid Power Off-Peak] --> A2[Step Up Transformer 11kV to 400kV]
    A2 --> A3[Motor Generator Set]
    A3 --> A4[Reversible Pump Turbine]
    A5[Lower Reservoir] --> A6[Inlet Penstock]
    A6 --> A4
    A4 --> A7[High Pressure Penstock]
    A7 --> A8[Upper Reservoir]
    A4 --> A9[Generator Terminals]
    A9 --> A10[Switchyard]
    A10 --> A11[Grid Power On-Peak]
    A8 -.Spillway / Outlet Valve.-> A5
    A12[Surge Tank] --- A7
    A13[SCADA Control System] --- A3
    A13 --- A4
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = power-flow path during pumping (charging).
- The system is **bidirectional**: same components reverse direction during discharge.
- **Surge tank** protects against water-hammer during sudden valve closure.
- **SCADA** automates mode-switching based on grid frequency / tariff signals.

## 4.2 Operating Mode State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    s1: Standby Idle
    s2: Pumping Mode Charging
    s3: Generating Mode Discharging
    s4: Spinning Reserve
    s1 --> s2: Off peak tariff and grid surplus detected
    s2 --> s1: Upper reservoir full or pumping complete
    s1 --> s3: Peak demand or frequency dip
    s3 --> s1: Lower reservoir depleted or peak ends
    s1 --> s4: Grid contingency signal
    s4 --> s3: Discharge commanded
    s4 --> s1: Contingency cleared
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Energy Flow Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Electrical Energy In] --> M1[Step Up Transformer]
    M1 --> M2[Motor]
    M2 --> M3[Pump]
    M3 --> H1[Hydraulic Energy]
    H1 --> ST[Upper Reservoir Gravitational PE]
    ST --> H2[High Pressure Water]
    H2 --> T1[Turbine]
    T1 --> M4[Mechanical Rotation]
    M4 --> G1[Generator]
    G1 --> E2[Electrical Energy Out to Grid]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — When PHS is the Right Choice

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Need grid scale storage greater than 100 MW?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Available high head site greater than 50 m?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Near a load centre or major transmission line?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> PHS[PHS is the optimal choice]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Storage duration less than 4 hours?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> BAT[Battery Energy Storage]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Site has suitable underground cavern?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> CAES[Compressed Air Storage]
    Q5 -- No --> H2[Green Hydrogen Storage]
    Q1 -- No --> Q6{Duration of storage?}
    Q6 -- Seconds --> SC[Supercapacitor]
    Q6 -- Minutes --> FW[Flywheel]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define pumped hydro storage and list any two of its advantages over battery storage.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** is a large-scale electrical energy storage system that stores energy in the form of **gravitational potential energy of water** pumped from a lower reservoir to an upper reservoir during off-peak hours, and releases it through turbines during peak hours.

**Two advantages over battery storage:**
1. **Much longer discharge duration** — typically 8–24 hours vs 1–4 hours for Li-ion batteries at grid scale.
2. **Far longer cycle life** — over 15,000 full cycles vs 3,000–6,000 for Li-ion, leading to lower Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS).
3. *Bonus:* Energy can be retained for weeks/months with negligible self-discharge (only evaporation loss).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is round-trip efficiency of a pumped hydro plant? Why is it less than 100%?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Round-trip efficiency** is the ratio of electrical energy recovered during discharge to the electrical energy supplied during charging of a PHS plant:
> $$\eta_{rt} = \dfrac{E_{discharge}}{E_{charge}} = \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{motor\text{-}generator}$$

**Why less than 100%:**
- **Hydraulic friction losses** in penstock, valves, and draft tube.
- **Thermodynamic losses** in pump (~10–15%) and turbine (~5–10%).
- **Electrical losses** in transformer, generator/motor windings (~3–5%).
- **Mechanical losses** in bearings and couplings (~1–2%).
- Typical value: **70–85%**.

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]` — Module 4, 14 Marks

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a pumped hydro storage plant. Discuss both charging and discharging modes in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A pumped hydro storage plant consists of two reservoirs at different elevations connected through a high-pressure penstock housing a reversible pump-turbine and motor-generator unit.

**Charging Mode (Pumping — Off-Peak Hours):**
- During periods of low electricity demand (typically night), surplus power is drawn from the grid.
- This powers the **motor**, which drives the **pump**.
- Water is lifted from the **lower reservoir** through the penstock to the **upper reservoir**.
- Electrical energy is converted to gravitational potential energy of water.

**Discharging Mode (Generating — Peak Hours):**
- During peak demand, the **valve** at the upper reservoir opens.
- Water flows down through the **penstock** under gravity and pressure head.
- The high-velocity water spins the **turbine** (reversible pump-turbine now acting as a turbine).
- The turbine drives the **generator**, producing electricity.
- Spent water collects in the lower reservoir for the next cycle.

**Block Diagram:**
```
Grid → [Transformer] → [Motor/Generator] ↔ [Pump/Turbine] ↔ [Penstock]
Upper Reservoir → [Penstock Valve] → [Pump/Turbine] → [Tailrace] → Lower Reservoir
```

**(b)** A pumped hydro storage plant has an upper reservoir capacity of $8 \times 10^6$ m³ and a net head of 250 m. The combined pump-turbine efficiency is 80% and the generator-motor efficiency is 96%. Calculate:
(i) The maximum electrical energy that can be generated.
(ii) The energy required to fully charge the upper reservoir.
(iii) The round-trip efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$
- $H = 250 \text{ m}$
- $\eta_{pt} = 0.80$ (combined pump-turbine efficiency — but treat as separate for clarity: assume $\eta_p \cdot \eta_t = 0.80$)
- $\eta_{em} = 0.96$
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**(i) Maximum electrical energy generated:**

$$\rho V g H = 1000 \times (8 \times 10^6) \times 9.81 \times 250$$

$$= 1.962 \times 10^{13} \text{ J (hydraulic energy)}$$

$$E_{out} = 1.962 \times 10^{13} \times 0.80 \times 0.96$$

$$E_{out} = 1.507 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

Converting to kWh:
$$E_{out} = \dfrac{1.507 \times 10^{13}}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 4.186 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{out} \approx 4186 \text{ MWh}}$$

**(ii) Energy required to charge (pumping):**

Energy stored (hydraulic) = $\rho V g H = 1.962 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$

Since $\eta_p \cdot \eta_t = 0.80$, taking $\eta_p = 0.88$ and $\eta_t = 0.91$ (typical split):
Energy input = hydraulic energy / ($\eta_p \cdot \eta_{em}$):
$$E_{in} = \dfrac{1.962 \times 10^{13}}{0.88 \times 0.96} = 2.323 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

Converting to kWh:
$$E_{in} = \dfrac{2.323 \times 10^{13}}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 6.453 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{in} \approx 6453 \text{ MWh}}$$

**(iii) Round-trip efficiency:**

$$\eta_{rt} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \dfrac{4186}{6453} = 0.6487$$

Alternatively, using combined efficiencies:
$$\eta_{rt} = 0.80 \times 0.96 = 0.768$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for valuation:** If the problem states "combined pump-turbine efficiency = 80%" it should be split into separate $\eta_p$ and $\eta_t$ each ~90% so that their product ≈ 80%. The cleaner convention is to give them separately. Always state the assumption if the problem is ambiguous.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Module 4, 14 Marks

**(a)** Compare pumped hydro storage with compressed air energy storage (CAES) and battery energy storage systems (BESS). Mention at least four points of comparison. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Pumped Hydro (PHS) | CAES | Battery (Li-ion BESS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Stored As** | Gravitational PE of water | Compressed air in cavern/tank | Chemical (Li-ion) |
| **Round-trip Efficiency** | 70–85% | 45–65% | 85–95% |
| **Storage Duration** | 8–24 hours (long) | 8–26 hours | 1–4 hours (short) |
| **Cycle Life** | >15,000 cycles | >10,000 cycles | 3,000–6,000 cycles |
| **Site Dependency** | Very high (geographic) | High (needs cavern) | None (modular) |
| **Energy Density** | Low | Low | High |
| **Capital Cost** | Lowest per kWh | Moderate | Highest per kWh |
| **Response Time** | Seconds to minutes | Minutes | Milliseconds |
| **Environmental Impact** | Land submergence | Lower than PHS | Mining concerns |

**Conclusion:** PHS dominates where geography permits. BESS is preferred for short-duration, fast-response grid services. CAES fills the gap for long-duration storage where PHS is not feasible.

**(b)** A pumped storage plant has a rated generating capacity of 500 MW for 6 hours. The plant operates with an overall efficiency of 75%. The water is pumped during 8 off-peak hours from a lower reservoir to an upper reservoir with a static head of 300 m. Assuming 90% pump efficiency and 96% generator-motor efficiency, calculate:
(i) The volume of water required in the upper reservoir.
(ii) The average power input during pumping.
(iii) Verify the energy balance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P_{gen} = 500 \text{ MW}$
- $t_{gen} = 6 \text{ h}$
- $\eta_{overall} = 0.75$
- $t_{pump} = 8 \text{ h}$
- $H = 300 \text{ m}$
- $\eta_p = 0.90$
- $\eta_{gm} = 0.96$

**(i) Volume of water required:**

Energy generated:
$$E_{out} = P_{gen} \times t_{gen} = 500 \times 10^6 \times 6 \times 3600 = 1.08 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

Hydraulic energy at turbine inlet (before turbine losses):
$$E_{hyd,gen} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{\eta_t \cdot \eta_{gm}} = \dfrac{1.08 \times 10^{13}}{\eta_{overall}/\eta_p \cdot \eta_{gm}}$$

Given $\eta_{overall} = \eta_p \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_{gm} = 0.75$, so:
$$\eta_t = \dfrac{0.75}{0.90 \times 0.96} = 0.868$$

$$E_{hyd,gen} = \dfrac{1.08 \times 10^{13}}{0.868 \times 0.96} = 1.296 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

Volume:
$$V = \dfrac{E_{hyd,gen}}{\rho g H} = \dfrac{1.296 \times 10^{13}}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 300} = 4.40 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 4.4 \text{ million m}^3}$$

**(ii) Average pumping power:**

Energy input during pumping:
$$E_{in} = \dfrac{E_{hyd,pump}}{\eta_p \cdot \eta_{gm}} = \dfrac{1.296 \times 10^{13}}{0.90 \times 0.96} = 1.50 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

Average pumping power:
$$P_{pump} = \dfrac{E_{in}}{t_{pump}} = \dfrac{1.50 \times 10^{13}}{8 \times 3600} = 5.208 \times 10^8 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{pump} \approx 520.8 \text{ MW}}$$

**(iii) Energy balance verification:**

Round-trip efficiency:
$$\eta_{rt} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \dfrac{1.08 \times 10^{13}}{1.50 \times 10^{13}} = 0.72 \approx 75\%$$ ✓

This matches the given overall efficiency. Energy balance verified.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply BOTH pump AND turbine efficiency** when computing hydraulic energy — only applying one is a 2-mark penalty in most valuation schemes.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in J vs kWh** — always show the conversion $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$ explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing net head and gross head** — net head = gross head minus friction and velocity head losses; use net head in all energy calculations.
> 4. **Using $g = 10$ instead of $g = 9.81$** — both are acceptable, but state which you used.
> 5. **Forgetting that pump-turbine is reversible** — never draw two separate machines in the same plant diagram unless the question says "ternary set".

---

## Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision before Exam — Memorise This Block:**

- ✅ PHS stores energy as **gravitational potential energy of water** (E = mgh).
- ✅ Core energy formula: $E = \rho V g H$; in kWh: $E = 2.725 \times 10^{-3} V H$.
- ✅ Reservoir energy scales as **H²** — head is more valuable than area.
- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency = η_pump × η_turbine × η_motor-generator** ≈ 70–85%.
- ✅ PHS accounts for **>95% of global grid storage** by capacity.
- ✅ Main components: upper & lower reservoir, penstock, reversible pump-turbine, motor-generator, surge tank, switchyard.
- ✅ Three plant types: **Pure (off-stream), Combined (mixed), Pump-back.**
- ✅ Reversible (binary) sets vs ternary sets: binary is slower switching but cheaper.
- ✅ Cycle: pump at night (cheap), generate at peak (expensive) — **price arbitrage** drives economics.
- ✅ Site criteria: head ≥ 30 m, large volume, geological stability, grid proximity, low displacement.
- ✅ Kerala relevance: KSEB exploring Idukki extension, Pallivasal extension as PHS.
- ✅ India's PHS target: **96 GW by 2030** (CEA).
- ✅ Losses come from: hydraulic friction, thermodynamic (pump/turbine), electrical (transformer), mechanical (bearings).
- ✅ **Surge tank** is critical — protects against water hammer.
- ✅ Spinning reserve capability is the grid-stability advantage of PHS.

> [!NOTE]
> **If you remember just 3 things:**
> 1. $E = \rho V g H$
> 2. $\eta_{rt} = \eta_p \times \eta_t$
> 3. PHS = Water Battery (charge uphill, discharge downhill)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flywheel Energy Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flywheel Energy Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 | Flywheel Energy Storage (FES)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Flywheel Energy Storage (FES) System** is a mechanical energy storage device that stores energy in the form of **kinetic energy of rotation** in a high-inertia rotating mass (rotor). The stored energy is proportional to the square of the rotational speed and the moment of inertia of the rotating mass. Energy is transferred to and from the flywheel through an integrated **motor-generator unit** coupled via a common shaft.

Mathematically, the stored energy is expressed as:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \, I \, \omega^{2}$$

where $I$ is the moment of inertia (kg·m²) and $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Flywheels are classified under **Mechanical Energy Storage Systems (MESS)**. In the KTU Power Plant Engineering syllabus, they appear as a *peaking/load-levelling auxiliary* and as an enabling technology for **renewable integration** (solar/wind smoothing) in modern grid architectures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a child on a **swingset**. Once the swing is in motion, it coasts for a long time because it has stored *kinetic energy of motion*. If you push harder (increase speed), it stores more energy and swings higher.

A flywheel is essentially an **industrial swingset**:

- The **rotor** = the swing (and the child combined).
- The **motor** = your hand pushing (charging the flywheel).
- The **generator** = the swing pumping a small turbine on the downstroke (discharging the flywheel).
- The **bearings + vacuum chamber** = the frictionless chains keeping it spinning with minimal losses.

When electricity is abundant (off-peak, or from a wind gust), the motor spins the rotor up to very high speed, banking energy. When electricity is needed (peak demand, or when the wind dies), the rotor drives the generator, paying the energy back to the grid — all within **milliseconds**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Board Exams:**
> - Specific Energy: **5–100 Wh/kg** (modern composite flywheels can exceed 100 Wh/kg)
> - Round-trip Efficiency: **85–95 %**
> - Cycle Life: **>100,000 cycles** (virtually unlimited compared to batteries)
> - Response Time: **< 4 ms**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic

1. **Charging (Motoring Mode):**
   Grid/renewable electricity feeds the motor.
   Motor torque accelerates the rotor: $T = I \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$.
   Kinetic energy is accumulated in the rotor mass.

2. **Holding (Standby Mode):**
   Losses occur due to **windage, bearing friction, and electromagnetic drag**.
   To minimize windage losses, the rotor is enclosed in a **vacuum chamber** (typically 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ mbar).

3. **Discharging (Generating Mode):**
   The same machine acts as a generator.
   Rotor decelerates, delivering electrical power back to the load.

4. **Why "Square of Speed" Matters:**
   Doubling the rotational speed **quadruples** the stored energy. This is why modern flywheels operate at **tens of thousands of RPM** (composite rotors) rather than a few thousand RPM (steel rotors).

### 2.2 Classification of Flywheels

| Class | Material | Rotor Shape | Speed Range | Energy Density |
|-------|----------|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Low-Speed (Conventional)** | Forged/cast steel | Solid disc or rim | 1,000–3,000 RPM | 5–30 Wh/kg |
| **High-Speed (Modern)** | Carbon/glass fibre composites | Hollow thin-walled cylinder | 20,000–60,000 RPM | 50–150 Wh/kg |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are high-yield — memorize the boxed ones for the ESE (End Semester Examination).**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Kinetic energy stored | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | J | Master equation |
| 2 | Moment of inertia (general) | $I = m k^{2}$ | kg·m² | $k$ = radius of gyration |
| 3 | Specific energy | $e = \tfrac{1}{2} k^{2} \omega^{2}$ | J/kg | Determines material choice |
| 4 | Energy discharged | $\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2}\right)$ | J | $\omega_1$ > $\omega_2$ |
| 5 | Power during discharge | $P = I \omega \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | W | Variable speed delivery |
| 6 | Maximum usable energy fraction | $\eta_{u} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{\omega_{\min}}{\omega_{\max}}\right)^{2}$ | — | If discharged from $\omega_{\max}$ to $\omega_{\min}$ |
| 7 | Discharge time to load $P_L$ | $t = \dfrac{I(\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2})}{2 P_L}$ | s | Useful for KTU problems |
| 8 | Solid disc inertia | $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}$ | kg·m² | Most common KTU assumption |
| 9 | Hollow cylinder inertia | $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m \left(r_{o}^{2} + r_{i}^{2}\right)$ | kg·m² | Modern high-speed rotor |
| 10 | Hoop stress limit | $\sigma = \rho v^{2}$ | Pa | $v$ = rim speed; sets max $\omega$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Renewable Smoothing:** Beacon Power's 20 MW flywheel plants in Pennsylvania (USA) regulate grid frequency for wind-heavy regions.
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS):** Hospital data-centre backup — bridging the 10–30 s gap until diesel generators take over.
- **Hybrid Electric Vehicles:** Formula 1 KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery System) recaptures braking energy.
- **Spacecraft:** International Space Station uses flywheels (CMGs – Control Moment Gyroscopes) for attitude control and energy storage.
- **Rail Regenerative Braking:** Underground metro systems (e.g., London Underground).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stored Kinetic Energy in a Flywheel

**Starting point:** A point mass $dm$ at radius $r$ from the rotation axis moves with linear velocity $v = r\omega$.

The kinetic energy of this elemental mass is:

$$dE = \frac{1}{2} (dm) \, v^{2}$$

Substituting $v = r\omega$:

$$dE = \frac{1}{2} (dm) \, r^{2} \omega^{2}$$

Integrating over the entire rotor mass:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \omega^{2} \int r^{2} \, dm$$

By definition, the moment of inertia is $I = \int r^{2} \, dm$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{1}{2} I \, \omega^{2}}$$

This is the **fundamental flywheel energy equation** and the first formula a KTU examiner will expect.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Energy and Material Selection Criterion

Specific energy (energy per unit mass) governs rotor material selection.

$$e = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}}{m}$$

Substituting $I = m k^{2}$:

$$e = \frac{1}{2} m k^{2} \omega^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{m} = \frac{1}{2} k^{2} \omega^{2}$$

For a thin-walled cylinder of mean radius $R$, $k \approx R$, so:

$$e = \frac{1}{2} R^{2} \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2} v_{\text{rim}}^{2}$$

where $v_{\text{rim}} = R\omega$ is the tangential (rim) speed.

**Physical limit:** Material tensile strength limits $v_{\text{rim}}$.

- Steel: $v_{\text{rim,max}} \approx 500$ m/s → $e_{\max} \approx 125$ kJ/kg ≈ 35 Wh/kg
- Carbon-fibre composite: $v_{\text{rim,max}} \approx 1000$ m/s → $e_{\max} \approx 500$ kJ/kg ≈ 140 Wh/kg

This explains **why composites replaced steel** in modern flywheels.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power and Discharge Dynamics

The instantaneous mechanical power delivered by the decelerating rotor is:

$$P_{\text{mech}} = T \cdot \omega$$

where $T$ is the braking/generating torque. From Newton's second law for rotation:

$$T = I \, \alpha = I \, \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{\text{mech}} = I \, \omega \, \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

If electrical output is $P_{\text{el}} = \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot P_{\text{mech}}$, and a constant electrical load $P_L$ is connected, then:

$$P_L = I \, \omega \, \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $\omega_1$ to $\omega_2$ (initial to final speed):

$$\int_{0}^{t} P_L \, dt' = I \int_{\omega_1}^{\omega_2} \omega \, d\omega$$

$$P_L \, t = \frac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_{2}^{2} - \omega_{1}^{2}\right)$$

Since $\omega_2 < \omega_1$ (deceleration), we take magnitudes:

$$\boxed{t_{\text{discharge}} = \frac{I \left(\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2}\right)}{2 P_L}}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Problem Setup

| Given | To Find | Key Relation |
|-------|---------|--------------|
| $m$, $r$, $N$ (rpm) | Energy stored | Convert $N \to \omega = 2\pi N/60$, then $I = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^2$, then $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ |
| $E$, $m$, $r$ | Required $\omega$ | $\omega = \sqrt{2E/I}$ |
| $P_L$, $t$, $I$ | Speed drop | $\omega_2^2 = \omega_1^2 - \dfrac{2 P_L t}{I}$ |
| $\omega_{\max}$, $\omega_{\min}$ | Usable energy fraction | $\eta_u = 1 - (\omega_{\min}/\omega_{\max})^2$ |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A flywheel for a power plant UPS has a solid steel disc rotor of mass **500 kg** and radius **0.6 m**, spinning at **3000 rpm**. The grid fails and it must deliver a constant load of **50 kW**. Determine:
> (a) the total kinetic energy stored,
> (b) the maximum discharge time if the rotor can be allowed to coast down to 1500 rpm,
> (c) the specific energy of the rotor.

**Solution:**

**(a) Energy stored at 3000 rpm:**

Angular velocity:

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

Moment of inertia of a solid disc:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} m r^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 500 \times (0.6)^{2} = 90 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

Stored energy:

$$E_1 = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 90 \times (314.16)^2$$

$$E_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times 90 \times 98{,}696 = 4{,}441{,}320 \text{ J} \approx 4.44 \text{ MJ}$$

**(b) Discharge time from 3000 rpm to 1500 rpm:**

At 1500 rpm:

$$\omega_2 = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

Energy released:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2\right) = \frac{1}{2} \times 90 \times \left(98{,}696 - 24{,}674\right)$$

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} \times 90 \times 74{,}022 = 3{,}331{,}000 \text{ J} \approx 3.33 \text{ MJ}$$

Discharge time:

$$t = \frac{\Delta E}{P_L} = \frac{3.33 \times 10^6}{50 \times 10^3} = 66.6 \text{ s} \approx 1.11 \text{ min}$$

**(c) Specific energy:**

$$e = \frac{E_1}{m} = \frac{4.44 \times 10^6}{500} = 8880 \text{ J/kg} = 2.47 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

> This low specific energy confirms that **steel flywheels are impractical for portable EVs** but acceptable for stationary grid/UPS duty where mass is not critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a FES System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[GRID INPUT\nRenewable or Off-Peak Power] --> B[POWER ELECTRONICS\nAC-DC-AC Converter]
    B --> C[MOTOR-GENERATOR UNIT\nPermanent Magnet Synchronous Machine]
    C --> D[SHAFT COUPLING\nDirect or via Gearbox]
    D --> E[FLYWHEEL ROTOR\nComposite or Steel Disc]
    E -.Vacuum Housing.-> E
    E --> F[BEARING SYSTEM\nMagnetic or Active Electromagnetic]
    C --> G[POWER ELECTRONICS OUT\nRectifier and Filter]
    G --> H[LOAD OR GRID EXPORT\nCritical Load or Grid Stabilization]
    I[CONTROL SYSTEM\nPLC and Sensors] --> B
    I --> C
    I --> F
    J[VACUUM PUMP\nMaintains 10^-3 mbar] -.-> E
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STANDBY
    STANDBY --> CHARGING: Grid surplus detected\nMotor accelerates rotor
    CHARGING --> STANDBY: Target omega reached\nMotor idles
    STANDBY --> DISCHARGING: Grid deficit or fault\nGenerator delivers power
    DISCHARGING --> LOW_SPEED: omega drops below threshold
    LOW_SPEED --> CHARGING: Recharge cycle initiated
    STANDBY --> [*]: System shutdown
    DISCHARGING --> EMERGENCY: Critical fault
    EMERGENCY --> [*]: Rotor safe-park
```

### 4.3 Component Interaction Matrix

| Subsystem | Connects to | Function |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| Power Electronics | Motor-Generator | Bidirectional power conversion at variable frequency |
| Motor-Generator | Shaft | Converts electrical ↔ mechanical energy |
| Shaft | Rotor | Transmits torque |
| Rotor | Bearings | Rotational support |
| Bearings | Vacuum Housing | Minimize frictional losses |
| Vacuum Pump | Housing | Reduces windage drag |
| Control System | All subsystems | Sensors, PLC logic, safety interlocks |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1. Define a flywheel energy storage system. State any two of its advantages over a battery storage system.**

**Model Answer:**
A Flywheel Energy Storage (FES) system stores energy in the form of kinetic energy of a rotating mass. The stored energy is $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$.

Advantages over batteries:
1. **Long cycle life** (>100,000 cycles vs. 500–2,000 for Li-ion).
2. **Very high power density** — can deliver rated power within milliseconds.
3. **Wide operating temperature range** — no thermal runaway risk.
4. **Easy state-of-charge monitoring** — simply measure $\omega$.

*(Any two well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Q2. Why are modern high-speed flywheels made of carbon-fibre composites instead of steel?**

**Model Answer:**
Because **specific energy** scales as $e = \tfrac{1}{2}v_{\text{rim}}^{2}$. Composite materials permit rim speeds up to ~1000 m/s (vs. ~500 m/s for steel) due to their higher tensile strength-to-density ratio. This allows:
- Higher rotational speed ($\omega$),
- Lower rotor mass for the same energy,
- Reduced bearing loads and self-discharge losses.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the kinetic energy stored in a flywheel of moment of inertia $I$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$. Explain why modern flywheels prefer composite rotors over steel. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from elemental kinetic energy $dE = \tfrac{1}{2}(dm)v^2$ and $v = r\omega$, we obtain by integration:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\omega^2 \int r^2 \, dm = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

**Why composites win:** Since $e = \tfrac{1}{2} k^2 \omega^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} v_{\text{rim}}^2$, specific energy depends purely on rim speed (a material strength limit). Carbon-fibre composites reach ~1000 m/s rim speeds vs. ~500 m/s for steel, giving ~4× the specific energy. Additionally, lower rotor mass reduces bearing friction and containment requirements.

---

> **(b)** A flywheel rotor is a solid steel cylinder of mass 800 kg, diameter 1.2 m, rotating at 3600 rpm. It supplies a constant load of 80 kW until its speed drops to 1800 rpm. Calculate:
> (i) the initial kinetic energy stored,
> (ii) the energy delivered to the load,
> (iii) the discharge time. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Initial KE:**

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi \times 3600}{60} = 376.99 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$I = \frac{1}{2} m r^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 800 \times (0.6)^2 = 144 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

$$E_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times 144 \times (376.99)^2 = 72 \times 142{,}121 = 10.23 \text{ MJ}$$

**(ii) Energy delivered:**

$$\omega_2 = \frac{2\pi \times 1800}{60} = 188.50 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} \times 144 \times \left[(376.99)^2 - (188.50)^2\right]$$

$$\Delta E = 72 \times (142{,}121 - 35{,}532) = 72 \times 106{,}589 = 7.674 \text{ MJ}$$

**(iii) Discharge time:**

$$t = \frac{\Delta E}{P_L} = \frac{7.674 \times 10^6}{80 \times 10^3} = 95.9 \text{ s} \approx 1.6 \text{ min}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional components of a flywheel energy storage system. List the main losses in the system and explain how they are minimized. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Schematic Sketch + Text):**

**Components:**

1. **Rotor** — stores kinetic energy.
2. **Motor/Generator** — reversible machine (typically PMSM).
3. **Power Electronics** — bidirectional converter (AC-DC-AC).
4. **Bearing System** — active magnetic or mechanical bearings.
5. **Vacuum Chamber** — housing for the rotor.
6. **Control System** — sensors, PLC, safety interlocks.

**Losses and Mitigation:**

| Loss Mechanism | Mitigation |
|----------------|------------|
| Windage drag | Vacuum enclosure (<10⁻³ mbar) |
| Bearing friction | Magnetic levitation / active bearings |
| Electrical losses in motor-generator | High-efficiency PMSM with copper rotor |
| Converter switching losses | SiC/GaN wide-bandgap semiconductors |
| Rotor hysteresis/eddy currents | Non-magnetic composite rotor |

---

> **(b)** A composite flywheel rotor has moment of inertia $I = 12 \text{ kg·m}^2$ and operates between $10{,}000 \text{ rpm}$ and $4{,}000 \text{ rpm}$. Calculate:
> (i) the usable energy stored,
> (ii) the maximum continuous load it can supply for **5 minutes** before reaching minimum speed, and
> (iii) the usable energy fraction. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Usable energy:**

$$\omega_{\max} = \frac{2\pi \times 10{,}000}{60} = 1047.2 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{\min} = \frac{2\pi \times 4{,}000}{60} = 418.9 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times \left[(1047.2)^2 - (418.9)^2\right]$$

$$\Delta E = 6 \times (1{,}096{,}623 - 175{,}474) = 6 \times 921{,}149 = 5.527 \text{ MJ}$$

**(ii) Max continuous load for 5 minutes ($t = 300$ s):**

$$P_L = \frac{\Delta E}{t} = \frac{5.527 \times 10^6}{300} = 18.42 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Usable energy fraction:**

$$\eta_u = 1 - \left(\frac{\omega_{\min}}{\omega_{\max}}\right)^2 = 1 - \left(\frac{4000}{10000}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.16 = 0.84$$

So **84%** of the total energy is extractable. (Setting $\omega_{\min} \to 0$ gives 100%, but a finite minimum is kept to maintain grid frequency stability.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting rpm → rad/s conversion.** Students often plug $N$ directly into $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ — instant **zero** for that sub-part.
> 2. **Mixing mass and moment of inertia.** If asked for "specific energy," divide by mass $m$, NOT by $I$.
> 3. **Sign errors in discharge equations.** Energy discharged is $\tfrac{1}{2}I(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$, never $(\omega_2^2 - \omega_1^2)$.
> 4. **Assuming uniform deceleration without a stated load.** Without a constant $P_L$, you cannot derive $t$ — you only get $\Delta E$.
> 5. **Confusing "energy stored" with "usable energy."** Always read the wording carefully.
> 6. **Skipping units in the final answer.** Examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the ESE:**
>
> ✅ **Master equation:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ — never forget it.
> ✅ **Conversion:** $\omega \text{ (rad/s)} = 2\pi N / 60$ where $N$ is in **rpm**.
> ✅ **Solid disc:** $I = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^2$.
> ✅ **Hollow cylinder:** $I = \tfrac{1}{2}m(r_o^2 + r_i^2)$.
> ✅ **Specific energy:** $e = \tfrac{1}{2}v_{\text{rim}}^2$ — material limit governs design.
> ✅ **Discharge time:** $t = I(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2) / (2 P_L)$.
> ✅ **Usable fraction:** $\eta_u = 1 - (\omega_{\min}/\omega_{\max})^2$.
> ✅ **Components:** Rotor, M/G, Power Electronics, Bearings, Vacuum, Control.
> ✅ **Losses:** Windage, friction, electrical — all minimized via vacuum + magnetic bearings + wide-bandgap converters.
> ✅ **Applications:** UPS, grid frequency regulation, renewable smoothing, rail regenerative braking, F1 KERS, ISS attitude control.
> ✅ **Key advantages:** Long cycle life (>10⁵ cycles), high power density, fast response (<4 ms), no chemical degradation.
> ✅ **Key disadvantage:** High self-discharge (standby losses) — FES is **not for long-duration storage**; it excels in **short-duration, high-cycle** duties.
> ✅ **Numerical approach:** Convert everything to SI first, then compute; carry units to the final line.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Energy Storage
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Energy Storage

# Module 4 — Magnetic Energy Storage

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus-Standard Wording)**
> **Magnetic Energy Storage (MES)** is a direct method of storing electrical energy in the form of a **static magnetic field** established in a coil carrying a persistent DC current. When the coil is wound from a **superconducting material** and operated below its **critical temperature (T_c)**, the resistive (I²R) losses become virtually zero, allowing the magnetic energy to circulate indefinitely. This configuration is termed **Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage (SMES)** and is used in power plants as a high-power, rapid-response buffer between generation, transmission, and load.

### 1.1 Key Sub-Classes of Magnetic Energy Storage

| Class | Material State | Typical Storage Duration | Power Plant Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inductive Storage (Conventional)** | Copper / Aluminium coil | Milliseconds | Pulsed power, fault current limiters |
| **Superconducting (SMES)** | Niobium-Titanium (NbTi), BSCCO, YBCO | Seconds to Minutes | Grid frequency regulation, spinning reserve, voltage stability |
| **Hybrid SMES + Battery / Capacitor** | Composite | Minutes to Hours | Renewable firming, microgrid stabilization |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electromagnetic Flywheel"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a huge, frictionless water tank placed at the top of a hill (a hydroelectric reservoir). Water (= magnetic energy) sits calmly inside, but it holds enormous **potential** to do work. A mechanical flywheel stores kinetic energy in a spinning mass; an **SMES stores electromagnetic energy in a stationary, invisible magnetic "mass"** — the magnetic field itself. Because the superconducting coil has effectively **zero electrical resistance**, the current — and therefore the magnetic field — behaves like a perfectly frictionless wheel: it can be drawn upon in microseconds, then refilled just as fast.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants (Required for All Numericals)

- Permeability of free space: **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m ≈ 1.2566 × 10⁻⁶ H/m**
- Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- Speed of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Critical temperature of NbTi: **T_c ≈ 9.2 K**
- Critical temperature of YBCO (High-T_c): **T_c ≈ 92 K**

> [!NOTE]
> A **superconductor** exhibits three hallmark properties that make SMES feasible: **(i) Zero DC resistance** (Meissner state), **(ii) Perfect diamagnetism / flux expulsion (Meissner effect)**, and **(iii) A definite critical surface** defined by the triple limits **T < T_c**, **B < B_c**, and **I < I_c**. Violating any one quenches the system back to the normal resistive state.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Energy-Storing Mechanism — How and Why

The energy storage is **not** in the coil wire itself — it resides in the **magnetic field** that fills the space inside and around the coil. The logic chain is as follows:

- **Step 1 — Current Builds the Field:** When DC current **I** flows through an **N**-turn coil, each ampere-turn contributes a small magnetic flux density **dB**. The net field inside a long solenoid is **B = μ₀ n I**, where **n = N / ℓ** is the turns per unit length.
- **Step 2 — Field Stores Energy:** Building the field requires work against the back-EMF. The differential work done is **dW = L I dI**, integrated from 0 to **I** to give **W = ½ L I²**.
- **Step 3 — Superconductor Preserves It:** In a normal metal, this energy would be **dissipated as I²R heat** within milliseconds. In a superconductor, **R → 0**, so the energy remains stored as long as the cryogenic environment is maintained.
- **Step 4 — Energy is Released on Demand:** A power-conditioning system (PCS) using an **IGBT / GTO voltage-source converter (VSC)** converts the DC current into AC at grid frequency, delivering power **P = dW/dt** to the load.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are **high-yield** for KTU University Exam derivations and numerical problems. Master the boxed expressions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy in an inductor | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ | Joules (J) | Valid for any coil, R or R = 0 |
| 2 | Inductance of long solenoid | $L = \dfrac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell}$ | Henries (H) | Length ℓ ≫ radius a |
| 3 | Magnetic field inside solenoid | $B = \mu_{0} n I = \dfrac{\mu_{0} N I}{\ell}$ | Tesla (T) | Uniform only at center |
| 4 | Energy density of B-field | $u = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}}$ | J/m³ | Universal, region outside coil counts too |
| 5 | Total energy via volume | $W = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot A \cdot \ell$ | J | Substitute from (3) to recover ½LI² |
| 6 | Power delivered by SMES | $P = \dfrac{dW}{dt} = V_{coil} \cdot I = L I \dfrac{dI}{dt}$ | W (J/s) | Controlled by chopper / VSC |
| 7 | Self-inductance of toroid | $L = \dfrac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{2\pi r_{m}}$ | H | r_m = mean radius of toroid |
| 8 | Stored energy per unit mass | $\dfrac{W}{m} = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0} \rho}$ | J/kg | ρ = mass density of conductor |
| 9 | Discharge time constant | $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R_{eq}}$ | s | For R → 0 (SC), τ → ∞ |
| 10 | Force between coil faces | $F = \dfrac{B^{2} A}{2\mu_{0}}$ | N | Hoop-stress design constraint |

> **Practical Engineering Note:** SMES systems in power plants are typically **solenoidal (cylindrical) or toroidal**. Toroidal geometry is preferred for **stray-field containment** — it keeps the external magnetic field below **5 × 10⁻⁴ T** (5 Gauss) at the fence line, satisfying IEEE C95.6 public-exposure limits.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Load Levelling in Thermal & Nuclear Plants:** SMES absorbs the sudden **kW-to-MW excursions** during boiler-pump switching, freeing the steam turbine from rapid load swings that cause thermal fatigue.
- **Renewable Integration (Solar / Wind):** Acts as a **sub-second smoothing buffer** for photovoltaic ramp-rates exceeding **0.1 pu/s**.
- **Power-Quality & UPS Replacement:** Provides **ride-through** during voltage sags up to **0.5 seconds** with sub-cycle response.
- **Pulsed Power:** Military and fusion research (e.g., **ITER**) uses SMES-like coils to drive megamp currents for plasma confinement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every step below is shown **explicitly** for KTU's 14-mark derivation questions. Skipping algebraic steps in the answer script is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction per the KTU valuation key.

### 3.1 Derivation I — Energy Stored in an Inductor (½ L I²)

**Statement to Prove:** *The energy stored in the magnetic field of an inductor carrying current **I** is **W = ½ L I²**.*

**Starting Premise (Faraday's Law for Self-Inductance):**
When the current in a coil changes, the induced back-EMF opposes the change.

$$\varepsilon = -L \frac{dI}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Differential Work Done by the External Source:**
To push a small charge **dq = I dt** against this back-EMF, the external source must supply the differential work.

$$dW = \varepsilon \, dq = \left(L \frac{dI}{dt}\right) (I \, dt)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the Differential:**

$$dW = L I \, dI$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from Zero Current to Final Current I:**
Assuming the inductor is initially de-energized (I = 0 at t = 0):

$$\int_{0}^{W} dW = \int_{0}^{I} L I \, dI$$

$$W = L \left[ \frac{I^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{I} = L \cdot \frac{I^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The result is **quadratic in I** — doubling the current **quadruples** the stored energy. This is why SMES designers aggressively push **I** toward the critical current **I_c** of the superconductor.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Energy Density of a Magnetic Field (B² / 2μ₀)

**Statement to Prove:** *The energy density of a magnetic field in free space is **u = B² / (2 μ₀)**.*

**Step 1 — Substitute the Solenoid Inductance into the Energy Formula:**
For a long solenoid of length **ℓ**, cross-sectional area **A**, and **N** turns:

$$L = \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell}$$

Substituting into $W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell} \right) I^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Express the Result in Terms of B:**
Recall that for a long solenoid, $B = \dfrac{\mu_{0} N I}{\ell}$, hence $N I = \dfrac{B \ell}{\mu_{0}}$.

Substituting:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_{0} A}{\ell} \cdot \left( N I \right)^{2}$$

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_{0} A}{\ell} \cdot \left( \frac{B \ell}{\mu_{0}} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Expand and Cancel:**

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_{0} A}{\ell} \cdot \frac{B^{2} \ell^{2}}{\mu_{0}^{2}}$$

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{A \ell}{\mu_{0}} \cdot B^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that the Magnetic Field Fills the Cylindrical Volume V = A ℓ:**

$$W = \frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}} \cdot (A \ell)$$

**Step 5 — Define Energy Density u = W / V:**

$$\boxed{\,u = \frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}} \;\; \left[ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^{3}} \right]\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Valuation:** A common mistake students make is forgetting that the field energy exists in **all space**, not just inside the solenoid. The energy density formula above is **local and universal** — apply it point-by-point and integrate over the field volume to get the total energy for non-solenoidal geometries.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Total Energy of a Solenoid in Terms of B, A, ℓ

**Starting from $u = \dfrac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}}$** and assuming the field is approximately uniform inside the solenoid (volume $V = A \ell$):

$$W = u \cdot V = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot A \ell$$

**Step 1 — Substitute B = μ₀ N I / ℓ:**

$$W = \frac{1}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot A \ell \cdot \left( \frac{\mu_{0} N I}{\ell} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$W = \frac{1}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot A \ell \cdot \frac{\mu_{0}^{2} N^{2} I^{2}}{\ell^{2}}$$

$$W = \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A I^{2}}{2 \ell}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize This is ½ L I²:**

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell} \right) I^{2} = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Verification Successful.** Both derivations arrive at the same expression, confirming the self-consistency of the magnetic energy framework — a point KTU examiners appreciate when students explicitly write this verification in the answer script.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Numerical (Typical KTU-Style)

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A superconducting solenoid has **N = 1000 turns**, **length ℓ = 0.50 m**, **radius a = 0.05 m**, and carries a current of **I = 200 A**. Compute: (a) Inductance L, (b) Magnetic field B, (c) Total stored energy W, and (d) Energy density u.

**Given Data:**
- N = 1000, ℓ = 0.50 m, a = 0.05 m, I = 200 A
- μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m
- Cross-sectional area: A = π a² = π (0.05)² = 7.854 × 10⁻³ m²

**Part (a) — Inductance:**

$$L = \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(10^{3})^{2}(7.854 \times 10^{-3})}{0.50}$$

$$L = \frac{(1.2566 \times 10^{-6})(10^{6})(7.854 \times 10^{-3})}{0.50} = \frac{9.8696}{0.50}$$

$$\boxed{L = 19.74 \;\text{mH}}$$

**Part (b) — Magnetic Field:**

$$B = \frac{\mu_{0} N I}{\ell} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(1000)(200)}{0.50}$$

$$B = \frac{(1.2566 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{5})}{0.50} = \frac{0.25133}{0.50}$$

$$\boxed{B = 0.5027 \;\text{T}}$$

**Part (c) — Total Energy:**

$$W = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2} = \frac{1}{2} (19.74 \times 10^{-3})(200)^{2}$$

$$W = \frac{1}{2} (19.74 \times 10^{-3})(4 \times 10^{4}) = \frac{1}{2}(789.6)$$

$$\boxed{W = 394.8 \;\text{J}}$$

**Part (d) — Energy Density:**

$$u = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} = \frac{(0.5027)^{2}}{2 (4\pi \times 10^{-7})}$$

$$u = \frac{0.2527}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{u \approx 1.005 \times 10^{5} \;\text{J/m}^{3} \;\;(\approx 100.5 \;\text{kJ/m}^{3})}$$

**Cross-check using $W = u \cdot V$:**

$$V = A \ell = (7.854 \times 10^{-3})(0.50) = 3.927 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{3}$$

$$W = (1.005 \times 10^{5})(3.927 \times 10^{-3}) = 394.7 \;\text{J} \;\; ✓$$

The cross-check matches Part (c) within rounding error, validating both methods.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The two diagrams below are **mermaid flowcharts** depicting the **functional architecture** of an SMES unit and the **energy-conversion pathway** from the grid to the magnetic field. These are the diagram types most frequently requested in KTU 14-mark questions.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — SMES Plant Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR["GRID OR POWER PLANT BUS 3 PHASE AC 50 HZ"] -->|STEP DOWN| XFMR["UNIT TRANSFORMER 11 kV to 480 V"]
    XFMR --> RECT["AC to DC CONVERTER 6 PULSE OR 12 PULSE THYRISTOR / IGBT RECTIFIER"]
    RECT --> CHOP["DC CHOPPER IGBT BASED REGULATES CHARGE DISCHARGE RATE"]
    CHOP --> COIL["SUPERCONDUCTING COIL SOLENOID OR TOROID NIOBIUM TITANIUM 4.2 K HELIUM BATH"]
    COIL -->|MAGNETIC FIELD ENERGY| STORED["STORED MAGNETIC ENERGY W EQUALS 0.5 L I SQUARED"]
    STORED -->|ON DEMAND| INV["DC to AC INVERTER VOLTAGE SOURCE CONVERTER VSC"]
    INV -->|RECONNECT| PWR
    CRYO["CRYOGENIC COOLING SYSTEM LIQUID HELIUM 4.2 K OR LIQUID NITROGEN 77 K FOR HTS"] -->|MAINTAINS T BELOW T_c| COIL
    CTRL["SCADA BASED ENERGY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM EMS"] -->|SETPOINT| CHOP
    CTRL -->|SETPOINT| INV
```

### 4.2 Energy-Flow Diagram — Charge / Hold / Discharge Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT POWER FROM GENERATOR OR GRID"] --> B["RECTIFIER STAGE CONVERTS AC TO DC"]
    B --> C["DC CURRENT ENTERS SUPERCONDUCTING COIL"]
    C --> D["MAGNETIC FIELD B BUILDS UP STORES ENERGY W EQUALS 0.5 L I SQUARED"]
    D --> E{"OPERATING MODE"}
    E -->|CHARGING| F["CURRENT RISES FROM 0 TO I FINAL ENERGY ACCUMULATES"]
    E -->|HOLD| G["CURRENT AND FIELD CONSTANT NO I SQUARED R LOSSES VIRTUALLY INFINITE STORAGE"]
    E -->|DISCHARGING| H["CURRENT DECREASES ENERGY EXTRACTED CONVERTER INVERTS TO AC"]
    F --> I["GRID OR LOCAL LOAD"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.3 Subsystem Interaction Summary (Verbal Schematic)

- **Power Conditioning System (PCS):** Comprises the rectifier, chopper, and inverter. The chopper uses **pulse-width modulation (PWM)** at 1–5 kHz to regulate the rate of change of current **dI/dt**, which directly controls the discharge power **P = V I**.
- **Magnet Subsystem:** The solenoid / toroid with its persistent-current switch. In **persistent mode**, the current flows in a **closed superconducting loop** with no external leads — the only loss is the AC loss in the superconductor (typically < **0.1 %/hr**).
- **Cryogenic Subsystem:** Maintains the conductor below **T_c**. For **Low-T_c (LTS)** NbTi magnets, **liquid helium at 4.2 K** is used. For **High-T_c (HTS)** YBCO, **liquid nitrogen at 77 K** is sufficient, drastically reducing cryogenic cost.
- **Control & Protection Subsystem:** Monitors **T, B, I** against the **critical surface**. If any limit is approached, the **quench-detection circuit** fires the dump resistor, dissipating the magnetic energy safely.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMET631]**
*Define Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage (SMES). State two advantages and one limitation of SMES in power plant applications.*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **Definition:** SMES is a device that stores electrical energy in the magnetic field of a superconducting coil maintained below its critical temperature. Because the coil has **virtually zero DC resistance**, the current circulates persistently, retaining the energy $W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$.
- **Advantages (any two):**
  1. Very **high power density** and **sub-cycle response time** (≪ 100 ms), ideal for grid frequency regulation.
  2. **Long cycle life** (> 10⁵ charge–discharge cycles) since there is no chemical or mechanical wear.
  3. **High round-trip efficiency** (≈ 95 %), since I²R losses in the superconducting state are negligible.
- **Limitation (any one):**
  1. Requires **continuous cryogenic cooling**, which imposes a parasitic load (typically **1–3 kW per MJ** of stored energy).
  2. **High capital cost** of superconducting wire (especially HTS) and cryostat.
  3. Strong **stray magnetic fields** require shielding (mitigated by toroidal geometry).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Question]**
*Derive the expression for energy density of a magnetic field. State its SI unit.*

**Model Answer Outline:**
- Start with the energy in a long solenoid: $W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$.
- Substitute $L = \mu_{0} N^{2} A / \ell$ and the field relation $B = \mu_{0} N I / \ell$.
- Show the algebraic cancellation leading to $W = (B^{2}/2\mu_{0}) \cdot A \ell$.
- Recognize $A \ell$ as the field volume **V**, and define energy density $u = W/V$.
- **Final result:** $u = B^{2}/(2 \mu_{0})$ with **SI unit J/m³** (equivalently **Pa**, since J/m³ = N/m²).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall**
> For derivation questions on SMES, students **frequently lose 2–3 marks** for the following errors:
> 1. **Forgetting to state the boundary conditions** of integration (i.e., I = 0 to I = I_final, and the initial field B = 0).
> 2. **Mixing up** the solenoid-inductance formula with the **toroid** formula — read the question carefully!
> 3. **Failing to specify units** in numerical sub-parts. KTU's valuation key explicitly allocates **½ mark per numerical for the correct unit**.
> 4. **Skipping the final cross-check** between $W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ and $W = u \cdot V$. Showing this consistency adds **+1 examiner-goodwill mark** in borderline cases.
> 5. **Not drawing the functional block diagram** in 7-mark descriptive questions — KTU requires a labeled diagram wherever the question says "explain with a neat sketch."

---

#### **Question Choice A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
*(a) With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of a Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage (SMES) system used in a power plant. Discuss the role of the cryogenic system and the power-conditioning unit. (7 marks)*

*(b) Derive the expression for the energy stored per unit volume in a magnetic field. A long solenoid of 1500 turns, length 1.0 m, and radius 0.08 m carries a current of 250 A. Calculate (i) the inductance, (ii) the magnetic field B inside the coil, (iii) the total energy stored, and (iv) the energy density. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Working (7 marks):**
*(Draw the block diagram from Section 4.1 above — see the Mermaid ASCII equivalent below.)*

```
GRID ──► TRANSFORMER ──► RECTIFIER (AC→DC) ──► CHOPPER (DC-DC) ──► SC COIL
                                       ▲                              │
                                       │                              ▼
                              SCADA / EMS ◄── INVERTER (DC→AC) ◄── DUMP RESISTOR
                                       │
```
