# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define the following terms with their formulas:**
**(a)** Load Factor
**(b)** Diversity Factor
**(c)** Capacity Factor

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Load Factor:** It is the ratio of the average load to the maximum demand occurring during a specified period.

$$LF = \frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Maximum Demand}} = \frac{\overline{L}}{L_{max}}$$

**(b) Diversity Factor:** It is the ratio of the sum of the individual maximum demands of various consumers to the maximum demand on the power station.

$$DF = \frac{\sum \text{Individual Maximum Demands}}{L_{max,\text{station}}}$$

**(c) Capacity Factor:** It is the ratio of the actual energy generated by the plant to the maximum energy that could have been generated at its installed capacity during the same period.

$$CF = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{installed}} \times T}$$

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A power station has a maximum demand of 80 MW and a daily load factor of 0.6. Calculate the daily energy generated and the average load.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L_{max} = 80$ MW, LF = 0.60, T = 24 hours

**Step 1:** Average Load

$$\overline{L} = LF \times L_{max} = 0.60 \times 80 = 48 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 2:** Daily Energy

$$E_{day} = \overline{L} \times T = 48 \times 24 = 1152 \text{ MWh}$$

**Final Answer:** Average Load = **48 MW**, Daily Energy = **1152 MWh**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **load curve** and a **load duration curve**. With the help of neat sketches, distinguish between them. Discuss their significance in power plant engineering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A power station supplies the following loads:
- Domestic consumers: 1500 kW
- Commercial consumers: 2000 kW
- Industrial consumers: 3000 kW

The maximum demands on the station are:
- Domestic: 1000 kW
- Commercial: 1500 kW
- Industrial: 2500 kW

Calculate the **Diversity Factor** and **Demand Factor** for each consumer type. Comment on the economic implications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Load Curve vs. Load Duration Curve:**

A **load curve** is a plot of load versus time (chronological order), typically over a day. It shows when peak loads occur and their timing — morning peak, evening peak, etc.

A **load duration curve (LDC)** is obtained by rearranging the load curve in **descending order of magnitude**, irrespective of time sequence. It shows how many hours a particular load level persists.

**Differences Table:**

| Feature | Load Curve | Load Duration Curve |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| X-axis | Time (hours) | Hours of occurrence |
| Order | Chronological | Descending load |
| Shape | Variable (peaks/valleys) | Monotonically decreasing |
| Use | Operational scheduling | Plant capacity selection |
| Shows peak time | Yes | No |

**Significance:**
- Helps in **forecasting** future loads.
- Essential for **unit commitment** and **economic dispatch**.
- Determines **base load vs. peak load plant** selection.

**(b) Diversity and Demand Factor Calculations:**

**Diversity Factor (DF):**

$$DF = \frac{\sum L_{max,i}}{L_{max,\text{station}}}$$

Given individual maximum demands:
$$\sum L_{max} = 1000 + 1500 + 2500 = 5000 \text{ kW}$$

Assuming station maximum demand is the sum (worst case without diversity, but using given station MD):

For this problem, we use the **connected loads as proxy** if station MD is not specified distinctly. Assume station MD = 4000 kW:

$$DF = \frac{5000}{4000} = 1.25$$

**Demand Factor for each type:**

$$\text{Demand Factor} = \frac{\text{Maximum Demand}}{\text{Connected Load}}$$

| Consumer Type | Max Demand (kW) | Connected Load (kW) | Demand Factor |
|---------------|----------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Domestic | 1000 | 1500 | $1000/1500 = 0.667$ |
| Commercial | 1500 | 2000 | $1500/2000 = 0.750$ |
| Industrial | 2500 | 3000 | $2500/3000 = 0.833$ |

**Economic Implications:**
- Higher DF (1.25) means the station can serve **larger total demand with smaller installed capacity**, reducing capital cost.
- Industrial consumers have the **highest demand factor (0.833)** — they are more reliable consumers; utilities prefer them.
- Domestic consumers have the **lowest demand factor (0.667)** — their loads are intermittent, requiring more standby capacity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Internal Choice]

**(a)** What is **economic load dispatch**? Derive the condition for **optimum generation** when the cost functions of two plants are given. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A power plant has the following cost characteristics:
- Plant 1: $F_1 = 0.02 P_1^2 + 20 P_1 + 100$ ₹/hr
- Plant 2: $F_2 = 0.04 P_2^2 + 15 P_2 + 150$ ₹/hr

Total load to be supplied: $P_1 + P_2 = 200$ MW. Both plants have minimum output 20 MW and maximum 120 MW. Determine the **economic generation schedule** and the **total cost**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Economic Load Dispatch and Optimum Condition:**

**Economic load dispatch** is the process of allocating the total required generation among the available generating units such that the **total fuel cost is minimized** while meeting all constraints (load balance, generation limits).

**Mathematical Formulation:**

Minimize: $F_T = F_1(P_1) + F_2(P_2)$

Subject to: $P_1 + P_2 = P_D$ (load balance) and $P_{i,min} \leq P_i \leq P_{i,max}$

**Lagrange Method:**

$$\mathcal{L} = F_1(P_1) + F_2(P_2) - \lambda (P_1 + P_2 - P_D)$$

Partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_1} = \frac{dF_1}{dP_1} - \lambda = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P_2} = \frac{dF_2}{dP_2} - \lambda = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = -(P_1 + P_2 - P_D) = 0$$

**Optimum Condition (Equal Incremental Cost):**

$$\boxed{\frac{dF_1}{dP_1} = \frac{dF_2}{dP_2} = \lambda}$$

The **incremental fuel cost** (₹/MWh) must be **equal** across all online plants for optimal dispatch.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute incremental cost functions:

$$\frac{dF_1}{dP_1} = 0.04 P_1 + 20$$

$$\frac{dF_2}{dP_2} = 0.08 P_2 + 15$$

**Step 2:** Apply equal incremental cost condition:

$$0.04 P_1 + 20 = 0.08 P_2 + 15$$

$$0.04 P_1 - 0.08 P_2 = -5$$

$$P_1 - 2 P_2 = -125$$

$$P_1 = 2 P_2 - 125 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply load balance constraint:

$$P_1 + P_2 = 200 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4:** Solve simultaneously. Substituting (i) into (ii):

$$(2 P_2 - 125) + P_2 = 200$$

$$3 P_2 = 325$$

$$P_2 = 108.33 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 5:** Find P1:

$$P_1 = 200 - 108.33 = 91.67 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 6:** Check generation limits (20 ≤ P ≤ 120):

- Plant 1: $91.67$ MW ∈ [20, 120] ✓
- Plant 2: $108.33$ MW ∈ [20, 120] ✓

**Step 7:** Verify equal incremental cost:

$$\frac{dF_1}{dP_1} = 0.04 \times 91.67 + 20 = 3.667 + 20 = 23.667 \text{ ₹/MWh}$$

$$\frac{dF_2}{dP_2} = 0.08 \times 108.33 + 15 = 8.667 + 15 = 23.667 \text{ ₹/MWh}$$

Both equal **23.667 ₹/MWh** ✓ (System lambda λ = 23.667)

**Step 8:** Calculate Total Cost:

$$F_1 = 0.02 \times (91.67)^2 + 20 \times 91.67 + 100$$

$$F_1 = 0.02 \times 8403.4 + 1833.4 + 100 = 168.07 + 1833.4 + 100 = 2101.47 \text{ ₹/hr}$$

$$F_2 = 0.04 \times (108.33)^2 + 15 \times 108.33 + 150$$

$$F_2 = 0.04 \times 11735.4 + 1624.95 + 150 = 469.42 + 1624.95 + 150 = 2244.37 \text{ ₹/hr}$$

**Total Cost:**

$$F_T = 2101.47 + 2244.37 = 4345.84 \text{ ₹/hr}$$

**Final Answer:** $P_1 = 91.67$ MW, $P_2 = 108.33$ MW, Total Cost = **₹ 4345.84/hr**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Students write Load Factor as a number > 1, forgetting it must be ≤ 1. Always check at the end.
> 2. **CF vs LF confusion:** Capacity Factor uses *installed capacity* in denominator; Load Factor uses *maximum demand*. Many students swap these and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Tariff skipping:** In tariff problems, students calculate fixed + energy but forget the **PF penalty** sub-step. Always read the question 3 times before solving.
> 4. **Economic dispatch constraint violation:** After solving the equations, students forget to verify that $P_i$ is within [min, max] limits. If violated, the constrained plant must be set at its limit, and the balance re-dispatched.
> 5. **Symbol confusion:** Writing $L_{max}$ when meaning $P_{inst}$ (or vice versa). Use clear notation in your script.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Before KTU Exam — Bookmark This Section**

### Key Definitions
- **Connected Load:** Sum of ratings of all connected devices (always ≥ Maximum Demand).
- **Maximum Demand:** Highest load on the station during a period.
- **Average Load:** Total energy divided by total hours.
- **Base Load:** Minimum load occurring 24×7.
- **Peak Load:** Maximum demand (short duration).

### The Five Factors (Priority Order)
1. **Demand Factor** = Max Demand / Connected Load (< 1)
2. **Diversity Factor** = Σ Individual Max Demands / Station Max Demand (> 1)
3. **Load Factor** = Average Load / Maximum Demand (< 1)
4. **Capacity Factor** = Actual Energy / (Installed Cap × Hours) (< 1)
5. **Use Factor** = Actual Energy / (Installed Cap × Hours of Use) (> CF)

### Universal Inequalities to Memorize
$$\text{CF} \leq \text{LF} \leq 1$$
$$1 \leq \text{DF}$$
$$\text{Demand Factor} \leq 1$$
$$\text{PUF} = \frac{L_{max}}{P_{inst}} \leq 1$$

### Economic Dispatch Formula
$$\frac{dF_1}{dP_1} = \frac{dF_2}{dP_2} = \lambda \quad \text{(optimum condition)}$$

### Tariff Types (Most to Least Common)
1. **Two-Part Tariff** (KSEB standard) — Fixed + Energy
2. **Three-Part Tariff** — Fixed + Demand + Energy
3. **Block Tariff** — Stepwise energy rates
4. **Time-of-Use Tariff** — Peak/Off-peak pricing
5. **Power Factor Tariff** — Penalty/reward based

### Energy Calculation Shortcuts
- Daily Energy (kWh) = Average Load (kW) × 24
- Annual Energy (kWh) = CF × Installed Capacity (kW) × 8760
- Area under load curve = Total energy

### Load Curve vs. Load Duration Curve
- Load curve: time-ordered (chronological)
- LDC: load magnitude-ordered (descending)
- LDC always used for plant capacity selection

### Useful Life Reference Values
- Steam Plant: 30 years
- Hydro Plant: 50+ years
- Nuclear Plant: 40–60 years
- Solar PV: 25–30 years

### Common Mistakes to Avoid
- Forgetting to convert MW to kW or vice versa (1 MW = 1000 kW)
- Using installed capacity instead of maximum demand in LF formula
- Skipping PF penalty in tariff calculations
- Not verifying generation limits in economic dispatch
- Mixing up time units (8760 hours/year vs. 24 hours/day)

### KTU 2024 Trend Alert
Recent KTU papers show increasing emphasis on:
- **Renewable integration** (calculating effective CF of solar/wind)
- **Smart grid tariffs** (Time-of-Use)
- **Depreciation methods** (Sinking Fund vs. Straight Line)
- **Numerical problems** combining 2–3 factors in a single question

### Final Confidence Boost
> [!NOTE]
> If you can solve **Worked Examples 1, 2, and 3** from Section 3 of these notes without looking, you are well-prepared for at least **70% of the marks** in this module. The remaining 30% comes from the economic dispatch numerical (Example 4 style) and tariff design with PF penalty.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Pollution and Its Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET631 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Environmental Pollution and Its Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Environmental Pollution** in the context of power plant engineering is defined as the introduction of contaminants (in the form of chemical substances, energy such as heat or noise, or particulate matter) into the natural environment by thermal, nuclear, or renewable power-generating stations, causing adverse ecological, biological, and human-health impacts.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET631)**, the operational impact is classified into four primary vectors:

1. **Air Pollution** — release of SOx, NOx, CO₂, CO, PM (Particulate Matter), Mercury (Hg), and Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs).
2. **Water Pollution** — thermal discharge, ash-pond leachate, acid mine drainage, and cooling-tower blowdown.
3. **Land/Soil Pollution** — Fly ash, bottom ash, FGD (Flue Gas Desulfurization) sludge, and spent SCR catalyst disposal.
4. **Noise Pollution** — high dB emissions from mills, fans, turbines, and boiler safety-valve vents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Regulatory Bodies (India):** Central Pollution Control Board (**CPCB**), Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (**MoEFCC**), and the Central Electricity Authority (**CEA**). Power plants must comply with the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** and the **EPA Notifications, 2015 & 2024** (TPP emission standards).

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **large restaurant kitchen** burning coal-fired ovens. The smoke contains soot (PM), sour smell (SO₂), and acrid gas (NOx). Now imagine **100 such kitchens** combined into a single **1000 MW power plant chimney**.

A 1000 MW coal plant burns roughly **10,000–12,000 tonnes of coal/day**, producing:

- **600 tonnes/day of SO₂** (acid rain precursor)
- **300 tonnes/day of NOx** (smog + respiratory irritant)
- **200–400 tonnes/day of fly ash** (PM₂.₅, carcinogen)
- **20,000 tonnes/day of CO₂** (greenhouse gas)

> [!NOTE]
> **Without control equipment**, a 1000 MW coal plant would emit as much particulate matter in 1 day as **10 million diesel trucks** running simultaneously. Hence, **pollution control devices (PCDs)** like ESPs, FGDs, and SCRs act as the "kidneys and lungs" of a thermal power plant.

---

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Power-Plant Environmental Footprint

| Pollution Vector | Primary Source in Plant | Critical Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Air Pollutants** | Boiler flue gas, coal handling, ash silos | **PM ≤ 30 mg/Nm³**, **SO₂ ≤ 100 mg/Nm³** |
| **Thermal Discharge** | Condenser cooling water outfall | **ΔT ≤ 7 °C** (CPCB) |
| **Solid Waste (Ash)** | ESP hoppers, bottom-ash slurry | **Fly Ash Notification 1999/2021** |
| **Noise** | Mills, fans, TG sets | **75 dB(A) day, 70 dB(A) night** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Focus):** Pollutant concentration is measured in **mg/Nm³** (milligrams per Normal cubic meter) at reference conditions of **1 atm, 0 °C, 6% O₂** (dry basis for stack measurements per Indian EPA 2015 norms).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Air Pollution — Generation Mechanism

#### 2.1.1 Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)
**Source:** Oxidation of organically and pyritically bound sulphur in coal during combustion.

**Formation Chemistry (Stepwise):**
- Sulphur in fuel: S (solid) + O₂ → SO₂
- Catalytic oxidation: 2SO₂ + O₂ → 2SO₃
- Hydration: SO₃ + H₂O → H₂SO₄ (sulphuric acid aerosol)

$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{SO}_2$$

$$2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{V}_2\text{O}_5,\,400^\circ\text{C}} 2\text{SO}_3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SO₂ matters:** It causes **acid rain** (pH < 5.6), corrodes metallic structures, and aggravates **Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)**. Control is mandated under the **CPCB Emission Regulations, 2015** for all TPPs above 500 MW.

#### 2.1.2 Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)
Three forms occur: **NO (~95%)**, **NO₂ (~5%)**, **N₂O (trace)**.

- **Thermal NOx** — Zeldovich mechanism at T > 1300 °C:
$$\text{N}_2 + \text{O} \longrightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}$$
- **Fuel NOx** — from nitrogenous compounds in coal (pyridines, pyrroles).
- **Prompt NOx** — from CH radicals in fuel-rich zones.

> [!NOTE]
> **Control hierarchy:** (1) Combustion modification (Low-NOx Burners, OFA — Over-Fired Air), (2) Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR), (3) Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction (SNCR).

#### 2.1.3 Particulate Matter (PM)
Includes **PM₁₀ (≤10 μm)** and **PM₂.₅ (≤2.5 μm)**. PM₂.₅ is the most dangerous (deep lung penetration → cardiovascular mortality).

**Size distribution from a coal boiler:**
- **Sub-micron (<1 μm):** Condensable vapours of heavy metals, sulphates.
- **1–10 μm:** Fly ash (silicates, oxides).
- **>10 μm:** Bottom ash + cenosphere.

#### 2.1.4 Greenhouse Gases & Trace Species
- **CO₂:** ~1 tonne CO₂ per MWh for coal; ~0.45 t/MWh for gas; ~0 t/MWh for hydro/nuclear.
- **Mercury (Hg):** ~0.1 mg/Nm³ elemental + oxidized forms. Bioaccumulates in fish.
- **N₂O:** Greenhouse potency ≈ 310 × CO₂.

---

### 2.2 Water Pollution Sources

| Stream | Pollutant | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| **Cooling-tower blowdown** | TDS, Cl⁻, biocides (Cl₂) | Drift eliminator inefficiency |
| **Ash-pond decant** | Heavy metals (As, Se, Hg, B), high pH | Leaching from fly ash |
| **Boiler blowdown** | Phosphates, hydrazine | HP/LP drum chemical dosing |
| **DM plant effluent** | Acid/alkali regenerant | Cation + anion exchange regeneration |

**Thermal Pollution Rule:** CPCB mandates the discharge water temperature not exceed **7 °C above intake** temperature to protect aquatic ecology.

---

### 2.3 Solid Waste — Fly Ash Generation

A 1000 MW plant produces **~2,00,000 tonnes of ash/year**. Under **Fly Ash Notification (MoEFCC, 2021 amendment)**, the utilisation targets are:

- 100% utilisation within 5 years (revised 2024: within 3 years).
- End uses: cement, brick manufacturing, road embankment, mine-filling, geopolymer concrete.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **most frequently tested** in KTU PEMET631 End-Semester Examinations. Master these for the Part B (14-mark) questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units & Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pollutant emission rate (mass)** | $\dot{m}_p = C \times \dot{V}_g \times 10^{-6}$ | $C$ in **mg/Nm³**, $\dot{V}_g$ in **Nm³/h**, $\dot{m}_p$ in **kg/h** |
| 2 | **Specific emission per unit fuel** | $E_{sf} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_p}{\dot{m}_f}$ | kg of pollutant per kg fuel |
| 3 | **Specific emission per unit energy** | $E_{se} = \dfrac{10^6 \, \dot{m}_p}{P_{elec}}$ | kg/MWh ; $P_{elec}$ in kW |
| 4 | **Collection efficiency (any PCD)** | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | % removal |
| 5 | **Penetration** | $P_t = 1 - \eta$ | Fractional breakthrough |
| 6 | **Deutsch–Anderson ESP efficiency** | $\eta_{ESP} = 1 - e^{-w A / Q}$ | $w$ = drift velocity (m/s), $A$ = plate area (m²), $Q$ = gas flow (m³/s) |
| 7 | **CPCB stack height (Q < 80 t/d)** | $H = 14\,\sqrt{Q_s}$ | $H$ in m, $Q_s$ in **t/day** of SO₂ |
| 8 | **CPCB stack height (Q ≥ 80 t/d)** | $H = 74\,Q_s^{0.27}$ | $H$ in m |
| 9 | **Plume rise (Briggs)** | $\Delta h = 1.6 \, F^{1/3} \, x_f^{2/3} / u$ | $F$ = buoyancy flux, $u$ = wind speed |
| 10 | **Heat rate → CO₂ emission** | $E_{CO_2} = 0.785 \times \dfrac{HR}{1000}$ | kg CO₂/kWh ; $HR$ = heat rate in kcal/kWh |
| 11 | **Stoichiometric SO₂ from fuel** | $\dot{m}_{SO_2} = 2 \,\dot{m}_f \, S \times \dfrac{64}{32} \times \dfrac{80}{100}$ | $S$ = sulphur content (mass fraction) |
| 12 | **Emission index (NOx)** | $EI_{NO_x} = \dfrac{\text{kg NO}_2 \text{ emitted}}{10^6 \text{ kg fuel burned}}$ | g/kg fuel |

> **Note on notation:** All vertical bars in equations above (e.g., $\eta$) are written using `$\vert$` style, never raw `|`, to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ESP design** uses the Deutsch–Anderson equation to size plate area for a target efficiency (typically **99.9%** for Indian coal-fired plants).
- **Stack height selection** governed by the CPCB formula ensures ground-level concentration (GLC) of SO₂ does not exceed **80 μg/m³ (24-hr average)** — derived from the **Gaussian Plume Model**.
- **Carbon capture integration** is now mandatory for new supercritical TPPs under the **Indian Energy Storage Mission, 2024** roadmap — reducing specific CO₂ emissions from 0.95 to 0.10 t/MWh (target).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Deutsch–Anderson Equation for ESP Collection Efficiency

**Context:** The Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) is the workhorse particulate-control device in modern thermal power plants, achieving 99.5%–99.9% efficiency.

**Physical Setup:** A particle entering an ESP moves with the gas stream at velocity $u$ (horizontal) but simultaneously drifts toward the collecting plate at velocity $w$ (transverse, due to electrostatic Coulomb force). A particle is collected if it reaches the plate before exiting the duct.

**Step 1 — Geometric Definition of Drift Path**
The time the gas takes to traverse the ESP length $L$ is:
$$t_g = \frac{L}{u}$$

**Step 2 — Required Transverse Drift**
The total transverse distance available to a particle is $b/2$, where $b$ is the spacing between adjacent collecting plates (typically 200–400 mm). The required drift velocity to reach the plate is:
$$w = \frac{b/2}{t_g} = \frac{b\,u}{2\,L}$$

**Step 3 — Probability of Collection**
For a particle of drift velocity $w_{\text{actual}}$ through a collection length $L$, the probability it is collected is the ratio:
$$P_{\text{coll}} = \frac{2\,L\,w_{\text{actual}}}{b\,u}$$

**Step 4 — Plug-Flow Probability Density**
Consider an ensemble of particles at position $x$ in the duct. The fraction remaining uncollected after traversing an infinitesimal length $dx$ is:
$$\frac{dn}{n} = -\frac{w_{\text{actual}}}{u} \cdot \frac{2}{b}\,dx$$

**Step 5 — Integration Over Total ESP Length**
Integrating from $x = 0$ to $x = L$:
$$\int_{n_0}^{n_L} \frac{dn}{n} = -\frac{2\,w}{b\,u}\int_{0}^{L} dx$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{n_L}{n_0}\right) = -\frac{2\,L\,w}{b\,u}$$

**Step 6 — Express in Terms of Plate Area and Gas Flow**
The total collecting plate area $A = n_p \cdot b \cdot L$ (where $n_p$ = number of plates), and the gas flow rate $Q = A_{\text{duct}} \cdot u$. Substituting:
$$\frac{2\,L}{b\,u} = \frac{A}{Q \cdot n_p \cdot b} \cdot \frac{2 \cdot b}{1} = \frac{A}{Q\,b \cdot n_p} \cdot 2$$

Standard simplification (recognising $b \cdot n_p \approx$ projected width) yields:
$$\eta_{\text{ESP}} = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Deutsch–Anderson Equation:**
> $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{ESP}} = 1 - e^{-\frac{wA}{Q}}}$$
> This single equation is the **design cornerstone** of every ESP installed in KTU-affiliated power engineering problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — CPCB Stack Height for SO₂ Dispersion

**Context:** The stack must be tall enough that the maximum ground-level concentration (GLC) of SO₂ does not exceed the **National Ambient Air Quality Standard (NAAQS)** of **80 μg/m³ (24-hr)**.

**Step 1 — Gaussian Plume Model for Centreline GLC**
For a continuous point source with effective stack height $H_e$ and downwind distance $x$:
$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q_s}{\pi \, u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H_e^{2}}{2\sigma_z^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Maximum GLC Condition**
Differentiating with respect to $x$ and setting $\partial C / \partial x = 0$ gives the **critical downwind distance**:
$$\sigma_z(x_{\max}) = \frac{H_e}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Pasquill–Gifford Class B–D Correction**
Empirically, $\sigma_z \approx 0.08\,x^{0.9}$ for neutral stability. Substituting and equating to the critical value:
$$0.08\,x_{\max}^{0.9} = \frac{H_e}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Empirical Reduction to CPCB Form**
The CPCB committee, after extensive field trials, condensed the Gaussian derivation into the **engineering formula** used in all Indian TPPs:

**Case A — When $Q_s < 80$ t/day:**
$$\boxed{H = 14\,\sqrt{Q_s}}$$
where $H$ is in metres, $Q_s$ is the daily SO₂ emission rate in tonnes/day.

**Case B — When $Q_s \geq 80$ t/day:**
$$\boxed{H = 74\,Q_s^{0.27}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two cases?** The square-root form over-predicts height for very large plants; the exponential decay model fits field data better for $Q_s > 80$ t/d.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** A 500 MW thermal power plant burns coal with **2.0% sulphur** and **40% ash** at a rate of **200 t/h**. Combustion is complete, fly-ash fraction is **80%**, and ESP efficiency is **99.5%**. Calculate:
- (i) Daily SO₂ emission in t/day.
- (ii) Daily fly-ash emission (mass) to atmosphere.
- (iii) Minimum stack height.

**Step 1 — SO₂ Mass Generation**
Sulphur fed per hour = $200 \times 0.02 = 4.0$ t/h.
Stoichiometric conversion: $S + O_2 \to SO_2$ (mass ratio SO₂/S = 64/32 = 2.0).
$$\dot{m}_{SO_2} = 4.0 \times 2.0 = 8.0 \text{ t/h}$$

**Step 2 — Daily SO₂ Emission**
$$Q_s = 8.0 \times 24 = 192 \text{ t/day}$$

**Step 3 — Fly-Ash Generation**
Total ash fed = $200 \times 0.40 = 80$ t/h.
Fly-ash fraction (carried by flue gas) = 80% of total ash.
$$\dot{m}_{\text{fly ash}} = 80 \times 0.80 = 64 \text{ t/h}$$

**Step 4 — Fly-Ash Emission After ESP**
Penetration $P_t = 1 - 0.995 = 0.005$.
$$\dot{m}_{\text{emitted}} = 64 \times 0.005 = 0.32 \text{ t/h}$$
Daily: $0.32 \times 24 = 7.68$ t/day emitted to atmosphere.

**Step 5 — Stack Height**
Since $Q_s = 192$ t/day $> 80$ t/day, use Case B:
$$H = 74 \times (192)^{0.27}$$

Computing: $\ln(192) = 5.257$, so $192^{0.27} = e^{0.27 \times 5.257} = e^{1.4194} = 4.134$.
$$H = 74 \times 4.134 = 305.9 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Real plants of this capacity use stacks of **220–275 m**, supplemented by **SCR + FGD** to reduce $Q_s$ and thus permit shorter stacks while complying with the 100 mg/Nm³ SO₂ standard.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Comparison of Air Pollution Control Devices (APCDs)

| Device | Target Pollutant | Typical Efficiency | Pressure Drop (mmWC) | Operating Cost | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ESP (Electrostatic Preciprator)** | PM₁₀, PM₂.₅ | 99.5 – 99.9% | 10 – 25 | Medium | **Very High** (4-mark Q) |
| **Baghouse (Fabric Filter)** | PM (all sizes) | 99.9%+ | 100 – 250 | Medium-High | High |
| **Wet Limestone FGD** | SO₂ | 90 – 98% | 150 – 300 | High (gypsum by-prod) | **Very High** |
| **Dry Sorbent Injection (DSI)** | SO₂, SO₃, HCl | 70 – 90% | Negligible | Low | Moderate |
| **SCR** | NOx | 80 – 95% | 30 – 50 | High (NH₃/V₂O₅) | Very High |
| **SNCR** | NOx | 30 – 60% | Negligible | Low-Moderate | Moderate |
| **Low-NOx Burner (LNB)** | NOx (formation) | 30 – 50% | Negligible | Low | High |
| **Wet Scrubber (Venturi)** | PM + SO₂ | 95% PM, 80% SO₂ | 250 – 750 | High | Moderate |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define particulate matter (PM) and distinguish between PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅. Why is PM₂.₅ more harmful to human health?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Particulate matter is the collective term for solid and liquid particles suspended in the flue gas stream, originating from incomplete combustion, fly-ash entrainment, and condensation of volatile metallic species.
- **Distinction (1 mark):** **PM₁₀** = particles with aerodynamic diameter ≤ 10 μm (retained in upper respiratory tract). **PM₂.₅** = particles ≤ 2.5 μm (penetrate deep into alveolar region of lungs).
- **Harmfulness (1 mark):** PM₂.₅ has a higher surface-area-to-mass ratio, adsorbs toxic heavy metals (As, Cd, Hg) and PAHs (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons), and reaches the bloodstream via alveolar gas exchange, causing **COPD, bronchitis, lung cancer, and cardiovascular mortality**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*State the Deutsch–Anderson equation for ESP efficiency and explain the significance of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Equation (1.5 marks):**
$$\eta_{ESP} = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$$
- **Term significance (1.5 marks):**
  - $w$ = **drift velocity** of charged particle (m/s) — increases with particle size, electric field, and decreases with gas viscosity.
  - $A$ = **total collecting plate area** (m²) — designer parameter, increased by adding fields.
  - $Q$ = **gas volumetric flow rate** (m³/s) — fixed by boiler load.
  - $wA/Q$ is the dimensionless "specific collection area" (SCA). For 99.9% efficiency, $wA/Q \approx 6.9$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain with a neat schematic the working principle of a Wet Limestone Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD) system. List the major chemical reactions involved and state the typical removal efficiency achieved.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A 210 MW thermal power plant uses coal containing 1.5% sulphur and 38% ash at a consumption rate of 110 t/h. The ESP has an efficiency of 99.4%. Calculate:*
*(i) Daily SO₂ emission in t/day.*
*(ii) Daily particulate matter emitted to the atmosphere.*
*(iii) The minimum stack height as per CPCB norms.*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Wet Limestone FGD (7 marks):**

- **Principle (2 marks):** SO₂ from flue gas is absorbed by a limestone (CaCO₃) slurry in a spray tower. The gas–liquid contact occurs on packing or in a venturi section. The SO₂ reacts to form calcium sulphite, which is oxidised to gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) — a saleable by-product for cement industry.

- **Major Reactions (2.5 marks):**
$$\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_3\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$$

$$\text{CaSO}_3\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4\cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{SO}_3 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4\cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$$

- **Efficiency (1 mark):** 92 – 98% SO₂ removal; outlet SO₂ typically 100–200 mg/Nm³ (down from 2000–3000 mg/Nm³).

- **Schematic (1.5 marks):** Refer to FGD block diagram in Section 4.3 — pre-cooler → absorber → oxidation → gypsum dewatering.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Daily SO₂ Emission:**
- Sulphur feed: $110 \times 0.015 = 1.65$ t/h.
- SO₂ generated (mass ratio 2.0): $1.65 \times 2.0 = 3.30$ t/h.
- Daily: $Q_s = 3.30 \times 24 = 79.2$ t/day.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Daily PM Emission:**
- Total ash: $110 \times 0.38 = 41.8$ t/h.
- Fly-ash fraction: $41.8 \times 0.80 = 33.44$ t/h.
- ESP penetration: $1 - 0.994 = 0.006$.
- Emitted: $33.44 \times 0.006 = 0.2006$ t/h.
- Daily: $0.2006 \times 24 = 4.815$ t/day.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Stack Height:**
Since $Q_s = 79.2$ t/day $< 80$ t/day → use **Case A**:
$$H = 14\sqrt{79.2} = 14 \times 8.900 = 124.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Final Answer Summary:**
- Daily SO₂ = **79.2 t/day**
- Daily PM emitted = **4.815 t/day**
- Minimum stack height = **≈ 125 m**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students use Case B (exponential form) when $Q_s < 80$ t/day, leading to a **wrong stack height and 1–2 marks lost**. Always check the CPCB threshold! Also, students frequently forget to multiply fly-ash by the **80% carryover fraction**, assuming total ash equals PM emission — a direct 1-mark deduction.

---

#### **Question B — Alternative Choice (Same 14-Mark Pattern)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat block diagram, describe the working of an Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP). Derive the Deutsch–Anderson equation and discuss the factors affecting ESP efficiency.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A 500 MW thermal power plant emits 1.5 t/h of SO₂ and 0.45 t/h of particulate matter. The heat rate is 2300 kcal/kWh. Calculate:*
*(i) The specific CO₂ emission in kg/kWh, assuming coal is 100% carbon with no unburnt loss.*
*(ii) The stack height as per CPCB norms.*
*(iii) The PM concentration in mg/Nm³ at the ESP outlet, given a flue gas flow of 8,00,000 Nm³/h.*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) ESP Working and Deutsch–Anderson (7 marks):**

- **Working (2.5 marks):** Dirty flue gas enters the ESP and passes over negatively charged discharge electrodes. Corona discharge generates electrons that attach to dust particles, giving them a net negative charge. The charged particles drift toward the positively charged collecting plates and are deposited. Periodic rapping dislodges the collected dust into hoppers. Clean gas exits to the stack.

- **Derivation (3 marks):** As derived in **Section 3.1** of these notes:
$$\eta_{ESP} = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$$

- **Factors (1.5 marks):** Particle resistivity (optimum 10⁴ – 10¹¹ Ω·cm), gas temperature, gas velocity, plate spacing, electrode design, dust loading, and rapping intensity.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Specific CO₂ Emission:**
Using the empirical formula $E_{CO_2} = 0.785 \times \dfrac{HR}{1000}$:
$$E_{CO_2} = 0.785 \times \dfrac{2300}{1000} = 1.806 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Step 2 (2.5 marks) — Stack Height:**
- Daily SO₂: $Q_s = 1.5 \times 24 = 36$ t/day.
- Since $Q_s < 80$ t/day → **Case A**:
$$H = 14\sqrt{36} = 14 \times 6 = 84 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 (2.5 marks) — PM Concentration at ESP Outlet:**
Concentration (mass/volume) using the formula from Section 2.4:
$$C_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_p \times 10^6}{\dot{V}_g}$$

$$C_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{0.45 \times 10^6}{8{,}00{,}000} = 0.5625 \text{ mg/Nm}^3$$

This is well below the 30 mg/Nm³ CPCB limit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** When asked to convert emission rate (kg/h) to concentration (mg/Nm³), students forget the factor of $10^6$ for unit conversion. Conversely, going from mg/Nm³ to kg/h requires the **inverse** — the most-derivation mistake in KTU valuation for **2-mark deductions**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU PEMET631 Module 4**

- **Pollution types:** Air > Water > Land > Noise. Identify the source in each system (boiler, condenser, ash silo, mills).
- **Air pollutants from coal:** SO₂ (S fuel + O₂), NOx (Zeldovich thermal), PM (fly ash), CO₂ (complete combustion of C), Hg (trace metal).
- **Master the 4-step approach** for any pollution problem: (1) Identify pollutant, (2) Write formation/generation rate, (3) Apply control device efficiency, (4) Compute emission.
- **Deutsch–Anderson equation** is **mandatory**: $\eta = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$. Memorise the "specific collection area" concept.
- **CPCB stack height formula** — **two cases, threshold at 80 t/day of SO₂.** Square root below, exponential above.
- **ESP vs Baghouse:** ESP = dry, low pressure drop, suitable for high-T gases. Baghouse = high efficiency, fabric limited to 200 °C, used as backup.
- **FGD by-product** is **gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O)**, marketable to cement industry — important 2-mark point.
- **SCR vs SNCR:** SCR uses catalyst (V₂O₅/TiO₂) at 300–400 °C, 80–95% efficiency. SNCR uses urea/ammonia at 850–1100 °C without catalyst, 30–60% efficiency.
- **Fly ash utilisation** is mandatory under Indian notification — mention cement, bricks, road embankment, mine void filling.
- **Thermal discharge rule:** ΔT ≤ 7 °C above intake water (CPCB).
- **Standard unit:** Always express emissions in **mg/Nm³ (dry, 6% O₂)** for Indian context.
- **CPCB + MoEFCC + CEA** = key Indian regulatory bodies (frequent 3-mark question).
- **Common KTU mistakes:** (a) Wrong case in stack height formula, (b) forgetting 80% fly-ash carryover, (c) missing unit conversion in concentration calculations, (d) confusing SCR and SNCR operating temperatures.
- **Current trend (2024–25):** Carbon Capture, Utilisation & Storage (CCUS) for net-zero goals, AMI (Activated Mercury Injection) systems, and ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge) policy by 2030.
